cards.description:
The Multi-Instance Support section of README.md currently says:

> File watching: detects changes written by other TUI or MCP instances

This is misleading. The TUI is the only component that holds board state in memory across interactions, so it is the only one that needs a file watcher. MCP tool calls are stateless and one-shot: each call loads the file, applies a mutation, writes atomically, and exits. The CLI works the same way.

The accurate framing is: the TUI watches for writes from any external writer, which includes MCP tool calls, CLI commands, and other TUI windows. MCP is a writer that the TUI watches for, not itself a watcher.

Fix the description to reflect this accurately. Suggested wording:

> File watching: TUI detects changes written by any external writer (CLI commands, MCP tool calls, another TUI window)
cards.title: clarify README: TUI is the only file watcher; MCP and CLI are external writers

cards.description:
## Summary

Replace the project's blanket "prefer `&str` over `String`" rule with a nuanced, behaviour-driven rule, and apply it to `kanban-domain` constructors and setters. The previous framing of this card ("switch everything to `&str`") was wrong — it would force internal allocations in store-this constructors even when callers already own a `String`, which is the opposite of zero-cost.

---

## Motivation

### What's wrong today

1. **The CONTRIBUTING.md rule is too coarse.** Line 58 says: *"Prefer `&str` over `String` for function parameters."* That's correct for read-only inspection, but misleading for constructors and unconditional setters that always store the value. The rule, applied uniformly, makes things worse for the common case.

2. **`kanban-domain` constructors lie about intent.** Methods like `Card::new(title: String, ...)` and `Board::update_name(name: String)` advertise that they want ownership, but the body just stores the field. That's fine when the caller already holds a `String`, but call sites holding a `&str` (tests, string literals, borrowed fields) have to write `.to_string()` at every call. The noise accumulates.

3. **There is no documented heuristic** for *when* to prefer each variant, so contributors (including future-Claude) end up either following the misleading blanket rule or guessing.

### Why the previous "&str everywhere" answer is wrong

Mapping every store-this signature to `&str` forces one allocation inside the function — *every call*, even when the caller already owns a `String` (which is the dominant case in this codebase because most values come from clap, serde, or the editor as owned `String`). That's a regression for the common path and a no-op for the rare path. The "win" of removing `.to_string()` at the call site is real but small, and is better captured by `impl Into<String>`.

### Why now

- The rule lives in the docs already; either fix the code to match, or fix the docs to match reality. Drift between docs and code is its own form of debt.
- The fix is mechanical and compiler-driven.
- Cost is negligible at human-interaction rates (kanban operations are gated by file I/O and terminal redraw, not allocator throughput).
- The signature change unblocks future zero-copy paths (`&'static str` constants, `Cow<'_, str>`) without locking the API.

---

## Idiomatic Rust: the nuanced rule

Per Rust community consensus (sources below):

| Function behaviour | Idiomatic parameter type |
|---|---|
| Read-only inspection / parsing / logging | `&str` |
| Validate-then-maybe-store (rejection possible) | `&str` |
| Always store (constructors, unconditional setters) | `String` or `impl Into<String>` |
| In-place mutation of existing string | `&mut String` |
| Return value | `String` |

**`impl AsRef<str>` is deliberately omitted.** Community sentiment is strongly against it: signature clutter, monomorphization cost, and confusing move semantics when the caller forgets the `&`. One Rust forum contributor wanted Clippy to lint against ever using it.

**Why `impl Into<String>` for store-this constructors:**
- `String` callers: `into()` is a no-op, zero new allocations.
- `&str` and literal callers: one allocation inside, but no `.to_string()` at the call site.
- Monomorphized at compile time, no dynamic dispatch.
- Net diff at call sites is usually negative (chars removed).

---

## Suggested change

### Step 1 — Update `CONTRIBUTING.md`

Replace the single line at `CONTRIBUTING.md:58`:

```diff
- - Prefer `&str` over `String` for function parameters
+ - Choose string parameter types by what the function does with the value:
+   - Read-only inspection / parsing: `&str`
+   - Validate-then-maybe-store (rejection possible): `&str`
+   - Always store (constructors, unconditional setters): `impl Into<String>`
+     (or plain `String` if the call site always passes owned data)
+   - In-place mutation: `&mut String`
+   - Avoid `impl AsRef<str>` — signature clutter without ergonomic gain
```

This anchors the rule in the docs before we touch any code, so the rationale is discoverable.

### Step 2 — Apply `impl Into<String>` to unconditional-store domain methods

#### `crates/kanban-domain/src/card.rs`

```rust
// Before
pub fn new(board: &mut Board, column_id: ColumnId, title: String, position: i32) -> Self {
    Self { title, /* ... */ }
}
pub fn update_title(&mut self, title: String) { self.title = title; }
pub fn update_description(&mut self, description: Option<String>) { self.description = description; }
pub fn assign_to_sprint(
    &mut self, ...,
    sprint_name: Option<String>,
    sprint_status: String,
    ...
) { /* stores both unconditionally */ }

// After
pub fn new(board: &mut Board, column_id: ColumnId, title: impl Into<String>, position: i32) -> Self {
    Self { title: title.into(), /* ... */ }
}
pub fn update_title(&mut self, title: impl Into<String>) { self.title = title.into(); }
pub fn update_description(&mut self, description: Option<impl Into<String>>) {
    self.description = description.map(Into::into);
}
pub fn assign_to_sprint(
    &mut self, ...,
    sprint_name: Option<impl Into<String>>,
    sprint_status: impl Into<String>,
    ...
) { /* .into() at each store site */ }
```

#### `crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs`

```rust
// Before
pub fn new(name: String, card_prefix: Option<String>) -> Self { ... }
pub fn update_name(&mut self, name: String) { self.name = name; }
pub fn update_description(&mut self, description: Option<String>) { self.description = description; }

// After
pub fn new(name: impl Into<String>, card_prefix: Option<impl Into<String>>) -> Self { ... }
pub fn update_name(&mut self, name: impl Into<String>) { self.name = name.into(); }
pub fn update_description(&mut self, description: Option<impl Into<String>>) {
    self.description = description.map(Into::into);
}
```

#### `crates/kanban-domain/src/column.rs`

```rust
// Before
pub fn new(board_id: BoardId, name: String, position: i32) -> Self { ... }
pub fn update_name(&mut self, name: String) { self.name = name; }

// After
pub fn new(board_id: BoardId, name: impl Into<String>, position: i32) -> Self { ... }
pub fn update_name(&mut self, name: impl Into<String>) { self.name = name.into(); }
```

#### `crates/kanban-domain/src/sprint.rs`

```rust
// Before
pub fn update_prefix(&mut self, prefix: Option<String>) { self.prefix = prefix; }

// After
pub fn update_prefix(&mut self, prefix: Option<impl Into<String>>) {
    self.prefix = prefix.map(Into::into);
}
```

#### `crates/kanban-domain/src/tag.rs`

```rust
// Before
pub fn new(name: String, color: String) -> Self { ... }

// After
pub fn new(name: impl Into<String>, color: impl Into<String>) -> Self { ... }
```

### Step 3 — Audit the rest

The 11 sites above came from a spot-check. Surface the remaining offenders with:

```bash
rg "pub fn.*: (String|Option<String>)" crates/kanban-domain/src/ --type rust
```

For each hit, classify:
- **Unconditional store** → `impl Into<String>`
- **Validate-then-maybe-store** → `&str` (don't take ownership you might throw away)
- **Read-only inspection** → `&str`
- **Already correct (e.g. command structs as serde DTOs)** → leave alone

### Step 4 — Clean call sites

Once the signatures change, the compiler will flag callers that still have a redundant `.to_string()` on a `&str` argument. Remove those. Call sites holding a `String` keep working unchanged because `String: Into<String>` is a no-op.

### Step 5 — Sibling refactor (out of scope, follow-up card)

Service-layer methods that thinly wrap domain constructors should follow the same convention once the domain is updated. Track separately to keep this PR focused.

---

## Out of scope

- Command structs (`CreateCard { title: String, ... }`, etc.) — these are serde DTOs and need owned `String` fields for deserialization. Leave as `String`.
- Service-layer and CLI argument parsing — separate sweep.
- Introducing `Cow<'_, str>` anywhere — bigger design question, not part of this card.

---

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `CONTRIBUTING.md:58` rewritten to the nuanced rule (see Step 1 diff)
- [ ] All unconditional-store domain constructors and setters in `kanban-domain/src/` take `impl Into<String>` (or `Option<impl Into<String>>`)
- [ ] Validate-then-maybe-store domain methods (if any) take `&str`
- [ ] No `pub fn` in `kanban-domain/src/` takes bare `String` for a parameter that is unconditionally moved into storage
- [ ] No `impl AsRef<str>` introduced anywhere in this PR
- [ ] Redundant `.to_string()` removed from call sites where the new signature makes it unnecessary
- [ ] All `kanban-domain` tests pass
- [ ] `cargo clippy --workspace -- -D warnings` is clean

---

## Sources

- [Rust: using `String` and `&str` in your APIs (dx13, 2024)](https://dx13.co.uk/articles/2024/02/24/rust-string-and-str/) — recommends `impl Into<String>` for constructors; explains the no-op-on-`String` / allocate-on-`&str` mechanic
- [Best practices for string argument types — Rust users forum](https://users.rust-lang.org/t/best-practices-for-string-argument-types/68411) — community consensus against `AsRef<str>`, in favour of plain `String` or `Into<String>` for store-this
- [Idiomatic string parameter types — Rust users forum](https://users.rust-lang.org/t/idiomatic-string-parmeter-types-str-vs-asref-str-vs-into-string/7934) — monomorphization and performance notes
- [Rust API Guidelines: Predictability](https://rust-lang.github.io/api-guidelines/predictability.html) — official guidance on conversion-trait parameters and when to use `From`/`Into`
- [Design of Rust APIs (AsRef, Into, Cow) — Phil's Blog](https://www.philipdaniels.com/blog/2019/rust-api-design/) — taxonomy of conversion traits and when each is appropriate

cards.description:
## Problem

The CLI currently returns raw or unhelpful errors when given wrong input. Example: passing `--status done` to `card update` (which has no such flag) gives a generic clap error with no hint of what to do instead. First-time users hitting these errors get no guidance.

## Goal

Every error a user can trigger should be:
- **Clear**: say what went wrong in plain language
- **Actionable**: suggest the correct command or flag
- **Consistent**: same tone and format across all subcommands

## Areas to audit

- Unknown/invalid flags (e.g. `--status` on `card update`)
- Wrong argument types (e.g. non-UUID where UUID expected)
- Entity not found (board, column, card, sprint) — include the identifier that was looked up
- Missing required args — name the arg and show an example
- File not found / unreadable kanban.json
- Permission errors on kanban.json

## Approach

1. Audit all CLI subcommands for error paths that reach the user
2. For each: write a test that triggers the error, assert on the message
3. Improve the message to be clear and actionable
4. Consider a shared error formatting layer in `kanban-cli` so tone stays consistent
cards.title: Improve CLI error messages and user-facing error handling

The ci woes continue

The current progress is that of 

---

  New Idempotent Release Pipeline

  Two new minimal scripts:
  - aggregate-changesets.sh - Finds highest priority bump type (major > minor > patch)
  - update-changelog.sh - Merges changesets into CHANGELOG.md with version header

  Updated workflows:

  1. aggregate-changesets.yml (on develop PR close):
    - Aggregates all changesets into CHANGELOG.md
    - Single version bump per release cycle
    - Cleans up changesets
    - Commits to develop
  2. release.yml (on master PR close):
    - Compares Cargo.toml version vs last git tag
    - If version changed → publish & tag
    - If version unchanged → skip (idempotent!)
    - No changeset checking, no pushing back to master

  Benefits:
  - ✅ Idempotent: Version is source of truth, safe to re-run
  - ✅ Maintainable: Simple version comparison logic
  - ✅ Single version bump: One bump per release cycle
  - ✅ Changelog preserved: Full history in CHANGELOG.md
  - ✅ Zero race conditions: No pushing to trigger branch

---

Moving cards to and from columns also seem broken
cards.title: Fix the CI

cards.description:
## Goal

CI handles the compute-heavy recording. The human reviews the output before it ships. No auto-commit.

On every change to `demo/demo.tape` or `demo/fixtures/**`, CI records `demo.gif` and uploads it as a downloadable artifact. To ship it, run the workflow manually with `commit: true` — an explicit gate that separates "record for review" from "record and push".

---

## Trigger

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches: [master, develop]
    paths:
      - 'demo/demo.tape'
      - 'demo/fixtures/**'
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      commit:
        description: 'Commit and push demo.gif after recording (only after manual review)'
        type: boolean
        default: false
```

---

## Implementation

### Nix shell

`demo/shell.nix` already provides everything needed — `vhs`, `neovim`, and the `kanban` binary — via `nix develop .#demo`. No extra apt installs required.

### EDITOR env var

`demo/shell.nix`'s `shellHook` sets `EDITOR` to `demo/nvim-editor.sh`, but shellHooks don't run under `nix develop --command`. Set it explicitly in the job:

```yaml
env:
  EDITOR: ${{ github.workspace }}/demo/nvim-editor.sh
```

### Record and upload artifact

```yaml
- name: Record demo
  run: nix develop .#demo --command bash demo/record.sh

- name: Upload demo.gif artifact
  uses: actions/upload-artifact@v4
  with:
    name: demo-gif
    path: demo/demo.gif
    retention-days: 14
```

### Commit and push (only when commit input is true)

```yaml
- name: Setup SSH deploy key
  if: inputs.commit == true
  run: |
    mkdir -p ~/.ssh
    echo "${{ secrets.DEPLOY_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/deploy_key
    chmod 600 ~/.ssh/deploy_key
    ssh-keyscan github.com >> ~/.ssh/known_hosts

- name: Configure Git
  if: inputs.commit == true
  run: |
    git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
    git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
    git remote set-url origin git@github.com:fulsomenko/kanban.git

- name: Commit and push demo.gif
  if: inputs.commit == true
  env:
    GIT_SSH_COMMAND: "ssh -i ~/.ssh/deploy_key -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no"
  run: |
    git add demo/demo.gif
    if git diff --cached --quiet; then
      echo "demo.gif unchanged, skipping commit"
    else
      git commit -m "chore(demo): re-record demo.gif"
      git push origin HEAD
    fi
```

---

## Workflow

1. Push a tape/fixture change → CI records → download artifact from Actions → watch it
2. Looks good → re-run workflow with `commit: true` → GIF pushed to repo
3. Looks broken → fix the tape/fixture → repeat from step 1

---

## Why NOT to auto-commit on tape change

The demo GIF is a quality signal. A release that introduces a TUI regression (e.g. the selector not jumping to a newly created card — KAN-403) would cause the GIF to silently record and ship broken behaviour. Re-recording must be a deliberate, verified act.

---

## Risk: GIF non-determinism

VHS output may differ pixel-by-pixel between runs even with identical input if system fonts differ. With Nix pinning the entire toolchain (vhs, neovim, fonts), output should be reproducible. Validate on first run: record once locally and once in CI, then compare. Non-determinism here only affects the artifact, not the repo — no spurious commits possible with this design.

---

## New file

`.github/workflows/demo.yml`
cards.title: CI job: auto-record and commit demo.gif on tape changes

# At the board level. Be able to define columns

Default columns when creating a new board
---
- TODO
- Doing
- Complete

Features
---
- Be able to change the name of columns
- Be able to change order of columns
- Be able to add new columns
- Be able to remove columns
- Be able to add tasks to column
- Using 'c' is equivalent to moving a card to the last column. If there is only one column in a board, we can only set complete manually like the current implementation does.

UI
---
- hitting `c` in Board Details opens up Columns. In columns we see one panel listing the columns in order.
- In the 

### Task list

- Be able to view tasks in column view grouped by columns
- Be able to view tasks in list view grouped by columns
- Be able to view the task list without columns (current behavior)
- Extend the task list to have column task list
- Extend the task list to have list grouped by columns
- Choose which view is active in the task list panel by opening a list dialogue with the choices with `v`. The current view is saved to the board and loaded on startup.

Dupe of `KAN-31/update-status-of-a-card-board-columns`

Implementation
---
Implementation Plan: Column Definitions at Board Level                                                                               │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
Overview                                                                                                                             │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
Add full column management capabilities to the kanban board, allowing users to define, edit, reorder, and manage columns at the      │ │
board level. This includes default columns on board creation, a dedicated column management UI, and integration with task completion │ │
 workflows.                                                                                                                          │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
Architecture Changes                                                                                                                 │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
1. Domain Layer (kanban-domain)                                                                                                      │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
File: crates/kanban-domain/src/column.rs                                                                                             │ │
- Add update_name(&mut self, name: String) method                                                                                    │ │
- Add validation for column names (non-empty, unique within board)                                                                   │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
File: crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs                                                                                              │ │
- Add default_column_definitions: Vec<String> field (default: ["TODO", "Doing", "Complete"])                                         │ │
- Add task_list_view: TaskListView enum field (default: TaskListView::Flat)                                                          │ │
- Add methods for column management at board level                                                                                   │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
New: crates/kanban-domain/src/task_list_view.rs                                                                                      │ │
- Define TaskListView enum: Flat, GroupedByColumn, ColumnView                                                                        │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
2. Application State (kanban-tui/src/app.rs)                                                                                         │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
Add new AppMode variants:                                                                                                            │ │
- ColumnManagement - Main column management view                                                                                     │ │

- RenameColumn - Dialog for renaming column                                                                                          │ │

Add to App struct:                                                                                                                   │ │

- active_column_index: Option<usize> - Currently selected column                                                                     │ │
- task_list_view_selection: SelectionState - For view selection popup                                                                │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │

Add rendering functions:                                                                                                             │ │

Column Management View                                                                                                               │ │

- n - Create new column                                                                                                              │ │
- r - Rename selected column                                                                                                         │ │

- j/k or ↓/↑ - Navigate columns                                                                                                      │ │
- J or Ctrl+Down - Move column down in order                                                                                         │ │
- K or Ctrl+Up - Move column up in order                                                                                             │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
Task List View                                                                                                                       │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
- v - Open view selection dialog (Flat / Grouped by Column / Column View)                                                            │ │

                                                                                                                                     │ │
Implementation Sequence                                                                                                              │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
1. Domain models - Update Board, Column, add TaskListView enum                                                                       │ │
2. Board initialization - Auto-create default columns on board creation                                                              │ │
3. App state - Add new modes, focus types, selections                                                                                │ │
4. Column handlers - Implement all column management logic                                                                           │ │
5. UI rendering - Add column management view and popups                                                                              │ │
6. Task list views - Implement three view modes (Flat, Grouped, Column)                                                              │ │
7. Card completion - Update to move to last column                                                                                   │ │
8. Export/Import - Update serialization to handle new fields                                                                         │ │
9. Testing - Verify column CRUD, reordering, view switching, card movement                                                           │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
Suggestions for Future Enhancements                                                                                                  │ │
                                                                                                                                     │ │
- WIP limits per column (already in Column model, just needs UI)                                                                     │ │
- Customizable column colors                                                                                                         │ │
- Archive columns instead of delete                                                                                                  │ │
- Column templates for new boards                                                                                                    │ │

Move Card
---

1. Manual Card Column Movement (H/L keys):
   - H (Shift+h) - Moves selected card to the previous (left) column
   - L (Shift+l) - Moves selected card to the next (right) column
   - Focus tracking: The card selection stays on the moved card after movement
   - Edge case handling: Warns if already at first/last column
 2. Updated Card Completion Behavior (c key):
   - When pressing c on a card:
       - If the board has multiple columns: Moves card to the last column AND marks it as Done
     - If the board has only 1 column: Just toggles the status (fallback behavior)
   - Works for both single card completion and multi-select completion
   - Cards moved to the last column are automatically marked as complete
 3. UI Updates:
   - Footer help text now shows: H/L: move card
   - All functionality is ready to use

 How It Works:

 The implementation intelligently:
 - Sorts columns by position to determine left/right
 - Calculates the new position in the target column (appends to end)
 - Updates the card's column_id and position
 - Re-sorts the card list and finds the moved card's new index
 - Updates the selection to track the moved card

 The code compiles successfully and is ready for testing! 🎉
 
 Three interfaces
 ---
 Implement task list view modes rendering:                                                                                            │ │

1. Refactor render_main function to check view mode:                                                                                 │ │
  - For Flat and Grouped views: Keep current 2-panel layout (Projects 30% | Tasks 70%)                                               │ │
  - For ColumnView/Kanban: Hide projects panel, use full width for kanban board                                                      │ │
  - Check board.task_list_view to determine layout                                                                                   │ │
2. Refactor render_tasks_panel function to route based on view:                                                                      │ │
  - Match on board.task_list_view enum                                                                                               │ │
  - Call appropriate rendering function:                                                                                             │ │
      - Flat → existing flat list logic                                                                                              │ │
    - GroupedByColumn → new grouped rendering                                                                                        │ │
    - ColumnView → new kanban rendering                                                                                              │ │
3. Implement Grouped by Column view (render_tasks_grouped_by_column):                                                                │ │
  - Get sorted columns for the board                                                                                                 │ │
  - For each column:                                                                                                                 │ │
      - Render column name as a header (styled/bold, e.g., "TODO (3 tasks)")                                                         │ │
    - Filter cards by column_id                                                                                                      │ │
    - Render cards under that header                                                                                                 │ │
  - Handle card selection across all groups (track global index)                                                                     │ │
4. Implement Column/Kanban view (render_tasks_kanban_view):                                                                          │ │
  - Takes full area (no projects panel)                                                                                              │ │
  - Split area into N vertical panels (one per column)                                                                               │ │
  - Calculate equal-width constraints dynamically based on column count                                                              │ │
  - For each column panel:                                                                                                           │ │
      - Render as bordered panel with column name as title                                                                           │ │
    - Filter cards by column_id                                                                                                      │ │
    - Render cards in that panel                                                                                                     │ │
  - Handle card selection (track which card across all columns in order)                                                             │ │
  - Navigation: j/k moves through all cards left-to-right, top-to-bottom                                                             │ │
5. Update card selection tracking:                                                                                                   │ │
  - Keep card_selection as simple index into flat sorted list                                                                        │ │
  - All views use same underlying sorted card list                                                                                   │ │
  - Just render differently based on view mode                                                                                       │ │
6. Handle edge cases:                                                                                                                │ │
  - Empty columns in grouped/kanban view (show placeholder text)                                                                     │ │
  - Selection persistence when switching views                                                                                       │ │
  - Proper focus indicators in all modes                                                                                             │ │
  - Projects panel focus: disabled in kanban mode (only Cards focus active)                                                          │ │

cards.description:
## Context
The current conflict resolution uses `LastWriteWinsResolver` in `kanban-persistence/src/conflict/resolver.rs`. When external file changes are detected via `FileWatcher` (`watch/file_watcher.rs`), the system compares `saved_at` timestamps from `PersistenceMetadata`.

## Current Behavior
1. `JsonFileStore::save()` checks `last_known_metadata` (Mutex-wrapped) before writing
2. If file changed externally, `KanbanError::ConflictDetected` is raised
3. TUI shows dialog: "Keep Local" or "Reload from Disk"
4. Last-write-wins means the newer `saved_at` timestamp wins

## Documentation Required
Create `docs/CONFLICT_RESOLUTION.md` covering:
- How conflict detection works (mtime + size + content hash triple-check)
- Scenarios where data loss occurs:
  - Two instances edit different cards simultaneously → one set of changes lost
  - Rapid saves within same second → timestamp collision possible
  - Network filesystems with clock skew
- Why proper solutions (OT/CRDT) are complex for this use case
- Recommendation: "Single-instance recommended for data integrity"

## Files to Reference
- `crates/kanban-persistence/src/conflict/detector.rs` - FileMetadata struct
- `crates/kanban-persistence/src/conflict/resolver.rs` - LastWriteWinsResolver
- `crates/kanban-persistence/src/watch/file_watcher.rs` - ChangeDetector trait
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app.rs` - conflict dialog handling

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] New docs/CONFLICT_RESOLUTION.md with clear warnings
- [ ] README.md updated with link and brief warning
- [ ] Code comments in resolver.rs explaining limitations
cards.title: Document conflict resolution limitations

cards.description:
## Context
CLI outputs JSON for scripting, but schema is undocumented. Users need stable guarantees.

## Current JSON Structures (from codebase analysis)

### Board Export
Fields: id (uuid), name, description, sprint_prefix, card_prefix, task_sort_field (enum), task_sort_order (enum), prefix_counters (map), sprint_counters (map), created_at, updated_at

### Card Structure
Fields: id, column_id, title, description, priority (Low|Medium|High|Critical), status (Todo|InProgress|Blocked|Done), position, points (0-255), card_number, sprint_id, assigned_prefix, card_prefix, sprint_logs, completed_at, created_at, updated_at

### Column Structure
Fields: id, board_id, name, position, wip_limit, created_at, updated_at

### Sprint Structure
Fields: id, board_id, sprint_number, name_index, prefix, card_prefix, status (Planning|Active|Completed|Cancelled), start_date, end_date, created_at, updated_at

## Documentation to Create
1. `docs/JSON_SCHEMA.md` - Human-readable with examples
2. `schemas/` directory with JSON Schema files for validation
3. Versioning strategy (SemVer for schema changes)

## Files to Create
- `docs/JSON_SCHEMA.md` - comprehensive schema docs
- `schemas/board.schema.json` - JSON Schema for Board
- `schemas/card.schema.json` - JSON Schema for Card
- `schemas/column.schema.json` - JSON Schema for Column
- `schemas/sprint.schema.json` - JSON Schema for Sprint
- `schemas/envelope.schema.json` - V2 file format wrapper

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All domain types documented with examples
- [ ] JSON Schema files for programmatic validation
- [ ] CLI `--schema` flag to output schema
- [ ] Version field in schema for compatibility tracking
cards.title: Document JSON export schema

cards.description:
Currently tracing is initialised in three separate, divergent places:

1. init_tracing_cli() — kanban-cli/src/app.rs
   - Writes to KANBAN_DEBUG_LOG file (if set) at debug level, otherwise stderr at warn level
   - No in-memory layer

2. init_tracing_tui() — kanban-cli/src/app.rs
   - Same file/stderr branching as CLI
   - Additionally layers InMemoryLogLayer (kanban-tui::error_log) for in-TUI error display
   - Takes Arc<Mutex<ErrorLogState>> as parameter

3. Inline in McpServer::run() — kanban-mcp/src/server.rs
   - Simpler: stderr only, no file fallback, info default level (not warn/debug)
   - No in-memory layer

Goal: extract a single shared init_tracing() that all three paths call, so cross-cutting startup logging (e.g. build info) has one place to live.

Suggested interface:
  fn init_tracing(in_memory: Option<Arc<Mutex<ErrorLogState>>>)

- None  -> CLI / MCP path (no in-memory layer)
- Some  -> TUI path (adds InMemoryLogLayer)

The MCP default level (info) vs CLI/TUI default (warn/debug) can be handled via an additional parameter or RUST_LOG env var — worth deciding at implementation time.

Where to put it: kanban-cli/src/app.rs is fine if MCP imports it, but a better home may be a new kanban-tracing (or kanban-logging) crate, or kanban-core if the dependency is acceptable. Decide at implementation time.

Once consolidated, add log_build_info() as the last call inside the shared function — this was the original motivation (KAN-421 scoped it out because the shared root didn't exist yet).
cards.title: Consolidate tracing init into a shared root function

cards.description:
## Context
Error handling uses `KanbanError` enum in `kanban-core/src/error.rs` with `thiserror`. Recovery procedures are undocumented.

## Error Types to Document
- Connection(String) - Future: DB/network errors
- NotFound(String) - Entity lookup failures
- Validation(String) - Invalid input
- Io(std::io::Error) - File operations
- Serialization(String) - JSON parse/write failures
- Internal(String) - Unexpected states
- ConflictDetected { path, source } - External file modification

## Failure Scenarios to Document

### 1. Corrupted JSON File
- Cause: Disk corruption, incomplete write, manual edit error
- Current behavior: Serialization error, app won't start
- Recovery: Check for .v1.backup, use kanban export, manual JSON repair

### 2. Disk Full During Atomic Write
- Cause: Filesystem at capacity during temp file write
- Current behavior: Io error in atomic_writer.rs
- Recovery: Original file preserved, free disk space and retry

### 3. External Editor Crash
- Cause: $EDITOR crashes during card description edit
- Current behavior: Edit cancelled, original preserved
- Recovery: Automatic - no data loss

### 4. File Permission Errors
- Cause: Read-only file, directory permissions
- Current behavior: Io error with OS message
- Recovery: chmod fix, check directory ownership

### 5. File Watcher Failure
- Cause: Too many watches (ulimit), unsupported filesystem
- Current behavior: Warning logged, continues without watching
- Recovery: Increase ulimit, use polling mode

## Documentation to Create
- `docs/ERROR_HANDLING.md` with all scenarios
- Troubleshooting section in README
- Error codes table with recovery steps

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] All KanbanError variants documented
- [ ] Recovery procedure for each failure mode
- [ ] Troubleshooting guide with common issues
- [ ] Error messages improved with actionable hints
cards.title: Document error handling behavior
Lift ImportEntities orchestration into service layer

cards.description:
Add developer-facing documentation covering how to capture and interpret debug logs.

Depends on KAN-422 (consolidated tracing init) being merged first — the docs should reflect the final, stable logging setup rather than the current three-path state.

## Suggested location

A new '## Debugging' section in CONTRIBUTING.md. If it grows large enough it can be extracted to a dedicated DEBUGGING.md and linked from CONTRIBUTING.md.

## Content to cover

### Capturing logs

Explain the two env vars:
- KANBAN_DEBUG_LOG=<path>  — redirects logs to a file (required for TUI, which owns the terminal)
- RUST_LOG=<level>         — controls verbosity (warn / debug / trace)

Show the canonical invocation:
  RUST_LOG=trace KANBAN_DEBUG_LOG=/tmp/kanban.log kanban my-board.json

### Log level reference

| Level | What is logged |
|-------|----------------|
| warn  | default — unexpected conditions only |
| debug | persistence events, file-watch, save coordination, build info (after KAN-422) |
| trace | everything above + raw key events (every keypress) |

### Reading key event traces

Explain the 'raw key event' trace lines: code, kind (Press/Release/Repeat), modifiers.
Show what a healthy vs broken Windows key filter looks like in the log (one line vs two per keypress).

### Useful grep patterns

  grep 'raw key event' /tmp/kanban.log
  grep -o 'kind=[^ ]*' /tmp/kanban.log | sort | uniq -c
  grep -v 'raw key event' /tmp/kanban.log   # suppress key noise, see everything else

### Cross-platform notes

Note that RUST_LOG=trace with the TUI running produces one trace line per keypress — this can be noisy in long sessions. Recommend grepping rather than reading raw.
cards.title: Add logging/debugging documentation
publish to crates.io

cards.description:
## Context
Claude is listed as a contributor. Transparency about AI-assisted development builds trust.

## Content to Add to CONTRIBUTING.md

### Section: "AI-Assisted Development"

This project uses AI assistance (Claude by Anthropic) for:
- Code generation and refactoring
- Documentation writing
- Test case generation
- Code review suggestions

### Review Process for AI-Generated Code
All AI-generated code undergoes the same review as human contributions:
1. Automated CI checks (clippy, tests, formatting)
2. Human review for correctness and style
3. Integration testing before merge

### Patterns to Watch For
AI-generated code may exhibit:
- Over-abstraction (unnecessary traits/generics)
- Verbose error handling
- Missing edge cases in generated tests
- Documentation that restates code instead of explaining intent

### Attribution
AI-assisted commits are attributed to the human who reviewed and approved them.
The AI tool is listed in commit trailers when significant generation occurred.

## Files to Modify
- `CONTRIBUTING.md` - add new section

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Clear disclosure of AI tool usage
- [ ] Review process documented
- [ ] Common AI patterns listed for reviewers
- [ ] Attribution policy clarified
cards.title: Add AI contribution disclosure to CONTRIBUTING.md
treesitter for syntax highlighting

cards.description:
## Problem

PR #254 (KAN-420) set `rust-version = "1.74"` in the workspace `Cargo.toml` and updated CONTRIBUTING.md to match, based on the MSRV of `ratatui 0.29` and `clap 4.5`. However, the actual dependency floor is higher.

## Root cause

The `arboard` crate (clipboard support, used by `kanban-tui`) pulls in `image`, which pulls in `moxcms` and `pxfm`, all of which declare `rust-version = "1.85"`. Confirmed via `cargo metadata`:

```
kanban-tui → arboard → image → moxcms → pxfm (rust-version = 1.85)
```

This means:
- The workspace declares 1.74 as the minimum
- Cargo enforces 1.74 at the workspace level and gives a clear error for anything below that
- But a build on 1.74–1.84 will still fail due to dependency `rust-version` declarations, just with a less actionable error from deep in the dependency tree rather than from our own workspace

## Context

Surfaced by a contributor hitting build failures on cargo 1.75.0. PR #254 addressed the symptom (1.70 was clearly wrong) but did not verify the full dependency MSRV floor via `cargo metadata`.

## Fix

- Bump `rust-version` in workspace `Cargo.toml` from `"1.74"` to `"1.85"`
- Update CONTRIBUTING.md prerequisites from `Rust 1.74+` to `Rust 1.85+`
cards.title: Fix minimum Rust version in CONTRIBUTING.md (1.74 is too low)

cards.description:
## Context
Current architecture (JSON + last-write-wins) fundamentally supports single-user only.
Roadmap mentions HTTP API, collaboration, sync - these require different foundations.

## Decision Framework

### Option A: Personal Tool (Recommended)
Keep current architecture, optimize for single-user experience:
- SQLite for performance (not collaboration)
- No server component
- File sync via external tools (Dropbox, git)
- Document limitations clearly

Implications: Remove collaboration from roadmap, focus on offline-first, simpler maintenance

### Option B: Team Tool
Rewrite for collaboration:
- PostgreSQL or similar server DB
- HTTP API with authentication
- Real-time sync (WebSockets)
- CRDT or OT for conflict resolution

Implications: 6-12 month rewrite, different deployment model, hosting concerns

### Option C: Hybrid
Personal-first with optional sync:
- SQLite locally
- Optional sync to remote server
- Conflict resolution for sync only

Implications: Complexity of both approaches, edge cases in sync logic

## Decision Process
1. Write ADR (Architecture Decision Record) in `docs/adr/`
2. Update README with chosen scope
3. Update roadmap to match
4. Close/adjust related issues

## Files to Create/Modify
- `docs/adr/001-project-scope.md` - new ADR
- `README.md` - update project description
- `ROADMAP.md` - align features with scope

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] ADR documenting decision and rationale
- [ ] README clearly states scope
- [ ] Roadmap updated to match
- [ ] Collaboration features removed or deferred
cards.title: Decide project scope: personal vs team tool

cards.description:
The behaviour of moving cards between columns isnt quite working. I have one card stuck in in doing and I cant move it with either `h`/`l` or `c`.

It doesnt seem to me to be working to move cards freely back and forth between the columns. something is off...

so I select the second column with `2` and then hit `e` to edit that cards description. It edits another card..

2025-10-20 17:39:14
---

Somewhere along the lines we lost the ability to move cards with `H` / `L`

Toggle complete also does not move said card into the last column

Fix this 

2025-10-21 23:14:58
---

I think it only doesnt happen in kanban view!

kanban/crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs, the handle_move_card_left() and handle_move_card_right()

handlers have a critical bug:

In kanban view (ColumnView), the code only updates column selection but never calls refresh_view():

if self.is_kanban_view() {
    if let Some(current_col_idx) = self.column_selection.get() {
        if current_col_idx > 0 {
            self.column_selection.set(Some(current_col_idx - 1));
        }
    }
} else {
    self.refresh_view();  // <-- This is only in the else branch!
    self.select_card_by_id(card_id);
}

This means:
- The card's column_id and position ARE updated in memory
- But the UI is NOT re-rendered to show the change
- The kanban view doesn't redraw with the card in its new location

In flat/grouped views, it works because refresh_view() IS called.

There may also be a secondary issue: the handlers depend on get_selected_card_in_context() returning a card, which relies on the view
strategy having a selected card. If card selection isn't being maintained properly, the handlers wouldn't execute at all.

Plan to Fix

Here's what needs to be done:

1. Add refresh_view() call to the kanban view branch in handle_move_card_left() (line ~349)
2. Add refresh_view() call to the kanban view branch in handle_move_card_right() (line ~405)
3. Add select_card_by_id() call after refresh in both handlers to maintain card selection
4. Verify that card selection is being properly initialized when switching views
5. Test the fix to ensure cards move and stay selected in all view modes

> TLDR; it does happen it just doesnt update

---

Remains to be tested how this all behaves in a one column board

cards.description:
## Problem
Import accepts corrupted JSON where references point to non-existent entities. Cards become invisible, relationships break.

## Impact
- Invisible cards (invalid column_id)
- Broken relationships (invalid sprint_id)
- Orphaned graph edges
- Silent data corruption

## Tasks
1. Create new validation module with comprehensive checks
2. Implement validate_referential_integrity() function:
   - Check card.column_id exists in columns
   - Check card.sprint_id exists in sprints
   - Check dependency graph edges reference existing cards
   - Check archived_cards reference valid columns
3. Call validation after import (fail import if invalid)
4. Call validation after load (warn only, don't fail)
5. Return detailed error messages listing all issues

## Files to Create/Modify
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/validation.rs` (NEW)
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/lib.rs` (add pub mod validation)
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/export/importer.rs`
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/state/mod.rs`

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Validation module created
- [ ] All 6 validation checks implemented
- [ ] Import fails with detailed errors for invalid data
- [ ] Load warns but doesn't fail
- [ ] Integration test: import_with_invalid_references_fails passes
- [ ] Error messages list all issues, not just first one
cards.title: Add Referential Integrity Validation on Import/Load

cards.description:
## Background

We've had two near-misses with the same failure class — tests that pass on regular Linux CI but fail in the Nix build sandbox:

1. **2026-05-07** — 5 tests in `crates/kanban-tui/tests/settings_config_edit_tests.rs` called `config::save()` which silently fell back to `$HOME/.config/kanban/config.toml`. In the nixpkgs build sandbox `$HOME` is non-writable (typically `/homeless-shelter`), so `create_dir_all` returned `EACCES` and the tests panicked. Fixed reactively by KAN-396 (PR #241).
2. **2026-05-14** — a new test `test_apply_config_edit_with_non_default_content_writes_config` in PR #267 reintroduced the exact same pattern. Caught only by manual audit (KAN-449 / PR #269), not by CI. Would have blocked the next nixpkgs-update bump if not for the manual catch.

The pattern: any test that exercises `apply_config_edit` (or anything else that ends up calling `config::save()`) without explicitly setting `configuration_location` hits `dirs::config_dir()` and fails in a sandbox. Linux CI doesn't catch this because Linux CI has `$HOME` set to a writable path. Only the **Nix build sandbox** reproduces the failure.

We need CI to run a real Nix sandbox build with `doCheck = true` so this class of regression is caught before it ships.

## Verified locally — end-to-end successful

I built v0.5.1 in the actual Nix sandbox using an inline expression mirroring nixpkgs's `pkgs/by-name/ka/kanban/package.nix` and confirmed:

- `nix-build` exit code **0**
- Final store path: `/nix/store/503c486n3ycmz8gnrkhisk0w2jzlpnvd-kanban-0.5.1`
- `cargoCheckHook` ran inside the sandbox with `--profile release --target x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu --offline`
- Approximately **1400+ tests across 67 test binaries** all passed, zero FAILED, zero panics, zero `Permission denied`

This confirms the *derivation shape* (rustPlatform.buildRustPackage with doCheck = true, importCargoLock, the same nativeBuildInputs/buildInputs as `default.nix`) works end-to-end inside the sandbox. The CI job should adopt that same shape, exposed as a flake output so it integrates cleanly with `nix flake check`.

## Recommended approach: flake output + `nix flake check`

Add a `checks.${system}.workspace-tests` output to `flake.nix` that overrides `default.nix` to flip `doCheck = true` and run the full workspace test suite:

```nix
checks.${system}.workspace-tests =
  (pkgs.callPackage ./default.nix { src = self; gitRev = self.rev or null; }).overrideAttrs (old: {
    doCheck = true;
    cargoBuildFlags = [];
    cargoTestFlags = [ "--workspace" ];
  });
```

CI step:

```yaml
- uses: cachix/install-nix-action@v27
  with:
    extra_nix_config: |
      experimental-features = nix-command flakes
      substituters = https://cache.nixos.org https://nix-community.cachix.org
      trusted-public-keys = cache.nixos.org-1:6NCHdD59X431o0gWypbMrAURkbJ16ZPMQFGspcDShjY= nix-community.cachix.org-1:mB9FSh9qf2dCimDSUo8Zy7bkq5CX+/rkCWyvRCYg3Fs=
- run: nix flake check --print-build-logs
```

**Why this approach:**

- One canonical hermetic-CI entry point (`nix flake check`) — same command developers can run locally.
- Lives next to existing flake outputs in `flake.nix`, no separate file to maintain.
- Equivalent sandbox semantics to the verified inline expression: the sandbox guarantees (non-writable `$HOME`, blocked network, ephemeral `/tmp`) come from the builder, not the derivation shape.

**Verification gap to close before relying on this gate:**

The exact override path through `default.nix` was not run end-to-end during the v0.5.1 local verification — that used a standalone expression (see fallback below). Before marking the gate required, locally run `nix flake check` on current `develop` HEAD and confirm:

1. It goes green when the codebase is healthy.
2. It goes red when one of KAN-396 or KAN-449's test fixes is reverted (e.g. removing the `configuration_location` line from `test_apply_config_edit_with_non_default_content_writes_config`).

That regression-test-the-gate exercise is required by acceptance anyway; it doubles as the verification step for the override path.

## Fallback: standalone `nix/nixpkgs-build.nix`

If the override path through `default.nix` proves fragile (cargoBuildFlags clearing, doCheck overriding, etc.), drop in a standalone file that mirrors nixpkgs's `pkgs/by-name/ka/kanban/package.nix` literally:

```nix
# nix/nixpkgs-build.nix
{ pkgs ? import <nixpkgs> {}
, src ? ./..
}:
let
  lockFile = "${src}/Cargo.lock";
in
pkgs.rustPlatform.buildRustPackage {
  pname = "kanban";
  version = "nixpkgs-sim";

  inherit src;

  cargoDeps = pkgs.rustPlatform.importCargoLock { inherit lockFile; };

  nativeBuildInputs = [ pkgs.pkg-config ];
  buildInputs = pkgs.lib.optionals pkgs.stdenv.isLinux [
    pkgs.wayland
    pkgs.xorg.libxcb
  ];

  env.GIT_COMMIT_HASH = "0000000000000000000000000000000000000000";

  # doCheck defaults to true — DO NOT override; that's the whole point of this job.
}
```

CI step:

```yaml
- run: nix-build nix/nixpkgs-build.nix --no-out-link -vvv
```

This is the path that was verified end-to-end against v0.5.1. Reach for it only if the flake-output route doesn't hold up.

The exact expression I used (for reference, against a local nixpkgs checkout):

```nix
let
  pkgs = import /home/max/fulsomenko/nixpkgs {};
  src = /tmp/kanban-v0.5.1;
in
  (pkgs.callPackage /home/max/fulsomenko/nixpkgs/pkgs/by-name/ka/kanban/package.nix {}).overrideAttrs (old: {
    inherit src;
    version = "0.5.1";
    cargoDeps = pkgs.rustPlatform.importCargoLock { lockFile = "${src}/Cargo.lock"; };
    env = (old.env or {}) // { GIT_COMMIT_HASH = "5e54f85f0000000000000000000000000000000000"; };
  })
```

## Where it should run

**Recommendation: PR-to-develop**, not just PR-to-master. The 2026-05-14 near-miss landed in develop via PR #267 and was only caught at release time. Gating develop merges catches it earlier and cheaper than gating only the release PR.

Workflow trigger:

```yaml
on:
  pull_request:
    branches: [develop, master]
```

If develop-gating proves too expensive on initial rollout, fall back to PR-to-master only — that still would have caught the 2026-05-14 issue at release time.

## Cost / cache

- **Cold runner**: ~30 minutes. The build does two cargo compiles (buildPhase in dev profile, checkPhase in release profile) plus ~600 crates of vendored deps. cargoCheckHook re-runs cargo with `--profile release`, which doesn't share artifacts with buildPhase's default profile.
- **Warm cache**: 2–5 minutes for the deps (substituters do almost all the work); the workspace recompile is the dominant remaining cost.
- **Substituters to enable**: `cache.nixos.org` (default) + `nix-community.cachix.org`. The local verification pulled ~80% of its dep store paths from those two caches.
- **Optional**: project-owned Cachix or S3 cache (`cachix/cachix-action@v15`) for caching the kanban-* derivations between PRs. Not strictly needed initially.

## Implementation tips / gotchas

- **`nix-build` vs `nix build`** are different CLIs with different flags. The legacy `nix-build` does NOT accept `-L` (modern's verbose flag); use `-vvv` instead. `nix flake check` uses the new CLI, so `-L` / `--print-build-logs` is fine there.
- **Disk pressure** — running this on a self-hosted runner with <50GB free space will fail mid-build (the vendoring + dep compile + workspace compile produces ~15-20GB of intermediate `/nix/store` paths). GitHub-hosted `ubuntu-latest` has ~14GB free which may not be enough — verify with `df -h` before committing to that runner.
- **Soft-launch path**: land the workflow as a non-required check first. Tune it until it's reliably green on `develop` HEAD. Then mark it required.

## Acceptance

- A CI job exists that runs a Nix sandbox build with `doCheck = true`, exposed as `checks.${system}.workspace-tests` and invoked via `nix flake check` (preferred), or via `nix-build nix/nixpkgs-build.nix` if the fallback was needed.
- The job runs on every PR to `develop` (preferred) or `master` (fallback).
- **Regression test for the gate itself**: reverting either KAN-396 OR KAN-449 (just the test file changes, e.g. removing the `configuration_location` line from `test_apply_config_edit_with_non_default_content_writes_config`) and pushing as a PR causes the new CI job to fail red. Document this regression-test recipe so future maintainers can verify the gate is still meaningful.
- Cache configured so steady-state CI time is acceptable (under 10 min for unchanged deps; ≤5 min preferred).
- Document in `CONTRIBUTING.md` that this gate exists, what it catches, and how to reproduce locally:
  ```
  nix flake check --print-build-logs
  ```

## Why this matters beyond `apply_config_edit`

The class of bugs this catches is not limited to `config::save()` fallbacks. Anything that:

- Writes under `$HOME` / `~/.config` / `~/.local/share` / `~/.cache` (any `dirs::*` lookup)
- Requires network access (Nix sandbox blocks it)
- Depends on `/tmp` lifetime semantics beyond the build
- Assumes a TTY / terminal capabilities

…will be caught by this single sandbox build. Currently **zero of these classes are caught pre-release** — we rely entirely on ryantm flagging it post-release, which delays the next nixpkgs bump until we land a reactive fix.

## Related cards / PRs

- **KAN-396 / PR #241** — first reactive fix for this failure class.
- **KAN-445 / PR #267** — added the Windows CI job (precedent for adding platform-specific CI gates).
- **KAN-449 / PR #269** — the 2026-05-14 near-miss this card prevents recurring.
- **Verified store path**: `/nix/store/503c486n3ycmz8gnrkhisk0w2jzlpnvd-kanban-0.5.1`
cards.title: Add CI job: Nix sandbox build with tests (pre-release nixpkgs simulation)

## Purpose
Use property-based testing to verify invariants hold under all conditions.

## Setup
Add to `crates/kanban-domain/Cargo.toml`:
```
[dev-dependencies]
quickcheck = "1.0"
quickcheck_macros = "1.0"
```

## Properties to Test
Create new file: `crates/kanban-domain/tests/property_tests.rs`

1. prop_validation_catches_orphaned_cards
   - Property: If card references non-existent column, validation must catch it

2. prop_cascade_delete_removes_all_edges
   - Property: After removing card's edges, no edges should reference that card

3. prop_delete_column_with_cards_always_fails
   - Property: DeleteColumn must fail if any cards reference the column

4. prop_validation_is_idempotent
   - Property: Running validation twice gives same result

## Arbitrary Implementations Needed
- impl Arbitrary for Board
- impl Arbitrary for Column
- impl Arbitrary for Card
- impl Arbitrary for DependencyGraph

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] QuickCheck dependencies added
- [ ] All 4 properties implemented
- [ ] All Arbitrary implementations added
- [ ] Tests pass with 100+ random inputs
- [ ] Properties catch edge cases not in integration tests

cards.description:
**File:** `crates/kanban-domain/src/history.rs`

Unbounded memory growth in long sessions. Each Snapshot clones the entire application state. Every command pushes a full clone onto the undo stack with no limit.

Add a `max_depth` field to `HistoryManager` using `VecDeque`, enforce it in `capture_before_command` by dropping oldest snapshots when limit exceeded. Default to 100. Expose `with_max_depth(n)` constructor.

Tests: verify stack never exceeds max_depth, oldest snapshots are dropped FIFO, redo stack unaffected.

cards.description:
**File:** `crates/kanban-domain/src/search/mod.rs:61-95`

`BranchNameSearcher::get_branch_name` runs on every `matches()` call, performing sprint lookup, string formatting, title slugification, and branch prefix resolution. During interactive search this is 500 string allocations per keystroke for 500 cards.

Option A: Pre-compute branch names into `HashMap<CardId, String>` at search session start, pass to `CachedBranchNameSearcher`.
Option B: Accept the cost — for typical board sizes (<200 cards) overhead is negligible. Profile before optimizing.
cards.title: Cache branch names in BranchNameSearcher

sprint prefix should be a sprint setting and not a board setting as in the case we lose our sprint prefix after changing it 

the board level sprint prefix setting should be an override for the application level sprint prefix

2025-11-01 17:59:34
---

we move sprint prefix from branch settings into sprint level settings so that we can have a sprint prefix per sprint

sprint prefix should be a sprint setting and not a board setting as in the case we lose our sprint prefix after changing it 

the board level sprint prefix setting should be an override for the application level sprint prefix

we dont have application level prefix yet, we have board and sprint. which are both configured from board settings. I want to move sprint prefix into sprint settings so that it can be unique for each sprint

fallback order for prefix

default prefix

application prefix (doesnt exist)

board prefix

sprint prefix (configuration to be moved now, both in interface and data)

2025-11-01 22:23:04
---

I think I made a slight mistake during the design of this addition

these are all for use with branch names to specific cards. that we got right.

Here are the overrides with example prefixes

```sh
default prefix (DEFAULT)

|
v

_application settings prefix (APP)_ [future]

|
v

board settings sprint prefix (BRANCH)

|
v

sprint settings prefix (SPRINT)
```

Creating sprints with just the default prefix yields sprints of

```sh
sprint-1/first 
sprint-2/second 
sprint-3/third 
```

Then adding branch prefix transforms these into 

```sh
BRANCH-1/first 
BRANCH-2/second 
BRANCH-3/third 
```

Then assigning the first sprint a sprint prefix

```sh
SPRINT-1/first 
BRANCH-2/second 
BRANCH-3/third 
```

Then creating four cards and assigning them to each sprint

```sh
task1 (SPRINT-1/first)
task2 (BRANCH-2/second)
task3 (BRANCH-3/third)
task4 (SPRINT-1/first)
```

They get the following branch names

```sh
git checkout -b SPRINT-1/task1
git checkout -b BRANCH-2/task2
git checkout -b BRANCH-3/task3
git checkout -b SPRINT-4/task4
```

The branch naming sequence isnt unique for each prefix!

It is a global sequence where as the sequence for the cards should be unique to each

So. To clarify. There is a couple of sequences going on here.

We have one sequence for each new sprint of the same prefix.

We have a seperate sequence for each new card that also uses the sprint prefix.

There is an unfortunate collision in that the sprint names use the same prefix as the cards.

This was not supposed to be the case. The sprint prefix we had from before this feature was supposed to give us a way to customize the sprint name for each sprint sequence. we moved this into the sprint level definition. This was good for the tasks. but bad for the sprint sequence. I think we have identified all the issues here above. Can we come up with a good plan for resolution?
cards.title: we probably should move sprint prefix into sprint level settings

cards.description:
## Context

During the Sprint::assignable refactor (KAN-194 follow-up), we identified a design gap: domain business rules like "only Planning/Active sprints are assignable" are encoded as functions in kanban-domain (e.g. Sprint::assignable), but nothing prevents consumers (TUI, CLI, MCP, future API) from bypassing them by accessing raw data directly.

This is exactly how the original sprint index mismatch bug happened — the UI filtered sprints for display but handlers accessed the unfiltered list.

## Current State

- KanbanOperations trait handles data access (CRUD) — enforced on all consumers
- Domain logic lives as pure functions on types (Sprint::assignable) — shared but not enforced
- TuiContext/CliContext fields like sprints: Vec<Sprint> are pub, so consumers can hand-roll their own filters

## Research Needed

Investigate making domain data private on context types and only exposing it through methods that enforce business rules. Questions to answer:

- Which fields/operations have business rules that consumers must conform to?
- What would the API surface look like if fields were private?
- How much of the codebase directly accesses ctx.sprints, ctx.cards, etc. vs going through methods?
- What is the ripple effect of making these fields private?
- Is there a middle ground (e.g. private for writes, pub for reads)?

## Goal

Ensure all consumers (TUI, CLI, MCP, future API) cannot accidentally bypass domain business rules, without over-engineering the abstraction layer.
cards.title: Research enforcing domain business rules via private accessors

cards.description:
## Gap

The `KanbanOperations` trait (`crates/kanban-domain/src/operations.rs`) defines 3 bulk card operations:
- `bulk_archive_cards` (line 62)
- `bulk_move_cards` (line 63)
- `bulk_assign_sprint` (line 64)

But `bulk_restore_cards` and `bulk_delete_cards` are missing entirely — no trait method, no CLI handler, no TUI handler, no MCP tool. The single-item `restore_card` and `delete_card` are fully implemented across all layers.

## Implementation

### 1. Trait — `crates/kanban-domain/src/operations.rs`
Add two methods alongside the existing bulk ops (after line 64):
```rust
fn bulk_restore_cards(&mut self, ids: Vec<Uuid>, column_id: Option<Uuid>) -> KanbanResult<usize>;
fn bulk_delete_cards(&mut self, ids: Vec<Uuid>) -> KanbanResult<usize>;
```

### 2. CLI context — `crates/kanban-cli/src/context.rs`
Implement both methods following the `bulk_archive_cards` pattern (lines 494-501): loop over ids, call single-item method, count successes.

### 3. CLI handler — `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/card.rs`
Add `BulkRestore` and `BulkDelete` variants to the card command enum and wire handler functions (parse `--ids` CSV, call context method, print result).

### 4. TUI context — `crates/kanban-tui/src/tui_context.rs`
Same loop-and-count pattern as CLI context (mirror `bulk_archive_cards` at lines 391-398).

### 5. MCP context — `crates/kanban-mcp/src/context.rs`
Delegate to CLI via `execute_with_retry`, following `bulk_archive_cards` pattern (lines 392-402):
- `bulk_restore_cards` → `["card", "bulk-restore", "--ids", &ids_csv]`
- `bulk_delete_cards` → `["card", "bulk-delete", "--ids", &ids_csv]`

### 6. MCP tools — `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs`
Add `tool_bulk_restore_cards` and `tool_bulk_delete_cards` alongside existing bulk tools (lines 828-860), using the `spawn_op!` macro.
cards.title: Add bulk_restore and bulk_delete to KanbanOperations trait

cards.description:
**From PR #165 review**

In `crates/kanban-mcp/src/context.rs:75-81`, `add_field_str` only handles `FieldUpdate::Set`. When `FieldUpdate::Clear` is passed, it silently does nothing.

This is currently correct for sprint prefix/card_prefix (which use separate `--clear-*` flags handled elsewhere), but the method name doesn't signal this limitation. If someone uses `add_field_str` for a field that needs clear support without adding explicit handling, it will silently drop the clear intent.

Consider either:
- Renaming to make the limitation explicit
- Adding a `clear_flag` parameter to handle the Clear variant
- Adding a doc comment warning about the behavior
cards.title: ArgsBuilder.add_field_str silently ignores FieldUpdate::Clear

cards.description:
**From PR #165 review**

In `crates/kanban-mcp/src/context.rs:276-310`, `update_card` handles `FieldUpdate::Clear` for `due_date` (line 303-305) but NOT for `description` (line 281-283) or `points` (line 294-296).

If `FieldUpdate::Clear` is passed for description or points, it silently does nothing — the CLI args won't include a `--clear-description` or `--clear-points` flag.

The PR intentionally removed broken `clear_description`/`clear_points` from the MCP request schema (UpdateCardRequest), which is correct. But the `McpContext` implementation of `KanbanOperations` should still handle the trait contract correctly if called programmatically (e.g., from tests or future code).

Needs corresponding `--clear-description` and `--clear-points` CLI flags to be added first, then the context can handle them.
cards.title: McpContext.update_card doesn't handle Clear for description/points
SyncExecutor error classification relies on fragile string matching

cards.description:
**From PR #165 review**

In `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:593`:
```rust
req.wip_limit.map(|w| FieldUpdate::Set(w as i32))
```

And similarly in `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/column.rs:52`:
```rust
args.wip_limit.map(|w| w as i32)
```

Both use `as i32` which is a potentially lossy cast. If `wip_limit > i32::MAX` (2,147,483,647), it wraps to a negative value. While unlikely in practice (WIP limits are typically single digits), the schema accepts `u32` so technically values up to 4 billion are valid input.

Consider using `TryFrom` with an error, or capping at a reasonable maximum (e.g., 1000), or changing the domain type to `u32` to match.
cards.title: Lossy u32-to-i32 cast for wip_limit in MCP and CLI handlers

cards.description:
**From PR #165 review**

The integration tests in `crates/kanban-mcp/tests/integration.rs` cover happy paths thoroughly (13 tests for board CRUD, column CRUD, card lifecycle, sprint lifecycle, bulk ops, export/import). However, there are no tests for:

- **Invalid UUID handling** — verify parse_uuid returns proper MCP error
- **Conflict retries** — verify execute_with_retry actually retries on ConflictDetected
- **Timeout behavior** — verify the 30s timeout fires and returns the right error
- **Operations on nonexistent entities** — only `board_get_nonexistent` is tested; missing column_get_nonexistent, card_get_nonexistent, sprint_get_nonexistent
- **Delete of nonexistent entity** — verify proper error propagation
- **Bulk operations with invalid IDs** — partial failure behavior

Unit tests cover parser edge cases well (32 tests), but the integration layer needs error path coverage to prevent regressions.
cards.title: MCP integration tests lack error path coverage

Using VHS record a demo so that we can post a gif of the tui to our github.

```
 Complete Demo Script: Building a Web API Project

  Scene 1: Launch & Create Board (0-15s)

  $ cargo run
  [TUI loads]
  Press 'n'
  Type: "Web API Project"
  Press Enter
  Narration: "Creating a new project for our web API development"

  Scene 2: Add Cards with Different Priorities (15-50s)

  Press '2' (switch to Tasks panel)
  Press 'n'
  Type: "Setup development environment"
  Press Enter

  Press 'n'
  Type: "Design database schema"
  Press Enter

  Press 'n'
  Type: "Implement authentication API"
  Press Enter

  Press 'n'
  Type: "Write integration tests"
  Press Enter
  Narration: "Adding tasks to our backlog"

  Scene 3: Edit Card Details - Full Metadata (50-90s)

  Press 'k' (move up to first card)
  Press Enter (open card detail)

  [Show Title panel highlighted]
  Press 'e'
  [External editor opens]
  Type: "Setup development environment\nInstall Rust, PostgreSQL, Docker"
  Save & exit

  Press '2' (switch to Metadata panel)
  Press 'e'
  Type: "3" (for 3 story points - Yellow badge)
  Press Enter

  Press 's' (change status)
  Type: "InProgress"
  Press Enter

  Press 'p' (change priority)
  Type: "High"
  Press Enter

  Press '3' (switch to Description panel)
  Press 'e'
  [External editor opens]
  Type: "Dependencies:\n- Rust 1.70+\n- PostgreSQL 15\n- Docker & docker-compose\n\nSetup steps:\n1. Install Rust toolchain\n2.
  Configure database\n3. Run initial migrations"
  Save & exit

  Press Escape (back to main view)
  Narration: "Setting story points, priority, status, and detailed description"

  Scene 4: Mark Card Complete & Copy Branch Name (90-110s)

  Press 'c' (toggle completion)
  [Card shows checkmark ✓]

  Press 'j' (move down)
  Press Enter (open second card)
  Press 'b' (copy branch name to clipboard)
  [Shows: "Copied branch name to clipboard"]

  Press 'y' (copy card ID)
  [Shows: "Copied card ID to clipboard"]

  Press Escape
  Narration: "Marking tasks complete and copying branch names for git workflow"

  Scene 5: Board Settings with Branch Prefix (110-130s)

  Press '1' (switch to Projects)
  Press 's' (open board settings)
  Press 'p' (set branch prefix)
  Type: "feat"
  Press Enter
  [Shows: "Branch prefix set to: feat"]
  Press Escape
  Narration: "Configuring git branch prefixes for consistent naming"

  Scene 6: Edit Board Details (130-150s)

  Press 'e' (edit board)
  [BoardDetail view opens]
  Press '2' (Description panel)
  Press 'e'
  [External editor]
  Type: "RESTful API for user management\n\nTech Stack:\n- Rust + Actix-web\n- PostgreSQL\n- JWT authentication"
  Save & exit
  Press Escape
  Narration: "Adding project documentation"

  Scene 7: Export Board (150-165s)

  Press 'x' (export current board)
  Type: "web-api-project.json"
  Press Enter
  [Shows: "Board exported successfully"]
  Narration: "Exporting project data to JSON"

  Scene 8: Complete More Tasks (165-185s)

  Press '2' (Tasks panel)
  Press 'j' (move down to third card)
  Press Enter
  Press '2' (Metadata)
  Press 'e'
  Type: "5" (5 story points - Red badge for critical)
  Press Enter
  Press 's'
  Type: "Blocked"
  Press Enter
  Press Escape

  Press 'j' (fourth card)
  Press 'c' (mark complete)
  Narration: "Managing task progress - marking some blocked, others complete"

  Scene 9: Import Demo (185-205s)

  Press 'i' (import)
  [File list shows: web-api-project.json, example.json]
  Press 'j'
  Press Enter
  [Shows: "1 board(s) imported successfully"]
  Narration: "Importing previously exported projects"

  Scene 10: Final Overview & Quit (205-215s)

  Press '1' (Projects)
  [Show list of boards]
  Press '2' (Tasks)
  [Show cards with different colors for story points, completion marks]

  Press 'q'
  [Auto-save message]
  [Exit to shell]
  Narration: "All changes are auto-saved on exit"

  ---
  Key Features Showcased:

  ✅ Board creation (n)✅ Card creation (n)✅ External editor integration (title & description with e)✅ Story points with color coding
  (1-5: e in Metadata)✅ Status management (s: Todo/InProgress/Blocked/Done)✅ Priority levels (p: Low/Medium/High/Critical)✅ Task
  completion (c)✅ Branch name copying (b)✅ Card ID copying (y)✅ Board settings (s)✅ Branch prefix configuration (p)✅ Board details
  editing (e from Projects)✅ Export single board (x)✅ Import boards (i)✅ Auto-save on quit✅ Panel navigation (1/2/3)✅ Vim-style
  navigation (j/k)

  Total runtime: ~3:35 minutes
  ```

cards.description:
## Goal
Reduce token consumption when listing cards via MCP and CLI by making descriptions opt-in and adding pagination.

## Problem
- MCP `list_cards` returns full `Card` objects including description and sprint_logs by default — very verbose
- No pagination means all cards are returned at once, which can be huge on large boards

## Plan

### 1. MCP: default to `CardSummary` in `list_cards` (`kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs`)
- Change return type from `Vec<Card>` to `Vec<CardSummary>` by default
- Add `include_description: Option<bool>` to `ListCardsRequest` — when true, return full `Card` objects instead
- Update tool description to document this behaviour

### 2. CLI: add `--description` flag to `card list` (`kanban-cli/src/cli.rs` + `handlers/card.rs`)
- CLI already returns `CardSummary` by default (good)
- Add `#[arg(long)] pub description: bool` to `CardListArgs`
- When `--description` is set, collect `Vec<Card>` instead of `Vec<CardSummary>`

### 3. Pagination for both MCP and CLI
- Add `page: Option<u32>` and `page_size: Option<u32>` to `ListCardsRequest` (MCP) and `CardListArgs` (CLI)
- Default `page_size`: 50
- Apply pagination after filtering in the handler layer (no domain changes needed)
- Return pagination metadata in response: `total`, `page`, `page_size`, `total_pages`

## Files Changed
- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs` — `ListCardsRequest` fields + handler logic
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/cli.rs` — `CardListArgs` fields
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/card.rs` — pagination + description opt-in logic
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/output.rs` — paginated list response type (if needed)
cards.title: Add description opt-in and pagination to card listing

cards.description:
## Context
Follows from KAN-212 which adds `CardSummary` as the default list response and introduces `PaginatedList<T>` as a shared response wrapper for MCP and CLI.

## Goal
Extend those primitives into the TUI so the entire stack shares the same lean data model for card listing.

## Design Discussion

### Current TUI behaviour
- Loads full `Card` objects into memory for the list view (includes description, sprint_logs)
- Manages its own viewport/scroll pagination via `Page` in `kanban-core/src/pagination.rs`
- `Page` is viewport-aware: tracks `scroll_offset` + `viewport_height` → produces `visible_indices` for rendering, with above/below scroll indicators

### Proposed architecture
- TUI card list view uses `CardSummary` instead of `Card` (no description or sprint_logs in memory)
- Full `Card` is fetched on demand only when opening the card detail/edit panel
- `PaginatedList<T>` (introduced in KAN-212) becomes the fetch/response layer
- The existing `Page` scroll logic continues to own viewport slicing — it sits on top of `PaginatedList<CardSummary>` as the data source rather than a raw `Vec<Card>`

### Layering
```
PaginatedList<CardSummary>   ← data fetch / response layer (shared with CLI + MCP)
         ↓
      Page / PageInfo         ← scroll + viewport logic (TUI-only, unchanged)
         ↓
   render_strategy.rs         ← rendering (unchanged)
```

### Key distinction
`PaginatedList` is a static response wrapper (total, page, page_size, items).
`Page` is a dynamic scroll state that reacts to viewport resize and key events.
They are complementary, not replacements for each other.

## Scope

### Changes required
- `kanban-tui`: change card list state from `Vec<Card>` to `Vec<CardSummary>`
- `kanban-tui`: fetch full `Card` only on detail panel open (lazy load)
- `kanban-tui`: ensure all list-rendering paths use `CardSummary` fields only
- `kanban-tui`: card detail panel continues to use full `Card`
- Verify no description/sprint_log fields are accessed during list rendering

### Out of scope
- Changing `Page` / `PageInfo` scroll logic (leave untouched)
- Server-side pagination for the TUI (TUI still loads all summaries into memory and slices locally — network/IO is not the bottleneck here)
cards.title: Refactor TUI card list to use CardSummary and PaginatedList
