In a Nix build sandbox `$HOME` is a non-writable stub (typically `/homeless-shelter`), so `config::save`'s `create_dir_all` returns `EACCES`, `apply_config_edit` returns `Err`, and `assert!(result.is_ok())` panics.

Same shape as the [2026-05-07 nixpkgs-update log](https://nixpkgs-update-logs.nix-community.org/kanban/2026-05-07.log) that KAN-396 fixed for the five tests in `settings_config_edit_tests.rs`.

## Fix

Pin `app.app_config.configuration_location` to a `tempfile::tempdir()` path **before** building the DTO, so `effective_configuration_location` returns the tempdir path instead of the OS config dir.

```rust
let dir = tempfile::tempdir().unwrap();
let config_path = dir.path().join("config.toml");
let mut app = App::test_default();
app.app_config = kanban_core::AppConfig {
    default_card_prefix: Some("feat".into()),
    configuration_location: Some(config_path.to_string_lossy().into_owned()),
    ..Default::default()
};
```

## Verification

Reproduced the failure end-to-end on the dev machine by running the **pre-fix** test binary with `env -u XDG_CONFIG_HOME HOME=/var/empty` (forces `dirs::config_dir()` to resolve to an unwritable path, simulating the Nix sandbox).

- Pre-fix binary: **FAILED** at `settings_ui_tests.rs:367:5` (the `assert!(result.is_ok())` line).
- Post-fix binary, same environment: **PASSED**.

## Status

- PR #269 against `develop` (branch `KAN-449/make-apply-config-edit-test-sandbox-safe`). Replaces closed PR #268 (renamed branch to follow naming convention).
- Two commits: the test fix + a changeset.

## Latent risk NOT addressed in this card

Two other tests in `settings_config_edit_tests.rs` (the `_ = app.apply_config_edit(...)` pattern at lines 274 and 294) currently pass on Nix only by accident — `save()` fails early, `self.app_config` is never updated, and the assertions hold for the wrong reason. They are not panicking today, so they ride along. The durable fix is to make `config::save()` return an explicit error when `configuration_location` is unset, rather than silently falling back to `dirs::config_dir()`. That is a production-code change, separate card if/when it comes up.

## Acceptance

- PR #269 merged into `develop`.
- The fix is verified by running the test binary with `env -u XDG_CONFIG_HOME HOME=/var/empty`.
- The next nixpkgs-update bump survives without hitting this failure class.
cards.title: Make apply_config_edit non-default-content test sandbox-safe
MCP Cannot Perform Undo

cards.description:
## Issue

When an MCP agent sets a card's status to 'done', the card is marked complete but remains in its current column. It is not automatically moved to the board's completion column.

This creates a three-step manual workaround in the TUI:
1. Open the TUI
2. Navigate to the card
3. Move it to the Complete column manually

Additionally, pressing 'c' in the TUI on an already-completed card does not move it — it unmarks it as complete instead.

## Observed (2026-05-05) — inverse direction also broken

Moving a card to the last column via MCP (tool_move_card) does NOT set the card's status to 'done' or populate completed_at. The completion column has no special meaning to the MCP layer — it treats it as an ordinary column move.

This confirms the bug is bidirectional: the status field and column position are not kept in sync in either direction by the MCP server.

## Expected behaviour

When a card's status is set to 'done' (via MCP or TUI), it should be automatically moved to the board's designated completion column (if one is configured). Conversely, when a card is moved to the completion column (via MCP or TUI), its status should be set to 'done' and completed_at populated. 'c' in the TUI should move a done card to the completion column, not toggle completion off.

## Suggested fix

- MCP move_card: after moving, check if destination is completion_column_id; if so, call the same lifecycle hook that sets status=done and completed_at
- MCP update_card status=done: after updating, move card to completion_column_id if set
- TUI 'c': on an already-done card in a non-completion column, move it to completion column rather than unmarking it
- Root fix: see card 324 — remove CardStatus entirely and derive completion state solely from column position

---

# Implementation discussion (2026-05-11)

## What was implemented (branch `KAN-394/bug-marking-card-done-via-mcp-...`)

Auto-sync of the status ↔ completion-column invariant baked into the **domain command layer**:

**Files changed (382 lines added, 1 removed):**
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/card_lifecycle.rs` — two new pure helpers:
  - `target_column_for_status(card, new_status, board, columns, cards) -> Option<(Uuid, i32)>`
  - `target_status_for_column_move(card, new_column_id, board, columns) -> Option<CardStatus>`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/card_commands.rs` — auto-sync wired into `UpdateCard::execute` and `MoveCard::execute`; 6 inline unit tests + a `CompletionFixture` helper
- `crates/kanban-mcp/tests/integration.rs` — 3 end-to-end MCP integration tests
- `.changeset/kan-394-mcp-sync-card-status-with-completion-column.md` — patch bump

**Behaviour:**
- `UpdateCard { status: Done }` (no column_id) → auto-moves card to `Board::resolve_completion_column` (which falls back to the last column when `completion_column_id` is unset), sets `completed_at`
- `UpdateCard { status: Todo }` on a card in the completion column (no column_id) → moves to second-to-last column, clears `completed_at`
- `MoveCard` to the completion column → sets `status=Done`, populates `completed_at`
- `MoveCard` away from the completion column on a Done card → sets `status=Todo`, clears `completed_at`
- When the caller passes **both** `column_id` and `status` (e.g. the TUI's atomic toggle), explicit caller intent wins; auto-sync is bypassed

**Verification:**
- 6 new unit tests in `card_commands.rs::tests` — all green
- 3 new MCP integration tests (`mcp_update_card_status_to_done_moves_to_completion_column`, `mcp_move_card_to_completion_column_sets_status_done`, `mcp_move_card_away_from_completion_column_clears_done_status`) — all green
- Full workspace test suite green (no regressions in TUI h/l/c handlers)
- `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` clean
- `cargo fmt --check` clean

## Open design questions raised in review (not yet committed)

### Q1. Is the TUI now doing redundant work?

The TUI `toggle_card_completion` (in `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs`) still pre-computes target column + status via `compute_completion_toggle`, then issues `UpdateCard` with **both** fields populated. The auto-sync's "respects_both" branch lets these pass through untouched, so the TUI is unaffected — but the pre-computation is now duplicate work relative to a simplified TUI that just sends `status: Some(Done)`.

**Trade-off:**
- Simplified TUI = less code, single source of truth
- But TUI already has board/columns/cards in `Model`; the domain auto-sync re-queries the store (3 extra reads per op)

**Decision (tentative):** leave TUI as-is; file a separate "simplify TUI completion handlers" follow-up if pursued. Per CLAUDE.md "a bug fix doesn't need surrounding cleanup."

### Q2. Is auto-sync the right default? Should it be configurable?

The "last column = completion" assumption was already baked into the codebase via:
- `Board::resolve_completion_column` falling back to last column
- TUI 'c' key, h/l with completion-column transitions
- `Card::update_status` setting `completed_at`

The change makes that pre-existing opinion **consistent** across entry points rather than introducing a new one — but it does so on *any* status/column change, not just on user gestures in the TUI. A user setting `status=Done` programmatically (CLI, MCP) without intending a column move would be surprised.

**Real concern:** some workflows treat "Done" column as a stage (e.g. "Done by dev, awaiting review") rather than a completion gate. Auto-syncing forces a stronger semantic than those users want.

**Proposed config knob (deferred):**
- `Board.auto_sync_completion: bool` (default true), or
- App-level `AppConfig.completion_sync_mode: { Strict | Suggest | Off }`

**Decision (tentative):** ship default-on behaviour to fix the filed bug; file a follow-up card for configurability if real demand emerges.

### Q3. Would chained commands be a cleaner architecture?

Yes — and likely the right long-term design. Instead of baking the side-effect into `UpdateCard::execute()`, the cleaner pattern is to compose at the service layer:

```rust
// In KanbanContext::update_card or a command-builder helper
let mut cmds = vec![UpdateCard { status: Done, .. }];
if board.auto_sync_completion {
    if let Some((col, pos)) = target_column_for_status(...) {
        cmds.push(MoveCard { card_id, column_id: col, new_position: pos });
    }
}
self.execute(cmds)?;  // already executes batches as a single undo unit
```

**Benefits:**
- Each command stays single-purpose (SRP — the principle CLAUDE.md cites)
- Auto-sync is observable in the command log (better for debugging and the eventual cross-client undo work in KAN-409/410)
- Config gates the chain at one place, not deep inside each command
- Undo already works on batches, so atomicity is preserved
- The pure helpers (`target_column_for_status`, `target_status_for_column_move`) remain reusable regardless of where they are called

**Cost:**
- Composition logic moves to a higher layer (service or a builder helper)
- Two call sites need to opt into the chain (UpdateCard path + MoveCard path)

**Decision (pending):** the current branch implements the in-command approach. A refactor to chained commands at the service layer (`KanbanContext::update_card` and `KanbanContext::move_card`) is feasible without throwing away the work: keep the pure helpers, move the orchestration up. The unit tests on the commands would migrate to service-layer integration tests; the MCP integration tests stay valid since they test end-to-end behaviour.

## Status

- **Branch:** `KAN-394/bug-marking-card-done-via-mcp-does-not-move-it-to-the-completion-column`
- **Tests passing:** yes (workspace-wide)
- **Not yet committed:** working tree changes pending decision on Q3 (chained-commands refactor or ship as-is)
- **Out of scope for this card:** Q2 config knob, Q1 TUI simplification, `MoveCards` batch path, TUI 'c' edge case for legacy-data Done-not-in-completion-column state
cards.title: bug: marking card done via MCP does not move it to the completion column

cards.description:
# Evaluate replacing custom release shell scripts with off-the-shelf tooling

This card defers and tracks the "should we replace `bump-version.sh` + `aggregate-changelog.sh` + `publish-crates.sh` + `validate-release.sh` with an off-the-shelf release tool" decision, surfaced during PR #208 review. The spike under [validate-release.sh staleness card] (sibling card) addresses the immediate symptom; this card addresses the underlying maintenance burden.

## Origin

PR #208 review surfaced that `scripts/validate-release.sh` and `scripts/publish-crates.sh` carry duplicate hand-curated 9-entry crate lists — and one had silently fallen out of sync (validate-release missed `kanban-service`, `kanban-persistence-json`, `kanban-persistence-sqlite`). The hardcoded list was the visible symptom; the deeper question is whether maintaining ~400 lines of bespoke release shell across 4 scripts still earns its keep when off-the-shelf tools cover most of the same ground.

The user's framing: *"can't we make it dynamic instead of a hardcoded list?"* — answer: yes, via `cargo metadata` topo-sort, but that's a 15-line patch on top of a 400-line surface that has other rough edges (dependency-order coupling, idempotency, partial-publish recovery, etc.). Worth evaluating the bigger move.

## Summary verdict (preliminary)

Three options to consider, ordered by migration cost:

1. **Stay with custom scripts but make the crate list dynamic** (smallest change — ~15 lines of `cargo metadata | jq | tsort` shell, adopt as immediate follow-up).
2. **Migrate to `cargo-workspaces`** (mid-size — replaces 3 of 4 scripts, keeps `aggregate-changelog.sh` or pairs with git-cliff; preserves the changesets workflow contributors already use).
3. **Migrate to `release-plz`** (largest — replaces all 4 scripts, switches commit convention to Conventional Commits, switches release model from on-merge to release-PR).

Recommendation: **start with option 1 immediately** to fix the duplication that bit us in PR #208, then **spike option 2 on a branch** to see what release.yml shrinks to. Park option 3 as a longer-term consideration — its workflow shape (release PRs, Conventional Commits) is a bigger ergonomic shift for contributors.

---

## Current state — what we'd be replacing

**4 scripts, ~400 lines of bash, wired into `.github/workflows/release.yml`:**

```
PR merged to master
  ↓
release.yml step 5: bump-version.sh        — reads .changeset/*.md, bumps Cargo.toml + path-dep ^X.Y
release.yml step 6: aggregate-changelog.sh — writes CHANGELOG.md, deletes .changeset/*.md
release.yml step 7: git commit             — "chore: release vX.Y.Z"
release.yml step 8: validate-release.sh    — dry-run check (currently with --no-verify, see sibling card)
release.yml step 9: git push master
release.yml step 10: publish-crates.sh     — 9 crates, topo-ordered, 10s pacing, skip-if-published
release.yml step 11: git tag v$VERSION + push
release.yml step 12: softprops/action-gh-release  — creates GitHub Release (triggers aur-publish.yml)
release.yml step 13: git merge master → develop  — sync-back
```

**Changeset format** (`.changeset/*.md`):
```markdown
---
bump: minor
---

- feat(domain): WipLimitExceeded error variant
- test(domain): add failing WIP enforcement tests
- ...
```
Single frontmatter field (`bump: major|minor|patch`). Bump-version.sh aggregates max-level across all changesets. Aggregate-changelog.sh formats the body into CHANGELOG.md with PR link, then deletes the file.

**Coupling between scripts:**
- bump-version → aggregate-changelog (latter reads versions written by former)
- aggregate-changelog → publish-crates (former deletes changesets so re-runs of latter don't think there's pending work)
- publish-crates calls validate-release internally before any cargo publish
- All four assume they run in `release.yml`'s exact order; none are individually idempotent except validate-release

**Things that DON'T live in any of the 4 scripts but are still part of the release surface:**
- The changeset *creation* tool (`nix run .#changeset`, interactive)
- The PR-number → CHANGELOG link (passed as arg to aggregate-changelog)
- The develop sync-back step (release.yml step 13, plain git)
- The AUR publish (separate workflow, triggered by `release: published` event)
- The git tag + GitHub Release creation (release.yml steps 11–12)

---

## Option 1 — Make the crate list dynamic (no tool replacement)

**What changes.** Both `validate-release.sh` and `publish-crates.sh` derive their CRATES array dynamically via `cargo metadata` instead of hardcoding it.

**Implementation sketch (~15 lines):**

```bash
# scripts/lib/crate-order.sh
get_publish_order() {
  cargo metadata --no-deps --format-version 1 \
    | jq -r '
        .packages
        | map({name: .name, deps: [.dependencies[] | select(.path != null) | .name]})
        | .[]
        | "\(.name) \(.deps | join(" "))"
      ' \
    | tsort \
    | tac    # reverse: leaves first
}

# in publish-crates.sh and validate-release.sh:
source "$(dirname "$0")/lib/crate-order.sh"
mapfile -t CRATES < <(get_publish_order | sed 's|^|crates/|')
```

**What this fixes:**
- ✅ Dual-list drift (the PR #208 bug) — single source of truth via cargo
- ✅ Adding a new crate becomes a Cargo.toml change only; scripts auto-discover
- ✅ Topo-sort comes for free from `cargo metadata`'s dep graph

**What this does NOT fix:**
- ❌ Still 4 scripts of bash to maintain
- ❌ `--no-verify` in validate-release (separate sibling card)
- ❌ Partial-publish recovery still manual (run again, skip-if-published catches existing versions, but failure-mid-publish still requires human attention)
- ❌ Changeset format remains custom (no ecosystem tooling can read it)

**Effort:** half-day. Can ship as the immediate follow-up to PR #208's validate-release fix.

**Motivation:** highest value-per-line-changed. Eliminates the exact regression that bit us in PR #208 without introducing any new tooling, conventions, or contributor friction.

---

## Option 2 — Migrate to `cargo-workspaces`

**What it is.** A `cargo` subcommand (invoked as `cargo ws`) that handles workspace version bumping and topo-sorted publishing. v0.4.2 (Dec 2025), 586 stars, actively maintained, originally built for `clap-rs/clap`.

Docs: https://github.com/pksunkara/cargo-workspaces

**Replaces:**
- `bump-version.sh` — `cargo ws version <level> --yes --no-git-commit --no-git-push` does the workspace + path-dep rewrite (this is the part of the script with the most risk per line of bash). The script's `.changeset/*.md` reading-and-aggregating logic must be kept (or replaced) — ~30 lines.
- `publish-crates.sh` — `cargo ws publish --publish-interval 10 --from-git --skip-published` does topo-order + pacing + skip-already-published. Direct replacement.
- `validate-release.sh` — `cargo ws plan --skip-published` (lists what would be published) + `cargo ws publish --dry-run` (per-crate verify). Replaces structural checks; the existing script's `cargo check --workspace --all-features` step can stay as an explicit CI step.

**Does NOT replace:**
- `aggregate-changelog.sh` — cargo-workspaces has no changelog support. Either keep the script (it's the most stable of the four) or pair with `git-cliff` driven by Conventional Commits.

**Net result of migration:**
- ~250 lines of bash removed across 3 scripts
- ~50 lines of `cargo ws` invocations added to release.yml
- Changeset workflow preserved (contributors keep using `.changeset/*.md` for bump-level signaling)

**Configuration file:** `[workspace.metadata.workspaces]` block in root `Cargo.toml`:
```toml
[workspace.metadata.workspaces]
allow_branch = "master"      # we currently push from master after merge
no_individual_tags = true    # we use a single vX.Y.Z tag, not per-crate name@X.Y.Z tags
```

**Gotchas to validate during spike:**
1. **`--skip-published` lives on `plan`, not `publish`.** Partial-publish recovery: re-run with `--from-git` to skip the version-bump step, but a crate that was mid-publish when CI died may need manual `cargo publish` from its directory. Not worse than today, just different.
2. **Default tag scheme** creates per-crate `name@X.Y.Z` tags in addition to the global `vX.Y.Z` — must explicitly suppress with `--no-individual-tags` to avoid breaking `aur-publish.yml`'s `release: published` trigger (which expects exactly one tag per release).
3. **Native alternative worth knowing:** Rust 1.90 added `cargo publish --workspace` with built-in topo-sort. Covers the publish step with zero deps but doesn't bump versions or skip-already-published — cargo-workspaces still wins for the bump+publish combo.
4. **`develop` branch defaults.** cargo-workspaces defaults push to `origin master`; release.yml runs on master so this is fine, but the `develop` sync-back step (release.yml step 13) stays as plain git regardless.
5. **`--allow-dirty` semantics differ.** Current publish-crates.sh uses `--allow-dirty` because `bump-version.sh` mutates Cargo.toml without committing. Under cargo-workspaces the bump+commit+publish flow is unified, so `--allow-dirty` shouldn't be needed; verify in spike.

**Effort:** 2–3 days including spike branch + dry-run release rehearsal.

**Motivation:** removes the highest-risk bash (`bump-version.sh`'s sed-based path-dep rewriting — every minor bump rewrites 9 manifests) in favor of a tool that does this as its primary feature. Preserves the existing changesets contributor experience; no commit-convention switch required.

---

## Option 3 — Migrate to `release-plz`

**What it is.** Full Rust release automation. Long-lived "chore: release" PR that release-plz keeps rebased; merging the PR triggers tag creation, crates.io publish, and GitHub Release creation. v0.3.157 (Feb 2026), 1.4k stars, actively maintained, used by `tokio-console`, `rabbitmq-stream-rust-client`.

Docs: https://release-plz.dev/docs

**Replaces:** all 4 scripts plus the `.changeset/` directory entirely.

**Workflow shift:**
- Today: every contributor PR ships a `.changeset/*.md` file declaring `bump: major|minor|patch`. Release happens automatically on merge to master.
- With release-plz: contributors write Conventional Commits (`feat:`, `fix:`, `feat!:` for breaking, etc.). release-plz parses commits since last tag, opens a "chore: release" PR with proposed version + CHANGELOG diff. Merging the release PR triggers publish.

**What replaces what:**
| Today | release-plz |
|---|---|
| `.changeset/*.md` with `bump:` frontmatter | Conventional Commit prefix on each commit |
| `bump-version.sh` | `release-plz update` (auto, runs in release-PR job) |
| `aggregate-changelog.sh` | git-cliff via release-plz (built-in, configurable templates) |
| `publish-crates.sh` | `release-plz release` (release job, runs after PR merge) |
| `validate-release.sh` | The release PR diff itself + `release-plz release --dry-run` |
| `release.yml` (~140 lines) | `.github/workflows/release-plz.yml` (~30 lines) using `release-plz/action@v0` |

**Configuration file:** `release-plz.toml` at repo root, with `[workspace]` defaults and `[[package]]` overrides per-crate. Per-crate `publish = false` for crates not on crates.io.

**Required secrets:**
- `GITHUB_TOKEN` (built-in)
- `CARGO_REGISTRY_TOKEN` (already present)
- Recommended: `RELEASE_PLZ_TOKEN` (PAT or GitHub App) so release-PR merges retrigger CI; with the default `GITHUB_TOKEN` they don't.

**Gotchas:**
1. **Convention switch is non-negotiable.** Every commit needs a Conventional Commits prefix. CONTRIBUTING.md needs updating; CI should add a commit-message linter (e.g. `commitlint`).
2. **Workspace path-dep handling has been rocky pre-0.3.157.** Issue #1181 (bump-loop on workspace path deps) was fixed in v0.3.157 (Feb 2026). For a 9-crate workspace with `^X.Y` constraints, pilot on a branch before cutting over.
3. **`cargo-semver-checks` runs against the *default* feature set.** A breaking change behind a feature flag (e.g. `sqlite`-only API change) may not be detected. Not a blocker — manual `bump: major!` overrides are still possible — but worth knowing.
4. **No equivalent to `validate-release.sh` as a single command.** The release PR's diff is the primary review surface; `release-plz release --dry-run` covers crates.io shape but not the structural checks our current script does. Some current checks (e.g. `cargo check --workspace --all-features`) become explicit CI steps rather than living inside the release script.
5. **`kanban-mcp` publishability.** If we don't publish kanban-mcp to crates.io, mark `publish = false` in `release-plz.toml`. Current behavior: `publish-crates.sh` does publish it. Decision needed.
6. **AUR workflow compatibility.** `aur-publish.yml` triggers on `release: published`. release-plz creates GitHub Releases via the official action — trigger should still fire correctly, but verify in spike.
7. **`develop` sync-back.** release-plz operates on a single trunk. The current `master` (release) / `develop` (integration) split needs reconsideration — either keep develop sync-back as a separate workflow (today's approach), or collapse to single trunk (typical release-plz pattern).

**Effort:** 1 week including spike, contributor docs update, commitlint wiring, dry-run rehearsal.

**Motivation:** maximally reduces release-infra surface (4 scripts + 140-line workflow → ~30-line workflow + config file). Brings the project onto the same release pattern as a large fraction of the modern Rust ecosystem, lowering onboarding cost for new contributors familiar with that pattern. Adds release-PR review gate (last chance to catch a bad bump before publish).

---

## Comparison matrix

| Concern | Today (4 scripts) | Option 1 (dynamic) | Option 2 (cargo-workspaces) | Option 3 (release-plz) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Lines of bash to maintain | ~400 | ~415 (slight increase) | ~150 (mostly aggregate-changelog) | 0 |
| Path-dep `^X.Y` rewriting | Bespoke sed | Bespoke sed (unchanged) | Native `cargo ws version` | Native `release-plz update` |
| Topo-sorted publish | Hand-curated array | Auto via `cargo metadata` | Native `cargo ws publish` | Native `release-plz release` |
| Skip-already-published | curl + grep | curl + grep (unchanged) | Native `--skip-published` | Native (registry query) |
| Pacing between publishes | `sleep 10` | `sleep 10` | `--publish-interval 10` | `publish_timeout` (default 30m, polls registry) |
| Changeset support | Custom format | Custom format (unchanged) | Custom format (kept) | None — Conventional Commits |
| Changelog generation | aggregate-changelog.sh | aggregate-changelog.sh (unchanged) | aggregate-changelog.sh OR git-cliff | Built-in (git-cliff) |
| Release model | On-merge automatic | On-merge automatic | On-merge automatic | Release PR (review gate) |
| Contributor friction | Add a `.changeset/*.md` file | Same | Same | Use Conventional Commits |
| Migration risk | — | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Time to migrate | — | 0.5 day | 2–3 days | ~1 week |
| AUR workflow compatibility | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (suppress per-crate tags) | Verify in spike |
| Develop sync-back | Plain git step | Plain git step | Plain git step | Reconsider trunk model |

---

## Recommended path

**Now (this week):** Execute option 1 as the immediate follow-up to the validate-release.sh staleness fix. Half-day. Eliminates the duplicate-list class of bug structurally.

**Next sprint (spike):** One-day branch with cargo-workspaces config, run a dry-run release end-to-end against a fork or a `0.4.0-rc1` tag. If clean, schedule a 2-day cutover. Decision criterion: does release.yml shrink, and does the `cargo ws publish` recovery story feel safer than the current bash on partial-publish failure?

**Later (defer):** release-plz evaluation. Only consider if (a) we want a release-PR review gate, or (b) the release-PR + Conventional Commits ergonomics are independently desired by contributors, or (c) cargo-workspaces hits a wall during the spike.

---

## TDD plan (Red → Green → Refactor)

The card's eventual implementation, per project conventions in CLAUDE.md.

### For option 1 (dynamic crate list)

- **Red:** Write a CI check `scripts/check-crate-list-dynamic.sh` that:
  1. Compares `get_publish_order` output against the current hardcoded array.
  2. Asserts every crate in `crates/` appears in the topo-sorted list.
  3. Asserts the topo-sort respects every inter-crate `path = ` dep.
  Run it now — the equality check FAILS (proving the test is real and the current scripts have hand-curated drift).
- **Green:** Replace the hardcoded arrays in `validate-release.sh` and `publish-crates.sh` with a call to `get_publish_order`. Re-run the check; passes. Run a full local rehearsal of `validate-release.sh` against a tempfile workspace.
- **Refactor:** Extract `get_publish_order` into `scripts/lib/crate-order.sh`, source from both call sites. Add a unit-test stub (using a fixture workspace under `tests/fixtures/`) so future contributors can verify the topo-sort against a known graph. Confirm the CI check still passes.

### For option 2 (cargo-workspaces spike)

- **Red:** Branch off develop. In CI, add a workflow that on `workflow_dispatch` runs `cargo ws version patch --yes --no-git-commit --no-git-push` against a fresh checkout, then runs `cargo check --workspace --all-features`. Trigger manually — initial run should fail because cargo-workspaces isn't installed. Add `taiki-e/install-action@cargo-workspaces`. Re-run; this time it should succeed but produce a *diff* — that diff is the test artifact: every Cargo.toml's version field bumped, every `version = "^0.3"` rewritten to `^0.3.6` (or whatever next patch is). Compare to the diff `bump-version.sh` produces from the same starting state. Discrepancies are bugs to triage.
- **Green:** Once the diffs match (modulo the changeset reading, which is upstream of cargo-workspaces), wire `cargo ws publish --dry-run --skip-published` and confirm the per-crate publish set matches `publish-crates.sh`'s output.
- **Refactor:** Replace `bump-version.sh`'s sed block with a `cargo ws version` call (keeping the changeset-reading prelude). Replace `publish-crates.sh` with `cargo ws publish`. Replace `validate-release.sh` Step 5 with `cargo ws plan --skip-published`. Delete the dead bash. Update CONTRIBUTING.md if any contributor-facing surface changed (it shouldn't — changesets stay the same).

### For option 3 (release-plz, deferred)

Same Red → Green → Refactor pattern, on a longer-lived spike branch. Red phase includes: (a) fixture commits with Conventional Commit prefixes, (b) verify release-PR opens correctly with expected version + CHANGELOG diff, (c) verify path-dep `^X.Y` rewrites match what bump-version.sh would produce. Skip until option 2 is evaluated.

---

## SOLID principles applied

- **Single Responsibility.** Today, the 4 scripts each have a single responsibility (good). The crate list, however, has *no* clear owner — both `validate-release.sh` and `publish-crates.sh` claim it. Option 1 gives it one owner (`scripts/lib/crate-order.sh`); Option 2/3 give it to the tool. All three options improve SRP at the workspace level.
- **Open/Closed.** Adding a 10th crate today requires editing 2 scripts. Option 1: edit zero (auto-discovered). Option 2: edit zero (auto-discovered by `cargo ws`). Option 3: edit zero (auto-discovered by release-plz). All three options achieve OCP for the "add a crate" extension.
- **Liskov Substitution.** N/A at the script layer.
- **Interface Segregation.** Today, `publish-crates.sh` calls `validate-release` *internally* — coupling the publish interface to the validation interface. Option 2 splits these (`cargo ws plan` vs `cargo ws publish`). Option 3 keeps them separate by design (release-PR is the validation gate, release job is the publish action).
- **Dependency Inversion.** Today's scripts depend on a concrete file path layout (`crates/*/Cargo.toml`) and a concrete version regex. Option 1 inverts via `cargo metadata`'s structured output. Options 2/3 invert via the tool's stable CLI contract.

---

## Open questions / decisions requiring user input

1. **Publish kanban-mcp to crates.io?** Currently it's published. If we keep that, no action; if we don't, set `publish = false` in whichever tool config we land on (and remove from `publish-crates.sh`'s array if staying on option 1).
2. **Stay on changesets, or adopt Conventional Commits?** This is the make-or-break for option 3. Worth a separate discussion before committing.
3. **Master/develop split, or collapse to single trunk?** Today: `develop` is the integration branch, `master` is the release branch, sync-back step closes the loop. release-plz typically operates on a single trunk. cargo-workspaces and option 1 are agnostic.
4. **Spike budget.** Allocate 1 day to option 1 (clear scope) and decide whether to allocate 2–3 more for option 2 spike based on option 1's outcome.

---

## References

- cargo-workspaces — https://github.com/pksunkara/cargo-workspaces
- release-plz — https://release-plz.dev/docs
- release-plz quickstart — https://release-plz.dev/docs/github/quickstart
- cargo metadata format — https://doc.rust-lang.org/cargo/commands/cargo-metadata.html
- Orhun's "Fully Automated Releases for Rust Projects" — https://blog.orhun.dev/automated-rust-releases/
- Rust 1.90 `cargo publish --workspace` — context for native alternative without third-party tooling
- Sibling card: PR #208 (release 0.4.0) — fix validate-release.sh staleness before merge
cards.title: Evaluate replacing custom release scripts with cargo-workspaces or release-plz

cards.description: kanban-service was missing from publish-crates.sh and kanban-mcp was listed before it. Fixed dependency order so kanban-service is published before kanban-mcp.
cards.title: Fix publish-crates order: add kanban-service before kanban-mcp

cards.description: TUI handlers catch command execution errors with tracing::error! but never call set_error() to show them to the user. Wire up Banner display for NotFound and other domain errors so the user gets visible feedback when an action fails silently. See PR #200 review for context.
cards.title: Surface command errors to user via Banner in TUI handlers

cards.description:
## Problem

The current storage backend system requires compile-time feature flags threaded through 3 crates (`kanban-service` → `kanban-cli` → `kanban-tui`) for each backend. Third-party backends cannot be added without forking or rebuilding the app. This defeats the pluggable storage goal.

Dynamic plugin approaches (.so/dlopen, WASM, process-based IPC) all introduce significant complexity and tradeoffs (ABI instability, latency, unsafe code, heavy dependencies).

## Solution: Inverted Dependency Model

Instead of kanban loading plugins, **the plugin builds kanban**. The third-party backend owns `main()` and depends on kanban as a library.

### Third-party usage

```rust
// kanban-s3/src/main.rs
use kanban_cli::App;
use kanban_s3_backend::S3StoreFactory;

fn main() {
    let mut app = App::default();
    app.register_backend(S3StoreFactory);
    app.run();
}
```

Users install with: `cargo install kanban-s3`

### Changes Required

#### 1. `kanban-cli` — expose as library + binary
- Add `lib.rs` alongside `main.rs`
- Export an `App` struct (or `AppBuilder`) with:
  - `App::default()` — creates app with no backends
  - `App::with_defaults()` — creates app with in-tree backends (JSON + SQLite)
  - `app.register_backend(impl StoreFactory)` — register additional backends
  - `app.run()` — starts the TUI/CLI
- `main.rs` just calls `App::with_defaults().run()`

#### 2. `kanban-mcp` — same pattern
- Expose `KanbanMcpServer` builder with `register_backend`
- Third parties can build a custom MCP server binary with their backend

#### 3. `kanban-service` — simplify
- Remove `default_registry()` and feature-gated backend registration
- Accept a `StoreRegistry` from the caller (passed down from `App`)
- Make all in-tree backends default deps (no more feature flags for JSON/SQLite)

#### 4. `kanban-persistence` — add Tier 1 contract tests
- Move persistence-level contract tests into `kanban-persistence` behind `test-helpers` feature
- Tests operate at `StoreSnapshot` save/load level only (no `KanbanContext` dependency)
- Third-party backends depend only on `kanban-persistence` for traits + test suite
- Keep existing Tier 2 roundtrip tests in `kanban-service` for in-tree backends

#### 5. Remove feature flag wiring
- Remove `sqlite` feature from `kanban-cli`, `kanban-tui`, `kanban-mcp`
- Remove `sqlite-storage`/`json-storage` features from `kanban-service`
- Both backends become unconditional dependencies

### Third-party backend developer experience

1. Create crate, depend on `kanban-persistence` (for traits) and `kanban-cli` (for `App`)
2. Implement `PersistenceStore` + `StoreFactory`
3. Run `kanban_persistence::contract_tests!(my_factory)` in tests (Tier 1 — persistence level)
4. Build binary: `App::default().register_backend(MyFactory).run()`
5. Publish to crates.io

### Multi-client compatibility (TUI, CLI, MCP)

All three client surfaces accept backends through the same pattern — they all flow to StoreRegistry -> KanbanContext:

```rust
// TUI
App::default().register_backend(PostgresStoreFactory).run();

// CLI (headless)
App::default().register_backend(PostgresStoreFactory).run_cli();

// MCP server
McpServer::default().register_backend(PostgresStoreFactory).serve();
```

Third-party crates can ship multiple binaries from one crate:

```toml
# kanban-postgres/Cargo.toml
[[bin]]
name = "kanban-postgres"
path = "src/main.rs"         # TUI with Postgres

[[bin]]
name = "kanban-postgres-mcp"
path = "src/mcp.rs"          # MCP server with Postgres
```

This requires both kanban-cli and kanban-mcp to expose their runners as library APIs that accept a StoreRegistry. The internal flow is unchanged — stores are created before any client-specific code runs, so TUI/CLI/MCP never need to know which backend is in use.

### What stays the same
- `PersistenceStore` and `StoreFactory` trait definitions unchanged
- `StoreRegistry` matching logic unchanged
- All existing domain/service/TUI code unchanged
- Default `kanban` binary ships with JSON + SQLite as today

### Precedent
This is the same pattern used by axum, bevy, mdbook — the "plugin" owns main(), the framework is a library dependency.

## Reference Implementation: PostgreSQL Backend (`kanban-persistence-postgres`)

Build a PostgreSQL storage backend as the first third-party-style plugin to validate the architecture. This serves as both a useful backend and a reference for third-party developers.

### Why PostgreSQL
- Validates the plugin model end-to-end with a real networked database (vs SQLite's embedded model)
- Exercises the `StoreFactory` pattern with connection strings (`postgres://...`) instead of file paths
- Proves the architecture works for backends where the locator is a URI, not a filesystem path
- High demand use case: shared/team kanban boards backed by a central database

### Implementation
- New crate: `kanban-persistence-postgres` — lives in-tree under `crates/` like JSON and SQLite (same workspace, same code patterns, same quality bar)
- **But wired as a plugin**: not registered in `default_registry()`, no feature flags in `kanban-service`/`kanban-cli`. Instead ships its own binary entrypoint using `App::default().register_backend(PostgresStoreFactory).run()`
- This is the dogfooding constraint: if the in-tree Postgres crate can integrate purely through the plugin API, any external crate can too
- Depends on `kanban-persistence` for traits, `sqlx` with `postgres` feature
- `PostgresStoreFactory` matches `postgres://` and `postgresql://` URI schemes
- Relational schema similar to SQLite backend but using PostgreSQL types (JSONB, TIMESTAMPTZ, etc.)
- Must pass all Tier 1 contract tests from `kanban-persistence`
- Must pass Tier 2 roundtrip tests from `kanban-service`
- Integration tests run against a real PostgreSQL instance (docker-compose or testcontainers)

### Validation criteria
- A third-party developer can look at this crate and replicate the pattern for their own backend
- The crate works both in-tree (as a default backend in the main binary) and as a standalone `cargo install kanban-postgres` binary
- Documentation in the crate serves as a plugin development guide
cards.title: Invert storage backend plugin architecture

cards.description:
## Bug

When kanban-mcp starts, it opens the SQLite database file and holds a file descriptor to that inode. If a git operation (checkout, reset --hard, merge) replaces the database file on disk with a different version (new inode), the MCP server continues writing to the old inode via its open file descriptor. The new inode (now at the same path) does not receive those writes.

## Symptoms observed

1. MCP tool calls return success (card created, sprint assigned, status updated).
2. The kanban CLI — which opens a fresh connection to the current file — cannot find the cards.
3. sqlite3 direct queries on the database file confirm the records do not exist.
4. The SQLite WAL file is 0 bytes (all MCP writes are silently lost or checkpointed to the orphaned old inode).
5. The issue persists until the MCP server process is restarted and reopens the file by path (getting the new inode).

## Reproduction

1. Start kanban-mcp against a SQLite-backed kanban file (e.g. docs/sock).
2. Run a git operation that replaces the file: git checkout <branch> -- docs/sock or git reset --hard.
3. Use an MCP tool to create a card. The response indicates success.
4. Run kanban docs/sock card get <new-card-id> via the CLI. Returns 'Card not found'.
5. Run sqlite3 docs/sock 'SELECT * FROM cards ORDER BY card_number DESC LIMIT 3;'. New card is absent.

## Root cause

SQLite identifies files by inode, not path. When git replaces the file (unlink + create new inode), the MCP server's open fd still points to the old inode. Writes go to the old inode which is no longer reachable via the filesystem path. The new inode at docs/sock is a fresh database that never receives those writes.

## Impact

- Silent data loss: users believe kanban state is persisted when it is not.
- Requires MCP server restart to recover; any changes made in the interim must be redone.
- Particularly affects workflows that use git branching (checkout, stash, merge) on the same file the MCP server has open.

## Suggested fix

Options in rough priority order:

1. **Reopen by path on each write** — detect stale fd via inode comparison (stat the path vs fstat the fd) before each write. If they differ, close and reopen by path. This is the most robust fix.
2. **Use inotify/kqueue to watch the file path** — receive IN_DELETE_SELF or IN_MOVE_SELF events and reopen automatically.
3. **Expose a SIGHUP or MCP tool to reload the file** — lets the client (e.g. Claude Code) trigger a reconnect after a known git operation.
4. **Document the limitation** — warn in the MCP server startup log that git operations replacing the file require a server restart.
cards.title: MCP server holds stale file descriptor when git replaces the database file on disk

cards.description:
## Problem

When a card is created, `assigned_prefix` is set from `board.card_prefix` at creation time. If the board prefix later changes, or the card is assigned to a sprint with its own `card_prefix`, the identifier lookup uses the stale `assigned_prefix` (it ranks above `sprint.card_prefix` in the hierarchy), making the card unreachable by its sprint prefix.

---

## Design Discussion

### Why multi-alias (track all historical prefixes) does not work

`prefix_counters` is per-prefix per-board. Two sprints can independently use the same `card_prefix`. If Card A (sprint 1, prefix SPR) and Card B (sprint 3, prefix SPR) both have card_number=5, `SPR-5` is genuinely ambiguous. The KAN-211 multi-match return handles ambiguity gracefully but does not eliminate it — two legitimately different cards with the same identifier is worse than a lookup oddity.

### Why hierarchy reorder has the same collision risk

Reordering to `sprint.card_prefix → card.assigned_prefix → board.card_prefix` shifts which scenario causes the collision but does not eliminate it. A card created as KAN-5 assigned to sprint SPR resolves as SPR-5. A card created as SPR-5 (board prefix was SPR at creation) also resolves as SPR-5. Same collision, different trigger.

### Sprint prefix uniqueness closes the collision

All constraints are scoped to a single board — `prefix_counters`, sprint `card_prefix` values, `assigned_prefix` values, and card numbers all live within one board. Cross-board sprints are not a realistic Scrum/Kanban scenario (that is program-level coordination, not sprint sharing).

If sprint `card_prefix` is enforced unique against `board.prefix_counters` (all historical board prefixes) AND against other sprints on the same board, then no sprint prefix can ever equal any card's `assigned_prefix`. The collision is structurally impossible.

### card.card_prefix is dead weight

`card.card_prefix` (the per-card override field) is always `None` at creation (card_commands.rs:154). The CLI handler hardcodes `card_prefix: FieldUpdate::NoChange`. The TUI has no UI for it. It has never been set in any code path. It adds a fourth level to the hierarchy with no practical benefit.

### card.assigned_prefix should also be dropped

`assigned_prefix` only exists to preserve the prefix a card was created under if `board.card_prefix` later changes. If the board prefix is locked (immutable once cards exist), `assigned_prefix` is always equal to `board.card_prefix` — redundant by definition. It can be dropped entirely and prefix resolved purely by function.

---

## Target Model

**Prefix resolution (fully functional, nothing stored on card):**
`sprint.card_prefix → board.card_prefix`

- Card created on board → board prefix + board counter → e.g. KAN-5
- Card assigned to sprint with prefix SPR → resolves as SPR-5 (same number, prefix is dynamic)
- Card dropped from sprint → resolves as KAN-5 again
- Card number is stable (assigned once at creation, never changes)

**Locking rules:**
- `board.card_prefix` is immutable once the first card is created
- `sprint.card_prefix` is immutable once the first card is assigned to the sprint
- Sprint `card_prefix` must not appear in `board.prefix_counters` (all historical board prefixes)
- Sprint `card_prefix` must not match any other sprint's `card_prefix` on the same board

**Counters:**
- Board: `card_counter: u32` — single value, replaces `prefix_counters: HashMap<String, u32>` (since prefix is now locked, the HashMap always had one meaningful key)
- Sprint: no card counter — sprint prefix is a label only, card numbers come from the board counter
- Sprint numbering counter (`sprint_counters`) unchanged

---

## Migration Required (both backends)

### Key insight: assigned_prefix IS the prefix change history

`card.assigned_prefix` on existing cards already records exactly what prefix each card was created under — it is the historical record of board prefix changes, just stored per-card rather than per-board. The migration can use it precisely:

1. Bucket all cards by `assigned_prefix` value
2. Each bucket's `max(card_number) + 1` is the correct counter for that prefix
3. The bucket matching `board.card_prefix` becomes `board.card_counter`
4. Collision detection: if card_numbers overlap across buckets, those cards would become ambiguous post-migration — surface as errors before dropping the field

This makes the migration fully deterministic. No guessing, no heuristics. The existing field contains exactly the information needed to reconstruct counters correctly and detect every collision case. After the migration, the field is dropped and locking prevents the situation from recurring.

Similarly, `sprint_logs` on cards already tracks which sprints a card has been assigned to with timestamps — the same pattern applied to boards would reconstruct the full prefix history if needed.

### JSON: V2 → V3

1. For each board: bucket cards by `assigned_prefix`, find bucket matching `board.card_prefix`, set `board.card_counter` to `max(card_number) + 1` from that bucket
2. Collision check: if any card_number appears in more than one `assigned_prefix` bucket, abort with an error listing the conflicting identifiers
3. Drop `card.assigned_prefix` on every card
4. Drop `card.card_prefix` on every card

### SQLite: schema v1 → v2

1. Same bucketing and collision check logic in a migration transaction
2. Populate `card_counter` on boards table from the above
3. `ALTER TABLE cards DROP COLUMN assigned_prefix`
4. `ALTER TABLE cards DROP COLUMN card_prefix`
5. Wrap in a single transaction — abort entirely on collision rather than partial migrate

### Hard edge case

If a board changed prefix mid-life (KAN-1..50, then PROJ-1..30), card_numbers 1-30 exist in both buckets. Migration aborts with:
```
Migration error: board "My Board" has ambiguous card numbers after prefix change.
  KAN-1..30 and PROJ-1..30 cannot both be identified post-migration.
  Resolve by deleting or re-numbering cards in one prefix group before migrating.
```

---

## Files Affected

- `crates/kanban-domain/src/card.rs` — drop `card_prefix`, `assigned_prefix` fields; update `CardUpdate`, `branch_name()`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs` — replace `prefix_counters: HashMap` with `card_counter: u32`; add prefix lock enforcement
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/card_commands.rs` — update `CreateCard` to use `board.card_counter`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/sprint_commands.rs` — enforce sprint prefix uniqueness and immutability
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/search/mod.rs` — simplify hierarchy to two levels
- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/lib.rs` — V2→V3 migration
- `crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/` — schema v1→v2 migration
- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs` — remove `card_prefix` from card update tool
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/cli.rs` — remove `card_prefix` from card update command
cards.title: Redesign card identifier model: drop stored prefixes, lock board/sprint prefix, simplify counters

cards.description:
Add shields.io / repology badges for AUR, nixpkgs, and the personal Homebrew tap, plus a CI status badge, matching the existing crates.io and license badges at the top of `README.md`.

## Badges to add

```markdown
[![CI](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/actions/workflows/ci.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/actions/workflows/ci.yml)
[![AUR](https://repology.org/badge/version-for-repo/aur/kanban.svg)](https://repology.org/project/kanban/versions)
[![nixpkgs](https://repology.org/badge/version-for-repo/nix_unstable/kanban.svg)](https://repology.org/project/kanban/versions)
[![Homebrew](https://img.shields.io/badge/dynamic/regex?url=https%3A%2F%2Fraw.githubusercontent.com%2Ffulsomenko%2Fhomebrew-tap%2Fmaster%2FFormula%2Fkanban.rb&search=version%20%22%28.%2A%29%22&replace=%241&label=homebrew)](https://github.com/fulsomenko/homebrew-tap)
```

## Context

- crates.io and license badges already exist on lines 3-4 of `README.md`.
- CI badge: auto-updates from the GitHub Actions `ci.yml` workflow.
- AUR / nixpkgs badges: repology as source (consistent, tracks both); kanban is packaged as `kanban` in both.
- Homebrew tap: personal tap at `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap`, default branch `master`, single formula `Formula/kanban.rb` (auto-bumped by `4f2cd4c ci: auto-bump Homebrew tap formula on each release`). No tags or releases on the tap, so the badge is a shields.io dynamic regex over the raw formula file matching the `version "X.Y.Z"` line.

## Placement

Insert after the existing two badges on lines 3-4 of `README.md`.

## Verification

- Each badge URL returns a 200 (not a placeholder "no result" SVG).
- All four new badges render and link to the right destination.
- Render the README on GitHub (or `gh repo view --web`) once merged to confirm.
cards.title: Add AUR, nixpkgs, and Homebrew tap version badges to README

cards.description:
**Bug:** When a card is assigned to a sprint with a prefix override (e.g. `CAT`), the card gets a new identifier (`CAT-260`). However, looking up the card using that identifier returns "Card not found" — only the original identifier (`KAN-260`) works.

**Observed:**
```
kanban card get CAT-260  →  Error: Card not found: 'CAT-260'
kanban card get KAN-260  →  success
```

**Expected:** The card should be retrievable by its sprint-assigned identifier (`CAT-260`).

**Steps to reproduce:**
1. Create a card on a board with prefix `KAN` — gets ID `KAN-260`
2. Assign the card to a sprint with prefix override `CAT` — branch name becomes `CAT-260`
3. Run `kanban card get CAT-260`
4. Observe "Card not found" error

**Root cause area:** Card lookup likely only indexes by the canonical `assigned_prefix` + `card_number`, not by the sprint-assigned prefix.
cards.title: Card not found when looked up by sprint-assigned prefix identifier

cards.description:
## Goal

Render the card description area in the card details view as styled markdown instead of plain text, using the `termimad` crate.

## Background

The card details panel currently renders description as raw text. The `glow` CLI (charmbracelet/glow) was considered but is a Go binary and cannot be used as a Rust library dependency. `termimad` is the idiomatic Rust alternative: it parses and renders CommonMark markdown into crossterm-compatible styled text and integrates cleanly with ratatui.

## Relevant files

- `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/` — card details rendering components
- `crates/kanban-tui/Cargo.toml` — add `termimad` dependency here

## Implementation steps

1. Add `termimad` to `crates/kanban-tui/Cargo.toml`
2. In the card details UI component, replace the plain-text description render with a `termimad`-rendered version
3. Create a helper function (e.g. `render_markdown(text: &str, width: u16) -> Text`) that converts a markdown string to ratatui `Text` using termimad's skin/area API
4. Wire the helper into the existing card details widget, replacing raw description output
5. Handle empty/None descriptions gracefully (render nothing or a placeholder)
6. Ensure the rendered output scrolls correctly if the description is long

## Acceptance criteria

- Headings, bold, italic, inline code, code blocks, bullet lists, and numbered lists all render with appropriate terminal styling
- Plain text descriptions with no markdown syntax render without visual artifacts
- The card details view is not visually broken at narrow terminal widths
- All existing `kanban-tui` tests continue to pass
- New unit test: `test_render_markdown_empty_string_returns_empty_text` and `test_render_markdown_bold_produces_styled_spans`

## TDD notes

Follow Red-Green-Refactor. Write the two unit tests above (in `crates/kanban-tui/src/` under `#[cfg(test)]`) and present them for review before touching any implementation code.
cards.title: Render card description markdown using termimad in the TUI details view

cards.title: Remove unused futures dependencies

cards.description:
## Concept

The command log is intentionally in-memory and session-scoped (see KAN-405). This card explores the next step: a message bus architecture that populates each client's in-memory command log from a shared stream, enabling cross-client undo/redo for both single-user multi-device and multi-user collaborative scenarios — without ever persisting the command log to the data file.

## Core idea

Each running kanban instance maintains its own in-memory command log as today. A lightweight shared **command bus** (a separate channel, not the data file) broadcasts executed commands to all subscribed clients. When a client receives a remote command, it appends it to its in-memory log and applies it to its local state. Undo/redo remains in-memory; the bus is the synchronisation mechanism, not a persistence layer.

## Key properties

- **In-memory command log preserved** — no command log in `kanban.json` or `kanban.sqlite`
- **Decoupled from the data layer** — command bus is a separate concern from `PersistenceStore`
- **Session-tagged commands** — each command batch carries a `session_id` so clients can distinguish local vs remote commands and handle undo scope accordingly
- **Graceful degradation** — if no bus is running, the client operates in single-session mode as today

## Architectural components

### CommandBus trait (new, in `kanban-core` or `kanban-service`)
```rust
pub trait CommandBus: Send + Sync {
    /// Publish a batch of commands to all subscribers.
    fn publish(&self, batch: CommandEnvelope) -> KanbanResult<()>;
    /// Subscribe to incoming command batches from remote sessions.
    fn subscribe(&self) -> CommandReceiver;
}

pub struct CommandEnvelope {
    pub session_id: Uuid,
    pub batch: Vec<Command>,
    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
}
```

### Possible bus implementations

| Backend | Description | Use case |
|---|---|---|
| `NullBus` | No-op (current behaviour) | Single-session, no collaboration |
| `FileBus` | JSON Lines append-only log file + `inotify`/`kqueue` watcher | Single-machine multi-instance |
| `UnixSocketBus` | Local Unix domain socket with a coordinator process | Single-machine low-latency |
| `WsBus` | WebSocket server/client | Multi-machine, multi-user |

### Integration in KanbanContext

`KanbanContext` gains an optional `CommandBus`. After `execute()` succeeds locally, the batch is published to the bus. A background task (or poll on the next `execute()`) drains incoming remote batches and applies them to the local in-memory store — similar to a git fetch that doesn't touch the working tree.

### Conflict handling

Remote commands that conflict with local undone state need a policy. Simplest approach for v1: **remote commands always win** and reset the local undo stack to the post-remote state (last-write-wins per session). CRDT-style operational transforms are a future option.

## Undo semantics across clients

Two reasonable models:

- **Local undo only** — `undo()` only undoes commands from `this` session; remote commands are not undoable locally. Simple and predictable.
- **Global undo** — `undo()` undoes the most recent command regardless of source session. Matches "collaborative undo" UX in editors like Figma.

Recommend starting with **local undo only** (flag remote commands in the log, skip them during undo replay).

## File-based bus as a starting point

A simple `FileBus` appends `CommandEnvelope` as JSON Lines to a sidecar file (e.g. `board.json.bus`). A file watcher notifies subscribers of new lines. This requires no network stack, works on a shared filesystem (NFS, Dropbox), and is easy to inspect and debug. The bus file can be deleted freely — clients reconnect and start receiving new commands from that point.

## Relevant crates to introduce

- `notify` (crate) — cross-platform file system watcher for `FileBus`
- `tokio::sync::broadcast` — in-process channel between `FileBus` watcher and `KanbanContext`

## Relationship to other cards

- KAN-405: established that command log must not be in the data file. This card is the forward path for users who want cross-session or cross-client undo.
- Separate from the nvim-style command log backup card (which is about crash recovery, not collaboration).
cards.title: Design: message-based in-memory command log for cross-client undo/redo
Fix misleading 'Card not found' error when board file does not exist

cards.description:
## Why

KAN-394 made the status ↔ completion-column auto-sync project-wide:
single-card and batch `update_card`/`move_card`/`update_cards`/`move_cards`
all chain commands to keep `CardStatus::Done` ⇔ "card lives in the
resolved completion column". That behaviour is opinionated. Some
workflows treat the last column as a regular stage (e.g. "Done by dev,
awaiting review") rather than a completion gate, and want
`status=Done` set without the card being yanked into that column —
or to move a card into the last column without it being marked
complete.

Today the auto-sync is unconditional. There is no way to opt out.

## What

Add a per-board configuration knob. Suggested shape:

```rust
pub struct Board {
    // ... existing fields
    pub auto_sync_completion: bool,  // defaults to true
}
```

When `false`:
- `KanbanContext::update_card(_, CardUpdate { status: Some(Done), ..)` sets the
  status without chaining a `MoveCard` to the completion column.
- `KanbanContext::move_card(_, completion_col_id, _)` moves the card without
  chaining `status=Done`.
- `update_cards` and `move_cards` (batch) follow the same rule.
- Atomic updates that pin both `column_id` and `status` are unaffected
  (caller intent always wins).

## Where the wiring lives

- `crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs` — add the field, surface it on
  `BoardUpdate` via `FieldUpdate<bool>`, migrate JSON V2 → V3 (default
  true for existing boards so behaviour stays the same on upgrade).
- `crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs` — the four service methods
  (`update_card`, `move_card`, `update_cards`, `move_cards`) each
  resolve the board once and skip the chained command when the flag is
  off. The pure helpers in `kanban_domain::card_lifecycle` stay
  unchanged — they remain "what would happen if auto-sync fired"; the
  service decides whether to call them.
- CLI: surface a flag on `kanban board update` (`--auto-sync-completion <bool>`).
- MCP: expose on `tool_update_board`.
- TUI: settings page / board configuration dialog.

## Acceptance

- New board defaults to auto-sync = on (backwards compatible).
- Existing boards on disk read as auto-sync = on after migration.
- Per-board toggle is honoured by every entry point — there's a test
  per surface (service, MCP integration, CLI integration, TUI handler).
- `crates/kanban-service/tests/completion_sync.rs` gains an
  `auto_sync_off` variant of each existing test.

## Notes / Out of scope

- This is a per-board flag, not a per-card override. Cards inherit from
  their board.
- A potential follow-up: app-level default for `auto_sync_completion`
  applied to new boards (probably overkill — board-level is enough).
- Touches no domain commands; they remain pure single-responsibility.
cards.title: feat(service): make status ↔ completion-column auto-sync configurable per board

## Concept

This card explores the next step: a message bus that populates each client's in-memory command log from a shared stream, enabling cross-client undo/redo for single-user multi-device and multi-user collaboration — without ever persisting the command log to the data file.

When a client receives a remote command, it appends it to its in-memory log and applies it to its local state. Undo/redo stays in-memory; the bus is the sync mechanism, not a persistence layer.

## CommandBus trait (new, in kanban-core or kanban-service)

    fn subscribe() -> CommandReceiver;
}

pub struct CommandEnvelope {
    pub session_id: Uuid,
    pub batch: Vec<Command>,
    pub timestamp: DateTime<Utc>,
}

## Possible bus implementations

- NullBus: no-op, current behaviour, single-session
- FileBus: JSON Lines append-only sidecar file + inotify/kqueue watcher, single-machine multi-instance
- UnixSocketBus: Unix domain socket with coordinator process, single-machine low-latency
- WsBus: WebSocket server/client, multi-machine multi-user

## Integration in KanbanContext

KanbanContext gains an optional CommandBus. After execute() succeeds locally, the batch is published to the bus. A background task drains incoming remote batches and applies them to the local in-memory store.

## Conflict handling

No network stack needed, works on shared filesystems, easy to inspect. The bus file can be deleted freely — clients reconnect and receive new commands from that point onward.

## Relevant crates

- notify (crate) — cross-platform file system watcher for FileBus
- tokio::sync::broadcast — in-process channel between FileBus watcher and KanbanContext

## Relationship to other cards

- KAN-405: established that command log must not be in the data file. This card is the forward path for cross-session or cross-client undo.
- Separate from the nvim-style command log backup card (crash recovery, not collaboration).
cards.title: Design: message-based in-memory command log for cross-client undo/redo

cards.description:
## Concept

The command log lives in-memory and is session-scoped (KAN-405). For users who want cross-session undo without sacrificing a clean data file, we can borrow nvim's approach: a separate, optional undo file that stores the command log independently from the data layer. The data file stays clean and human-readable; the undo file is an ephemeral sidecar that the user can opt into, delete freely, and version-control separately or ignore entirely.

## How nvim does it

nvim writes a .un~ file alongside each edited file. It stores a tree of undo/redo branches. The file is silently ignored if missing or corrupted. Kanban should follow the same contract: the undo file is opt-in, best-effort, and never required for correct operation.

## Proposed design

### File location and naming

- Default: same directory as the data file with a suffix, e.g. board.json -> board.json.undo
- Configurable via AppConfig: undo_dir = ~/.local/share/kanban/undo/ with filenames derived from the data file path hash
- If the undo file cannot be read or is malformed, silently discard it and start with a clean slate

### File format

JSON Lines or a simple binary envelope (baseline snapshot + ordered command batches):

{
  format_version: 1,
  data_file_hash: sha256 of the data file path (for validation),
  session_id: uuid of the session that wrote this file,
  baseline: <Snapshot as JSON>,
  commands: [ [batch0_cmd0, ...], [batch1_cmd0, ...], ... ]
}

The baseline is the snapshot at the time the undo file was started. Commands are the ordered batches applied after that baseline. This is exactly the in-memory state of KanbanContext serialised to disk — so loading it is a straightforward deserialise and populate.

### UndoStore trait (new, in kanban-persistence or kanban-service)

pub trait UndoStore: Send + Sync {
    fn load(&self) -> KanbanResult<Option<UndoState>>;
    fn save(&self, state: &UndoState) -> KanbanResult<()>;
    fn clear(&self) -> KanbanResult<()>;
}

pub struct UndoState {
    pub baseline: Snapshot,
    pub commands: Vec<Vec<Command>>,
    pub cursor: usize,
}

Implementations:
- NullUndoStore: no-op (default, current behaviour)
- FileUndoStore: reads/writes the sidecar file described above

### Integration in KanbanContext

On open: if an UndoStore is configured, call load(). If it returns Some(state), populate baseline_snapshot, command log, and undo_cursor from it. If it returns None or errors, fall back to a clean session.

On execute/undo/redo: after any state change, call save() asynchronously (debounced, same as the main save worker). Errors are logged but not fatal.

On session close / clear_history: call clear() to delete the undo file so it does not accumulate stale state.

### Activation

Opt-in via config:

[undo]
enabled = true          # default: false
dir = ~/.local/share/kanban/undo   # optional, defaults to data file directory

Or via CLI flag: kanban --undo-file board.json.undo

### Safety properties

- The undo file is never required for data integrity. Deleting it is always safe.
- If the data file changes between sessions (e.g. external edit, migration), the baseline hash check fails and the undo file is discarded.
- The undo file is not committed to version control (add *.undo to .gitignore in project templates).
- Undo file writes use atomic rename, same as the main JSON file.

## Relationship to other cards

- KAN-405: established that the command log must not be in the data file. This card is the opt-in escape hatch for users who want cross-session undo without polluting kanban.json.
- KAN-410: message-based bus for cross-client undo — complementary, not competing. Bus = live sync; undo file = crash recovery and single-user cross-session undo.
cards.title: Design: separate undo file for optional cross-session command log persistence

cards.description:
## Why

After KAN-394 moved the status ↔ completion-column orchestration from
the TUI handlers up into the service layer (`KanbanContext::update_card`,
`move_card`, `update_cards`, `move_cards`), several pure helpers in
`kanban_domain::card_lifecycle` lost all their production callers.
They remain in the codebase only to support their own inline unit tests.

Verified at HEAD with:

```
grep -rn "compute_completion_toggle" crates/ | grep -v "fn compute_completion_toggle\|card_lifecycle.rs"
# (empty — zero external callers)

grep -rn "\.new_status" crates/kanban-tui/src/ crates/kanban-service/src/ \
                       crates/kanban-cli/src/ crates/kanban-mcp/src/
# (empty — zero readers of CardMoveResult.new_status)

grep -rn "CompletionToggleResult" crates/ | grep -v card_lifecycle
# (empty — type is module-private in practice)
```

Carrying dead code creates two ongoing costs:

1. The inline unit tests give a false sense that the helper is
   exercised — but no production path runs through it.
2. The `new_status` field on `CardMoveResult` is a footgun: a future
   caller might read it expecting the lifecycle sync, not realise the
   service layer already chains the same logic, and end up double-syncing.

## What

Delete the dead surface from `crates/kanban-domain/src/card_lifecycle.rs`:

- `pub fn compute_completion_toggle(...) -> Option<CompletionToggleResult>`
- `pub struct CompletionToggleResult { new_status, target_column_id, new_position }`
- The `new_status: Option<CardStatus>` field on `pub struct CardMoveResult`
  (keep `target_column_id` and `new_position` — those *are* used)
- The inline tests under `// --- compute_completion_toggle ---` and the
  status-flip-related assertions inside the `compute_card_column_move` tests
- Update `compute_card_column_move` to return only `target_column_id` and
  `new_position` — the status sync is no longer this function's responsibility

Keep:

- `target_column_for_status` and `target_status_for_column_move` — both
  used by `KanbanContext::compute_target_column_for_status` and
  `KanbanContext::compute_target_status_for_move`
- `compute_card_column_move` itself — still used by
  `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs::handle_move_card` and
  `move_selected_cards` to resolve left/right direction → target column
- `sorted_board_columns`, `next_position_in_column`,
  `compact_column_positions`, `should_auto_complete_new_card`,
  `resolve_restore_column`, `migrate_sprint_logs`

## Where the wiring lives

- `crates/kanban-domain/src/card_lifecycle.rs` — delete the items above,
  shrink `CardMoveResult` to two fields, update its docstring.
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs` (lines ~425, ~502) —
  no code change needed: both call sites already ignore `result.new_status`
  after KAN-394's refactor. Confirm with `cargo build` after the field drop.
- Update the module-level doc comment at the top of `card_lifecycle.rs`
  if it references the deleted items.

## Acceptance

- `compute_completion_toggle` / `CompletionToggleResult` symbols are
  fully gone from the codebase (`rg compute_completion_toggle` returns
  zero hits, ditto `CompletionToggleResult`).
- `CardMoveResult` has exactly two fields: `target_column_id`,
  `new_position`.
- `cargo build --workspace`, `cargo test --workspace`,
  `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`, and
  `cargo fmt --check` all clean.
- No new test cases needed (we're deleting tests, not adding behaviour);
  the existing `crates/kanban-service/tests/completion_sync.rs` and
  `crates/kanban-tui/tests/batch_completion_sync_tests.rs` already lock
  in the actual lifecycle behaviour.

## Commit shape

Single `chore(domain): drop dead lifecycle helpers superseded by KAN-394`
commit. Roughly:

- ~60 lines removed in `card_lifecycle.rs` (function body + struct +
  inline tests + the `new_status` computation inside
  `compute_card_column_move`)
- No production callers touched
- Test count drops by ~6 inline tests; service + tui integration tests
  unchanged

## Notes / Out of scope

- This does **not** touch `target_column_for_status` or
  `target_status_for_column_move` — those are the live helpers the
  service layer now depends on.
- This does **not** revisit the auto-sync semantics — that's a separate
  follow-up (configurability: KAN-432).
- If a future contributor wants the toggle-completion shape back, they
  should reach for the service-layer method directly (`update_card`
  with `status` set), not reintroduce the helper.
cards.title: chore(domain): drop dead lifecycle helpers superseded by KAN-394
Refactor error pluralization so the narrating comment goes away

cards.description:
## Observed

Two places in the TUI have lists that support j/k navigation but do not scroll, making items below the visible area unreachable.

### 1. Board edit view — Sprints [4] and Columns [5] panels

render_board_sprints_list and render_board_columns_list in kanban-tui/src/ui/board_detail.rs both render a Paragraph::new(lines) with no scroll offset. The selection highlight (app.selection.sprint.get() / app.dialog_input.column_selection.get()) moves, but the paragraph never scrolls.

### 2. Card list sprint filter popup

The sprint list in the filter popup (kanban-tui/src/components/filter_popup.rs line 119) also renders Paragraph::new(sprint_lines) with no scroll offset. On boards with many sprints the list clips at the popup boundary.

## Root cause (shared)

All three Paragraph widgets clip at their widget boundary. A viewport offset must be passed explicitly via .scroll((row, 0)). The selection index is tracked in all cases but never used to derive an offset.

## Implementation — PR #273

https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/pull/273

The fix went deeper than the original "fix direction" suggested. Rather than re-deriving a sticky-bottom offset on every render, the PR matches the minimal-scroll behavior of the main card list:

- Extracted the pure scroll-math primitive `scroll_offset_to_keep_visible(current_offset, selected, viewport_height) -> usize` into `kanban_core::pagination`. `Page::scroll_to_visible` now delegates to it, so `ListComponent`, `card_list`, and every other Page-based list reuse the same logic with no behavior change.
- Added `Cell<usize>` scroll-offset fields alongside the existing selections: `SelectionHub.sprint_scroll`, `DialogInputState.column_scroll`, `FilterDialogState.item_scroll` (the last is reset on `next_section`/`prev_section`). Each renderer reads the cell, calls the pure function, writes back, and applies the offset via `Paragraph::scroll`.
- Filter popup uses a translated `selected_line_idx` to account for the title, unassigned-sprints checkbox, and separator that precede the sprint rows.

Tests added: 6 unit tests for the pure function in `kanban-core::pagination`, plus 4 integration tests in `kanban-tui/tests/scroll_board_edit_lists.rs` — including `test_board_sprints_scroll_offset_is_stable_when_selection_moves_within_viewport`, which verifies the property that distinguishes minimal-scroll from sticky-bottom.

Follow-up tracked in **KAN-456** — "Migrate all SelectionState-backed lists to ListComponent for unified scrolling". That card consumes the pure function added here and finishes the architectural unification across the ~10 other `SelectionState` fields in the app.
cards.title: No scrolling in board edit view sprint and column lists

cards.description:
## Problem

Every CLI command that references another entity requires a raw UUID, which no human knows by heart. Examples:

- `card move KAN-12 --column-id c2a0d5e2-...` — user must look up the column UUID first
- `card create --board-id e119c0... --column-id c1a0d5...` — two UUIDs before anything happens
- `card assign-sprint KAN-12 --sprint-id 3e05b8...` — user must look up the sprint UUID

## Goal

Accept a name (or sprint number) anywhere a UUID is accepted today. UUID still works for scripting.

## Resolution per flag

- `--column-id`: accept column name (case-insensitive); look up board via card's column → board chain
- `--board-id` on `card create`: accept board name (case-insensitive)
- `--sprint-id` on `assign-sprint` / `assign-cards-to-sprint`: accept sprint name or sprint number (e.g. `13`)
- `--column-id` on `card restore` and `move-cards`: same as move

## Error UX

When the name doesn't match, list available options:
`Column 'done' not found. Available: TODO, Doing, Complete`

## Scope

`kanban-cli` only — no service or domain changes needed.
cards.title: Accept entity names (not just UUIDs) in CLI --column-id, --board-id, --sprint-id args

cards.description:
Follow-up from PR #247 (Amanda Frost's Windows duplicate-keypress fix).

The fix in crates/kanban-tui/src/events.rs filters non-Press KeyEventKind variants on Windows. It was merged without tests because the EventHandler path polls crossterm directly, which makes isolated testing awkward.

Scope:
- Extract the filtering predicate into a small pure function (e.g. should_accept_key(&KeyEvent) -> bool) so it can be unit-tested without a real event loop.
- Add unit tests covering: Press accepted on all platforms; Repeat/Release dropped on Windows; Repeat/Release accepted on Linux (cfg-gated tests).

Note: the question of whether `Repeat` should be accepted (held-key behavior under Windows Terminal VT-input mode) is tracked separately in KAN-413. Pick the predicate behavior that matches the resolution of that ticket — if KAN-413 is still open when this is picked up, mirror the current filter exactly so tests lock in present behavior.
cards.title: Add tests for Windows KeyEventKind::Press filter in events.rs

cards.description:
## Why

After KAN-434 collapsed `update_card` and `assign_card_to_sprint` into
shorthands over their plural counterparts, code review surfaced an
inefficiency in the underlying pattern: three batch service methods on
`KanbanContext` compute their `KanbanResult<usize>` return value by
**reading the backend twice** (before-the-mutation count and
after-the-mutation count), then returning the delta.

The three offenders, all in `crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs`:

```
:1048  fn archive_cards            — list_archived_cards() × 2
:1057  fn move_cards               — list_cards_by_column(column_id) × 2
:1145  fn assign_cards_to_sprint   — list_cards_by_sprint(sprint_id) × 2
```

The before/after pattern is the only honest way to compute "how many
cards were actually changed" given today's command surface — some of
the input IDs may already be on the sprint / already archived /
already in the target column (no-ops), and some may be invalid (the
domain commands' `filter_valid_card_ids` helper silently skips them).
Counting the delta on the backend is correct, but each of those
`list_*` calls is wasted work that the underlying domain command
already knows the answer to.

There's a second cost surfaced specifically by KAN-434's collapse:
the singular wrappers (`assign_card_to_sprint`, and the pre-existing
`archive_card`) call the plural to get the `usize`, discard it, then
fetch the card. So for the single-card path the two reads are pure
overhead — the singular doesn't even use the count.

On in-memory and JSON backends the cost is microseconds. On SQLite
it's two additional indexed queries per call. Negligible per
operation; becomes measurable at high frequency or large boards.

## What

Push the mutation count down into the domain command's execute path
so the service can return it directly without re-reading the backend.

### Domain-layer change (the load-bearing part)

In `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/card_commands.rs`, change the
three offending commands' `execute` from `KanbanResult<()>` to
`KanbanResult<usize>`:

- `ArchiveCards::execute`         (line ~254)
- `MoveCards::execute`            (line ~294)
- `AssignCardsToSprint::execute`  (line ~336)

Each command already knows which IDs it actually mutated (it calls
`filter_valid_card_ids` and iterates them). Returning that count is a
local change inside each `execute` body — `Ok(valid_ids.len())`
typically.

### CardCommand enum dispatch

`crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/card_commands.rs:27` —
`CardCommand::execute` currently returns `KanbanResult<()>`. It needs
to either (a) also become `KanbanResult<usize>` with non-batch arms
returning `Ok(0)` or `Ok(1)`, or (b) keep its `()` return and add a
separate `executed_count` method per command. Option (a) is more
honest — every mutation is "n entities affected" — but it touches the
command-log replay path. Option (b) keeps the existing replay code
path untouched.

**Recommendation: Option (a).** It generalises naturally: `CreateCard`
returns 1, `DeleteCard` returns 1, `UpdateCard` returns 1, etc. Both
options are tracked here so the implementer can pick.

### Service-layer cleanup

In `crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs::execute(Vec<Command>)`
(line 158), propagate the per-command count out so callers can sum it.
Then rewrite the three plurals:

```rust
fn archive_cards(&mut self, ids: Vec<Uuid>) -> KanbanResult<usize> {
    let counts = self.execute(vec![Command::Card(CardCommand::Archive(ArchiveCards { ids }))])?;
    Ok(counts.into_iter().sum())  // exactly one command, one count
}
```
