```rust
fn move_cards(&mut self, ids: Vec<Uuid>, column_id: Uuid) -> KanbanResult<usize> {
    let chained_status_updates = self.chained_status_updates_for_batch_move(&ids, column_id)?;
    let mut batch = vec![Command::Card(CardCommand::MoveMultiple(MoveCards { ids, column_id }))];
    for (card_id, new_status) in chained_status_updates {
        batch.push(/* chained UpdateCard */);
    }
    let counts = self.execute(batch)?;
    Ok(counts[0])  // first command is the MoveCards batch; status flips are not "moves"
}

fn assign_cards_to_sprint(&mut self, ids: Vec<Uuid>, sprint_id: Uuid) -> KanbanResult<usize> {
    let counts = self.execute(vec![Command::Card(CardCommand::AssignToSprint(
        AssignCardsToSprint { ids, sprint_id }
    ))])?;
    Ok(counts.into_iter().sum())
}
```

The `KanbanContext::execute` signature changes from
`fn execute(&mut self, commands: Vec<Command>) -> KanbanResult<()>`
to
`fn execute(&mut self, commands: Vec<Command>) -> KanbanResult<Vec<usize>>`.

That return shape lets the caller pick whichever index they care about
(the first command, the sum, etc.) without forcing every caller to
care about the count.

### `archive_card` and the singulars naturally cheapen

After this change, `assign_card_to_sprint` (a shorthand over
`assign_cards_to_sprint` post-KAN-434) and `archive_card` (a shorthand
over `archive_cards`) no longer pay for the wasted reads. The count
they discard was the wasted work; removing the source of the count
removes the waste.

## Where the wiring lives

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/card_commands.rs` | `ArchiveCards::execute`, `MoveCards::execute`, `AssignCardsToSprint::execute` return `KanbanResult<usize>`. Same change propagates to the `CardCommand::execute` dispatch arm depending on Option (a) vs (b). |
| `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/mod.rs` | If Option (a): `Command::execute` returns `KanbanResult<usize>` from its match. |
| `crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs` | `execute(Vec<Command>)` returns `KanbanResult<Vec<usize>>` (or `KanbanResult<usize>` if collapsed at the service boundary — caller's choice). Plurals at `:1048`, `:1057`, `:1145` use the returned counts instead of before/after delta queries. |

The command-log replay machinery on the persistence side
(`kanban-persistence-*` crates) shouldn't care about the count —
replay applies commands for their side effects, not their return
values. Confirm by `grep`ping for callers of `Command::execute` /
`CardCommand::execute` outside `KanbanContext` to make sure the
signature change doesn't break them.

## Reuse — already in place

- `filter_valid_card_ids` at
  `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/mod.rs::CommandContext` — every
  affected command already calls this to compute the actually-mutated
  ID set. The count is literally `valid_ids.len()` at the point each
  command finishes its body.
- `KanbanContext::execute` infrastructure — atomic batch, rollback,
  undo cursor. Unchanged in behaviour; only the return type widens.

## Acceptance

- The three plurals (`archive_cards`, `move_cards`,
  `assign_cards_to_sprint`) no longer call `list_*` for the count.
- Existing tests in `crates/kanban-service/tests/`,
  `crates/kanban-mcp/tests/`, and the contract-test helpers continue
  to pass without modification — the count semantics are preserved.
- Add a microbench or instrumented log assertion (optional) showing
  that a single-card `assign_card_to_sprint` makes one backend
  mutation call and zero count-queries.
- `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`,
  and `cargo fmt --check` all clean.

## Behaviour change for existing callers

**None.** The return value (`usize`, count of cards actually mutated)
is identical to what the before/after delta produced. Pure
performance refactor.

## Risk

Medium. Touches the `Command::execute` signature, which is a
load-bearing trait used by:

- `KanbanContext::execute` (the atomic batch executor)
- The undo/redo replay path
- Any future cross-client command-log work

The change is mechanical but wide. Recommended approach:

1. Start with Option (b) on a feature branch: add per-command
   `executed_count()` methods alongside the existing `()` returns.
   This is additive — no existing caller breaks.
2. Switch the three service plurals to use the new method.
3. Verify all tests + the undo/redo path still work.
4. **Then** consider whether to converge on Option (a) (uniform
   `KanbanResult<usize>` from every `execute`) as a second step.

Doing Option (a) directly is shorter overall but harder to bisect if
something breaks.

## Notes / Out of scope

- This card does **not** address the `move_card` collapse blocker
  from KAN-434 (the `position: Option<i32>` mismatch with `move_cards`).
  That's a separate concern.
- This card does **not** introduce per-card success/failure detail —
  the `BatchOperationResult` returned by `*_detailed` inherent methods
  on `KanbanContext` already covers that case. Those methods have
  their own pattern and are out of scope unless the implementer
  decides to unify.
- The `update_cards` plural (post-KAN-394) does NOT have this issue —
  it returns `count = updates.len()` upfront because each input maps
  one-to-one to an `UpdateCard` command. Don't touch it.

## Relationship to other cards

- **KAN-434** (just merged) is the reason this gap is visible — the
  singular shorthands discard the count and pay for two extra reads
  per call. Resolving this card eliminates that overhead.
- **KAN-432** (per-board auto-sync opt-out) is independent. Both
  changes touch `update_cards` but in different ways.
- **KAN-433** (drop dead lifecycle helpers) is independent.

## Commit shape

If Option (b) (additive):

1. `feat(domain): card batch commands expose executed_count()` — add
   the new method to `ArchiveCards`, `MoveCards`,
   `AssignCardsToSprint`. Default to `0` for non-batch commands.
2. `refactor(service): replace before/after delta with command
   executed_count()` — switch the three plurals.
3. `chore: add changeset for KAN-43x`

If Option (a) (signature change):

1. `refactor(domain): Command::execute returns KanbanResult<usize>` —
   wide mechanical change.
2. `refactor(service): plurals use returned counts instead of
   before/after delta` — switch the three plurals.
3. `chore: add changeset for KAN-43x`

Either way: write the test commit first when introducing the new
return shape, per the project TDD convention.
cards.title: refactor(domain): commands return mutation count to remove before/after delta queries in service plurals

cards.description:
Follow-up from PR #247 (Amanda Frost's Windows duplicate-keypress fix).

The fix in crates/kanban-tui/src/events.rs filters with `key.kind != KeyEventKind::Press` on Windows, which also drops `KeyEventKind::Repeat` events. Amanda confirmed via manual testing that holding a key still repeats — the legacy Windows console typically emits multiple `Press` events at the OS level rather than `Repeat`, so this is fine today.

However, newer Windows Terminal can run with VT-input mode enabled, in which crossterm may emit `KeyEventKind::Repeat` events for held keys. Under the current filter those would be silently dropped, breaking key-repeat for Windows Terminal users with VT input on.

Scope:
- Reproduce: enable VT input mode in Windows Terminal and verify whether crossterm emits Repeat events for held keys.
- If yes: change the filter to `key.kind == KeyEventKind::Release` (drop only Release), or equivalently `matches!(key.kind, KeyEventKind::Press | KeyEventKind::Repeat)`.
- If the behavior is configuration-dependent, document the trade-off and pick the safer default.

References:
- https://github.com/veeso/tui-realm/issues/54
- https://github.com/ratatui/ratatui/issues/347
- https://docs.rs/crossterm/latest/crossterm/event/struct.KeyEvent.html
cards.title: Investigate KeyEventKind::Repeat handling on Windows Terminal VT-input mode

cards.description:
## Problem

`board create` currently has an implicit dual responsibility:

1. Creates a board (domain entity)
2. Initialises the storage file if it does not already exist

This is a separation-of-concerns violation. Storage bootstrapping is an infrastructure concern, not a domain concern. The result is a UX jagged edge: `board create` silently creates a new file, while every other CLI command requires the file to exist (enforced by the existence check added in KAN-323).

## Context

Discovered while implementing KAN-323 (fix misleading 'Card not found' error when board file does not exist). The fix gates all commands on file existence — but had to exempt `board create` to preserve the only CLI path to bootstrap a new file. This exemption is itself a design smell.

The CLAUDE.md historically showed `cargo run -- init --name "My Board"` as the initialisation command, suggesting an `init` subcommand was once planned or has since been removed.

## Proposed Fix

1. Add a top-level `init` subcommand:
   ```
   kanban <file> init [--name <board-name>]
   ```
   - Creates the file with an empty context (no board required)
   - Optionally accepts `--name` to also create a first board in one step
   - Errors if the file already exists (to avoid accidental overwrites)

2. Remove the `board create` exemption from the file-existence check in `kanban-cli/src/app.rs`:
   ```rust
   // Before: special-cased board create to bypass existence check
   let is_bootstrap = matches!(&cmd, Commands::Board(b) if matches!(b.action, BoardAction::Create { .. }));
   // After: all commands require the file to exist
   ```

3. Update CLAUDE.md to document `kanban <file> init` as the canonical initialisation flow.

## Files to Modify

- `crates/kanban-cli/src/cli.rs` — add `Init` variant to `Commands` enum with optional `--name` arg
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/app.rs` — handle `Commands::Init` before the existence check; remove `is_bootstrap` exemption
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/` — add `init.rs` handler that creates the file and optionally a board
- `crates/kanban-cli/tests/cli_integration.rs` — add `init_tests` module; update `test_board_create_on_new_file_succeeds` to use `init` instead
cards.title: add cli init subcommand to decouple storage bootstrapping from board create

cards.description:
Follow-up from PR #247 (Amanda Frost's Windows duplicate-keypress fix).

Amanda noted that the 'Areas for Contribution' section of CONTRIBUTING.md does not explicitly mention that bug fixes are accepted, which made her hesitant to open the PR. Update the section to state plainly that fixes — especially platform-portability fixes — are welcome.

Scope:
- Edit the 'Areas for Contribution' section of CONTRIBUTING.md to call out bug fixes as a welcomed contribution category alongside features.
- Consider adding a brief note that cross-platform / portability fixes are particularly valued.
cards.title: Update CONTRIBUTING.md to explicitly welcome bug fixes

cards.description:
## Why

KAN-403 fixed a TUI regression where `create_card` left the selector on
the prior card instead of jumping to the new one. The root cause was a
stale view-layer task list at the moment `select_card_by_id` ran. The
companion card (filed alongside this one) calls out the same shape at
`handle_move_card` and `move_selected_cards`.

The pattern recurs every time a handler:

1. Mutates the model in a way that adds a card to (or moves a card
   between) view-layer task lists, AND
2. Calls `select_card_by_id(card_id)` before the next render-loop
   `prepare_frame()` has refreshed the task list.

Today the workaround is local to each handler: insert
`self.prepare_frame()` right before `self.select_card_by_id(...)`.
That works but it duplicates the same fix across N handlers, and any
future handler that forgets to add it will silently regress the
selection behaviour.

The cleaner architecture: make `select_card_by_id` itself responsible
for ensuring the active task list is fresh. Selection / cursor logic
already lives entirely inside the TUI app (verified during KAN-403 —
no other crate touches it) and `select_card_by_id` is the canonical
entry point for "the user wants card `X` selected". Refreshing the
task list before searching for `X` is part of that contract.

## What

Modify `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:1454::select_card_by_id` to
call `self.prepare_frame()` before delegating to
`task_list.select_card(card_id)`:

```rust
pub fn select_card_by_id(&mut self, card_id: uuid::Uuid) {
    self.prepare_frame();
    if let Some(task_list) = self.view.strategy.get_active_task_list_mut() {
        task_list.select_card(card_id);
    }
}
```

Then remove the now-redundant `self.prepare_frame()` calls from:

- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs::create_card` (the
  one inserted by KAN-403 at line 390).
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs::handle_move_card`
  and `move_selected_cards` (only if the companion card landed first
  and added them — otherwise they were never inserted).

## Where the wiring lives

- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:1454` — single method change. ~3
  lines added (the `prepare_frame()` call + a brief doc comment if
  warranted).
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs:390` — remove the
  redundant call inserted by KAN-403.
- Any other handler that's accumulated the same workaround over time.

The `prepare_frame()` method (`app/mod.rs:1541`) and the
`select_card_by_id` method (`app/mod.rs:1454`) both live in
`impl App` in the `kanban-tui` crate. Service and domain layers are
untouched. The selection-logic-stays-in-TUI principle is preserved.

## Existing call sites

`select_card_by_id` is called from 8 handler sites in
`crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/`:

- `card_handlers.rs:252` (`toggle_card_completion`)
- `card_handlers.rs:297` (`toggle_selected_cards_completion`)
- `card_handlers.rs:391` (`create_card` — KAN-403's fix is the
  `prepare_frame` above this line)
- `card_handlers.rs:480` (`handle_move_card`)
- `card_handlers.rs:536` (`move_selected_cards`)
- `card_handlers.rs:603`/`612` (next/prev navigation — card is
  already in the list)
- `column_handlers.rs:537`

For the navigation cases (next/prev, line 603/612), the card is
already in the active task list — calling `prepare_frame` is wasted
work but doesn't change behaviour. For everything else, the refresh
either is the bug fix (create/move) or is a no-op (toggle).

## Performance consideration

Every `select_card_by_id` call now triggers a snapshot load + a
task-list rebuild. For the in-memory backend this is microseconds.
For SQLite it's a single indexed query. Negligible at user-driven
cadence. If a future hot path emerges that calls `select_card_by_id`
in a tight loop, that loop should batch via direct task-list access
anyway, not through this entry point.

## Acceptance

- The redundant `prepare_frame` call removed from `create_card` —
  `test_create_card_selects_newly_created_card_when_prior_selection_exists`
  still passes.
- If the companion card's tests exist:
  `test_move_card_right_selects_moved_card_in_kanban_view` and the
  multi-select variant still pass with the per-handler fixes removed.
- No new tests required — both bug-fix cards already pin down the
  behaviour. A negative test (e.g.
  `test_toggle_completion_does_not_drop_selection`) is optional belt-
  and-braces.
- `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings`,
  `cargo fmt --check` all clean.

## Files modified

| File | Change |
|---|---|
| `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs` | `select_card_by_id` calls `self.prepare_frame()` first. |
| `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs` | Remove the redundant `self.prepare_frame()` call from `create_card` (and from any other handler that accumulated the same workaround pre-merge). |

## Risk

Low.

- The change is purely additive at the entry point and removes
  duplication at the call sites — no behaviour change for working
  flows.
- Calls `prepare_frame` once on every selection update across all 8
  call sites. For toggle / navigation that's wasted work, but cheap.
- If a future bug surfaces where `select_card_by_id` is called from a
  context where `prepare_frame` would be wrong (e.g. mid-frame, with
  a partially-applied state), revisit with a `select_card_by_id_raw`
  escape hatch.

## Relationship to other cards

- **KAN-403** (merged in #260): introduced the first per-handler
  workaround in `create_card`. The redundant call goes away when this
  card lands.
- **Companion bug card** (filed alongside): adds per-handler
  workarounds at `handle_move_card` and `move_selected_cards`. Either
  card landing alone is fine; if both land, the order is:
  1. Companion lands first → this card removes its workaround too.
  2. This card lands first → companion is auto-resolved, close as dup.

## Commit shape

1. `refactor(tui): select_card_by_id refreshes task list before
   selecting` — change `select_card_by_id` body, remove the redundant
   `prepare_frame` call(s) from handlers.
2. `chore: add changeset for KAN-NNN` — user-facing description noting
   that the underlying flakiness is gone (most users won't notice
   anything since KAN-403 + the companion bug card already covered
   the visible cases).

## Out of scope

- A "deferred selection intent" mechanism (storing
  `pending_card_selection: Option<Uuid>` on the app and applying it
  during the next `update_cards`) is an alternative that avoids the
  extra `prepare_frame` call but adds plumbing across two layers. Not
  worth the complexity unless `prepare_frame` cost becomes measurable.
cards.title: refactor(tui): centralize task-list refresh in select_card_by_id

cards.description: Collapse all installation methods (Crates.io, Nix, AUR, Source) into one unified code block with commented sections like the Installation section added in KAN-465. Simplify Nix commands to single line: nix run nixpkgs/nixpkgs-unstable#kanban (removes the redundant dual-command Nix example). Keep Crates.io, Homebrew, Nix, AUR, and Source as clearly separated options within the same code block.
cards.title: Refactor web installation section into single code block

cards.description:
Surfaced by Amanda Frost while working on PR #247.

Two tests in crates/kanban-service/src/config.rs fail on Windows because their assertions hardcode forward-slash path separators while Windows produces backslashes:

```
---- config::tests::test_dto_from_config_with_data_file_shows_storage_fields stdout ----
thread 'config::tests::test_dto_from_config_with_data_file_shows_storage_fields' panicked at crates\kanban-service\src\config.rs:687:9:
got: Some("...coding\\kanban\\crates\\kanban-service\\kanban.json")

---- config::tests::test_dto_from_explicit_config_preserves_values stdout ----
thread 'config::tests::test_dto_from_explicit_config_preserves_values' panicked at crates\kanban-service\src\config.rs:712:9:
got: Some("...\\coding\\kanban\\crates\\kanban-service\\kanban.sqlite")
```

Scope:
- Locate the path-equality assertions at config.rs:687 and config.rs:712.
- Either normalise paths through std::path::PathBuf comparison, or compare against an OS-aware expected value built with PathBuf::from / Path::join, rather than a literal string with forward slashes.
- Verify the tests pass on both Linux and Windows.
- Sweep the rest of crates/kanban-service for similar string-based path assertions that may have the same problem.
cards.title: Fix Windows path-separator assumptions in kanban-service::config tests

cards.description:
## Background

Distribute `kanban` via Homebrew so macOS users and Homebrew-on-Linux users have a first-class install path. Today there is none — they must `cargo install kanban-cli` from source.

This card covers **Phase 1 only**: a personal tap at `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban`, installable via `brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban`. Phase 2 (submitting to homebrew-core) is a separate future card, gated on Phase 1 being stable across multiple version bumps **and** the project meeting Homebrew's self-submission notability bar (≥225 stars or ≥90 forks/watchers — see "Phase 2 gate" below).

PRD source: `~/Downloads/kanban-homebrew-prd.md`. This card adopts the PRD's structure but corrects several issues (see "Deviations from PRD" below).

## Deliverable shape

```
brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban
# or
brew tap fulsomenko/kanban && brew install kanban
```

Formula lives in `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` at `Formula/kanban.rb` — the tap repo is the single source of truth. `release.yml` in this repo updates the `url` and `sha256` lines on each release; no formula file is tracked in this repo.

## Formula

`fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban:Formula/kanban.rb`:

```ruby
class Kanban < Formula
  desc "Fast, keyboard-driven terminal kanban board"
  homepage "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban"
  url "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/archive/refs/tags/v0.6.0.tar.gz"
  sha256 "FILL_IN_AT_FIRST_RELEASE"
  license "Apache-2.0"
  head "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban.git", branch: "develop"

  depends_on "rust" => :build
  depends_on "pkg-config" => :build

  on_linux do
    depends_on "wayland"
    depends_on "libxcb"
  end

  def install
    system "cargo", "install", *std_cargo_args(path: "crates/kanban-cli")
  end

  test do
    assert_match version.to_s, shell_output("#{bin}/kanban --version")
    system bin/"kanban", testpath/"test.json", "board", "create", "--name", "BrewTest"
    assert_path_exists testpath/"test.json"
  end
end
```

Notes:

- `depends_on "pkg-config" => :build` + the `on_linux` block mirror `default.nix:21-25`. Without these, Homebrew-on-Linux installs will fail at compile time. Homebrew's canonical dep name is `pkg-config` (with dash).
- `std_cargo_args(path: "crates/kanban-cli")` builds only the `kanban-cli` workspace member (matches `default.nix:27`). Canonical Rust pattern — expands to `--locked --root=#{prefix} --path=#{path}`.
- `head` block lets tap users `brew install --HEAD kanban` for develop-tip builds. Acceptable in the tap; review for removal in Phase 2.
- Test block exercises real I/O (creates a board file) — sidesteps the Formula Cookbook's explicit "Bad tests" warning: *"`foo --version` and `foo --help` (despite widespread use)."*
- Reference Rust formulas in homebrew-core for cross-checking shape: `tuicr` (PR #282664), `rustnet` (PR #278674), `ttl` (PR #281118).

## Pre-flight (one-time, before first push)

Confirmed locally:

- `cargo run --package kanban-cli -- --version` prints `kanban 0.6.0` followed by a `commit:` line ✓
- License is SPDX `Apache-2.0` (verified at `default.nix:37`) ✓
- The `Apache-2.0` license is in Homebrew's allowed list ✓

Remaining pre-flight (run in `~/fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` after placing `Formula/kanban.rb`):

1. Compute the SHA256 of the current latest release tarball:
   ```
   curl -fsL https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/archive/refs/tags/v0.6.0.tar.gz | sha256sum
   ```
   Paste it into `Formula/kanban.rb`'s `sha256` line.

2. On a Mac:
   - `brew install --build-from-source ~/fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban/Formula/kanban.rb`
   - `brew test kanban`
   - `brew audit --strict --new kanban`
   - `brew style ~/fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban/Formula/kanban.rb`

   Fix any audit/style findings before pushing.

3. Commit and push the initial `Formula/kanban.rb` to the tap repo. The repo's default branch must match what `release.yml` will push to (`main` or `master`).

## Tap repo (one-time setup, manual)

- [x] Public GitHub repo `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` created (2026-05-15).
- [x] Cloned locally at `~/fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban`.
- [ ] Rename default branch from `madtmm` to `main` (or `master`) and align the `git push origin <branch>` in `release.yml` to match.
- [ ] Generate an SSH deploy key with **write** access to the tap repo. Add the public key as a deploy key on the tap repo. Add the private key to this repo's secrets as `HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY`.
- [ ] Author the initial `Formula/kanban.rb` locally (see "Formula" above), run pre-flight, commit, push.
- [ ] Author `README.md` (see content below).
- [ ] Copy `LICENSE.md` from this repo into the tap repo verbatim (Apache-2.0).

Target layout:

```
homebrew-kanban/
├── README.md             # install instructions + link to upstream (see content below)
├── LICENSE.md            # Apache-2.0, copied verbatim from this repo's LICENSE.md
└── Formula/
    └── kanban.rb         # the formula — single source of truth
```

### `README.md` content

```markdown
# homebrew-kanban

Homebrew tap for [kanban](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban) — a fast, keyboard-driven terminal kanban board.

## Install

    brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban

Or with explicit tap:

    brew tap fulsomenko/kanban
    brew install kanban

## Develop / HEAD

    brew install --HEAD fulsomenko/kanban/kanban

Installs from the `develop` branch of the upstream repo.

## License

The Ruby formula code in this repository is licensed under Apache-2.0 (see `LICENSE.md`). The `kanban` binary it installs is also licensed under Apache-2.0, owned by the upstream project at https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban.
```

### `LICENSE.md` content

Copy `LICENSE.md` from the kanban project repo verbatim. From the cloned tap repo:

```sh
cp ~/fulsomenko/kanban/LICENSE.md ~/fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban/LICENSE.md
```

(Confirmed: the upstream `LICENSE.md` is the standard Apache License Version 2.0 text.)

## CI integration (`release.yml`)

Add a new job step **inside the existing `release: Release to crates.io` job** in `release.yml`, after `Deploy to AUR` (line ~115) and before `Sync develop with master`. Mirrors the AUR pattern exactly — same `has_changesets` gate, same SHA computation idiom, same SSH-deploy-key push.

```yaml
- name: Compute upstream tarball SHA256 for Homebrew
  if: steps.check.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'
  id: brew-sha
  env:
    VERSION: ${{ steps.release.outputs.new }}
  run: |
    set -euo pipefail
    URL="https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/archive/refs/tags/v${VERSION}.tar.gz"
    # Same idiom as the AUR step at release.yml:84-89 (consistency > novelty).
    SHA256=$(curl -fsL "$URL" | sha256sum | cut -d' ' -f1)
    echo "sha256=${SHA256}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
    echo "url=${URL}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"

- name: Setup Homebrew tap SSH key
  if: steps.check.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'
  run: |
    mkdir -p ~/.ssh
    echo "${{ secrets.HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY }}" > ~/.ssh/homebrew_tap_key
    chmod 600 ~/.ssh/homebrew_tap_key

- name: Bump formula in homebrew-kanban tap
  if: steps.check.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'
  env:
    GIT_SSH_COMMAND: "ssh -i ~/.ssh/homebrew_tap_key -o IdentitiesOnly=yes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no"
    VERSION: ${{ steps.release.outputs.new }}
    SHA: ${{ steps.brew-sha.outputs.sha256 }}
    URL: ${{ steps.brew-sha.outputs.url }}
  run: |
    set -euo pipefail
    git clone git@github.com:fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban.git /tmp/homebrew-kanban
    # Tap is canonical: only update url + sha256 in place, never overwrite the file.
    sed -i "s|^  url \".*\"|  url \"${URL}\"|" /tmp/homebrew-kanban/Formula/kanban.rb
    sed -i "s|^  sha256 \".*\"|  sha256 \"${SHA}\"|" /tmp/homebrew-kanban/Formula/kanban.rb
    cd /tmp/homebrew-kanban
    git config user.name "github-actions[bot]"
    git config user.email "41898282+github-actions[bot]@users.noreply.github.com"
    git add Formula/kanban.rb
    if git diff --cached --quiet; then
      echo "No changes to commit"
    else
      git commit -m "kanban ${VERSION}"
      git push origin main   # update to match the tap's default branch
    fi
```

Why this shape (not the PRD's separate workflow):

- AUR publishes from this same workflow (`release.yml:81-115`). Brew matches that pattern: one workflow file, "everything that publishes on release", easy to reason about.
- Inherits `validate-release` upstream (`release.yml:78`) — no broken builds get mirrored to the tap.
- Reuses `steps.release.outputs.new` instead of parsing `GITHUB_REF_NAME` — version is already plumbed through.
- `curl + sha256sum` matches the AUR SHA256 idiom at `release.yml:84-89`. No new "compute a hash" pattern to maintain.

## Required new secret

| Secret | Scope | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY` | SSH deploy key with write access to `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` | Push formula updates to the tap |

## README update (this repo)

Add a section to `README.md` installation alongside existing AUR/Nix/cargo entries:

```markdown
### Homebrew (macOS & Linux)

    brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban
```

## Acceptance

- [x] `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` tap repo exists on GitHub (created 2026-05-15).
- [ ] Tap repo's default branch is renamed to `main` (or `master`) and matches what `release.yml` pushes to.
- [ ] `Formula/kanban.rb` is committed to the tap repo (initial seed, with correct `url`+`sha256` for the current latest release).
- [ ] `README.md` is committed to the tap repo with install instructions.
- [ ] `LICENSE.md` is committed to the tap repo (Apache-2.0, copied from upstream).
- [ ] `HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY` secret is configured on this repo and the corresponding deploy key is on the tap repo with write access.
- [ ] On a fresh Mac: `brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban` succeeds, `brew test kanban` passes, `brew audit --strict --new kanban` passes with no errors.
- [ ] On Linuxbrew: same three commands pass (validates the `on_linux` block).
- [ ] `release.yml` brew-tap-bump step has run successfully on at least one real release tag and the tap formula's `url` + `sha256` match the released tarball.
- [ ] `README.md` in **this** repo documents the Homebrew install path.

## Deviations from the PRD

For traceability — this card adopts the PRD's structure but corrects:

1. **Version**: PRD references v0.3.1; current is v0.6.0 (`Cargo.toml:16`). Card uses the live version, with CI computing the URL+SHA at release time.
2. **Linux deps**: PRD's formula is missing `pkg-config`, `wayland`, `libxcb`. Without them Homebrew-on-Linux compile fails. Added.
3. **Verify step**: PRD builds `nix build .#kanban-cli`, which has `withTui = false` per `flake.nix` — not what brew builds. Card uses `nix build .#default` and the verify step is implicit in `validate-release` (`release.yml:78`).
4. **SHA256 computation**: PRD introduces `nix store prefetch-file` + `nix hash convert`. Card reuses `curl + sha256sum`, matching the existing AUR idiom at `release.yml:84-89` for consistency.
5. **Workflow location**: PRD creates a new `.github/workflows/homebrew-tap-bump.yml`. Card integrates into `release.yml` next to the existing AUR steps.
6. **CI actions**: PRD uses `DeterminateSystems/nix-installer-action` and `magic-nix-cache-action` (the latter deprecated since Feb 2025). Card uses neither — `cachix/install-nix-action@v27` already in use.
7. **Test block**: PRD asserts only `--version`. Card adds a substantive I/O test (board create + file exists). Per the Formula Cookbook, `--version`-only tests are explicitly flagged as "Bad tests."
8. **Source of truth**: PRD treats the tap repo as canonical. Card agrees — no formula file in this repo. (An earlier plan draft proposed `packaging/homebrew/Formula/kanban.rb` here with the tap as a mirror; rejected in favor of single source of truth.)
9. **Phase 2 notability framing**: PRD treats "Stars: 105" as Phase 2 evidence. The actual self-submission bar is ≥225 stars (3× the general threshold). Card adds an explicit Phase 2 gate with the real numbers.

## Phase 2 gate (when to file the follow-up card)

The Phase 2 (homebrew-core submission) card should not be filed until **all** of these are true:

- Phase 1 tap has been stable across **≥3 version bumps** with zero post-publish formula fixes.
- The project meets Homebrew's self-submission notability bar: **≥225 GitHub stars** OR **≥90 forks** OR **≥90 watchers** (the bar is 3× the general threshold because we'd be self-submitting).
- Demonstrable use by "someone other than the author" — issues filed by non-authors, downloads of release artifacts, or a GitHub `Used by` section with non-author repos.
- No outstanding unpatched security advisories.
- `brew audit --strict --new --online` is clean against the tap formula (a dress rehearsal of what the core PR will run).

Per the live homebrew-core PR queue (40 open new-formula PRs as of 2026-05-15, ~1 in 4 carrying `notability` or `stale` labels), notability is enforced in practice — submitting prematurely wastes maintainer time and risks a `stale` close.

### Versioned formulae (`kanban@x.y`) — not needed now

Per [Homebrew Versions](https://docs.brew.sh/Versions), a versioned formula like `kanban@0.5` would only be created if/when:

- Upstream maintains a release branch with explicit security-update policy for that major/minor version, AND
- A large number of users would need to pin to that version (homebrew-core threshold is informally ~1000 analytics installs per 90 days).

Today kanban has neither — single active branch, no LTS policy, no install metrics from any registry yet. **Versioned formulae are out of scope for both Phase 1 and Phase 2 initial submission.**

## Out of scope

- Phase 2 / homebrew-core submission — separate future card.
- Versioned formulae (`kanban@x.y`).
- Adding macOS to the project's CI matrix — KAN-452 prereq; nice-to-have for Phase 1 tap (audience self-selects for risk) but blocking for Phase 2.
- Self-hosted bottles.
- The full `packaging/` directory restructure (moving `aur/` etc.) — KAN-452.

## Source-of-truth lifecycle

The formula lives in exactly one editable place at all times:

| Phase | Where the formula lives | Who edits it |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase 1 steady state** | `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` tap repo only | you (locally in `~/fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban`), plus `release.yml` for version+SHA bumps |
| **Phase 2 PR open (~1-4 weeks)** | Tap repo + the open PR branch on `fulsomenko/homebrew-core` fork | you (in the PR branch during review iterations) |
| **Phase 2 merged** | `Homebrew/homebrew-core` only | Homebrew's autobump bot (you stop touching it) |

Lifecycle transitions:

- **Phase 2 lands**: archive the tap repo, point users to `brew install kanban` (no tap prefix). Phase 2 card includes archive-the-tap as an explicit step.
- **At no point is the formula tracked in this repo**. Brew is the odd one out among the packaging registries — aur, chocolatey, winget all need files in this repo because their publishing CI consumes them. Brew's tap pattern is a fully external repo by convention.

## Tap maintenance burden (explicit)

Personal-tap maintainers are, per Homebrew docs, "responsible for updating, maintaining, fixing deprecations, and applying security updates for that formula." Concretely:

- Every kanban release triggers an auto-bump via `release.yml` — no manual work in the steady state.
- If Homebrew deprecates an API our formula relies on (e.g. `std_cargo_args` signature changes, `on_linux` block syntax changes), the formula needs an out-of-band update. Watch the `homebrew/brew` release notes; ~yearly cadence in practice.
- If a CVE is filed against `kanban` or a build-time dep we declare (`rust`, `pkg-config`, `wayland`, `libxcb`), we're expected to issue a patched release within a reasonable window.
- The tap repo's CI should run `brew audit --strict` on every push to catch regressions early (deferred to a follow-up card; not blocking Phase 1).

## Related cards / PRs

- **KAN-452** — broader packaging card (brew + choco + winget + packaging/ restructure). This card narrows scope to brew Phase 1 and supersedes KAN-452's brew section.
- **KAN-450** — Nix sandbox CI gate. Orthogonal but in the same "make releases hermetic and broadly available" theme.
- **PRD source**: `~/Downloads/kanban-homebrew-prd.md` (snapshot reference; PRD itself is not committed to the repo).
cards.title: Publish to Homebrew via personal tap (fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban)

cards.description:
## Why this exists

CLAUDE.md (Testing section) prefers `-> KanbanResult<()>` over `#[should_panic]` and notes it composes with `?` for better failure messages. However, a sweep of `crates/kanban-domain` shows **zero** tests use this pattern today — all ~20 tests in `sprint_commands.rs` (and similar counts in `board_commands.rs`, `card_commands.rs`, `column_commands.rs`, `dependency_commands.rs`) use `fn test_*()` with `.unwrap()` chains.

Raised during review of PR #262 (KAN-431). Deferred so the next developer makes the judgment call on full evidence.

## Proposed implementation

Convert tests to the form:

```rust
#[test]
fn test_x() -> KanbanResult<()> {
    let tc = TestContext::new();
    // ...
    let context = tc.as_command_context();
    cmd.execute(&context)?;
    // assertions
    Ok(())
}
```

Replace `.unwrap()` on `KanbanResult`-returning calls with `?`. Tests asserting on errors keep `.unwrap_err()` (no change there).

## Migration path

1. Pick one commands file (`sprint_commands.rs` is a reasonable pilot — well-covered, recently touched).
2. Convert all its tests in one commit: `test(domain): adopt KanbanResult<()> in sprint_commands tests`.
3. Review: does the diff actually read better, or does the noise of `?` everywhere hurt? Are there tests where `.unwrap()` was genuinely clearer?
4. If positive: open follow-ups to convert the other commands files (one commit each).
5. If negative: amend CLAUDE.md to reflect actual project preference for `.unwrap()` in unit tests.

## Decision points

- Worth doing at all? CLAUDE.md is aspirational; the codebase has settled on `.unwrap()`. Either is internally consistent.
- If yes: pilot one file first, evaluate before fanning out.
- Consider whether integration tests in `crates/kanban-service/tests/`, `crates/kanban-tui/tests/`, etc. should also migrate or stay as-is.
cards.title: Decide on KanbanResult<()> test return-type pattern

cards.description:
## Why this exists

Raised during review of PR #262 (KAN-431). The PR extracted three helpers out of `UpdateSprint::execute` in `crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/sprint_commands.rs`:

- `validate_card_prefix_not_locked` (sprint_commands.rs:76-92) — reads `list_cards_by_sprint` and `list_archived_cards` via `context.store`.
- `validate_card_prefix_unique` (sprint_commands.rs:94-133) — reads `list_sprints_by_board` via `context.store`.
- `allocate_sprint_name` (sprint_commands.rs:135-147) — reads sprint + board, mutates board, **writes** via `context.store.upsert_board`.

All three are technically domain-legal: `CommandContext.store: &'a dyn DataStore` is a domain-owned port (`kanban-domain::data_store`), not an infrastructure type. But semantically they are orchestration (fetch → check or mutate → persist), exactly what the PR description says is being lifted out of the domain layer.

## Proposed direction

The naming concern surfaced in review (e.g. "`allocate_sprint_name` hides an `upsert_board` side effect") is a symptom of misplaced layering, not a naming problem. Solving it with a rename would paint over the issue.

Two paths to evaluate:

### 1. Fold into KAN-427 / KAN-428 / KAN-429 / KAN-430

These remaining four cards in the same initiative are explicitly about moving orchestration to `kanban-service`. The three helpers in question are natural candidates to move when that work lands. No standalone work needed; just make sure the scoping of the remaining cards explicitly covers them.

### 2. Standalone follow-up

If the remaining cards focus on other command surfaces (not `UpdateSprint`), open a small PR that:

- Moves `validate_card_prefix_not_locked`, `validate_card_prefix_unique`, and the persistence half of `allocate_sprint_name` into `kanban-service`.
- Keeps the pure mutation (`Board::add_sprint_name_at_used_index`, already on `Board`) in domain.
- Reduces `UpdateSprint::execute` to a pure model-update; the service composes validation + execution + persistence.

## Decision points

- Read the scope of KAN-427, KAN-428, KAN-429, KAN-430 first. If `UpdateSprint` is in any of their scopes, fold this in there.
- If not, decide whether a small standalone PR is worth it now or whether to leave the helpers in domain until a more comprehensive layering pass.
- Either way, the rename suggestion from PR #262's review is dropped — relocating supersedes it.
cards.title: Review domain-vs-service layering of UpdateSprint helpers

cards.description:
Currently `kanban-mcp -V` outputs just the version without a commit hash, while `kanban -V` includes the full version with commit info.

Both binaries use `kanban_core::VERSION` which already conditionally includes the hash — the gap is in the Nix build files only (no Rust changes needed).

**Fix:** Add `gitRev ? null` param and a `preBuild` step to `crates/kanban-mcp/default.nix`, mirroring the top-level `default.nix` (lines 8, 30-32):

```nix
{ lib, rustPlatform, src, gitRev ? null }:
...
preBuild = lib.optionalString (gitRev != null) ''
  export GIT_COMMIT_HASH="${gitRev}"
'';
```

nixpkgs already handles this correctly (whole-workspace build + `env.GIT_COMMIT_HASH = finalAttrs.src.rev`).
cards.title: kanban-mcp -V should include git commit hash

cards.description:
## Principle

Storage backends must never be chosen by filename extension. Extensions are
a user-facing convenience for at-a-glance identification, not a dispatch
mechanism. The authoritative source of truth is:

1. **Existing files**: magic-byte / content sniffing (each backend's
   `StoreFactory::matches_content`).
2. **New files**: the user's explicit choice — CLI `--backend` flag, the
   choose-storage dialog radio, or config's `storage_backend`.
3. **Filename extension**: cosmetic only. A user picking SQLite in the
   dialog and typing `boards.json` should still get a SQLite backend; the
   extension is just a label.

Each storage backend crate (`kanban-persistence-json`,
`kanban-persistence-sqlite`) should expose a public `EXTENSIONS: &[&str]`
constant (or `StoreFactory::extensions() -> &'static [&'static str]`
trait method) so consumers stop hardcoding `.json` / `.sqlite` /
`.sqlite3` / `.db` strings throughout the codebase.

## Inventory of hardcoded extension knowledge today

### Production dispatch / state

- **`crates/kanban-service/src/store_manager.rs:47-57`** — `is_sqlite()`
  falls through to `locator.ends_with(".sqlite")` / `.sqlite3` / `.db`.
  This is the **primary violation**: `make_backend` calls it before
  doing content detection, so a non-existent file with a `.sqlite`
  extension gets a SQLite backend regardless of explicit selection.
  Authoritative SQLite-extension list lives here today.

- **`crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:120-129`** —
  `StorageBackendChoice::extension()` returns `.json` / `.sqlite`. Used
  by the dialog Tab toggle to swap the filename's extension. After this
  refactor: the radio still toggles the displayed filename for UX, but
  the backend choice is read from `app.choose_storage_backend`, not
  inferred from the path.

- **`crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:138-149`** —
  `swap_known_extension`'s `KNOWN: &[&str] = &[".json", ".sqlite",
  ".sqlite3", ".db"]`. Duplicates the list from `store_manager`. Should
  source from each backend crate's `EXTENSIONS` constant.

- **`crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/board_handlers.rs:146-162`** —
  `scan_import_files()` filters the import dialog by `.json` only.
  Probably intentional because JSON is the export format, but
  `ExportFormat::Sqlite` exists too — SQLite exports can't be re-imported
  through this dialog. Either widen to all backends' `EXTENSIONS` or
  document the JSON-only constraint.

### Backend factories (already correct — content-based)

- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/lib.rs:19-28` —
  `JsonStoreFactory::matches_content` checks for `{` / `[` after BOM and
  whitespace. **Content-based, no extension involved.** Model to follow.
- `crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/lib.rs:15-17` —
  `SqliteStoreFactory::matches_content` checks for the
  `b\"SQLite format 3\\0\"` magic bytes. **Same.**

### Test guardrails (lower priority — keep but consolidate)

`.ends_with(\".json\")` assertions in:
- `crates/kanban-cli/tests/cli_app_builder.rs:34, 46, 63`
- `crates/kanban-mcp/tests/mcp_server_builder.rs:35, 47`
- `crates/kanban-service/tests/store_manager_injection.rs:89-90`
- `crates/kanban-service/tests/make_store.rs:16, 70`

These all assert the **system default backend** is JSON. They're correct
guardrails — if the default flips they fail loudly. After exposing
`EXTENSIONS`, they should iterate the constant rather than inline
`\".json\"`.

## Proposed architecture

### 1. Per-crate `EXTENSIONS` exports

```rust
// crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/lib.rs
pub const EXTENSIONS: &[&str] = &[\".json\"];

// crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/lib.rs
pub const EXTENSIONS: &[&str] = &[\".sqlite\", \".sqlite3\", \".db\"];
```

Optionally extend the `StoreFactory` trait so consumers can iterate
without depending on each crate by name:

```rust
// crates/kanban-persistence/src/registry.rs
pub trait StoreFactory: Send + Sync {
    // ... existing methods ...

    /// File extensions (with leading dot) commonly used for this
    /// backend. Cosmetic — extensions never drive backend dispatch.
    fn extensions(&self) -> &'static [&'static str] { &[] }
}
```

### 2. `make_backend` signature change

From:
```rust
pub async fn make_backend(&self, locator: &str, config: &AppConfig)
    -> Result<Arc<dyn KanbanBackend>, KanbanError>
```

To something like:
```rust
pub async fn make_backend(
    &self,
    locator: &str,
    explicit_backend: Option<&str>,  // CLI flag, dialog radio, config
    config: &AppConfig,
) -> Result<Arc<dyn KanbanBackend>, KanbanError>
```

Resolution order:
1. **File exists**: try each factory's `matches_content` on the first N
   bytes; first match wins. Conflict between content and explicit
   backend → log and trust content.
2. **File does not exist**: use `explicit_backend` (or fall back to
   `config.effective_storage_backend()`).
3. **Filename extension**: ignored.

### 3. Drop / rewrite `is_sqlite(locator)`

The current implementation is the single biggest violation of the
principle. Either remove it entirely or keep only the content-detection
path (the `Some(\"sqlite\") => true` branch via `detect_backend`).

### 4. Dialog state plumbed through to `make_backend`

`adopt_storage_file` already knows `app.choose_storage_backend`. Pass
that to `make_backend` as `explicit_backend`. Tab in the dialog
continues to swap the visible filename extension for UX, but the
backend itself is determined by the radio.

### 5. Import dialog widens (optional)

`scan_import_files` should iterate
`json_factory.extensions()` ∪ `sqlite_factory.extensions()`
(or call into a helper on `StoreManager`) instead of hardcoding
`.json`.

## Open design questions

1. **`extensions()` as a trait method vs. crate-level `pub const`?** Trait
   method is more polymorphic (consumers iterate via the registry and
   never reach into specific crates); const is zero-cost and more
   discoverable in IDE. Probably trait method with a default `&[]` so
   third-party factories don't have to implement it.

2. **What if explicit backend conflicts with content detection?** E.g.
   user passes `kanban --backend sqlite my-existing-json-file.json`.
   - Trust content (current behaviour for the SQLite-magic case): warn
     loudly, use JSON.
   - Trust user: refuse to open and error out.
   - Default proposal: trust content + warn. Same shape as the existing
     `sync_backend_with_file` flow.

3. **For commands that genuinely do not have a file yet** (e.g., bare
   TUI launch, `kanban migrate <src> <target_backend>`): the explicit
   choice is the only signal. No change for these.

4. **Backwards compatibility**: changing `make_backend`'s signature is a
   breaking change to `kanban-service`'s public API. Ripples to TUI /
   CLI / MCP call sites. Worth a single PR that touches all three; not
   suitable as a partial migration.

## Acceptance criteria

- `kanban-persistence-json` and `kanban-persistence-sqlite` each expose
  their `EXTENSIONS` constant (and/or implement `StoreFactory::extensions`).
- `StoreManager::is_sqlite(locator)` removed, or rewritten to use only
  content sniffing (no `ends_with` calls).
- `StoreManager::make_backend` accepts an explicit backend choice and
  prefers it over filename inference.
- `swap_known_extension` in `kanban-tui` sources its known list from the
  persistence crates' `EXTENSIONS` (via `StoreManager` or directly).
- `StorageBackendChoice::extension()` returns the *first* (canonical)
  entry from each backend's `EXTENSIONS` rather than a hardcoded literal.
- `scan_import_files` either iterates all backends' extensions or has a
  comment justifying the JSON-only filter.
- The `.ends_with(\".json\")` test assertions cited above either iterate
  `JSON_EXTENSIONS` or are rewritten to assert on backend identity
  rather than path suffix.
- `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets`,
  `cargo fmt --all -- --check` all clean.

## Why this was deferred from PR #249

PR #249's scope was KAN-332 (gate subcommands behind explicit file) and
KAN-330 (startup dialog). Adding the radio in #249 *exposed* the latent
extension-vs-explicit-choice tension, but fixing it requires changing
`StoreManager::make_backend`'s public signature, which ripples through
TUI / CLI / MCP. A targeted PR for this refactor lets reviewers focus on
the dispatch model without being mixed up with dialog-UX changes.
cards.title: Backend dispatch must be content-driven, not filename-driven; expose extensions per backend crate
Decouple kanban-persistence from kanban-domain

cards.description:
After the boards.{json,sqlite} default-filename rename (PR #249, branch
KAN-332/require-explicit-file-path-no-implicit-kanban-json-default), three
groups of stale references were intentionally left in place. This card covers
sweeping them so the repo is fully consistent.

## Scope

### D. Test-internal temp filenames (mostly safe to rename, but pure churn)

Most of these are arbitrary `TempDir::new().path().join("…")` paths — the
filename has no semantic meaning, only the test's setup needs an identifier.
They don't break anything today; renaming is a hygiene/consistency pass.

Files (rename `kanban.json` → `boards.json`, `kanban.sqlite` → `boards.sqlite`,
`myboard.*` → `boards.*` unless the test specifically exercises a non-default
custom name):

- `crates/kanban-cli/tests/migrate.rs:250-251, 289-290`
  (`myboard.json` / `myboard.db` / `myboard.sqlite` — used as source/dest
   paths in migrate integration tests)
- `crates/kanban-cli/tests/cli_integration.rs:2333-2334`
  (asserts no `kanban.json` is auto-created — keep the assertion semantics
   but pick a name that matches today's docs; consider `boards.json`)
- `crates/kanban-cli/tests/cli_app_run_no_backends.rs:21`
  (constructs a json_path inside a TempDir)
- `crates/kanban-tui/tests/backend_selection_tests.rs:77-78`
  (cwd-guarded \"no `kanban.json` in cwd\" assertion — see KAN-332)
- `crates/kanban-tui/tests/import_failure_safety.rs:9, 65, 82`
  (three TempDir + join paths)
- `crates/kanban-tui/tests/conflict_detection_tests.rs:12, 69, 80, 115, 174,
   218` (five file_path locals + one substring assertion at :69 that
   checks the path contains \"kanban.json\")
- `crates/kanban-tui/tests/settings_config_edit_tests.rs:173, 200, 256`
  (comments referencing the old default — update to mention `boards.json`)
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:2410`
  (manually-constructed `App` for the `test_save_worker_does_not_send_completion_on_conflict`
   test — `config_storage_location: \"kanban.json\".into()` placeholder)

Plan:
1. `sed -i 's|\"kanban\\.json\"|\"boards.json\"|g; s|\"kanban\\.sqlite\"|\"boards.sqlite\"|g; s|\"myboard\\.json\"|\"boards.json\"|g; s|\"myboard\\.sqlite\"|\"boards.sqlite\"|g; s|\"myboard\\.db\"|\"boards.db\"|g'` over the test files listed above.
2. Update the substring assertion in `conflict_detection_tests.rs:69` to match
   the new filename.
3. Update the prose comments in `settings_config_edit_tests.rs:173, 200, 256`.
4. Run `cargo test --workspace` to confirm nothing relied on the old name.
5. One commit: `chore: rename test temp filenames to boards.{json,sqlite}`.

### E. Historical changeset wording (intentionally NOT renaming)

Two changesets describe behaviour from before the boards.{json,sqlite} rename,
and renaming inside them would distort the historical record:

- `.changeset/kan-332-require-explicit-file-path-no-implicit-kanban-json-default.md`
  — describes removing the implicit `./kanban.json` fallback. The literal
   file name is the *historical* default the user is being warned about.
- `.changeset/kan-405-json-backend-should-not-persist-command-log-between-sessions.md:7`
  — \"the command log is never written to `kanban.json` or SQLite\". This
   sentence describes legacy persistence behaviour at a point in time when
   the default was still `kanban.json`.

Resolution: leave both alone. Add a single line at the top of each
referencing this card so future readers know the wording is deliberate.
Or, alternatively, rephrase to not mention the literal filename at all
(\"the implicit default JSON file\" / \"the JSON file or SQLite\").

Plan:
1. Decide between (a) leave + add cross-reference, or (b) genericise the
   wording. Default: (b), genericise — the changeset's job is to describe
   the *change* in behaviour, not to pin a filename in time.
2. One commit: `chore: genericise changeset wording for filename rename`.

## Acceptance criteria

- `grep -rn 'kanban\\.json\\|kanban\\.sqlite\\|myboard\\.' --include='*.rs' --include='*.md'` returns 0 hits across the repo (excluding the deliberate historical changeset references if (a) is chosen).
- `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets`, `cargo fmt --all -- --check` all pass.
- A single PR per group (D and E in separate PRs, or one combined) targeting `develop`.

## Why this was deferred

PR #249's primary scope was KAN-332 (gate subcommands behind explicit file)
and KAN-330 (startup dialog). The default-filename rename to boards.{json,sqlite}
landed in #249 because it directly touched user-visible defaults. The
test-internal renames and historical changeset wording are pure churn /
historical accuracy questions and would have widened the diff without
adding value to that PR's review.
cards.title: Sweep stale 'kanban.json' / 'myboard.*' references in tests and historical docs

cards.description: CreateCard.capture_inverse emits ArchiveCards to keep the card_counter advanced. Consequence: undo of a create card leaves an archived card behind, blocking undo of the parent column (DeleteColumn refuses to delete a column with archived cards). The composite_round_trip test had to avoid full undo-to-empty because of this. Pick one: (a) DeleteCard inverse instead of ArchiveCards + preserve card_counter via explicit board update; (b) DeleteColumn permits delete when the only archived cards belong to undone CreateCards; (c) some other reconciliation. Came up in KAN-191 third-pass review.
cards.title: CreateCard inverse: delete rather than archive (preserve card_counter another way)

cards.description: parse_editor() calls shell_words::split().unwrap_or_default(), which silently swallows an Err when EDITOR contains unclosed quotes (e.g. EDITOR='vim). The result is an empty program string, which propagates through resolve_editor and eventually fails with a cryptic OS error from Command::new("") rather than a clear message to the user. Fix: return a Result from parse_editor, propagate the error to edit_in_external_editor with an io::Error::new(io::ErrorKind::InvalidInput, ...) and an actionable message like 'Could not parse EDITOR value: unclosed quote'.
cards.title: editor: handle malformed EDITOR value (unclosed quotes)

cards.description:
## Stakeholder summary — Where, Why, How

**Where.** Both persistence backends (`kanban-persistence-json`, `kanban-persistence-sqlite`) on the open/load path, plus the TUI startup chrome and the save path (writer-stamp metadata). No domain or service layer changes.

**Why.** Today, when a user opens a file written by a newer kanban than the one on their PATH, they see *nothing* go wrong — the file loads, the TUI starts, the graph view is silently empty. They have no signal that the binary and the file are mismatched, no banner explaining it, no error to look up. We hit this exact failure mode during the PR #287 smoke test (2026-05-23): a V6 file opened by the pre-KAN-504 binary `c5d7bba8` rendered as if the 32 parent-child edges didn't exist, because the old binary coerced the unknown `version: 6` field down to `V2` semantics (`migrator.rs:15`) and the per-kind sub-graph fields it didn't know about (`graph.parent_child`, `graph.blocks`, `graph.relates`) fell on the floor during deserialise. The user shouldn't have to grep three files to figure out what happened — the binary should refuse loudly, and the TUI should display the file format and writer stamp at startup so a mismatch is *one glance* away from being diagnosed.

This is not just a one-time issue; the kanban app installs into multiple places (nix system path, project `target/release`, MCP-launched copies). It's normal for a user's environment to hold multiple kanban binaries at different commits. The format-vs-binary asymmetry is going to keep biting until the binary refuses to silently coerce and the UI surfaces the version contract.

**How.** Three pieces:
1. **Refuse-on-future-version at load time** in both backends. JSON: replace the silent fallback in `Migrator::detect_version_from_value` and `Migrator::detect_version` with a typed `PersistenceError::UnsupportedFutureVersion { file_version, binary_max }`. SQLite: add a parallel guard in `SqliteStore::open` checking `metadata.schema_version` against the binary's max.
2. **Writer-stamp metadata on save** in both backends. Add `metadata.writer_version` (semver string from `CARGO_PKG_VERSION`) and `metadata.writer_commit` (build-time git SHA, already present in `--version`). Old files without these fields stay readable; new files carry the stamp.
3. **Startup banner in the TUI** (and a one-liner in CLI verbose mode) showing `Loaded {path}: format vN, last writer kanban {writer_version} ({writer_commit}); this binary supports up to v{N}`. When file and binary diverge, the banner is what tells the user.

Note this *only* helps from KAN-504+ binaries forward — the already-shipped `c5d7bba8` binary will continue to silently mishandle V6 files. There is no in-tree way to retrofit it. Documenting this is part of KAN-518's matrix doc.

---

## Engineering investigation

### The exact failure path we hit

`crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs:12-23`:
```rust
pub fn detect_version_from_value(value: &Value) -> FormatVersion {
    if let Some(version) = value.get("version").and_then(|v| v.as_u64()) {
        return FormatVersion::from_u32(version as u32).unwrap_or(FormatVersion::V2);
        //                                              ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
        //                                              SILENT DOWNGRADE on unknown
    }
    if value.get("data").is_none() {
        return FormatVersion::V1;
    }
    FormatVersion::V2
}
```

When the pre-KAN-504 binary's `FormatVersion::from_u32` (its enum capped at V5) saw `version: 6` in the envelope, it returned `None`, and the `.unwrap_or(FormatVersion::V2)` fallback silently treated the file as V2. The reader then deserialised the `data` payload using V2 expectations, which has no concept of `parent_child` / `blocks` / `relates` sub-graphs, so those fields were ignored. The old TUI rendered "no parents, no children" everywhere even though 32 edges sat on disk.

The same `.unwrap_or(...)` pattern appears at:
- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs:15` (the line above)
- `detect_version` (the async file-based variant) ultimately calls into the same value-based detector at `migrator.rs:26-33` — needs to inherit the same fix.

### SQLite parallel

`crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/sqlite_store.rs:238` (`SqliteStore::open`) does *not* read `metadata.schema_version` at all today. It runs the legacy-table-drops and proceeds. The schema is currently at v1 and has never been bumped, so this hasn't bitten yet — but the next SQLite schema bump will hit the same class of bug: an older binary opens a newer DB and silently sees "no such table" or "missing column" errors with no top-level explanation that the binary is too old.

`schema.sql:9` declares `schema_version INTEGER NOT NULL DEFAULT 1`. The insert at `sqlite_store.rs:298` writes `schema_version = 1` on every save. So the field exists; nothing reads it on open.

### Where the writer-stamp metadata would live

Current envelope (JSON V6):
```json
{
  "version": 6,
  "metadata": {
    "instance_id": "...",
    "saved_at": "...",
    // ... other fields
  },
  "data": { ... }
}
```

Proposed addition (backwards-readable; old binaries ignore unknown fields in `metadata`):
```json
"metadata": {
  // ... existing
  "writer_version": "0.6.0",
  "writer_commit": "18e98c4810dca9f69c7a894aa7a9ba009740cb1e"
}
```

SQLite: same fields added to the `metadata` table — already has `schema_version`, would extend with `writer_version TEXT, writer_commit TEXT`.

`writer_version` comes from `env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION")`. `writer_commit` is already baked into `--version` output (see `kanban --version` → `commit: 18e98c4...`); reuse that source — probably a `build.rs` constant exposed as `pub const BUILD_COMMIT: &str`.

### Where the startup banner displays

TUI: at the bottom-bar area near the file path, or as a one-shot 3-second toast on launch. Probably belongs alongside the existing storage-file banner (the dialog at `app/mod.rs` that shows the loaded file path).

CLI: if `--verbose` (or always for non-machine output), print a `Loaded {path}: format v{N}, written by kanban {ver} ({short_sha})` line before the command executes.

When format ≤ binary max **and** writer ≤ binary version: no banner noise (just the path). When there's any disagreement: visible warning.

### Offenders (file:line list)

| File | Line | What it does today | Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs` | 15 | `from_u32(version).unwrap_or(V2)` silently coerces unknown future versions to V2 | Return `Err(PersistenceError::UnsupportedFutureVersion { file_version: n, binary_max: 6 })` |
| `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs` | 26-33 | `detect_version` async wrapper inherits the above | Propagate the new error path |
| `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/json_file_store.rs` | 281, 340 | Calls `detect_version*`; today gets a coerced version back; needs to propagate the typed error to the caller | Map the new error to the user-facing message |
| `crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/sqlite_store.rs` | 238 | `open()` does not check `metadata.schema_version` | Read schema_version after legacy-table-drops; if `> SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION`, return `UnsupportedFutureVersion` |
| `crates/kanban-persistence/src/errors.rs` | (add) | No variant for this class of error today | Add `UnsupportedFutureVersion { file_version: u32, binary_max: u32 }` |
| `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/json_file_store.rs` | save path (line tbd; the function that writes the envelope) | Doesn't include `writer_version` / `writer_commit` | Add both into metadata on every save |
| `crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/schema.sql` | 5-10 (metadata table) | No `writer_version` / `writer_commit` columns | Add via `ALTER TABLE IF NOT EXISTS` or schema bump |
| `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs` (banner site, exact line tbd) | n/a | No format/writer banner on startup | Read metadata after load; render banner |
| `crates/kanban-cli/src/main.rs` (or appropriate entry) | n/a | No format/writer banner in verbose mode | Same |

---

## Proposed solution

### Step 1 — Typed error and refusal

New error variant in `crates/kanban-persistence/src/errors.rs`:

```rust
#[error("file format v{file_version} is newer than this binary's max v{binary_max}; please upgrade kanban")]
UnsupportedFutureVersion { file_version: u32, binary_max: u32 },
```

`Migrator::detect_version_from_value` becomes fallible:

```rust
pub fn detect_version_from_value(value: &Value) -> PersistenceResult<FormatVersion> {
    if let Some(version) = value.get("version").and_then(|v| v.as_u64()) {
        let v = version as u32;
        return FormatVersion::from_u32(v).ok_or(PersistenceError::UnsupportedFutureVersion {
            file_version: v,
            binary_max: FormatVersion::V6.as_u32(),
        });
    }
    if value.get("data").is_none() {
        return Ok(FormatVersion::V1);
    }
    Ok(FormatVersion::V2)
}
```

`detect_version` (async path) and the two call sites in `json_file_store.rs` propagate the new error.

SQLite parallel — in `SqliteStore::open` after legacy-table-drops:

```rust
let schema_version: u32 = sqlx::query_scalar("SELECT schema_version FROM metadata WHERE id = 1")
    .fetch_one(&pool).await?;
if schema_version > SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION {
    return Err(KanbanError::Persistence(
        PersistenceError::UnsupportedFutureVersion {
            file_version: schema_version,
            binary_max: SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION,
        }
    ));
}
```

(`SUPPORTED_SCHEMA_VERSION = 1` today.)

### Step 2 — Writer-stamp metadata

Extend `PersistenceMetadata` in `kanban-persistence/src/types.rs` (or wherever the envelope metadata struct lives) with two new fields:

```rust
pub struct PersistenceMetadata {
    // ... existing
    #[serde(default)]
    pub writer_version: Option<String>,
    #[serde(default)]
    pub writer_commit: Option<String>,
}
```

Both fields are `Option` so old files load cleanly. Save sites in both backends populate them:

```rust
metadata.writer_version = Some(env!("CARGO_PKG_VERSION").to_string());
metadata.writer_commit = Some(crate::BUILD_COMMIT.to_string());
```

`BUILD_COMMIT` should already exist (or be threaded from the `--version` output's source). Verify with `grep -rn "BUILD_COMMIT\|git_commit\|GIT_HASH"`.

### Step 3 — Startup banner

CLI: in `kanban-cli/src/main.rs` after the store opens, log a line:

```
Loaded /path/to/board.json: format v6, written by kanban 0.6.0 (18e98c4)
```

When file format > binary max, this never runs because step 1 already errored out. When the file's writer was newer than this binary, downgrade the banner to a warning style.

TUI: add a small "Format v6 · written by kanban 0.6.0 (18e98c4)" annotation to the status bar (probably alongside the file path indicator). Visible at all times; the user can compare against `kanban --version` if a mismatch is suspected.

### Tests to add

```
crates/kanban-persistence-json/tests/version_guard.rs
├── test_load_rejects_future_format_version
│   Seeded fixture with version=99 → expect Err(UnsupportedFutureVersion { file_version: 99, binary_max: 6 })
├── test_load_accepts_known_versions_v1_to_v6
│   Round-trip per version
└── test_writer_stamp_present_on_save
│   Save → reload → expect writer_version and writer_commit populated

crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/tests/version_guard.rs
├── test_open_rejects_future_schema_version
│   Manually INSERT metadata with schema_version=99 → expect Err(UnsupportedFutureVersion {...})
└── test_writer_stamp_round_trips
```

For the TUI banner: snapshot test on the status bar rendering (probably already a pattern in `kanban-tui/tests/`).

### Alternatives weighed

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| **A — Refuse on future version + writer stamp + banner (proposed)** | Solves the asymmetric problem going forward; one banner makes mismatch self-diagnosable; metadata is extensible for future fields. | Doesn't retrofit old binaries (no in-tree fix possible). Three touch-points (read, save, UI). |
| B — Refuse on future version only; no banner, no stamp | Smaller PR. | The next time this happens, the user still has no signal in the UI. Telemetry-style stamp is the cheap part; skipping it loses the diagnostic affordance. |
| C — Banner only, no refusal | Banner shows "this file was written by a newer kanban" without breaking the load. | The actual data shape may be incompatible (as in V6 case where edges silently dropped). Letting the load proceed with broken data is exactly the symptom we're trying to kill. |
| D — Strict equality enforcement (file format must match binary exactly, no migrations) | Tightest contract; no surprises. | Breaks the entire migration story (V1→V6 chain). Off the table; existing users need their migrations. |
| E — Auto-upgrade prompt (file is newer → offer to download newer binary) | Slickest UX. | Massive scope; needs a release-channel and update mechanism we don't have. Future work. |

Recommendation: **A**. The refusal alone catches the silent-failure mode; the stamp+banner makes future incidents diagnosable in seconds instead of "let me read four crates."

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Opening a JSON file with `version > 6` returns `PersistenceError::UnsupportedFutureVersion`; the error message includes both the file's version and the binary's max.
- [ ] Opening a SQLite file with `metadata.schema_version > 1` returns the same error type.
- [ ] Saving a JSON file populates `metadata.writer_version` and `metadata.writer_commit`.
- [ ] Saving a SQLite file populates the corresponding columns.
- [ ] Loading a file written by a future kanban (i.e. the binary refuses) surfaces a clear top-level message in: TUI startup dialog, CLI stderr, MCP tool error.
- [ ] TUI status bar shows "Format vN · written by kanban X (sha)" whenever a board is loaded.
- [ ] CLI commands log a one-line "Loaded ...: format vN ..." in verbose mode.
- [ ] Old files (missing `writer_version` / `writer_commit`) load cleanly; the banner just shows "written by: unknown" for those.
- [ ] Workspace gate clean (fmt / clippy / test).

### Out of scope

- Retrofitting the refusal into pre-KAN-504 binaries (not possible — they're already shipped). KAN-518 documents this in the operator FAQ.
- Auto-update / fetch-newer-binary flow (alternative E above).
- A full update notification system / release channel.
- Cross-checking the `writer_version` against the running binary's version on read (could be a follow-up "warn if writer was newer" enhancement).

### Risk

Low. Read guards are additive (new error variant; existing callers gain a typed failure mode). Writer stamps are pure-add to a struct that already round-trips. Banner is rendering only.

The one subtle risk is in the SQLite case: bumping `schema_version` in tests must not break older fixtures. The pinned tests in `kanban-persistence-sqlite/tests/` should cover this.

### Suggested priority

Medium-high. This is the difference between "edges silently disappear and the user has no idea why" and "edges silently disappear and the binary explains exactly what happened." Real users will hit this whenever they install kanban in multiple places (nix system path, dev `target/release`, MCP server).

### Relations

- Child of KAN-504 (PR #287 follow-up).
- Companion to KAN-518 (matrix doc): docs document the contract, this card enforces it at runtime.
cards.title: Refuse-on-version-mismatch in persistence backends + writer-stamp metadata + startup banner

cards.description:
The JSON on-disk format exposes the dependency graph bucket as `parent_child`, but every other layer uses `Spawns` naming:

| Layer | Name |
|---|---|
| Edge struct | `SpawnsEdge` (`crates/kanban-domain/src/dependencies/edges.rs:30`) |
| Domain accessor | `spawns_edges()` (`crates/kanban-domain/src/dependencies/dependency_graph.rs:267`) |
| Persistence helpers | `add_archived_spawns`, `from_validated_per_kind_edges(spawns, ...)` |
| SQLite table | `spawns_edges` (`crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/sqlite_store.rs`) |
| **JSON field** | **`parent_child`** ← odd one out |

Source of the mismatch: the `DependencyGraph` struct names its field `parent_child` (`crates/kanban-domain/src/dependencies/dependency_graph.rs:27`), and serde uses that as the wire name. SQLite is internally consistent because it explicitly names its table `spawns_edges`.

## Scope

1. Add `#[serde(rename = "spawns")]` on `DependencyGraph.parent_child` (or rename the Rust field too) so V7+ writes `"spawns"`.
2. Bump envelope version V6 to V7 in `kanban-persistence-json` so V7 reads/writes `spawns`, and the V6-to-V7 migrator renames the bucket on load (write a `.v6.backup` per the legacy migration convention).
3. Reader stays tolerant of V6 files containing `parent_child` (via the migrator).
4. Update the V5-to-V6 `split_graph` migrator to emit `spawns` instead of `parent_child` (now that V6 is no longer the terminal format, or fold this into the same migrator chain).
5. Update fixtures and assertions in:
   - `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/split_graph.rs`
   - `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs`
   - any tests asserting on the literal `parent_child` JSON key.
6. CLAUDE.md mentions "Envelope: `{ version, metadata, data }`, current version V6; reader accepts V1..V6" — update to V7.

## Why

Single source of truth for naming. `Spawns` is the chosen domain term (it appears in struct names, SQLite tables, public methods); the JSON wire format leaking the old Rust field name `parent_child` is the only place it bleeds through. Reduces cognitive load when readers cross-reference a JSON file against the code or compare JSON and SQLite snapshots.

## Acceptance

- A V7 JSON file written and re-read round-trips with no warnings.
- A V6 JSON file on disk (with `parent_child`) loads cleanly, migrates to V7 (with `spawns`), and writes back a `.v6.backup`.
- SQLite and JSON snapshots produced from the same in-memory `DependencyGraph` use the same vocabulary (`spawns` everywhere).
- TDD: failing tests first, per `feedback_tdd_mandatory`. Split commits per `feedback_commit_convention` and `feedback_solid_per_step_commits`.
cards.title: rename json "parent_child" graph field to "spawns" to match domain naming

cards.description:
shell_words follows POSIX quoting rules where \ is an escape character. A Windows user who sets EDITOR to a backslash path (the natural cmd/PowerShell syntax) has the path silently mangled before it ever reaches Command::new.

Verified empirically against shell-words 1.1.1:

  EDITOR=C:\Program Files\vim.exe        -> ["C:Program", "Filesvim.exe"]   (two tokens, path mangled)
  EDITOR=C:\Users\foo\nvim.exe           -> ["C:Usersfoonvim.exe"]          (entirely nonsense path)
  EDITOR=C:\tools\code.exe --wait        -> ["C:toolscode.exe", "--wait"]   (path mangled, args OK)
  EDITOR="C:\Program Files\vim.exe"      -> ["C:\\Program Files\\vim.exe"]  (quoted works)
  EDITOR="C:\Program Files\vim.exe" --x  -> ["C:\\Program Files\\vim.exe", "--x"] (quoted+arg works)

Scope is broader than "users with spaces in the path": anyone on Windows whose EDITOR is an absolute path (rather than a binary name relying on PATH) is affected. Before the refactor, EDITOR flowed straight into Command::new(&editor) and the OS path APIs accepted a literal Windows path with backslashes. After the refactor, the string is fed through shell_words::split first, backslashes are eaten as escapes, and which::which / Command::new are then handed a nonsense path that doesn't exist.

Possible fixes:
1. Gate on cfg!(target_os = "windows") and skip shell-words parsing for the unquoted case (preserves backslash-path behavior but gives up multi-word args on Windows).
2. Use a Windows-aware command-line parser (more invasive, but correct for both quoted and unquoted inputs and arg-bearing EDITORs).
3. Minimum viable: keep current parsing but document the requirement to quote backslash paths in the README VS Code limitations note.

Also add a test case that exercises a raw-backslash Windows path so the limitation is visible at the test level.

Captured during review of #255; bundled into #266 as a follow-up.
cards.title: editor: document/handle Windows backslash paths in EDITOR
editor: strengthen resolve_editor test assertions

cards.description:
## Background

Create the static source files that constitute the Chocolatey package, checked into this repo under `packaging/chocolatey/`. These are the inputs to `choco pack` at release time. They do not get published on their own — release.yml (sibling subtask "release.yml: Chocolatey publish step") consumes them, substitutes per-release variables (`$version$`, `$checksum64$`), packs into a `.nupkg`, and pushes to community.chocolatey.org.

This card produces files only. No CI changes; no Chocolatey account needed; no actual publish.

## What ships in this package

One Chocolatey package, `kanban`, bundling both repo binaries:

- `kanban.exe` — the TUI/CLI (from `crates/kanban-cli`).
- `kanban-mcp.exe` — the MCP server (from `crates/kanban-mcp`).

This matches the existing registry precedent:

| Registry | Package(s) | Binary names on PATH after install |
|---|---|---|
| AUR | one package: `kanban` | `kanban`, `kanban-mcp` |
| Homebrew tap (live) | one formula: `kanban` | `kanban`, `kanban-mcp` |
| Nix flake | two packages: `kanban-cli`, `kanban-mcp` | `kanban`, `kanban-mcp` |
| Chocolatey (this card) | one package: `kanban` | `kanban`, `kanban-mcp` |

Chocolatey auto-shims every `.exe` it finds under `$toolsDir`, so both binaries land on PATH with no extra wiring.

## Target file layout

```
packaging/
└── chocolatey/
    ├── README.md
    ├── kanban.nuspec
    └── tools/
        ├── chocolateyinstall.ps1
        ├── chocolateyuninstall.ps1
        ├── LICENSE.txt
        └── VERIFICATION.txt
```

Sibling directories like `packaging/aur/`, `packaging/homebrew/`, `packaging/winget/` are out of scope for this card; the broader `packaging/` restructure that KAN-452 originally bundled is deferred to a separate card per the chocolatey-only scope.

## File contents

### `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec`

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<package xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/packaging/2015/06/nuspec.xsd">
  <metadata>
    <id>kanban</id>
    <version>$version$</version>
    <title>kanban</title>
    <authors>Max Blomstervall</authors>
    <owners>fulsomenko</owners>
    <projectUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban</projectUrl>
    <licenseUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/blob/master/LICENSE.md</licenseUrl>
    <requireLicenseAcceptance>false</requireLicenseAcceptance>
    <projectSourceUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban</projectSourceUrl>
    <packageSourceUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/tree/master/packaging/chocolatey</packageSourceUrl>
    <docsUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban#readme</docsUrl>
    <bugTrackerUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/issues</bugTrackerUrl>
    <tags>kanban tui terminal productivity rust mcp</tags>
    <summary>Fast, keyboard-driven terminal kanban board (with MCP server)</summary>
    <description><![CDATA[
A terminal-based kanban / project management tool written in Rust,
inspired by lazygit's interface design. Provides a fast,
keyboard-driven UI for tracking tasks across boards, columns, and
sprints. Stores data in a portable JSON or SQLite file.

This package installs two binaries:

  - `kanban`     — the TUI / CLI; run `kanban` for the interactive
                    interface or `kanban --help` for one-shot commands.
  - `kanban-mcp` — Model Context Protocol server for LLM integrations
                    (Claude Desktop, VS Code, etc.). Point your MCP
                    client at this binary.

Run `kanban --help` to get started.
    ]]></description>
    <releaseNotes>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/tag/v$version$</releaseNotes>
  </metadata>
  <files>
    <file src="tools\**" target="tools" />
  </files>
</package>
```
