Notes on the nuspec:

- `<id>kanban</id>` claims the bare `kanban` package ID. If this turns out to be taken on community.chocolatey.org (the account/key subtask will confirm), the fallback in priority order is `kanban-cli` → `fulsomenko-kanban`. Whichever wins must be reflected here; the install/uninstall scripts and README also reference the chosen ID.
- `<owners>fulsomenko</owners>` is the Chocolatey username (separate identity from upstream GitHub org membership).
- `<requireLicenseAcceptance>false</requireLicenseAcceptance>` matches Apache-2.0's no-click-through nature. `true` would trigger a per-install license prompt, which is hostile UX for an Apache-2.0 tool.
- `<licenseUrl>` points at `LICENSE.md` on the master branch; this URL must resolve at moderation time (Chocolatey's automated audit fetches it).
- `<tags>` includes `mcp` since this package now ships the MCP server too; helps discoverability for MCP users hunting on chocolatey.org.
- `<packageSourceUrl>` and `<docsUrl>` are required by Chocolatey policy for new packages. The latter resolves to the README; that is accepted.
- `$version$` and `$checksum64$` are NOT NuGet/MSBuild substitution syntax — they are conventional placeholders that release.yml will literally `-replace` over at pack time. Naming matches Chocolatey community templates.
- `<files>` only ships `tools/` contents into the nupkg. Do not ship the .nuspec itself (Chocolatey does that automatically) or any source files.

### `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1`

```powershell
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
$toolsDir = "$(Split-Path -parent $MyInvocation.MyCommand.Definition)"
$version  = '$version$'
$url64    = "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v$version/kanban-v$version-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"

$packageArgs = @{
  packageName    = 'kanban'
  unzipLocation  = $toolsDir
  url64bit       = $url64
  checksum64     = '$checksum64$'
  checksumType64 = 'sha256'
}

Install-ChocolateyZipPackage @packageArgs
```

Notes:

- `$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'` is mandatory. Without it, a failed download silently leaves the package in a half-installed state — a documented Chocolatey moderation flag.
- `Install-ChocolateyZipPackage` ships in the Chocolatey PowerShell module and is the canonical helper for the download + verify + unzip flow.
- We deliberately do NOT use `Install-ChocolateyInstallPackage` (msi/exe installer pattern) because we ship portable binaries.
- `checksum64 = '$checksum64$'` is substituted at pack time. Chocolatey's package validator verifies it matches the actual download before unzipping — that is the security boundary.
- No `Install-BinFile` calls needed: Chocolatey auto-shims every `.exe` it finds under `$toolsDir`. The Windows archive (sibling "Windows release archive" subtask) places both `kanban.exe` and `kanban-mcp.exe` at the archive root, so unpacking the ZIP into `$toolsDir` produces two .exe files and both get shimmed onto `%ChocolateyInstall%\bin`.

### `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyuninstall.ps1`

```powershell
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
Uninstall-ChocolateyZipPackage -PackageName 'kanban' -ZipFileName "kanban-*-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"
```

Notes:

- Matches the install. Chocolatey tracks the unzip via a manifest at install time so the uninstall reverses it cleanly. Both `.exe` shims get removed because Chocolatey tracks every file it auto-shimmed.
- The `kanban-*` glob covers version-specific archive names without templating.

### `packaging/chocolatey/tools/LICENSE.txt`

Required by Chocolatey moderation policy: every package that ships a redistributable binary must include the binary's license in the package itself, not just link to it.

Source of truth: `LICENSE.md` at repo root. The release.yml pack step copies it into place at pack time so the in-repo `LICENSE.txt` does not drift. Per `feedback_single_source_of_truth`, the in-tree file is just a placeholder; final content is produced at pack time. Commit this placeholder body:

```
This file is populated from the project root's LICENSE.md at package
pack time. See packaging/chocolatey/README.md for the local validation
workflow if you need to populate it manually.

(Pack-time substitution lives in .github/workflows/release.yml.)
```

Rationale (trade-offs explicit):

- Check-in copy: easier to inspect the packaging directory standalone; risk of silent drift if the project LICENSE changes.
- Pack-time copy: no drift risk; one source of truth.

Pick **pack-time copy** for the same reason the brew tap pulls `LICENSE.md` from upstream rather than embedding a copy.

### `packaging/chocolatey/tools/VERIFICATION.txt`

```
VERIFICATION

This package wraps the official kanban Windows release binaries
(`kanban.exe` and `kanban-mcp.exe`), which are downloaded at install
time from the project's GitHub release page as a single ZIP archive:

  https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases

The SHA256 checksum of the downloaded archive is embedded in
chocolateyinstall.ps1 via the `$checksum64` substitution variable,
populated at pack time from the corresponding release artifact's
SHA256SUMS line.

To verify the binaries manually:

  1. Download the release ZIP from:
       https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v<VERSION>/kanban-v<VERSION>-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip
  2. Compute its SHA256:
       Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 <file>.zip
  3. Confirm it matches the `checksum64` value in
     chocolateyinstall.ps1 of the published Chocolatey package
     (visible at https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/kanban).
  4. Unzip and confirm the archive contains `kanban.exe`,
     `kanban-mcp.exe`, `LICENSE.md`, and `README.md`.

The source for the binaries is at:
  https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban

The build that produced the binaries is reproducible from the
corresponding tagged commit using the project's release.yml workflow.
```

This file is REQUIRED by Chocolatey moderation for any package that downloads a binary at install time. The reviewer literally checks for its presence.

### `packaging/chocolatey/README.md`

Short developer-facing readme. Validation runs entirely in CI (we have no Windows dev box), so the README documents how to read the CI smoke output and how to override locally in the rare case someone does have a Windows machine.

```markdown
# Chocolatey package source

This directory holds the source files for the `kanban` Chocolatey
package. Pack and publish are automated in
`.github/workflows/release.yml` — local Windows isn't required.

The CI flow (publish-chocolatey job) does the equivalent of:

    # Substitute placeholders into a temp build directory.
    Copy-Item kanban.nuspec build/kanban.nuspec
    Copy-Item tools/* build/tools/ -Recurse
    Copy-Item ../../LICENSE.md build/tools/LICENSE.txt -Force
    (Get-Content build/kanban.nuspec) -replace '\$version\$', $VERSION | Set-Content build/kanban.nuspec
    (Get-Content build/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1) `
      -replace '\$version\$',   $VERSION `
      -replace '\$checksum64\$', $SHA `
      | Set-Content build/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1
    choco pack                                  # in build/
    choco install kanban -s . -y --force        # smoke test
    kanban --version
    kanban-mcp --version
    choco push kanban.$VERSION.nupkg ...

To re-run the smoke locally on a Windows box, copy the build/ steps above.
Do NOT commit substituted files.
```

## Acceptance

These are all checkable on the CI runner (no local Windows machine needed):

- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` exists with the content above.
- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1` exists with the content above.
- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyuninstall.ps1` exists with the content above.
- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/tools/LICENSE.txt` exists with the placeholder text above.
- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/tools/VERIFICATION.txt` exists with the content above (mentions both binaries).
- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/README.md` exists describing the CI-driven flow.

The smoke validation (`choco pack` + `choco install` + `kanban --version` + `kanban-mcp --version`) lives in the release.yml publish subtask, not in this card. This card produces the files only.

## Out of scope

- Any release.yml changes (separate subtask).
- Any Chocolatey-account / API-key work (separate subtask).
- Pushing to community.chocolatey.org.
- README/CONTRIBUTING.md docs in the project root (separate docs subtask).

## Related

- **Parent**: chocolatey umbrella card.
- **KAN-465** — sibling Homebrew card; the live formula at `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap` ships both `kanban` and `kanban-mcp`, this card mirrors that decision.
- **KAN-446** — AUR pipeline; reference for "packaging files in this repo, CI consumes them" idiom; AUR also ships both binaries.
cards.title: Author packaging/chocolatey/ skeleton (nuspec + install scripts + LICENSE + VERIFICATION)

cards.description:
Follow-up from PR #251 (Release 0.5.0) review. The AUR section of release.yml works for the obvious failure modes but has three latent risks worth addressing before they bite.

## Where

`.github/workflows/release.yml`, the "Update PKGBUILD version and sha256" + "Generate .SRCINFO" + "Commit and push AUR files" + "Deploy to AUR" steps (lines ~126-171).

## Risks (in order of likelihood)

### 1. Tag-push → tarball-fetch race

The "Tag version" step pushes `v$VERSION` to origin, then "Update PKGBUILD" curls `https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/archive/v$VERSION.tar.gz`. The archive endpoint is tag-based and usually serves immediately, but GitHub's CDN can transiently 404 right after a tag push. With `set -e` + `curl -fsL`, a single transient 404 fails the whole release **after** crates.io has already published — no clean retry path, requires manual re-tag or running the `aur-publish.yml` workflow_dispatch fallback.

Fix: wrap the curl in a retry loop with exponential backoff.

```bash
for i in 1 2 3 4 5; do
  if SHA256=$(curl -fsL "$URL" | sha256sum | cut -d' ' -f1); then break; fi
  sleep $((i*5))
done
```

### 2. No end-to-end SHA validation before AUR push

`makepkg --printsrcinfo` parses metadata only — it does not download or verify sources. So if the recorded sha is ever wrong (truncated body, sed regex miss, GitHub archive format drift), CI is green and the broken PKGBUILD ships to AUR. End users see `==> ERROR: One or more files did not pass the validity check!` at `pacman -S` time.

Fix: replace (or augment) the `--printsrcinfo` step with `makepkg -od --nocheck` inside the same nix shell. That re-downloads via makepkg's own logic and verifies against the sha we just wrote — closes the loop on the exact artifact end users will pull.

### 3. GitHub auto-generated tarballs aren't contractually byte-stable

GitHub has historically changed git's `archive` defaults (notably 2023-01 / 2023-09 gzip default changes), invalidating hashes across nixpkgs and AUR. When that happens, our recorded sha drifts even though the upstream commit is unchanged.

Two ways out:
- (a) Upload a built tarball as a GH Release asset (via `softprops/action-gh-release` `files:` input) and source the PKGBUILD from `.../releases/download/...` (immutable artifact).
- (b) Source from `git+https://...#tag=v$pkgver` and let makepkg verify by ref instead of by sha.

(b) is lower-effort. Slow-burning, no urgency.

### 4. sed assumes single-line sha256sums array

`sed "s|sha256sums=.*|...|"` works on the current `aur/PKGBUILD:11` which has `sha256sums=("...")` on one line. The moment anyone canonicalises to the multi-line form, the sed silently leaves the array half-replaced and the **old** sha survives. Pin the format with a `# do not reformat — release.yml depends on single-line` comment in the PKGBUILD, or rewrite the field with a tool that parses arrays.

### 5. Hardcoded pkgrel=1

If anyone ever pushes an AUR-only fix manually (standard convention is bumping `pkgrel` between upstream releases), the next release.yml run resets it to 1. Probably fine for this fully-automated flow, but worth a comment in the PKGBUILD header so a future maintainer isn't surprised.

## Recommended order

1. **Add curl retry** — addresses #1, ~4 lines, no semantic change.
2. **Add `makepkg -od --nocheck` validation step** — addresses #2, single nix-shell invocation.
3. **Pin sha256sums single-line format with a comment** — addresses #4, one-line change.
4. **Decide on (a) vs (b) for tarball stability** — addresses #3, larger change, can wait.

## Context

This is the first release that exercises the wired-up AUR pipe end-to-end (KAN-397 only just landed in 0.5.0). Decided to ship 0.5.0 with the current pipe and iterate based on what actually happens on the first real run, rather than pre-harden everything blind.

## Acceptance

- Curl retry implemented and tested via a manual `workflow_dispatch` run.
- `makepkg -od --nocheck` runs against the freshly-written PKGBUILD before commit/push.
- PKGBUILD has a header comment about sha256sums single-line format and pkgrel reset.
- Decision recorded on (a) vs (b) for #3 (can be left as a deferred sub-card).
cards.title: Harden release.yml AUR publish pipeline

cards.description:
Brings CLI and MCP up to parity with the TUI for card parent/child relations, restructures the underlying graph layer along SOLID lines, and introduces typed error boundaries at the CLI and MCP edges. Started as a four-method trait carve-out (KAN-504 original scope); during review the scope expanded to address structural concerns the carve-out only papered over.

## Surface added

- New top-level `kanban relation` subcommand with `add`, `remove`, `parents`, `children`. The mutation subcommands take positional args; `add <PARENT> <CHILD>...` accepts one or more children in a single call. Listings take a positional `<card>` with optional `--sort` / `--order` (defaults: `card-number asc`).
- Four MCP tools (`tool_set_card_parent`, `tool_remove_card_parent`, `tool_list_card_parents`, `tool_list_card_children`).
- All identifiers accept UUIDs or card identifiers like `KAN-5`. Cross-board parent/child remains permitted.
- TUI relationship popup now goes through `GraphOperations` like the other two apps; the direct `Command::Dependency(...)` construction at `popup_handlers.rs` is gone.

```
kanban relation add KAN-5 KAN-7              # KAN-7 is now a subtask of KAN-5
kanban relation add KAN-5 KAN-7 KAN-8 KAN-9  # attach several children in one call
kanban relation children KAN-5               # list direct children
kanban relation parents KAN-7                # list direct parents
kanban relation remove KAN-5 KAN-7
```

## Structural refactors (graph layer)

`GraphOperations` stays as the service-layer entry point. Behind it, the graph is now properly factored:

- **`kanban-core::graph`** is a small trait taxonomy where each trait has one cohesive purpose:
  - `Graph`: direction-agnostic edge contract (`add_edge`, `remove_edge`, `contains_edge`)
  - `Directed: Graph`: directed traversal vocabulary (`outgoing`, `incoming`)
  - `Undirected: Graph`: undirected traversal vocabulary (`neighbors`)
  - `Cascadable: Graph<NodeId=Uuid>`: node-level cascade mutations (`archive_node`, `unarchive_node`, `remove_node`)
  - `EdgeSet: Graph<NodeId=Uuid>`: set-like reads with stdlib-aligned names (`len`, `active_len`, `contains`, `is_empty`)
  - `GraphError` enum (`Cycle`, `SelfReference`, `EdgeNotFound`)

  Two concrete impls: `DagGraph` (cycle-rejecting) and `UndirectedGraph` (cycle-permitting). Both wrap an `EdgeStore<()>` (renamed from the previous `Graph<E>` struct) for archive parity. Direction-specific vocabulary lives only on the matching subtype trait, with no inherent shadow methods, so the type system refuses to ask an `UndirectedGraph` for directed semantics. `Cascadable` and `EdgeSet` are split so a read-only consumer of edge counts does not carry mutation authority through its trait choice.

- **`DependencyGraph` split into three sub-graphs**, replacing the single edge-type-tagged `CardDependencyGraph` and the `CardGraphExt` god-trait:
  - `parent_child: DagGraph`
  - `blocks:       DagGraph`
  - `relates:      UndirectedGraph`

  Convenience methods (`set_parent`, `set_block`, `relate`, `parents`, `children`, `blockers`, `blocked`, `related`, `ancestors`, `descendants`, `can_start`, ...) live on `DependencyGraph` and delegate. Cross-cutting cascades split by capability: node-level mutations (`archive_node`, `unarchive_node`, `remove_node`, `disconnect`) iterate over `[&mut dyn Cascadable; 3]`; edge-level reads (`len`, `active_len`, `contains`) iterate over `[&dyn EdgeSet; 3]`. Both come from private helpers, so adding a fourth component graph only updates the helpers. The old `blocked_by` (which paradoxically returned outgoing edges) is gone; the public surface is `blocked` (outgoing) / `blockers` (incoming) with names that match what they return.

- **Method-name cleanup on `DependencyGraph`** during review: `add_blocks → set_block`, `remove_blocks → unblock`, `blocks_targets → blocked`, `add_relates_to → relate`, `remove_relates_to → unrelate`, `try_remove_edge → disconnect`. Each name reads as the verb it represents; the old `add_*` / `_to` / `_targets` suffixes were boilerplate.

- **Persistence routes through `DependencyGraph`** via two new public methods: `insert_raw_edge(kind, edge)` and `edges_by_kind() -> impl Iterator<Item = (CardEdgeType, &Edge<()>)>`. SQLite read/write and command capture-inverse no longer reach into `graph.parent_child` / `graph.blocks` / `graph.relates` directly.

- **`DependencyCommand` collapsed** to typed `AddEdge` / `RemoveEdge`. The six per-kind structs (`AddBlocks`, `RemoveBlocks`, `AddRelatesTo`, `RemoveRelatesTo`, `SetParent`, `RemoveParent`) collapse into two, each carrying `kind: CardEdgeType` so the sub-graph is selected at execute time. Safe because neither backend serializes the command log on snapshot today (the JSON snapshot intentionally drops commands between sessions; SQLite command-log persistence is a separate ticket). Each impl's match collapses from six `(kind, op)` pairs to three kinds.

- **`edges_to_undo_commands` helper** centralises the kind→`AddEdge` mapping used by three capture-inverse sites (`RemoveDependencyCommand`, `DeleteCardEdges`, `DeleteCard`).

- **`GraphOperations` returns `Vec<Uuid>`** rather than `Vec<CardSummary>`. The TUI (cards already in memory) does not pay for resolution; CLI / MCP resolve at their own boundary. All three wrapper contexts (`CliContext`, `McpContext`, `TuiContext`) write the impl by hand; the earlier `delegate_graph_ops_to_inner!` macro is gone (two call sites was below the threshold where the macro paid for the indirection it cost at the reading site).

- **Service-level card-existence validation.** `KanbanContext::add_card_edge` and `remove_card_edge` reject unknown card ids up front, returning `NotFound { entity: "card", id }` before the command reaches the graph. Closes a data-integrity hole: the CLI's identifier resolver parses raw UUIDs but does not look them up, so a stale or fabricated UUID would silently land in the graph as a dangling edge whose endpoints reference no card.

## Persistence migration (JSON V6)

On-disk dependency graphs change shape from `graph.cards.edges: [{ edge_type, ... }]` to `graph.{ parent_child, blocks, relates }.edges: [{ ... }]`. Existing files at V1..V5 are auto-migrated on load through the appropriate chain of legacy steps and a single split-graph step, landing at V6. New saves write V6 directly. Both async and sync load paths run the migration in place. SQLite needs no schema change; its `card_edges.edge_type` column is read at load and routed to the correct sub-graph.

The V6 split-graph transform is idempotent: invoking it on an already-V6 envelope returns immediately without touching the existing sub-graphs (prevents a silent edge-wipe regression if the function is ever misinvoked outside its normal `from < V6` migration path).

## Typed error boundaries

- **`KanbanCliError`** (Domain / Resolution / Io / Serialization / Anyhow) wraps `KanbanError` with CLI-specific concerns. The relation handler returns `Result<_, KanbanCliError>` internally; every failure path threads through one boundary that emits the JSON `CliResponse` envelope. The `Resolution { hint }` variant now covers both identifier-resolution failures and handler-enriched messages (`Message` was folded in); `Resolution`'s `Display` renders hint-verbatim with no `identifier resolution failed:` prefix, matching the established CLI convention used by `card get` / `card delete` / `card archive`.
- **`KanbanMcpError`** (Domain / Resolution / InvalidParam) wraps `KanbanError` and implements `Into<rmcp::ErrorData>`. `kanban_err_to_mcp` now routes through this type. `locked_read` / `locked_write` are generic over `E: Into<McpError>` so typed closures plug in without per-handler conversion boilerplate. The MCP `Resolution` variant likewise renders hint-verbatim to match the CLI surface.

Only the relation surface is migrated to the typed boundary in this PR; board / card / sprint / export handlers follow in subsequent PRs.

## Testing

Workspace gate clean: `cargo fmt --all -- --check`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features -- -D warnings`, `cargo test --workspace` (1707 passed, 0 failed).

- `kanban-core`: unit tests across `Graph`, `DagGraph`, `UndirectedGraph`, `GraphError`, including object-safety, cycle / self-ref / edge-not-found, archive/unarchive interactions, transitive ancestors/descendants
- `kanban-domain`: inline tests on the new `DependencyGraph` shape (parent_child / blocks / relates / cross-cutting cascades / serialization round-trip), plus per-`AddEdge` / `RemoveEdge` command tests
- `kanban-persistence-json`: tests for the split-graph migration (per-edge-type routing, mixed-edge split, archive_at preservation, version bump, file round-trip, V4->V6 / V5->V6 skip-legacy, V6 idempotency)
- `kanban-service`: parameterised integration tests in `tests/card_graph.rs` across JSON and SQLite; covers add / remove / list / cycle / self-ref / cross-board, plus the new card-existence validation (rejects unknown source and target per kind per backend)
- `kanban-cli`: integration tests in `cli_integration::relation_tests` covering all four subcommands, positional and multi-child shapes, and clap-level argument validation
- `kanban-mcp`: integration tests; cycle and edge-missing tests pin `ErrorCode::INVALID_PARAMS` via the typed error path rather than substring-matching debug output

## Commit hygiene

78 commits on the branch, each cycle as `test(...)` / `feat(...)` / `refactor(...)` / `style(...)` / `docs(...)` / `fix(...)` typed scopes; build green at every commit. The Cycle 5 TUI rewiring has no new test commit because the refactor is internal: the existing relationship popup tests in `crates/kanban-tui/tests/` are the contract the refactor must preserve, and they continue to pass.

## Follow-up scope (not in this PR)

- Migrate remaining CLI/MCP handlers (board, card, sprint, export) to the typed error boundary; only relation is wired today.
- Extract `kanban-core::graph` into its own `kanban-graph` crate; discussed during review and explicitly deferred for this PR.
- Persist the command audit log in SQLite (separate ticket; today's snapshot intentionally drops commands between sessions).
cards.title: Expose card parent/child relations through CLI and MCP via a focused CardDependencyOperations trait

cards.description:
Follow-up from the 0.5.0 release audit (PR #251). The KAN-405 SQLite migration that drops the legacy `command_log` and `undo_state` tables works correctly in practice but has two hardening gaps worth closing.

## Where

`crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/sqlite_store.rs:300-366` — the `migrate()` method on the SQLite store.

## What's there today

- Two sequential `DROP TABLE` statements, each guarded by a `COUNT(*) > 0` check against `sqlite_master`.
- No explicit `BEGIN`/`COMMIT` wrapping the pair.
- The `schema_version` column exists in the metadata table but is **not consulted** by the migration logic — it relies entirely on table-existence checks.

## Why it's a nice-to-have, not a blocker

If the process crashes between dropping `command_log` and `undo_state`, the database is left half-migrated (one table gone, the other not). However, the migration is idempotent: the next 0.5.0 open sees the dropped table missing, skips it, sees the surviving one, drops it. End state converges to correct.

So shipping 0.5.0 without this fix is fine. But making it transactional removes the brief half-migrated window entirely, and adopting `schema_version` is a prerequisite for any future migration that can't rely on table-existence as the sentinel.

## Fix

1. Wrap both DROPs in a single transaction:

```rust
let mut tx = self.pool.begin().await?;
sqlx::query("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS command_log").execute(&mut *tx).await?;
sqlx::query("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS undo_state").execute(&mut *tx).await?;
sqlx::query("UPDATE metadata SET schema_version = 2 WHERE id = 1").execute(&mut *tx).await?;
tx.commit().await?;
```

2. Gate the migration on `schema_version < 2`, not on table existence. Bump `schema_version` to 2 in the same transaction.

3. The fresh-install path (schema.sql) should set `schema_version = 2` so we never run the migration on a brand-new DB.

## Tests

- Existing test at `crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/tests/data_store.rs:362-423` should continue to pass.
- Add: fresh-install path (no legacy tables, `schema_version = 2`) does not attempt the migration.
- Add: migration is no-op when `schema_version >= 2` even if legacy tables somehow exist (defensive).
- Consider documenting the half-migrated recovery scenario in a comment so future readers don't worry about it.

## Acceptance

- Both DROPs and the `schema_version` bump run in one transaction.
- The migration is gated on `schema_version`, not table existence.
- All existing migration tests pass; new tests cover fresh install and schema-version gating.
- The audit comment in `sqlite_store.rs` notes that this closes the half-migrated window.
cards.title: Wrap SQLite KAN-405 legacy-table drop in a transaction + use schema_version

cards.description:
## Background

The project currently ships to crates.io, AUR, GitHub Releases (notes only, no binaries), and nixpkgs (via ryantm). Three large user populations have no first-class install path today:

- **macOS users** — no Homebrew formula, no install path other than `cargo install kanban-cli` or building from source.
- **Windows users** — Windows CI binary exists (PR #267 / KAN-445) but it isn't packaged or published anywhere consumers look (Chocolatey, winget).
- **Discoverability on Linux beyond Arch** — AUR covers Arch, nixpkgs covers Nix, but no Homebrew-on-Linux path exists.

This card covers publishing to **Homebrew (tap)**, **Chocolatey**, and **winget**, restructuring all packaging under a unified `packaging/` directory, and updating the release workflow to drive all of them.

## Prerequisites (blockers — must land before any registry work)

### P1. Add macOS to CI

`.github/workflows/ci.yml` currently runs only `ubuntu-latest` and `windows-latest`. The TUI uses crossterm (cross-platform) and the Linux-only deps in `default.nix:22-25` are gated on `pkgs.stdenv.isLinux`, so the project *should* build on macOS, but this is unverified. Required before Homebrew because the tap is meaningless if it can't actually compile on macOS.

- Add `macos-latest` (Intel) and `macos-14` (Apple Silicon) to the test matrix.
- Run `cargo test --workspace` on both.
- Address any platform-specific failures that surface.

### P2. Build and upload release binaries

`.github/workflows/release.yml` currently only creates a GitHub Release with auto-generated notes — no artifacts. Chocolatey and winget both expect a downloadable binary from a stable URL. Required before either of those.

Add a build matrix step to `release.yml` (after `Create GitHub Release`) that produces and uploads:

- `kanban-v${VERSION}-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip` — Windows binary (cross-compile from Ubuntu via `cross`, or use a `windows-latest` job)
- `kanban-v${VERSION}-x86_64-apple-darwin.tar.gz` — macOS Intel
- `kanban-v${VERSION}-aarch64-apple-darwin.tar.gz` — macOS ARM
- `kanban-v${VERSION}-x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu.tar.gz` — Linux (useful for non-Arch, non-Nix users; also enables Homebrew-on-Linux)

Each archive contains just the `kanban` binary plus `LICENSE` and `README.md`. Use `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` (already in use) with the `files:` field to attach them.

Generate and publish SHA256 checksums alongside (a single `SHA256SUMS` file is the conventional shape).

## Repo restructure: `packaging/` directory

Move everything packaging-related under a unified `packaging/` directory. Current `aur/` becomes `packaging/aur/`, and new homes are created for the new registries.

Target layout:

```
packaging/
├── aur/
│   ├── PKGBUILD
│   └── .SRCINFO
├── homebrew/
│   └── Formula/
│       └── kanban.rb
├── chocolatey/
│   ├── kanban.nuspec
│   └── tools/
│       ├── chocolateyinstall.ps1
│       ├── chocolateyuninstall.ps1
│       ├── LICENSE.txt
│       └── VERIFICATION.txt
└── winget/
    └── manifests/
        └── f/
            └── fulsomenko/
                └── kanban/
                    ├── fulsomenko.kanban.installer.yaml
                    ├── fulsomenko.kanban.locale.en-US.yaml
                    └── fulsomenko.kanban.yaml
```

CI references to `./aur/...` in `release.yml` must be updated to `./packaging/aur/...` in the same PR that does the move, or the AUR pipeline breaks at next release.

## Task 1: Homebrew tap

### Scope: tap first, core later

Ship a Homebrew tap (`fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban`) as the initial deliverable. Migrating to homebrew-core is a separate future card — the Formula written here is designed to port cleanly when notability allows it (license is already SPDX `Apache-2.0`, test block goes beyond `--version`, etc.).

### Tap repo

Create a separate GitHub repo `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban`. The Formula's source of truth lives at `packaging/homebrew/Formula/kanban.rb` in this repo; release.yml mirrors it to the tap repo on each release.

### Formula skeleton

```ruby
class Kanban < Formula
  desc "Terminal-based kanban board inspired by lazygit"
  homepage "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban"
  url "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/archive/refs/tags/v#{VERSION}.tar.gz"
  sha256 "<filled in by release.yml>"
  license "Apache-2.0"
  head "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban.git", branch: "develop"

  depends_on "rust" => :build
  depends_on "pkg-config" => :build

  on_linux do
    depends_on "wayland"
    depends_on "libxcb"
  end

  def install
    system "cargo", "install", *std_cargo_args(path: "crates/kanban-cli")
  end

  test do
    assert_match "kanban", shell_output("#{bin}/kanban --version")
    system bin/"kanban", "init", "--name", "Test", testpath/"test.json"
    assert_predicate testpath/"test.json", :exist?
  end
end
```

Notes on the formula:

- `std_cargo_args(path: "crates/kanban-cli")` builds only `kanban-cli`, matching `default.nix:27`.
- `on_linux` mirrors the Wayland/libxcb deps from `default.nix:22-25`.
- The `test do` block must exercise actual behavior, not just `--version`, to satisfy homebrew-core audit when we eventually port. Initializing a board file is a cheap, meaningful test.

### User-facing UX

```sh
brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban
# or
brew tap fulsomenko/kanban
brew install kanban
```

Compile-from-source on install will take a few minutes per machine. Self-hosting bottles is out of scope for this card.

### README

Add a "Homebrew" section under installation alongside the AUR and Nix sections.

## Task 2: Chocolatey

### Files

`packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec`:

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="utf-8"?>
<package xmlns="http://schemas.microsoft.com/packaging/2015/06/nuspec.xsd">
  <metadata>
    <id>kanban</id>
    <version>$version$</version>
    <title>kanban</title>
    <authors>Max Blomstervall</authors>
    <projectUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban</projectUrl>
    <licenseUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/blob/master/LICENSE</licenseUrl>
    <requireLicenseAcceptance>false</requireLicenseAcceptance>
    <projectSourceUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban</projectSourceUrl>
    <packageSourceUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/tree/master/packaging/chocolatey</packageSourceUrl>
    <docsUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban#readme</docsUrl>
    <bugTrackerUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/issues</bugTrackerUrl>
    <tags>kanban tui terminal productivity rust</tags>
    <summary>Terminal-based kanban board inspired by lazygit</summary>
    <description><![CDATA[
A terminal-based kanban/project management tool written in Rust,
inspired by lazygit's interface design.
    ]]></description>
  </metadata>
  <files>
    <file src="tools\**" target="tools" />
  </files>
</package>
```

`packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1`:

```powershell
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
$toolsDir = "$(Split-Path -parent $MyInvocation.MyCommand.Definition)"
$version  = '$version$'
$url      = "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v$version/kanban-v$version-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"

$packageArgs = @{
  packageName    = 'kanban'
  unzipLocation  = $toolsDir
  url64bit       = $url
  checksum64     = '$checksum64$'
  checksumType64 = 'sha256'
}

Install-ChocolateyZipPackage @packageArgs
```

`packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyuninstall.ps1`:

```powershell
$ErrorActionPreference = 'Stop'
$toolsDir = "$(Split-Path -parent $MyInvocation.MyCommand.Definition)"
Uninstall-ChocolateyZipPackage -PackageName 'kanban' -ZipFileName "kanban-*-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"
```

`packaging/chocolatey/tools/LICENSE.txt` — copy of the project LICENSE (chocolatey audit requires this be redistributed alongside the binary).

`packaging/chocolatey/tools/VERIFICATION.txt`:

```
VERIFICATION

The binary in this package is the official kanban release for Windows,
downloaded from the project's GitHub release page:

  https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases

The SHA256 checksum is recorded in the chocolateyinstall.ps1 file via
the `$checksum64` substitution variable, populated at package build time
from the release artifact.

To verify manually:
  1. Download the release zip from the URL above.
  2. Compute its SHA256:  Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 <file>.zip
  3. Confirm it matches the checksum embedded in chocolateyinstall.ps1.
```

### Build/publish process

The `$version$` and `$checksum64$` placeholders are substituted at release time. release.yml:

1. After building Windows release artifact and computing SHA256.
2. `sed`-replace `$version$` and `$checksum64$` in copies under a build directory.
3. Run `choco pack` against the build directory.
4. Run `choco push kanban.<version>.nupkg --api-key=$CHOCO_API_KEY --source=https://push.chocolatey.org/`.
5. Wait for moderation (out-of-band; can take days to weeks for first version).

Need to obtain a Chocolatey community API key and add it to repository secrets as `CHOCO_API_KEY`.

### Moderation expectations

First version typically reviewed within 1-7 days. Common feedback: tighten the description, fix metadata, ensure LICENSE.txt and VERIFICATION.txt are present and accurate. Subsequent versions usually auto-pass once the maintainer relationship is established.

## Task 3: winget

### Files

`packaging/winget/manifests/f/fulsomenko/kanban/fulsomenko.kanban.yaml` (version manifest):

```yaml
PackageIdentifier: fulsomenko.kanban
PackageVersion: $version
DefaultLocale: en-US
ManifestType: version
ManifestVersion: 1.6.0
```

`packaging/winget/manifests/f/fulsomenko/kanban/fulsomenko.kanban.locale.en-US.yaml`:

```yaml
PackageIdentifier: fulsomenko.kanban
PackageVersion: $version
PackageLocale: en-US
Publisher: Fulsomenko
PublisherUrl: https://github.com/fulsomenko
PublisherSupportUrl: https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/issues
Author: Max Blomstervall
PackageName: kanban
PackageUrl: https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban
License: Apache-2.0
LicenseUrl: https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/blob/master/LICENSE
ShortDescription: Terminal-based kanban board inspired by lazygit
Description: |
  A terminal-based kanban/project management tool written in Rust,
  inspired by lazygit's interface design.
Tags:
  - kanban
  - tui
  - terminal
  - productivity
  - rust
ManifestType: defaultLocale
ManifestVersion: 1.6.0
```

`packaging/winget/manifests/f/fulsomenko/kanban/fulsomenko.kanban.installer.yaml`:

```yaml
PackageIdentifier: fulsomenko.kanban
PackageVersion: $version
InstallerType: zip
NestedInstallerType: portable
NestedInstallerFiles:
  - RelativeFilePath: kanban.exe
    PortableCommandAlias: kanban
Installers:
  - Architecture: x64
    InstallerUrl: https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v$version/kanban-v$version-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip
    InstallerSha256: $sha256
ManifestType: installer
ManifestVersion: 1.6.0
```

### Publish process

1. `winget` manifests cannot live in your repo as their canonical home — they must be submitted as a PR to `microsoft/winget-pkgs`.
2. The in-repo `packaging/winget/manifests/` is a dev/staging copy. release.yml uses `microsoft/winget-create` (or the `vedantmgoyal2009/winget-releaser@v2` action) to auto-generate the PR upstream on each release.
3. Validate locally with `winget validate --manifest packaging/winget/manifests/f/fulsomenko/kanban/` before submitting.

### Why use `vedantmgoyal2009/winget-releaser`

It abstracts away the manifest generation, PR creation, and authentication-via-PAT-token dance. Inputs are: GitHub release tag, installer regex, PackageIdentifier, GitHub token with `public_repo` scope on a fork of `microsoft/winget-pkgs`. Needs a fork of `microsoft/winget-pkgs` under `fulsomenko/`.

### Validation

`winget` does an automated validation pass on each PR (manifest schema, installer reachability, SHA256 match). Manual review by Microsoft maintainers typically merges within 1-3 days for well-formed PRs.

## Task 4: CI changes (`release.yml`)

All AUR path references currently point at `./aur/...` (`release.yml:81-100`). Update in the move PR:

```diff
- sed -i "s|...|...|" ./aur/PKGBUILD
+ sed -i "s|...|...|" ./packaging/aur/PKGBUILD
```

And:

```diff
- cd aur && makepkg --printsrcinfo > .SRCINFO
+ cd packaging/aur && makepkg --printsrcinfo > .SRCINFO
```

And:

```diff
- git add aur/PKGBUILD aur/.SRCINFO
+ git add packaging/aur/PKGBUILD packaging/aur/.SRCINFO
```

And the `KSXGitHub/github-actions-deploy-aur` action's `pkgbuild` input:

```diff
- pkgbuild: ./aur/PKGBUILD
+ pkgbuild: ./packaging/aur/PKGBUILD
```

### New release.yml steps to add

After "Push release commit" and before "Create GitHub Release":

1. **Build release binaries** (matrix job): Linux/macOS Intel/macOS ARM/Windows, each producing a tarball or zip + SHA256.

After "Create GitHub Release":

2. **Update Homebrew formula**: substitute version + SHA256 in `packaging/homebrew/Formula/kanban.rb`, push to `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` repo via deploy key.
3. **Publish to Chocolatey**: substitute version + checksum, `choco pack`, `choco push` with `CHOCO_API_KEY` secret.
4. **Submit to winget**: invoke `vedantmgoyal2009/winget-releaser@v2` with the release tag and a `WINGET_TOKEN` secret (PAT scoped to a `fulsomenko/winget-pkgs` fork).

### New repository secrets needed

- `CHOCO_API_KEY` — Chocolatey community API key.
- `WINGET_TOKEN` — GitHub PAT with `public_repo` scope on `fulsomenko/winget-pkgs` (fork of upstream).
- `HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY` — SSH deploy key with write access to `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban`.

## Documentation

Update `README.md` installation section to list all install paths:

```
### Install

- macOS / Linux:    brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban
- Windows (choco):  choco install kanban
- Windows (winget): winget install fulsomenko.kanban
- Arch (AUR):       yay -S kanban
- Nix:              nix profile install nixpkgs#kanban
- crates.io:        cargo install kanban-cli
```

Update `CONTRIBUTING.md` packaging section (if it doesn't exist, create one) listing each registry, its packaging file location under `packaging/`, and where in `release.yml` the publish step lives.

## Acceptance

- `packaging/` directory exists with subdirectories for `aur/`, `homebrew/`, `chocolatey/`, `winget/`.
- Existing AUR pipeline still works after the path move (verify by triggering a release or dry-running `release.yml` against a fork).
- macOS CI matrix entries are green on `develop` HEAD.
- Release artifacts (Linux, macOS x2, Windows zip + SHA256) are attached to v0.5.2 (or the next release after this card lands).
- Homebrew tap `fulsomenko/homebrew-kanban` exists; `brew install fulsomenko/kanban/kanban` installs the current version on macOS and Linux.
- Chocolatey package `kanban` exists on community.chocolatey.org; `choco install kanban` works on Windows (after first moderation passes).
- winget manifest merged into `microsoft/winget-pkgs`; `winget install fulsomenko.kanban` works on Windows.
- `release.yml` automates publishing to all four registries (AUR, Homebrew tap, Chocolatey, winget) on each release.
- README and CONTRIBUTING.md updated.

## Out of scope (separate future cards)

- Migrating Homebrew tap → homebrew-core (gated on notability; track separately).
- Self-hosting Homebrew bottles to avoid compile-from-source.
- Publishing to Scoop (third Windows package manager, less ubiquitous than choco/winget).
- Publishing to apt/dpkg or rpm/yum repositories.
- Snap or Flatpak packaging.

## Related cards / PRs

- **KAN-445 / PR #267** — added Windows CI; the Windows binary it produces is reused here.
- **KAN-450** — Nix sandbox CI gate; orthogonal but lives in the same "make releases hermetic and broadly available" theme.
- **AUR pipeline** — already automated in `release.yml:81-115`; this card preserves and relocates it, doesn't replace it.
cards.title: Publish to Homebrew, Chocolatey, winget; consolidate packaging/ directory

cards.description:
## Background

`release.yml` currently creates a GitHub Release with auto-generated notes but does not attach any binary artifacts. Chocolatey (this card's parent umbrella) needs a Windows ZIP at a stable, downloadable URL on `github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v$VERSION/...` — that is the URL `chocolateyinstall.ps1` will fetch from at user install time.

The future winget card has the same requirement; this subtask satisfies both. The card is filed under Chocolatey purely because Chocolatey is the first registry that needs it. If winget had been split out first, the card would have been filed there instead — the work is identical.

This card does NOT include macOS or Linux artifacts. Those are KAN-465's prerequisite (and do not need to be done together with this one).

## Required artifact

Single archive: `kanban-v$VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip`.

Contents:

```
kanban.exe              # the TUI/CLI, from crates/kanban-cli
kanban-mcp.exe          # the MCP server, from crates/kanban-mcp
LICENSE.md              # Apache-2.0, copied from repo root
README.md               # copied from repo root for context
```

Both binaries are shipped because every other registry (AUR, Homebrew tap, Nix) puts `kanban` and `kanban-mcp` on PATH for users; matching that here keeps Windows users from being a second-class audience and avoids "why does choco only have one?" support questions.

Sibling: a `SHA256SUMS` file at the same release URL listing this archive's SHA256. See KAN-452 for the full multi-artifact plan; for a Chocolatey-first cut, we include only the Windows ZIP. Future cards can append macOS/Linux without changing the schema.

## Where to build

Three viable approaches:

| Approach | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| A. `windows-latest` runner | Native build, no cross-compile setup | New runner OS in our release matrix; first-time setup needed |
| B. Cross-compile from Ubuntu via `cross` | Reuses existing Linux runner | Cross-compile sometimes surfaces target-specific bugs; debugging in CI is harder |
| C. Cross-compile from Ubuntu via `cargo build --target` + `mingw-w64` | Lighter than `cross`, no Docker | We don't currently have mingw in the runner image |

**Pick A.** KAN-445 / PR #267 already added `windows-latest` to CI for tests; reusing the same OS for release builds is consistent with that decision, and any platform-specific test breakage shows up in the CI matrix before release. Worth ~3 min of extra runner time per release.

## release.yml changes

Two changes, kept as separate commits for readability:

### Step 1 — Promote `release` step outputs to job outputs

The existing `release` job exposes `has_changesets` and `new` as step outputs (`steps.check.outputs.has_changesets`, `steps.release.outputs.new`). Step outputs are not visible to downstream jobs, so we promote them to job-level outputs. This is a 3-line addition; no behavior change in the `release` job itself.

```yaml
jobs:
  release:
    name: Release to crates.io
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      contents: write
    if: github.event.pull_request.merged == true
    outputs:                                                  # <-- added
      new: ${{ steps.release.outputs.new }}                   # <-- added
      has_changesets: ${{ steps.check.outputs.has_changesets }}  # <-- added
    steps:
      ...
```

Why bundle this with the `build-windows` job in the same PR rather than its own card: it's a 3-line enabler that's only useful when paired with a downstream job that consumes it. Splitting into a dedicated card would create a stub change that doesn't pull its weight in review.

### Step 2 — Add the `build-windows` top-level job

Add a new top-level job `build-windows` to `.github/workflows/release.yml`. Sibling to `release`, not inline inside it (the `release` job runs on `ubuntu-latest` and bundles AUR + Homebrew bumps; `build-windows` needs `windows-latest` and a separate runner is cleaner).

`needs: [release]` is the only dependency we need. The existing release job's "Validate release build" step (line 90 in the current file) is inside the `release` job; if validation fails, the rest of the `release` job's steps don't run, and the job exits non-zero, which means `build-windows` (which depends on `release`) won't run either. So we get validation gating for free without lifting validate-release into its own job — keeps the diff small.

```yaml
build-windows:
  name: Build Windows release archive
  needs: [release]
  if: needs.release.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'
  runs-on: windows-latest
  permissions:
    contents: write
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      with:
        ref: v${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}

    - uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
      with:
        targets: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

    - uses: Swatinem/rust-cache@v2

    - name: Build kanban + kanban-mcp (release)
      shell: pwsh
      run: |
        cargo build --release `
          --package kanban-cli `
          --package kanban-mcp `
          --target x86_64-pc-windows-msvc

    - name: Stage archive contents
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        $stage = "stage/kanban-v$env:VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc"
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path $stage -Force | Out-Null
        Copy-Item target/x86_64-pc-windows-msvc/release/kanban.exe     $stage/
        Copy-Item target/x86_64-pc-windows-msvc/release/kanban-mcp.exe $stage/
        Copy-Item LICENSE.md $stage/
        Copy-Item README.md  $stage/
        & "$stage/kanban.exe" --version
        & "$stage/kanban-mcp.exe" --version

    - name: Compress archive
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        $name = "kanban-v$env:VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc"
        Compress-Archive -Path "stage/$name/*" -DestinationPath "$name.zip" -Force

    - name: Write SHA256SUMS
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        $name = "kanban-v$env:VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"
        $sha  = (Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 $name).Hash.ToLower()
        "$sha  $name" | Out-File -Encoding ascii -FilePath SHA256SUMS

    - name: Upload to GitHub Release
      uses: softprops/action-gh-release@v2
      with:
        tag_name: v${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
        files: |
          kanban-v${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip
          SHA256SUMS
        fail_on_unmatched_files: true
```

Cross-references:

- `cargo build --package kanban-cli --package kanban-mcp` produces both binaries in one cargo invocation, ~30-40% faster than two sequential `cargo build --package` calls because the workspace dep graph is walked once.
- `actions/checkout@v4` with `ref: v${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}` checks out the tag that the `release` job just pushed. This is important — by default `actions/checkout` on a `pull_request` event checks out the merge commit, which would not include the version-bump commit the release job just made.
- `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` is already used elsewhere in `release.yml`. Reuse it over the deprecated `actions/upload-release-asset`.
- `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable` is the canonical Rust toolchain action; `Swatinem/rust-cache@v2` is the canonical cache.
- `fail_on_unmatched_files: true` is important. Without it, a typo'd filename silently uploads nothing and the chocolatey publish step fails with a 404 weeks later when the user tries to install.
- The two `--version` calls in the Stage step are cheap (< 1s combined) and catch the worst class of bug: a binary that compiled but cannot link/load at runtime (missing DLL, broken codegen). If either errors, the job fails before uploading anything to the release — protecting downstream registries.
- `Compress-Archive` does NOT use a deterministic timestamp ordering, so the ZIP SHA varies if the workflow runs twice on the same source. Acceptable: `SHA256SUMS` is computed from the actual ZIP that gets uploaded, so the user-visible promise (download matches recorded SHA) holds.

## Where exactly in the file

Insert `build-windows` as a top-level job sibling to the existing `release` job, at the bottom of the file (after the `release` job ends at the `Sync develop with master` step). Job order in the file doesn't affect runtime — jobs run per their `needs` graph.

## Acceptance

All verifiable in CI; no Windows dev box needed:

- [x] `release.yml` exposes `has_changesets` and `new` as job-level outputs on the `release` job.
- [x] `release.yml` contains the `build-windows` job as above.
- [ ] On the next real release tag with changesets, the job runs to completion in under 10 minutes on `windows-latest`.
- [ ] The "Stage archive contents" step's two `--version` calls both succeed (visible in the runner log).
- [ ] The GitHub Release page for the tag shows `kanban-v$VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip` and `SHA256SUMS` attached.
- [ ] Downloading the ZIP via `curl -fL` from the linux dev machine, listing its contents with `unzip -l`, shows both `kanban.exe` and `kanban-mcp.exe` (plus LICENSE.md, README.md). No need to *run* them locally.
- [ ] The SHA256 in `SHA256SUMS` matches `sha256sum` of the downloaded ZIP byte-for-byte.
- [ ] Existing AUR + brew (KAN-465) publish paths still trigger and succeed on the same release.

## Implementation (as of 2026-05-25)

Implemented on branch `KAN-539/release-yml-build-and-upload-windows-release-archive-on-tag-push` as two commits stacked on develop:

- `2f64742a` — `ci(release): expose has_changesets and new as job outputs`
- `72bf1c39` — `ci(release): add build-windows job producing kanban + kanban-mcp archive`

Worktree: `.claude/worktrees/agent-a1005d410b4d46bc3`. Not yet pushed.

## Out of scope

- macOS / Linux release artifacts (defer to a separate card if/when a non-Brew non-AUR registry needs them).
- ARM Windows (`aarch64-pc-windows-msvc`) — Chocolatey does not currently differentiate, and the user base is negligible.
- Code signing the binaries — unsigned binaries trigger SmartScreen warnings on first launch but Chocolatey moderation does not require signing.
- Reproducible / bit-identical builds — `Compress-Archive` is not deterministic; matters only if we ever want to assert byte equality across two CI runs, which we do not today.
- Lifting "Validate release build" into its own top-level `validate-release` job. The current single-job structure already gates build-windows correctly (release job exits non-zero on validation failure → build-windows doesn't run). Separation would double nix setup time per release for no gain.

## Related

- **Parent**: chocolatey umbrella card.
- **KAN-445 / PR #267** — added `windows-latest` to CI; this card uses the same runner for release builds.
- **Future winget card** — will consume the same ZIP and SHA256SUMS from this job.
cards.title: release.yml: build and upload Windows release archive on tag push

cards.description:
## Background

With the package source files, the Windows release archive, and the API key all in place (their own subtasks), this card wires up the actual `choco pack` + smoke-install + `choco push` step in `.github/workflows/release.yml`. This is the work that goes live: every upstream release (driven by changesets) auto-publishes to community.chocolatey.org.

This is the only Chocolatey card that produces a real registry side effect. Validate it carefully before merging.

The maintainer has no Windows dev box, so every validation step in this card runs inside the `windows-latest` GitHub runner. There is no manual local-validation acceptance item.

## Dependencies

- The `packaging/chocolatey/` skeleton must exist (sibling subtask).
- The `build-windows` job must exist and reliably produce the Windows ZIP on GitHub releases (sibling subtask). The ZIP contains both `kanban.exe` and `kanban-mcp.exe`.
- The `CHOCO_API_KEY` repo secret must be configured (sibling subtask).
- The `release` job must expose `outputs.has_changesets` and `outputs.new` (lifted in the same PR as `build-windows`, see KAN-539).

If any of those is incomplete, this card cannot ship.

## Where to insert

A new top-level job, `publish-chocolatey`, sibling to `build-windows`. Depends on `build-windows` (so the Windows ZIP exists at the release URL and the SHA can be computed against it) and `release` (for the version string).

Insert in `.github/workflows/release.yml` after the `build-windows` job. Order in the file does not affect runtime — jobs run per their `needs` graph — but for human readability the chocolatey-specific steps should sit adjacent to `build-windows`.

Final structure of release.yml jobs in the release flow:

```
release ──┬── build-windows ──── publish-chocolatey
          │       (top-level job, windows-latest)
          │
          └── (inline within `release` on ubuntu-latest:
                Validate release build,
                Publish to crates.io,
                Tag version,
                Create GitHub Release,
                AUR PKGBUILD bump + deploy,
                Homebrew tap formula bump,
                Sync develop)
```

Why publish-chocolatey is a separate top-level job (SRP):

- Distinct runner OS (`windows-latest`) from the AUR/brew steps inside `release` (`ubuntu-latest`).
- Distinct responsibility ("publish to one specific registry"); easy to disable or rerun in isolation via `workflow_dispatch` if we add that input later.
- Distinct dependency footprint (needs `CHOCO_API_KEY`, needs a published Windows ZIP).

## Job definition

```yaml
publish-chocolatey:
  name: Publish to Chocolatey
  needs: [release, build-windows]
  if: needs.release.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'
  runs-on: windows-latest
  steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      with:
        ref: v${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}

    - name: Wait for release asset to be fetchable
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        $url = "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v$env:VERSION/kanban-v$env:VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"
        $ok = $false
        for ($i = 0; $i -lt 30; $i++) {
          try {
            $r = Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -Method Head -UseBasicParsing -ErrorAction Stop
            if ($r.StatusCode -eq 200) {
              Write-Output "Asset live at $url"
              $ok = $true
              break
            }
          } catch {
            Write-Output ("Attempt {0}: not yet ({1})" -f ($i + 1), $_.Exception.Message)
          }
          Start-Sleep -Seconds 5
        }
        if (-not $ok) {
          Write-Error "Release asset never became fetchable after 30 attempts (~150s)"
          exit 1
        }

    - name: Compute Windows ZIP SHA256
      id: sha
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        $url = "https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/releases/download/v$env:VERSION/kanban-v$env:VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip"
        $tmp = New-TemporaryFile
        Invoke-WebRequest -Uri $url -OutFile $tmp -UseBasicParsing
        $sha = (Get-FileHash -Algorithm SHA256 $tmp).Hash.ToLower()
        Remove-Item $tmp
        Write-Output "Computed SHA256: $sha"
        "sha=$sha" | Out-File -FilePath $env:GITHUB_OUTPUT -Append

    - name: Stage Chocolatey package with substitutions
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
        SHA: ${{ steps.sha.outputs.sha }}
      run: |
        $build = "build/chocolatey"
        New-Item -ItemType Directory -Path "$build/tools" -Force | Out-Null
        Copy-Item packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec $build/kanban.nuspec
        Copy-Item packaging/chocolatey/tools/* $build/tools/ -Recurse
        Copy-Item LICENSE.md $build/tools/LICENSE.txt -Force

        (Get-Content "$build/kanban.nuspec") `
          -replace '\$version\$', $env:VERSION `
          | Set-Content "$build/kanban.nuspec"

        (Get-Content "$build/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1") `
          -replace '\$version\$',   $env:VERSION `
          -replace '\$checksum64\$', $env:SHA `
          | Set-Content "$build/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1"

    - name: choco pack
      shell: pwsh
      working-directory: build/chocolatey
      run: choco pack

    - name: Smoke install from local nupkg
      shell: pwsh
      working-directory: build/chocolatey
      run: |
        choco install kanban -s . -y --force --no-progress
        kanban --version
        kanban-mcp --version

    - name: choco push
      shell: pwsh
      working-directory: build/chocolatey
      env:
        CHOCO_API_KEY: ${{ secrets.CHOCO_API_KEY }}
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        choco push "kanban.$env:VERSION.nupkg" `
          --api-key="$env:CHOCO_API_KEY" `
          --source="https://push.chocolatey.org/"

    - name: Note moderation expectations
      shell: pwsh
      env:
        VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
      run: |
        Write-Output "==="
        Write-Output "Pushed kanban v$env:VERSION to community.chocolatey.org."
        Write-Output "First-time moderation typically takes 1-7 days."
        Write-Output "Status:"
        Write-Output "  https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/kanban/$env:VERSION"
        Write-Output "==="
```

Design notes:

- **`checkout` with `ref: v${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}`**: pull the just-created tag's tree so `packaging/chocolatey/` is at the released version. Default checkout for a `pull_request` event would pull the merge commit, missing the release version bump.
- **`Wait for release asset to be fetchable`**: `build-windows` uploaded the ZIP, but GitHub's CDN can take a few seconds to make it publicly fetchable. Up to ~150s of polling before failing — empirically generous; usually live within 5-15s of upload.
- **Smoke install gates the push.** `choco install -s .` installs from the just-packed `.nupkg`, which triggers the real chocolateyinstall.ps1 hitting the real GitHub release ZIP, validating the SHA we just embedded, unzipping both binaries, and shimming them. Then `kanban --version` and `kanban-mcp --version` are invoked through the shims. Any failure aborts before `choco push`. **A broken package never reaches the registry.** This step is the entire reason a Linux-only maintainer can ship Chocolatey safely without a Windows laptop.
- **Why download-and-hash the ZIP rather than reusing build-windows's SHA256SUMS**: defense-in-depth. The bytes Chocolatey embeds match the bytes a user actually downloads. The cost is one extra ~10MB download per release; trivial. Matches the AUR step pattern of computing the SHA against the live download.
- **All shell scripts use `pwsh`**: parity with the Windows install scripts; avoids the sed quoting headache when substituting `$version$` (sed in bash treats `$` specially; pwsh `-replace` does not, given the regex escape).
- **Substitution scope**: only the temporary `build/chocolatey/` copy is mutated. The in-tree `packaging/chocolatey/` stays as a template forever. This matches the AUR step which produces a build directory and never edits the in-tree PKGBUILD.
- **`choco push` exit codes**: zero on "submitted for moderation"; non-zero on validation failure. Moderator rejection arrives later by email and does NOT fail the CI. Catching late rejections requires polling, which is out of scope.
- **`--no-progress`** on choco install: keeps the runner log readable; the rolling-progress output is unhelpful in non-interactive logs.

## Failure modes worth knowing

1. **API key invalid** → `choco push` exits with `Failed to push the package`. Fix: rotate the key in `CHOCO_API_KEY` secret (procedure in the account/secret subtask).
2. **Package ID conflict** → `choco push` exits with `Package already exists at this version.` Fix: bump version in `Cargo.toml` (the normal release flow handles this); never re-push the same version. If a publish must be redone, push a new patch version with the corrected package.
3. **Validation failure** (e.g. broken XML in nuspec) → `choco pack` exits with the specific validation error. Caught in the same job before push.
4. **Smoke install fails: "Could not find kanban.exe / kanban-mcp.exe"** → archive layout drift between `build-windows` and `chocolateyinstall.ps1` expectations. Compare what `Compress-Archive` produced (visible in the build-windows log) with what `Install-ChocolateyZipPackage` unzips to `$toolsDir`. Both .exes should land directly in `$toolsDir` (not in a subdirectory).
5. **Smoke install fails with SHA mismatch** → the earlier "Compute Windows ZIP SHA256" step downloads and hashes the bytes Chocolatey will fetch, so this should not happen in practice; if it does, re-run the job.
6. **Moderator rejection** (1-7 days post-push) → arrives via email to the Chocolatey account. Common causes: VERIFICATION.txt missing or incorrect, LICENSE.txt missing inside the package, install script `$ErrorActionPreference` not set to `Stop`. All three are covered by the skeleton subtask; rejections usually indicate a regression. Fix: edit packaging files, bump version, re-push.
7. **GitHub Release ZIP not yet propagated** → covered by the explicit `Wait for release asset to be fetchable` step.

## Dry-running before the first real release

Recommended workflow for landing this card without exposing a broken publish to a real release tag:

1. On a feature branch, push the release.yml changes.
2. Add a no-op changeset (e.g. `chore: bump`) and merge to master. This triggers the full release flow including the new chocolatey publish step.
3. Watch the chocolatey moderation queue. First-time submissions can take 1-7 days; once approved, the `kanban` name is reserved permanently.

Riskier alternative: rename the nuspec id to `kanban-test` for the first run to keep moderation friction off the real package id. Cleanup afterward via the Chocolatey account UI. Not recommended unless you have specific reason to doubt the package.

## Acceptance

All checkable from CI logs and the chocolatey.org package page; no Windows dev box needed:

- [ ] `release.yml` contains the `publish-chocolatey` job as above (including the smoke install step).
- [ ] On the next real release (with changesets), the job runs to completion in under 12 minutes.
- [ ] The "Smoke install from local nupkg" step logs show `kanban --version` and `kanban-mcp --version` both printing the expected version string.
- [ ] `choco push` exits 0 and the package appears at `https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/kanban/<VERSION>` in "Submitted" status (then "Approved" once moderation passes).
- [ ] After moderation approves, downloading the published package via `nuget` from a Linux machine (`nuget install kanban -OutputDirectory /tmp`) succeeds and unpacks the expected `tools/` directory. (Confirms the public consumer flow works without needing to `choco install`.)
- [ ] No regressions in the existing AUR or brew (KAN-465) publish paths — both still trigger and succeed on the same release.
- [ ] The Workflow Summary on the GitHub Actions run shows the moderation URL printed by the final step (operational signal for the human watching the release).

## Implementation (as of 2026-05-25)

Implemented on branch `KAN-541/release-yml-add-chocolatey-publish-step`, stacked on `KAN-539/release-yml-build-and-upload-windows-release-archive-on-tag-push`:

- `da977dbd` — `ci(release): add publish-chocolatey job`

Worktree: `.claude/worktrees/agent-kan541`. Not yet pushed. Merge KAN-539 first, then KAN-541 (or rebase KAN-541 onto develop after KAN-539 lands).

## Out of scope

- **Post-publish moderation polling / verification.** This job pushes and exits; it does not confirm moderation passed. File a follow-up "verify chocolatey publish landed" card only if moderation friction grows; manually checking the email is the default.
- **Auto-bumping the version in nuspec for non-release branches.** We substitute at pack time; in-repo nuspec stays template-form forever.
- **Multi-architecture publish.** x64 only.
- **Splitting kanban + kanban-mcp into two Chocolatey packages.** Single-package, two-binaries matches AUR / brew tap and is the right default. Re-open if telemetry ever shows MCP users wanting to skip the TUI binary.

## Related

- **Parent**: chocolatey umbrella card.
- **Sibling**: packaging-skeleton (provides the files we substitute against).
- **Sibling**: windows-archive / outputs refactor (provides the URL we download to compute SHA, and the binaries the smoke install validates).
- **Sibling**: account-and-secret (provides the `CHOCO_API_KEY` secret).
- **KAN-465** — sibling brew CI step; the live tap formula ships both binaries, this card matches.
- **KAN-446** — AUR pipeline; same `has_changesets` gate idiom, same conditional `if:` shape, same "both binaries" decision.
cards.title: release.yml: add Chocolatey publish step (pack + push + post-publish verification log)

cards.description:
Follow-up from the 0.5.0 release audit (PR #251). The Windows key-doubling fix (windows/fix-keyevent-handling) + its generalisation to all platforms (KAN-426) is currently only exercised by integration tests on platforms where the filter is reachable (Windows CI), and is unreachable code on Linux/macOS where crossterm only emits `Press` events in standard terminal mode.

## Where

`crates/kanban-tui/src/events.rs:38-40` — the `if key.kind != KeyEventKind::Press { continue; }` filter inside the event loop.

## Why it matters

If the filter logic is ever broken (e.g. inverted condition, wrong `KeyEventKind` variant compared), on Linux/macOS we wouldn't notice — the filter is a no-op there. The only safety net today is the new `windows-latest` CI job; an inline unit test would catch regressions on any platform.

This is genuinely a no-op for runtime behaviour today, but cheap insurance against a future refactor flipping the predicate.

## Fix

Add a unit test under `#[cfg(test)]` in `events.rs` (or a small new test file if `events.rs` is already large). The test should:

1. Construct `KeyEvent { kind: KeyEventKind::Press, ... }` and verify the filter passes it through.
2. Construct `KeyEvent { kind: KeyEventKind::Release, ... }` and `KeyEventKind::Repeat` and verify both are filtered out.
3. Ideally exercise the filter as a pure function, not via the full event loop — refactor the predicate into a helper like `fn should_process_key(kind: KeyEventKind) -> bool` if needed.

## Acceptance

- A test in `crates/kanban-tui/src/events.rs` (or `crates/kanban-tui/tests/event_filter_tests.rs`) that proves Press → kept, Release/Repeat → filtered.
- The test passes on Linux (where the filter is unreachable in normal operation), giving us coverage that doesn't depend on Windows CI being healthy.
- No production behaviour change.

## Priority

Low. The fix shipped in 0.5.0 is correct on paper and validated by integration tests on Windows CI. This is hardening, not a blocker.
cards.title: Add unit test for KeyEventKind::Press filter (events.rs)

cards.description:
## Context

Follow-up to KAN-328 (PR #272). The error banner system in `crates/kanban-tui/src/components/banner.rs` is set via `App::set_error(...)` (`crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:663-669`) but is not cleared by success paths. Once an error banner appears, it persists until either:

1. Another call to `set_error` / `set_success` overwrites it, or
2. The banner TTL expires (see `Banner::is_expired`, but verify it is actually consulted from the render path — at the time of writing this card it does not appear to be wired up).

The result is that an error from a failed action stays visible on screen through subsequent successful actions, confusing the user.

## Reproduction

1. On a board with `active_sprint_id == None`, focus the Cards panel.
2. Press `t` — red banner appears: "No active sprint set for filtering". (KAN-328 behaviour, working as intended.)
3. Open sprint details, activate a sprint, return to cards.
4. Press `t` — toggle succeeds (info log: "Enabled sprint filter - showing active sprint only"), but the red banner from step 2 is still visible on screen.

The same issue affects every handler that emits ``tracing::info!`` on success while a prior error banner is on screen — ``handle_toggle_sprint_filter`` is just where it was noticed during PR review.

## Code references

- ``crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/card_handlers.rs:180-204`` — ``handle_toggle_sprint_filter``; the two ``tracing::info!`` branches (toggle on / toggle off) do not touch the banner.
- ``crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:663-669`` — ``set_error``, ``set_success``, ``clear_banner``.
- ``crates/kanban-tui/src/components/banner.rs`` — ``Banner::is_expired(ttl)`` exists but the render path (``crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/mod.rs`` near line 129) currently renders unconditionally on ``ui_state.banner.is_some()``, no TTL check.

## Approach options

1. Per-handler clears (narrow). Add ``self.clear_banner()`` (or set a fresh ``set_success`` where appropriate) at the success branches of state-mutating handlers. Minimal change but fragile: every new handler has to remember.
2. TTL-driven auto-dismiss (systemic). Wire ``Banner::is_expired`` into the render path so banners self-dismiss after a fixed duration (3-5 seconds feels right). Independent of handler discipline.
3. Both. Auto-dismiss with TTL plus an explicit clear on each success path so visible feedback always matches the latest action.

Preferred: option 2 first (one place, broad coverage). Layer option 1 on top only if specific flows still feel laggy after TTL is in.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Pressing ``t`` with no active sprint still shows the error banner (regression check for KAN-328).
- [ ] After activating a sprint and pressing ``t`` again, the stale banner is gone — either immediately on the next successful action, or via TTL within a defined window.
- [ ] Integration test in ``crates/kanban-tui/tests/`` reproducing the sequence above and asserting the banner is cleared after the success step.
- [ ] If the TTL approach is taken: a test that advances time (or uses a small TTL) and asserts ``ui_state.banner`` becomes ``None`` after render once expired.

## Out of scope

- New banner variants (Info / Warning).
- Redesign of banner UI placement or styling.
cards.title: Clear stale error banner when subsequent action succeeds

cards.description:
## Goal

Unify the in-app selectable lists so they all share a single component (`ListComponent`) with consistent scroll behavior, navigation, and (optionally) above/below indicators. KAN-455 (#272-ish) introduced the *pure scroll math* (`pagination::scroll_offset_to_keep_visible`) and applied it to three lists; this card finishes the architectural migration.

## Background

`ListComponent` (`crates/kanban-tui/src/components/generic_list.rs`) wraps a `kanban_core::SelectionState` plus a `kanban_core::pagination::Page` and exposes `navigate_up/down`, `ensure_selected_visible`, `get_render_info`, multi-select, and adjusted viewport math. The above/below indicators are NOT rendered by `ListComponent` itself — they're an opt-in caller concern handled via `scroll_indicators::render_above_indicator/below_indicator`. So adopting `ListComponent` does not force the indicator UX.

Today many lists use bare `kanban_core::SelectionState` instead. Some pair it with an ad-hoc scroll offset (after KAN-455). The result: inconsistent behavior, scroll math repeated in renderers, no easy way to add features like adjusted viewport or multi-select.

## Current consumers to migrate

Bare `SelectionState` fields (no scroll, scroll math handled externally if at all):

- `app/selection.rs` — `SelectionHub`
  - `board: SelectionState`
  - `sprint: SelectionState` *(KAN-455 added `sprint_scroll: Cell<usize>` alongside)*
  - `settings_config: SelectionState`
  - `settings_config_file: SelectionState`
  - `settings_storage: SelectionState`
- `app/dialog_input.rs` — `DialogInputState`
  - `import_selection: SelectionState`
  - `priority_selection: SelectionState`
  - `column_selection: SelectionState` *(KAN-455 added `column_scroll: Cell<usize>` alongside)*
  - `sprint_assign_selection: SelectionState`
  - `task_list_view_selection: SelectionState`
  - `carry_over_sprint_selection: SelectionState`
- `app/filter.rs` — `FilterState`
  - `sort_field_selection: SelectionState`
- `app/relationship.rs` — `RelationshipState`
  - `selection: SelectionState`

Raw `usize` selection that should also adopt `ListComponent`:

- `filters.rs` — `FilterDialogState.item_selection: usize` *(KAN-455 added `item_scroll: Cell<usize>` alongside)*

For each, the migration replaces the `SelectionState`/`usize` with a `ListComponent`, then updates handler call sites (`.get()/.set()/.next()/.prev()` → `ListComponent` equivalents) and ensures `ensure_selected_visible(viewport_height)` is called wherever navigation happens.

## Suggested refactor steps

1. For each list, replace the existing selection field with a `ListComponent`. Where a `Cell<usize>` scroll offset was added by KAN-455, remove it (now subsumed by `ListComponent`).
2. Migrate handler call sites (see `grep -rn 'selection\.sprint\|column_selection\|sprint_assign_selection\|item_selection'` etc.).
3. Add `.ensure_selected_visible(viewport_height)` after navigation. For lists whose viewport height is only known at render time, store it the way `app.view.viewport_height` is stored for the main card list.
4. Renderers: replace ad-hoc scroll-offset reads with `list.get_scroll_offset()` or use `list.get_render_info(viewport_height)`.
5. Decide per-list whether to render above/below indicators (they exist already as `scroll_indicators::render_above_indicator/below_indicator`).
6. Remove now-unused helpers — `scroll_offset_to_show` in `components/sprint_assign_list.rs:214` if no longer referenced. Consider also moving any remaining pure scroll math to `kanban_core::pagination`.

## What KAN-455 left in place that this card should consume

- Pure function `kanban_core::pagination::scroll_offset_to_keep_visible(current, selected, viewport_height) -> usize` (added in KAN-455). `Page::scroll_to_visible` already delegates to it. `ListComponent` transitively uses it via `Page`.

## Out of scope

- Visual redesign of any list.
- Adding above/below indicators where they don't already appear.
- Changing keybindings or navigation semantics.

## Acceptance

- No `SelectionState` field remains for lists rendered as a paragraph or list widget. (Standalone single-selection state — e.g. radio dialogs — may stay if they have no scroll.)
- All lists scroll with the same minimal-scroll behavior already used by the main card list.
- All existing tests pass; new tests cover at least one migration per state struct touched.

## Notes from KAN-455 review

Issues raised during review of KAN-455 (PR #273) that were deferred here because the `ListComponent` migration supersedes them:

- **Renderer duplication.** `board_detail.rs::render_board_sprints_list/render_board_columns_list` and `filter_popup.rs::render_filter_sprints_section` each contain ~8 lines of identical scroll boilerplate (read `Cell` → call `scroll_offset_to_keep_visible` → write `Cell` → `Paragraph::scroll`). Migrating to `ListComponent` removes this duplication wholesale.
- **Heterogeneous list in filter popup — design decision needed.** The sprints section in the filter popup is not a flat list: it has a title line, an unassigned-sprints checkbox row, a separator, then the sprint rows. Today this is handled by a `selected_line_idx` translation inside the renderer (item_selection 0 → line 1, k ≥ 1 → line k+2). When migrating, decide how to model this in `ListComponent`:
  - Option A: one `ListComponent` for the sprints only; render decorative rows (title, separator, unassigned) outside the list. Cleanest, requires a small change to which `Paragraph` block gets `.scroll(...)`.
  - Option B: a single `ListComponent` over all rows (treating decorative rows as non-selectable). Requires extending `ListComponent` to support non-selectable entries.
  Option A is recommended.
- **`as u16` cast on scroll offset.** All three renderers (and the prior-art sites in `ui/dialogs/cards.rs:104` and `components/selection_dialog.rs:369`) cast `usize` → `u16` when feeding `Paragraph::scroll((scroll, 0))`. For lists > 65535 items this truncates silently. The migration should pick a consistent strategy — likely `u16::try_from(scroll).unwrap_or(u16::MAX)` — and apply it across every site that ends up calling `Paragraph::scroll`.
- **Section-reset test.** `FilterDialogState::next_section/prev_section` reset `item_scroll` to 0 today. The migration removes `item_scroll` (it becomes the `ListComponent`'s internal scroll offset), but the *behavior* — section changes reset the sprints panel to top — must be preserved. Add a test that exercises this through the new abstraction.
cards.title: Migrate all SelectionState-backed lists to ListComponent

cards.description:
## Background

Three small cosmetic improvements to the Chocolatey package source, surfaced during KAN-538's code review. None are blockers; all are quality-of-life polish that helps on the chocolatey.org package page, in moderator review, and for future contributors reading the packaging directory standalone.

## Scope

### 1. Add `<iconUrl>` to `kanban.nuspec`

Chocolatey package pages display the package's icon prominently. Without an `<iconUrl>`, the page uses a generic placeholder, which makes the listing look unprofessional and harder to recognize next to other Rust-CLI packages.

Requirements:
- A 64x64 PNG (or larger, square aspect ratio).
