A. Add Enter and Backspace to `CardDetailProvider::get_context`, focus-conditional (Enter when on Parents/Children; Backspace when `card_navigation_history` is non-empty). Surfaces in the help footer for free.

B. Lift the focus restriction in `detail_view_handlers.rs:336`. Backspace navigates back whenever history is non-empty, from any of the five focus panels. The text-editing concern that motivated the original guard is moot: inline title/description editing in the detail view is delegated to the external editor (`edit_card_field`), so Backspace at the panel level isn't competing with character deletion in a live input.

C. Render a breadcrumb header in `ui/card_detail.rs`: `KAN-12 ← KAN-45 ← KAN-3 (current)`, oldest left, current right. Truncate with an ellipsis prefix when depth > 3. Implementation reads from `selection.card_navigation_history` plus the current `active_card`.

D. Add `card_forward_history: Vec<usize>` to `SelectionHub` (paired with the existing `card_navigation_history`). Browser semantics: push to forward on Backspace, pop on a new forward-nav binding, clear forward on a new Enter into a relation. Bind to `L` (Vim-style mirror of `h`) and `Alt+Right`.

E. Render a `↵` or `→` affordance on the selected row in the Parents/Children list when the panel is focused. Single character, no layout change, telegraphs the action.

F. Stale-card recovery surfaces a status message (`set_error` or a non-error status helper if one exists) explaining "card no longer exists, returned to previous card". Optionally, before bailing to `None`, the recovery skips the stale entry and tries the next-deeper history entry until one resolves or the stack is empty.

G. Esc-when-history-non-empty: step back one card instead of exiting. Exit the detail view only when history is empty. Alternative: keep current Esc behavior but introduce a confirmation prompt when history is non-empty. Either way, prevents accidental loss of drill-down state.

H. Fix the `r`/`R` duplication in `keybindings/card_detail.rs:99-111`. Drop the unconditional `extend` entries since the focus-specific match arm already adds them on the relevant panels.

I. Add the relationship-navigation bindings to the `?` help overlay for the detail view.

### Concrete plan (phased)

**Phase 1 — discoverability + always-on back-nav (~1 day, ships independently)**
- (offenders 1, 2, 3, 6) Add Enter and Backspace entries to `CardDetailProvider::get_context` with focus / history conditions.
- Lift the focus restriction in `detail_view_handlers.rs:336` so Backspace fires from any focus when history is non-empty.
- Surface stale-card recovery via a status message instead of silent `None`.
- Drop the duplicate `r`/`R` bindings.
- TDD: inline test that Backspace from `CardFocus::Title` with history walks back; inline test that stale recovery sets the expected status message.

**Phase 2 — breadcrumb + forward stack (~1-2 days)**
- (offenders 4, 7) Add `card_forward_history: Vec<usize>` to `SelectionHub`. Wire push/pop/clear semantics in `return_to_previous_card_from_detail_history` and `navigate_to_related_card`.
- Add `navigate_forward_in_detail_history` helper + binding (`L`, `Alt+Right`).
- Render breadcrumb in `ui/card_detail.rs` header strip; truncate with ellipsis at depth > 3.
- TDD: forward-nav restores the prior card; new Enter clears the forward stack; breadcrumb renders the expected chain via the existing render-to-string test helpers.

**Phase 3 — polish (~half day)**
- (offenders 5, 8) Esc-when-history-non-empty steps back instead of exiting (or: gated by a confirmation prompt — pick one in implementation).
- Render the `↵` affordance on the focused relation row.
- Render-to-string tests for both.

### Test coverage

Per the project's TDD convention each step is Red → Green → Refactor with separate commits.
- Backspace-from-Title pins (Phase 1).
- Stale-card status-message pin (Phase 1).
- Forward-nav round-trip pin (Phase 2).
- Breadcrumb rendering pin (Phase 2).
- Esc-steps-back pin (Phase 3).
- Affordance rendering pin (Phase 3).

### Risk

- **Behavioural change**: Backspace currently does nothing from Title/Metadata/Description. After Phase 1 it navigates back when history is non-empty. Mitigated by the footer hint and the breadcrumb landing in the same window.
- **Esc semantics change** (Phase 3): opt for the confirmation prompt if step-back-on-Esc proves too surprising.
- All changes are scoped to `kanban-tui`; no persistence or domain impact.

### Out of scope

- Mouse interactions.
- Cross-board drill-down or archived-card drill-down semantics (the stale-card recovery is best-effort).
- Persisting navigation history across detail-view exits (history clears on Esc, by design).
cards.title: Improve card-detail relationship-navigation UX: discoverability, key bindings, and visible drill-down history

cards.description:
## Stakeholder summary — Where, Why, How

**Where.** TUI render path for the card detail view. `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs::get_card_for_detail_view` (the lookup at the renderer boundary), `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/card_detail.rs::render_card_detail` (the consumer that goes blank on miss), `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs` (sidebar relationship accessors), plus every mutation handler that gates on `self.model.card(active_card_id).is_some()` (priority popup, sprint assign, points dialog, manage parents/children, edit metadata, edit description) — those gates already short-circuit correctly for archived cards but need an audit.

**Why.** Observed during KAN-534 smoke-testing: while the TUI views card A in the detail view, an external writer (CLI / MCP / another TUI session) archives A. The detail panel goes blank. The user is stranded with no card and no signal that A moved to the archive — the only recourse is to back out and re-navigate via the archived-cards view.

Root cause: `get_card_for_detail_view` resolves via `model.card(active_card_id)` which consults only the live-card map. After archive, A lives in `model.archived_card(id)` instead. The lookup never falls back. The renderer at `ui/card_detail.rs:66` then takes the implicit `else` branch (no content), producing the blank panel.

**How.** A one-line substitution at the lookup boundary, plus an audit of downstream consumers to confirm they fail safely (no-op) rather than silently mutating archived cards.

---

## Engineering spec

### Current state

`crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:1462-1466`:

```rust
pub fn get_card_for_detail_view(&self) -> Option<Card> {
    self.selection
        .active_card_id
        .and_then(|id| self.model.card(id).cloned())  // live-only
}
```

`Model::card(id)` (`app/model.rs:31-34`) consults only the live cards HashMap. After archive the card lives in `model.archived_card(id)` (parallel map at `app/model.rs:48-51`), but the lookup never falls back.

Renderer at `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/card_detail.rs:66`:

```rust
if let Some(card) = app.get_card_for_detail_view() {
    // render the card
}
// implicit empty path on None — panel goes blank
```

### Proposed solution

Substitute `get_card_for_detail_view` to use the existing fallback helper at `app/mod.rs:1455-1460`:

```diff
 pub fn get_card_for_detail_view(&self) -> Option<Card> {
     self.selection
         .active_card_id
-        .and_then(|id| self.model.card(id).cloned())
+        .and_then(|id| self.get_card_by_id(id))
 }
```

`get_card_by_id` already does the fallback:

```rust
pub fn get_card_by_id(&self, card_id: uuid::Uuid) -> Option<Card> {
    self.model
        .card(card_id)
        .or_else(|| self.model.archived_card(card_id))
        .cloned()
}
```

For downstream surfaces that need to branch on archive state (the visual cues in KAN-536), expose:

```rust
pub fn active_card_is_archived(&self) -> bool {
    self.selection
        .active_card_id
        .map(|id| self.model.archived_card(id).is_some())
        .unwrap_or(false)
}
```

### Offender table

| File:line | Symbol | Today | Required change |
|---|---|---|---|
| `app/mod.rs:1462-1466` | `get_card_for_detail_view` | `model.card(id).cloned()` — live-only | `get_card_by_id(id)` — falls back to archived |
| `ui/card_detail.rs:66` | `render_card_detail` | Takes implicit `else` branch on `None`, renders nothing | Same code path; lookup fix above means we no longer hit `else` for archived cards |
| `handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs::get_current_card_parents` | Sidebar parent list | `self.model.graph().parents(id)` — live edges only | Route through `parents_including_archived` (introduced by KAN-516; introduce locally if KAN-516 not landed) when active card is archived |
| `handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs::get_current_card_children` | Sidebar child list | Same | Same |
| `handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs:961` `handle_manage_parents` | Manage Parents dialog | Gated on `self.model.card(id).is_some()` | Stays strict — confirm clean short-circuit for archived active card (do not open dialog) |
| `handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs:1014` `handle_manage_children` | Manage Children dialog | Same | Same |
| `handlers/popup_handlers.rs:54` | Priority popup Enter branch | Gated | Stays strict |
| `handlers/popup_handlers.rs:257` | Sprint-assign popup Enter branch | Gated | Stays strict |
| `handlers/popup_handlers.rs:593` | Manage-relationship toggle | Gated | Stays strict |
| `handlers/dialog_handlers.rs:138` | Points dialog | Gated | Stays strict |
| `app/mod.rs:1789` `edit_card_field` | Title / description editor | Gated | Stays strict |
| `app/mod.rs:2349` `get_current_priority_selection_index` | Priority dialog seed value | Returns 0 on miss | Confirm acceptable for archived cards |
| `app/mod.rs:2370` `get_current_sprint_selection_index` | Sprint dialog seed value | Same | Same |

### Alternatives considered

**1. `get_card_for_detail_view → get_card_by_id` substitution (proposed).**

- *Pros*: one-line change at the lookup boundary; reuses an existing helper; no new types; downstream consumers see no API change; ships in a single commit.
- *Cons*: archived-vs-live distinction must be re-derived at any consumer that needs to branch (currently just the visual cues in KAN-536, served by a single boolean getter); no compile-time enforcement that consumers know they might see an archived card.

**2. Introduce a `RenderableCard` enum.**

```rust
pub enum RenderableCard {
    Live(Card),
    Archived { card: Card, archived_at: DateTime<Utc> },
}
```

Change `get_card_for_detail_view` to return `Option<RenderableCard>`.

- *Pros*: the type system enforces archived-handling at every consumer; mutation paths can refuse to compile against the archived variant; clean and explicit.
- *Cons*: large API surface change; every consumer (detail renderer, sidebar accessors, manage-parents/children, popups, dialogs) must pattern-match; ripples into theme + accessor APIs; significantly bigger PR.

**3. Extend `Model` with a unified `card_view(id) -> CardView` returning a rich struct.**

```rust
pub struct CardView<'a> {
    pub card: &'a Card,
    pub archived_at: Option<DateTime<Utc>>,
}
```

- *Pros*: single lookup primitive; renderers depend on a stable abstraction that carries archive metadata for free; less churn than #2 because consumers can still treat the inner `Card` normally.
- *Cons*: introduces a new type; needs construction at every consumer; doesn't enforce archive-handling at the type level the way #2 does.

**Recommendation**: ship #1 first as the user-visible fix. Promote to #2 or #3 only if KAN-536's visual cues end up needing more than a single boolean from this card.

### Tests

- Inline TUI unit test in the existing `setup_reload_resort_fixture` style: seed A live, set `active_card_id = Some(A.id)`, externally archive A via `app.ctx.archive_card(A.id)`, reload model, assert `get_card_for_detail_view()` returns `Some(card)` with `card.id == A.id`.
- Assert `get_current_card_parents()` and `get_current_card_children()` return A's archive-time relationships, not empty.
- Inline test that each mutation gate (priority popup, sprint assign, manage parents/children, points dialog, edit description) is a no-op for an archived active card — neither mutates state nor panics.

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] After an external `card archive` against the active card, the detail panel continues to render the card's title and description.
- [ ] The sidebar Parents and Children lists show the card's archive-time relationships.
- [ ] All mutation surfaces (priority popup, sprint-assign popup, points dialog, manage parents / children, edit title / description, `e` Metadata) are no-ops when the active card is archived — no state mutation, no panic, no silent-wrong-card edit.
- [ ] Restoring the card externally and reloading returns the detail view to its live appearance with no manual re-navigation.

### Risks

- **Mutation creep.** A consumer that today returns early on `model.card(id) = None` could be edited later to skip the strict guard. New tests pin the no-op behaviour at each surface.
- **Performance.** `get_card_by_id` performs two HashMap lookups per render frame instead of one. Negligible at TUI render rates with O(1) lookups, but worth noting under heavy archive populations.
- **Stale archive metadata.** If the in-memory model briefly disagrees with on-disk state during the file-watcher debounce window, the detail view may render the live card for ~500ms after an external archive. Acceptable transient.

### Out of scope

- Visual cues to communicate archived state (KAN-536).
- Editing or mutating archived cards from the detail view (rejected — strict mutation guard remains).
- CLI / MCP archived-card read paths (KAN-516).
- Edge-collection refactor (KAN-517).
- Restoring archived cards from the detail panel — separate UX question; for now restore stays in the archived-cards view.

### Key file references

- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:1455-1466` — `get_card_by_id`, `get_card_for_detail_view`
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/model.rs:31-51` — `Model::card`, `Model::archived_card`
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/card_detail.rs:65-167` — `render_card_detail`
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs:961-1135` — manage parents/children, sidebar accessors, navigation
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/popup_handlers.rs:41-595` — priority, sprint-assign, manage-relationship popups
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/dialog_handlers.rs:115-167` — points dialog
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:1788-2380` — `edit_card_field`, priority/sprint selection index helpers
cards.title: TUI: detail view stays usable when the active card is externally archived

cards.description:
## Background

Distribute `kanban` via Chocolatey, the de-facto package manager for Windows. Today Windows users have no first-class install path: they must download release binaries by hand from GitHub releases (and as of this writing, even that download isn't automated — see prereq subtask). Chocolatey covers the bulk of Windows package-manager users; winget complements it (separate future card, will be filed analogously to this one).

This card is the umbrella for all Chocolatey work, scoped narrowly to one registry. It supersedes the Chocolatey section of KAN-452 and is structurally analogous to KAN-465 (the Homebrew sibling).

## Deliverable shape

```powershell
choco install kanban
# or
choco install kanban --version=0.6.0
```

End state:

- A `kanban` package exists on community.chocolatey.org, owned by `fulsomenko` (or comparable username — see subtask on account/key).
- Each upstream release (driven by `release.yml`) auto-publishes the corresponding Chocolatey version. Moderation is asynchronous (1-7 days for the first version; usually well under 24h for subsequent versions).
- The Chocolatey package downloads the official Windows release archive from GitHub at install time and verifies it against an embedded SHA256.

## Approach summary

Chocolatey packages are NuGet packages wrapping PowerShell installer scripts. Unlike Homebrew (which builds from source), Chocolatey packages ship the precompiled binary URL + SHA, and the user's machine downloads and unpacks it at install time. This means we need:

1. A Windows release archive published to GitHub releases at a stable URL (prereq subtask).
2. A `.nuspec` metadata file plus PowerShell install/uninstall scripts (the package source).
3. CI that, on each upstream release, computes the SHA256 of the Windows archive, substitutes version + SHA into the package, runs `choco pack` and `choco push`.
4. A Chocolatey community account with an API key in a CI secret.
5. User-facing docs (README) and maintainer docs (CONTRIBUTING.md).

## Subtask breakdown

This card is the umbrella. Each piece below is its own child card:

1. **packaging/chocolatey/ skeleton** — author `kanban.nuspec` + `tools/*.ps1` + `LICENSE.txt` + `VERIFICATION.txt`.
2. **release.yml: Windows release archive** — build and upload `kanban-v$VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip` + `SHA256SUMS` on tag push. Also serves future winget work.
3. **Chocolatey community account + CHOCO_API_KEY secret** — register the account, reserve the package ID, generate the API key, add the repo secret.
4. **release.yml: Chocolatey publish step** — sed/replace substitution, `choco pack`, `choco push`, log post-publish verification URL.
5. **Docs: README + CONTRIBUTING** — Windows install path; maintainer playbook (file layout, release flow, secret rotation, local validation, troubleshooting).

Suggested execution order: (1) and (2) and (3) in parallel — they have no inter-dependencies. (4) depends on all three. (5) depends on (4) landing and producing a real, installable package.

## Maintenance burden expectations

- **Moderation lag**: first push typically reviewed within 1-7 days. Subsequent versions usually auto-pass once the maintainer relationship is established, but Chocolatey occasionally re-flags packages after policy changes.
- **Per-release work**: zero in the steady state. release.yml handles version+SHA substitution and push automatically; moderator approval is the only async piece, and that lands without intervention if the package is well-formed.
- **CVE / security**: if a CVE is filed against the published Windows binary or one of its dependencies, file a new release with the fix; Chocolatey moderation will typically expedite security-tagged updates.
- **Package ownership transfer**: out of scope for this card. If the package is ever to be transferred to another maintainer, that is a manual Chocolatey support ticket.
- **API key rotation**: out-of-band; track separately if/when the key is suspected of being leaked.

## Out of scope (separate cards if/when)

- **winget** — analogous registry, will be split out as a parallel sibling card filed separately. Reuses the Windows release archive from prereq subtask #2.
- **Self-publishing to a Chocolatey internal feed** — only relevant for enterprise distribution.
- **Bundling auxiliary tools** (e.g. shell completions, man pages) into the Chocolatey package — initial release ships only the `kanban.exe` binary + LICENSE.
- **Code signing the binary** — unsigned binaries trigger SmartScreen warnings on first launch but Chocolatey moderation does not require signing.
- **Polling for moderation approval** in CI — post-publish verification is intentionally manual; if friction grows, file a follow-up "verify chocolatey publish landed" card.

## Related

- **KAN-452** — superseded for the Chocolatey portion; this card is the new home for choco work.
- **KAN-465** — sibling, Homebrew equivalent. Mirror its CI patterns and structural style where applicable.
- **KAN-446** — AUR pipeline hardening; reference idioms (SHA256 computation, conditional gating on `has_changesets`) are reused.
cards.title: Publish to Chocolatey (community.chocolatey.org)

cards.description:
## Stakeholder summary — Where, Why, How

**Where.** TUI render layer. `crates/kanban-tui/src/components/card_list_item.rs` (per-row rendering), `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/card_detail.rs` (detail panel border and title block), `crates/kanban-tui/src/theme/colors.rs` and `crates/kanban-tui/src/theme/styles.rs` (accent colour and reusable border style), and every list consumer that constructs a `CardListItemConfig` (sprint task lists, card list view, archived-cards view).

**Why.** Once the detail view can render archived cards (sibling KAN-535), the user must be able to tell at a glance that the card on screen is archived. Without a visual signal, an externally archived card is indistinguishable from a live one — the user might attempt to edit, assign sprints, or manage relationships, all of which silently no-op against the strict mutation guard, producing a confusing dead-feeling UI.

The cue needs to surface on every list view (active board, sprint detail, archived-cards view itself) plus on the detail panel.

**How.** Add an `is_archived: bool` field to `CardListItemConfig` (driving an `[A] ` badge plus `Modifier::DIM` on rows). Extend the detail panel renderer to swap its border to the existing `deleted_view_focused_border` style and append `(ARCHIVED on YYYY-MM-DD)` to the title block when the active card is archived. Source the accent colour from the theme module so it tracks any user-configured palette.

---

## Engineering spec

### Current state

**Card list row** at `crates/kanban-tui/src/components/card_list_item.rs:9-122`:

```rust
pub struct CardListItemConfig<'a> {
    // ... no is_archived field
}

pub fn render_card_list_item(config: CardListItemConfig) -> Line<'static> {
    let is_done = config.card.status == CardStatus::Done;
    // ...
    if is_done {
        title_style = title_style.add_modifier(Modifier::CROSSED_OUT);
    }
    // ...
}
```

The only status-driven cue today is `CROSSED_OUT` for `Done`. Archive state is not communicated at all.

**Detail panel** at `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/card_detail.rs:65-167`: builds a `Block` with the default theme border. Title block renders the card's title verbatim, no archive marker.

**Archived-cards view panel border** at `crates/kanban-tui/src/render_strategy.rs:326-330,490-493` already uses `deleted_view_focused_border()` (defined at `crates/kanban-tui/src/theme/styles.rs:13`). This is the existing convention; the work below extends it to the per-card detail panel.

**Theme module** at `crates/kanban-tui/src/theme/colors.rs:1-32` defines `Color` constants (`FOCUSED_BORDER`, `DONE_TEXT`, `PRIORITY_*`, etc.). No `ARCHIVE_ACCENT` constant exists yet; `deleted_view_focused_border()` inlines its colour.

### Proposed solution

#### 1. Theme

Add to `theme/colors.rs`:

```rust
pub const ARCHIVE_ACCENT: Color = Color::Yellow;
```

Refactor `deleted_view_focused_border()` at `theme/styles.rs:13` to consume `ARCHIVE_ACCENT` instead of inlining the colour. Single source of truth.

#### 2. Card list row

Extend `CardListItemConfig`:

```rust
pub struct CardListItemConfig<'a> {
    // ... existing fields
    pub is_archived: bool,
}
```

In `render_card_list_item`:

```rust
let mut spans = vec![];
if config.is_archived {
    spans.push(Span::styled(
        "[A] ",
        Style::default().fg(theme::colors::ARCHIVE_ACCENT),
    ));
    title_style = title_style.add_modifier(Modifier::DIM);
}
// ... existing rendering with title_style now possibly DIM'd
```

Update every `CardListItemConfig::new(...)` construction site (active card list, sprint task lists, archived-cards-view list) to pass the correct `is_archived` value. Archived-cards-view always passes `true`; live lists pass `false`; once KAN-535 surfaces archived cards in mixed contexts, compute via `model.archived_card(card.id).is_some()`.

#### 3. Detail panel border

In `ui/card_detail.rs::render_card_detail`, after fetching the card:

```rust
let archived = app.active_card_is_archived();  // new helper, see KAN-535
let mut panel_config = PanelConfig::new(&title).with_focus_indicator(&title);
if archived {
    panel_config = panel_config.with_custom_border_style(deleted_view_focused_border());
}
```

This mirrors the archived-cards-view panel border at `render_strategy.rs:326-330` exactly.

#### 4. Detail title block

When archived, append the formatted archive date to the title row. The timestamp lives on `ArchivedCard::archived_at`, not on `Card`, so surface it via a new App helper:

```rust
pub fn active_card_archived_at(&self) -> Option<chrono::DateTime<chrono::Utc>> {
    let id = self.selection.active_card_id?;
    self.model
        .archived_cards()
        .iter()
        .find(|ac| ac.card.id == id)
        .map(|ac| ac.archived_at)
}
```

Title block in `card_detail.rs`:

```rust
let mut title_spans = vec![Span::raw(&card.title)];
if let Some(archived_at) = app.active_card_archived_at() {
    title_spans.push(Span::styled(
        format!(" (ARCHIVED on {})", archived_at.format("%Y-%m-%d")),
        Style::default().fg(theme::colors::ARCHIVE_ACCENT),
    ));
}
```

### Offender / surface table

| File:line | Surface | Today | Change |
|---|---|---|---|
| `theme/colors.rs:1-32` | Colour constants | No archive accent | Add `pub const ARCHIVE_ACCENT: Color = Color::Yellow;` |
| `theme/styles.rs:13` | `deleted_view_focused_border` | Inlines colour | Refactor to use `ARCHIVE_ACCENT` |
| `components/card_list_item.rs:9-21` | `CardListItemConfig` struct | No `is_archived` field | Add `pub is_archived: bool` |
| `components/card_list_item.rs:21-122` | `render_card_list_item` | No archive branch | Prepend `[A] ` span when `is_archived`; apply `Modifier::DIM` to title |
| `ui/card_detail.rs:65-95` | Detail panel `Block` + title | Default border; plain title | Swap to `deleted_view_focused_border()` when archived; append ` (ARCHIVED on YYYY-MM-DD)` to title in `ARCHIVE_ACCENT` |
| `app/mod.rs` (new helpers) | `App` API | None | Add `active_card_is_archived` and `active_card_archived_at` |
| `app/model.rs` | `Model::archived_card` returns `Option<&Card>` | Doesn't expose `archived_at` (lives on `ArchivedCard` wrapper) | Either add a sibling `archived_card_metadata(id) -> Option<&ArchivedCard>` or iterate `archived_cards()` from the App helper |
| `render_strategy.rs` + every `CardListItemConfig::new(...)` site | Construction sites | Don't pass `is_archived` | Pass `false` in live contexts; `true` in archived-view contexts; once KAN-535 mixes archived rows into live lists, compute via `model.archived_card(card.id).is_some()` |

### Alternatives considered

#### Badge style

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| `[A]` prefix + `Modifier::DIM` (proposed) | Compact; works in monochrome terminals; doesn't shift row layout | Cryptic to first-time users; competes visually with sprint markers |
| `Modifier::CROSSED_OUT` (strikethrough) | Conveys "removed" intuitively | Misleads — archived is not deleted; restore is normal. Already used for `Done` status, would collide for archived-Done cards |
| Unicode icon (📦 archive box) | Distinctive | Inconsistent font support; column alignment breakage in some terminals |
| Suffix ` (archived)` | Verbose but unambiguous | Eats horizontal real-estate; truncation hides the cue |

#### Border style

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| Solid orange via `deleted_view_focused_border()` (proposed) | Matches existing archived-list panel convention; one source of truth | Colour-only cue; no fallback for accessibility |
| Solid orange + dashed | Distinct from any current style | Ratatui has no `BorderType::Dashed` for box-drawing in all terminals; renders inconsistently |
| Solid orange + bold corners | Stronger visual weight | Ratatui has no per-corner styling; would need a custom block widget |

#### Title format

| Option | Pros | Cons |
|---|---|---|
| Suffix ` (ARCHIVED on YYYY-MM-DD)` (proposed) | Reads naturally; title stays prominent | Long suffix; can wrap on narrow terminals |
| Prefix `[ARCHIVED] ` | Banner-style; eye catches first | Title visually demoted behind the marker |
| Separate row above title | More space; bigger marker | Eats vertical space; complicates the existing layout in `card_detail.rs` |

### Tests

- `crates/kanban-tui/tests/card_detail_tests.rs`: render the detail panel for an archived card to a `TestBackend`; assert the border style equals `deleted_view_focused_border()` and the title row contains `"(ARCHIVED on "`.
- `crates/kanban-tui/tests/card_list_component.rs`: render `CardListItemConfig` with `is_archived = true`; assert the `[A] ` prefix is present and the title `Style` has `Modifier::DIM` set.
- Theme test: assert `deleted_view_focused_border()` derives its colour from `ARCHIVE_ACCENT` (single source of truth).

### Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Every list row that represents an archived card shows the `[A] ` prefix in `ARCHIVE_ACCENT` and renders the title with `Modifier::DIM`.
- [ ] The card detail panel's border uses `deleted_view_focused_border()` when the active card is archived.
- [ ] The card detail title row ends with ` (ARCHIVED on YYYY-MM-DD)` in `ARCHIVE_ACCENT`; the date comes from `ArchivedCard::archived_at`.
- [ ] `deleted_view_focused_border()` and the row badge both source their colour from `theme::colors::ARCHIVE_ACCENT` (single source of truth).
- [ ] Snapshot / render-to-string tests cover the badge, border, and title suffix.

### Dependency

Depends on KAN-535 landing first — without the lookup fix, the detail panel can't open an archived card, so border / title cues have nowhere to render. The badge work on list rows is independent and can ship first.

### Risks

- **Theme conflicts.** Orange may clash with user-configured palettes. Mitigation: source the colour from `theme::colors::ARCHIVE_ACCENT` so any theme override takes effect.
- **Accessibility / colour-blindness.** Colour-only cues fail. The `[A]` badge is the non-colour fallback; document it as the primary signal and colour as secondary.
- **TTY without colour.** `Modifier::DIM` may not render in pure TTY; the `[A]` badge remains visible regardless.
- **Snapshot test brittleness.** Render-to-string tests over visual styles are fragile if `Color` rendering varies across ratatui versions. Assert on text content (`[A]`, `(ARCHIVED on `) and `Modifier` flags rather than raw style bytes.
- **Status marker collision.** `Done` already applies `CROSSED_OUT`. An archived `Done` card would have both — verify they compose correctly and aren't visually noisy.

### Out of scope

- CLI / MCP archived-card visual cues (KAN-516).
- Editing or mutating archived cards from any UI (rejected — strict mutation guard).
- Restoring archived cards from the detail panel — separate UX question.
- Richer theming infrastructure (existing colour-constant model is the in-scope shape).

### Key file references

- `crates/kanban-tui/src/theme/colors.rs:1-32` — existing colour constants
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/theme/styles.rs:13` — `deleted_view_focused_border()`
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/components/card_list_item.rs:9-122` — `CardListItemConfig`, `render_card_list_item`
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/card_detail.rs:65-167` — detail panel renderer
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/render_strategy.rs:326-330,490-493` — existing archived-list border usage
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/model.rs:40-51` — `Model::archived_cards`, `Model::archived_card` (returns `Option<&Card>`; `archived_at` lives on the wrapping `ArchivedCard`)
cards.title: TUI: visual cues for archived cards — [A] badge, orange border, ARCHIVED suffix

cards.description:
## Background

Pushing to community.chocolatey.org requires (a) a community.chocolatey.org account, (b) an API key tied to that account, and (c) the API key configured as a CI secret in this GitHub repo. None of those exist today. This card stands up the credentials side so the release.yml Chocolatey publish step (separate subtask) can `choco push` without further setup.

This card is operational, not code. There is no PR; the deliverables are an account, a decided package ID, a key in a password manager, and a GitHub Actions secret. Acceptance is verified manually.

## Steps

### 1. Create the community.chocolatey.org account

- Sign up at `https://community.chocolatey.org/account/Register` with the project email (`max.blomstervall@gmail.com`).
- Choose username `fulsomenko` (matches GitHub org). If taken, fall back to `fulsomenko-kanban`. Username appears on the published package page as the maintainer.
- Confirm the email.

### 2. Reserve the package ID

Chocolatey IDs are global across the registry. Before doing CI work, sanity-check availability:

```
# from any machine with `choco` installed:
choco search kanban --by-id-only --exact
```

If `kanban` is taken by an unrelated project, fall back (in priority order):

1. `kanban-cli` — matches the workspace crate name and the install command.
2. `fulsomenko-kanban` — namespaced.

Pick once and update `<id>...</id>` in `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` (the packaging-skeleton subtask) accordingly. README and CONTRIBUTING.md install instructions (the docs subtask) must use the chosen ID.

As of 2026-05-25, the availability of `kanban` on community.chocolatey.org is unverified. **Confirm and decide** at the time this card is executed. Document the decision in this card before closing — that decision propagates to every other Chocolatey-related card.

### 3. Generate the API key

- Sign in, go to account settings, find the "API Key" section.
- Generate a fresh key. **Copy immediately** — it cannot be re-read once you navigate away.
- Stash in a password manager (1Password, Bitwarden) under an entry named "Chocolatey community API key — kanban".

### 4. Add as repo secret

GitHub Settings → Secrets and variables → Actions → New repository secret:

- Name: `CHOCO_API_KEY`
- Value: the generated key

Or via the `gh` CLI (preferred for reproducibility and audit trail):

```
gh secret set CHOCO_API_KEY --repo fulsomenko/kanban --body "$YOUR_KEY"
```

### 5. Smoke-test the key locally (optional, only if a Windows machine is handy)

```
choco apikey --api-key="$YOUR_KEY" --source="https://push.chocolatey.org/"
# Stores the key locally for `choco push`. Does NOT push anything.
```

If this errors, the key is wrong; regenerate. If it succeeds, no further action — the same key value is what release.yml will use.

## Security notes

- The API key has full publish authority for any package owned by the account. Treat it like a deploy token.
- Rotate the key after any suspected leak. Chocolatey supports key regeneration via the same UI; the old key revokes immediately on regeneration.
- The key is NOT scoped per-package on community.chocolatey.org (Chocolatey-for-Business has scoped tokens; community does not). Implication: do not share this account with Chocolatey package work outside this project — one leaked key would compromise every package the account owns.
- Add `CHOCO_API_KEY` to the repo's documented secrets list (CONTRIBUTING.md, secrets section). If no such section exists, defer the doc update to the docs subtask.

## Why this isn't bundled into the CI subtask

Two reasons:

1. **Sequencing**: the package ID decision in step 2 propagates to the nuspec, install scripts, and README. Locking the ID in early — before the CI work — prevents rework if `kanban` turns out to be taken.
2. **Operational vs code**: account registration / key handling sits outside the PR review cycle. Splitting it out keeps the CI subtask focused on diffable code and avoids "this PR is waiting on Max to click a button on a website."

## Acceptance

- [ ] community.chocolatey.org account exists for `fulsomenko` (or fallback username, documented here).
- [ ] Package ID decision logged in this card and reflected in `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` (skeleton subtask).
- [ ] API key generated, stashed in password manager.
- [ ] Repo secret `CHOCO_API_KEY` configured under `fulsomenko/kanban` with the key value (verifiable via `gh secret list --repo fulsomenko/kanban | grep CHOCO_API_KEY`).
- [ ] Local `choco apikey --api-key ...` smoke test succeeds (or, if no Windows machine accessible, defer to the CI subtask as the first real test — note the deferral here).

## Out of scope

- Any release.yml changes (separate subtask).
- Any nuspec / packaging files (separate subtask).
- Verifying the key actually pushes a package successfully — that happens in the CI subtask as part of the first real release.

## Related

- **Parent**: chocolatey umbrella card.
- **KAN-465** — sibling Homebrew card; the parallel is the `HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY` secret. Same lifecycle: provision once, rotate on suspected leak.

## Findings (2026-05-25)

### Local secret status

The API key already exists locally, sops-encrypted, at `~/secrets/chocolatey/api.yaml`:

- File present, contains a `sops:` metadata block with an age recipient and a PGP-fallback recipient (fingerprint `D14A78F2AAC59A1B3E1F0547044B046E5745CC2B`). Encrypted with sops 3.12.2.
- YAML shape (verified via the encrypted file's structure, not by decrypting): `chocolatey.api.key`.
- Companion NixOS module at `~/secrets/chocolatey/default.nix` declares `secrets.chocolatey.enable` and a `sops.secrets."chocolatey/api/key"` entry owned by `max`, sourced from `./api.yaml`.

**Deployment state: NOT DEPLOYED.** `/run/secrets/chocolatey/` does not exist on this host, and `/run/secrets/` itself is not user-readable (so no sibling secrets are visible either). The chocolatey nix module is not currently activated on this machine. Implication: there is no runtime plaintext path to copy from — decryption must be done explicitly via sops.

### Current GitHub secrets on fulsomenko/kanban

```
AUR_SSH_KEY                  2026-03-23
CARGO_REGISTRY_TOKEN         2025-10-12
DEPLOY_KEY                   2025-10-13
HOMEBREW_KANBAN_DEPLOY_KEY   2026-05-15
HOMEBREW_TAP_DEPLOY_KEY      2026-05-15
```

No `CHOCO_API_KEY` (or any chocolatey-related secret) is configured. This is the missing piece this card delivers.

### Package ID availability on community.chocolatey.org

Verified via the public OData feed (`/api/v2/Packages()?$filter=Id eq '<id>'`) and the package page HTML route (`/packages/<id>`):

- `kanban` — **AVAILABLE** (OData feed empty; package page 404). Sanity-checked against `git` (found) to confirm the query isn't broken.
- `kanban-cli` — available (also empty + 404), but not needed.
- `fulsomenko-kanban` — available (also empty + 404), but not needed.

**Decision: use `kanban`.** This propagates to `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` `<id>kanban</id>` and to README / CONTRIBUTING.md install instructions (`choco install kanban`).

### Setting CHOCO_API_KEY (commands for the user)

The runtime sops-decrypted path is not available on this machine, so the recommended one-liner is the direct sops-decrypt route. Run from any machine that has the age key required for `~/secrets/chocolatey/api.yaml`:

**Recommended (sops-decrypt, run by the user):**

```
sops -d ~/secrets/chocolatey/api.yaml | yq -r '.chocolatey.api.key' | gh secret set CHOCO_API_KEY --repo fulsomenko/kanban
```

`gh secret set` reads the value from stdin when `--body` is omitted, so the key never lands on disk or in shell history as plaintext.

**Fallback (only if the nix module ever gets activated and `/run/secrets/chocolatey/api/key` is readable as user `max`):**

```
sudo cat /run/secrets/chocolatey/api/key | gh secret set CHOCO_API_KEY --repo fulsomenko/kanban
```

After running, verify with:

```
gh secret list --repo fulsomenko/kanban | grep CHOCO_API_KEY
```

### What's still left for the user

1. Create the community.chocolatey.org account (step 1 above) if not already done, and generate the API key tied to it. If the value already at `~/secrets/chocolatey/api.yaml` came from a previous account, confirm the account is still active before relying on it.
2. Run the one-liner above to push the secret into GitHub Actions.
3. Tick the acceptance checkboxes once both are done.
cards.title: Provision Chocolatey community account + API key; add CHOCO_API_KEY repo secret

cards.description:
## Background

Once Chocolatey publish lands (the release.yml subtask), Windows users have a real install path: `choco install kanban`. This needs to surface in user-facing docs so Windows users actually find it, and the maintainer-facing CONTRIBUTING.md needs the operational details so future contributors can debug or take over the Chocolatey pipeline.

This card is pure docs. No code, no CI, no Chocolatey-side action. It depends on the release.yml publish subtask actually landing first — otherwise the install command in README would be a lie.

## Files to update

### `README.md`

Locate the installation section (currently has cargo, AUR, nixpkgs). Add a Windows subsection:

```markdown
### Windows (Chocolatey)

    choco install kanban

Requires [Chocolatey](https://chocolatey.org/install) on Windows 10/11
or Windows Server 2016+. The package wraps the official Windows release
binary from GitHub Releases; verify locally with:

    choco info kanban
```

Order in the install list: put Chocolatey above AUR (Arch is niche; Chocolatey is the broadest Windows path), but below cargo (the universal-truth install). Final ordering: cargo, Chocolatey, AUR, nixpkgs, Homebrew (when KAN-465 lands).

If a "All install paths at a glance" mini-table exists or is added by KAN-465, append the row:

```markdown
| Windows (Chocolatey) | `choco install kanban`                  |
```

Tone match: README sets a "short, scannable" tone that this section must respect — no marketing prose, no install-time troubleshooting (push those to CONTRIBUTING.md).

### `CONTRIBUTING.md`

Add a Chocolatey-specific subsection inside the Packaging section. If no "Packaging" section exists (i.e. KAN-465 hasn't added one yet), create it as a top-level section in CONTRIBUTING.md and add Chocolatey as its first subsection. The brew subsection will join it later.

Content:

```markdown
## Packaging — Chocolatey

The Chocolatey package source lives at `packaging/chocolatey/`.
The release pipeline publishes to community.chocolatey.org on every
release.

### File layout

- `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` — package metadata (templated).
- `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1` — downloads the
  Windows release ZIP from GitHub at install time, verifies SHA256.
- `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyuninstall.ps1` — reverses the
  install.
- `packaging/chocolatey/tools/LICENSE.txt` — placeholder; populated at
  pack time from `LICENSE.md` at repo root.
- `packaging/chocolatey/tools/VERIFICATION.txt` — required by
  Chocolatey moderation; explains how to verify the binary.

### Release flow

On each release (driven by changesets), `.github/workflows/release.yml`
runs the `publish-chocolatey` job:

1. Builds the Windows release ZIP (`build-windows` job).
2. Downloads the published ZIP from GitHub releases and computes its
   SHA256.
3. Stages a copy of `packaging/chocolatey/` under `build/chocolatey/`
   with `$version$` and `$checksum64$` substituted.
4. Runs `choco pack` then `choco push`, authenticated via the
   `CHOCO_API_KEY` repo secret.

Moderation is asynchronous (1-7 days for a first version, typically
under 24 hours subsequently). Status:

    https://community.chocolatey.org/packages/kanban/<VERSION>

### Required repo secret

| Secret           | Scope                                          | Where issued                                |
|------------------|------------------------------------------------|---------------------------------------------|
| `CHOCO_API_KEY`  | Push access to community.chocolatey.org/api/v2 | community.chocolatey.org account settings   |

To rotate: regenerate the key in the Chocolatey account UI; update the
repo secret. Old key revokes immediately on regeneration.

### Local validation

On a Windows machine:

    cd packaging/chocolatey
    # Manually substitute placeholders for a local pack:
    (Get-Content kanban.nuspec) -replace '\$version\$', '0.6.0' | Set-Content kanban.nuspec
    Copy-Item ..\..\LICENSE.md tools\LICENSE.txt
    choco pack
    choco install kanban -s . -y --force
    kanban --version

Revert the in-tree substitutions before committing.

### Troubleshooting

- `choco push` exits non-zero with "Package already exists": expected
  if re-running for the same version; bump the version in `Cargo.toml`.
- Moderator rejection arrives via the account email. Common causes:
  missing VERIFICATION.txt, missing LICENSE.txt at the *package* level
  (not just the project root), or `$ErrorActionPreference` not `Stop`
  in install scripts. All three are covered by the current packaging
  files; rejections most likely indicate a regression.
- Install fails on a fresh Windows machine with "Could not find
  kanban.exe": the archive layout changed between the published ZIP
  and what `chocolateyinstall.ps1` expects. Re-pack against the actual
  artifact layout.
```

If CONTRIBUTING.md already has a "Packaging" or "Distribution" top-level section (created earlier by KAN-446 or KAN-465), nest "Chocolatey" under it rather than creating a sibling top-level. Don't duplicate the "release flow" prose if it already covers AUR/brew — just add the Chocolatey-specific bits.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `README.md` has a "Windows (Chocolatey)" installation entry in the right position relative to other install paths.
- [ ] `CONTRIBUTING.md` has a Chocolatey packaging section covering file layout, release flow, secrets, local validation, and troubleshooting.
- [ ] Install command in README is verified live: `choco install kanban` on a Windows machine succeeds (or, if moderation has not yet approved, this card is marked as "pending moderation" and revisited).
- [ ] Cross-references: `README.md` Chocolatey section links to `https://chocolatey.org/install` for users new to Chocolatey itself.
- [ ] No prose conflicts: the new sections do not contradict adjacent sections (e.g. cargo install instructions still correct, no "coming soon" prose left over from when Chocolatey wasn't yet shipping).
- [ ] If KAN-465 lands before this card, the existing Homebrew docs structure is reused (no duplicated Packaging top-level section).

## Out of scope

- Marketing the Chocolatey package (announcements, blog posts).
- Adding a Chocolatey badge to README header.
- Translating docs.
- Updating any external (off-repo) documentation site.

## Related

- **Parent**: chocolatey umbrella card.
- **Depends on**: release.yml publish subtask landing so the install command is live.
- **KAN-465** — analogous docs work for Homebrew may have created the top-level packaging section in CONTRIBUTING.md; nest under it if present.
cards.title: Docs: README + CONTRIBUTING — Chocolatey install path and maintainer playbook

cards.description:
## Background

Architectural direction set 2026-05-25 during the chocolatey publish work: as additional package registries get added, **each registry gets its own dedicated GitHub repo**, triggered by `repository_dispatch` from this main repo's `release.yml`. This matches what Homebrew and Nix already do today by necessity or convention.

The decision is not blocking. The chocolatey work (KAN-537 and children) is being delivered in the main repo first under the current Pattern A, with the explicit understanding that it will be migrated out under this card when the dispatch infrastructure is set up.

## Precedent (current state of each registry)

| Registry | Current location | Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| Homebrew | `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap` (separate repo) | B (forced by `brew tap user/X` convention) |
| Nix flake | self-managed separately | B (forced by nixpkgs PR model) |
| AUR | inline in this repo's `aur/PKGBUILD` + `release.yml` steps | A (in-repo) |
| Chocolatey | being added in `packaging/chocolatey/` + `release.yml` jobs (KAN-537 chain) | A (in-repo, transitional) |
| winget (future) | not yet | B target — should be filed straight into a dedicated repo |
| Homebrew core (future) | not yet | special: PR to `Homebrew/homebrew-core`, not a fulsomenko repo |

## What "Pattern B" looks like

Per-registry repo, named `fulsomenko/<registry>-kanban` (matching the existing `homebrew-tap` precedent on convention).

Contents at repo root:
- The registry's packaging source files (nuspec, PKGBUILD, winget manifests, etc.).
- `.github/workflows/publish.yml`, triggered by:
  - `workflow_dispatch` (manual re-publish lever).
  - `repository_dispatch` with event type `kanban-release` (fired from main repo on release).

The main repo's `release.yml` ends with one dispatch step per registry. Each registry repo's workflow handles its own pack + push, downloading the binary artifact from the main repo's GitHub release.

```
fulsomenko/kanban (main)
  release.yml:
    - publish crates.io
    - tag, GH release
    - build-windows job (artifact upload — stays here, KAN-539)
    - dispatch → fulsomenko/chocolatey-kanban
    - dispatch → fulsomenko/aur-kanban
    - dispatch → fulsomenko/homebrew-tap (replaces inline brew bump)
    - dispatch → fulsomenko/winget-kanban (future)
```

## Migrations in scope under this card

| Registry | Source location to drain | New repo to create |
|---|---|---|
| Chocolatey | `packaging/chocolatey/` + `publish-chocolatey` job in `release.yml` | `fulsomenko/chocolatey-kanban` |
| AUR | `aur/PKGBUILD` + AUR-related steps in `release.yml` | `fulsomenko/aur-kanban` |
| Homebrew tap workflow | inline "Bump formula in homebrew-tap" step in `release.yml` | already `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap` — move the bump *logic* to a workflow in the tap repo, replace inline step with a dispatch |

The `build-windows` job (KAN-539) stays in the main repo. It produces the GitHub release artifact that all Pattern-B registry repos download. Building artifacts is "the project's release", not "a particular registry's package".

## Prerequisites

### Dispatch credentials

Need a way for the main repo's release.yml to trigger workflows in each registry repo. `GITHUB_TOKEN` cannot trigger workflows in another repo by default. Two options:

1. **Fine-grained PAT** — simpler. One PAT per target repo (or one PAT scoped to all of them). Set as secrets like `CHOCOLATEY_KANBAN_DISPATCH_TOKEN`. Faster to set up, harder to audit and rotate at scale.

2. **GitHub App** — better long-term. One App installed on the main repo and each registry repo, with `actions:write` scope. Revocable, scoped per-repo, observable in the App's audit log. ~30 min setup once. **Recommended given that we'll have ≥3 registry repos.**

### Repo conventions (apply to each new registry repo)

- Apache-2.0 `LICENSE.md` (copied verbatim from main repo).
- `README.md` pointing at main repo (analogous to the existing `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap` README).
- `CODEOWNERS` (Max).
- Minimal CI on PRs: `actionlint` for any workflows + registry-specific syntax check for the package source file (e.g. `xmllint` for nuspec, `namcap` for PKGBUILD, `winget validate` for winget).
- Branch protection on default branch matching main repo's conventions (signed commits if applicable, PR review required for non-trivial changes).

## Trade-offs (explicit)

**Pro Pattern B:**

- SRP at the repo level. Each registry's quirks are isolated; one registry's moderation rejection doesn't stall another.
- Independent versioning of packaging logic. A typo fix in `chocolateyinstall.ps1` doesn't bloat the main repo's git history.
- Easier to deprecate or hand off a single registry without surgery on the main repo.
- Matches what brew and nix already require, so onboarding "how do we publish?" has a single mental model rather than two ("brew/nix in their own repos, others inline").

**Con Pattern B:**

- More repos to maintain. Each registry repo needs branch protection, secrets, CI hygiene of its own.
- Cross-repo orchestration via dispatch tokens. One more piece of infrastructure to babysit; a broken token silently breaks publishing.
- Discoverability cost: "where does the choco package live?" → "the `chocolatey-kanban` repo." Mitigation: link from main README and CONTRIBUTING.
- Per-release CI cost goes up slightly (more parallel jobs across more repos) — negligible in practice.

## Acceptance (when this card is executed)

- [ ] Decision made between PAT and GitHub App for dispatch credentials. (Recommend App.)
- [ ] Empty `fulsomenko/chocolatey-kanban` and `fulsomenko/aur-kanban` repos exist on GitHub.
- [ ] Each has: LICENSE, README, CODEOWNERS, branch protection, minimal PR CI.
- [ ] Main repo's `release.yml` no longer has the `publish-chocolatey` job — replaced by a `repository_dispatch` step at the end of the `release` job.
- [ ] Main repo's AUR-related steps similarly extracted; only the dispatch step remains.
- [ ] Main repo's inline "Bump formula in homebrew-tap" step extracted into a workflow inside `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap` (triggered by dispatch); main repo just dispatches.
- [ ] `CHOCO_API_KEY` and `AUR_SSH_KEY` secrets move from main repo to their respective registry repos.
- [ ] At least one end-to-end release tag validates the new pipeline (all three registries publish successfully from a single main-repo release).
- [ ] Main repo's `packaging/chocolatey/` and `aur/` directories deleted; README install instructions still accurate.

## Not in scope (file separately)

- **The chocolatey publish work itself** — that is KAN-537 and its children, executing under Pattern A. This card is about migrating that work *out* later.
- **Adding new registries (winget, etc.)** — those should follow Pattern B from day 1; file separately and don't wait on this card.
- **Splitting the main repo's `release.yml`** further (e.g. extracting `build-windows` to its own workflow file). Tangential cleanup.
- **Homebrew-core submission** (KAN-465 Phase 2) — unrelated; uses a different mechanism (PR to upstream Homebrew/homebrew-core).

## Open questions to resolve before starting

- Naming convention: `fulsomenko/chocolatey-kanban` or `fulsomenko/kanban-chocolatey`? (`homebrew-tap` precedent says `<registry>-kanban`-ish but isn't perfectly analogous.) Pick a convention up front and apply uniformly.
- Do we want a single dispatch token covering all registry repos (one secret, simpler) or one per repo (more secrets, narrower blast radius)?
- Should the per-registry workflows be allowed to run on `workflow_dispatch` independently (manual re-publish of a specific version) — yes, recommend; comes for free.

## Related

- **Parent: KAN-452** — the umbrella for packaging restructure.
- **KAN-465 (Homebrew Phase 1)** — already Pattern B (`fulsomenko/homebrew-tap`). Reference for the dispatch-from-main-repo idiom, and for the README/CODEOWNERS/CI shape of a registry repo.
- **KAN-537 (Chocolatey umbrella)** — being delivered under Pattern A; this card represents the eventual migration out.
- **KAN-538 / KAN-541** — Pattern A files and CI job that will be moved out under this card.
- **KAN-539 (build-windows job)** — stays in main repo even after this card lands; produces the artifact every registry repo consumes.
- **KAN-446 (AUR pipeline hardening)** — current Pattern A AUR pipeline; subject to migration under this card.
cards.title: Extract per-registry packaging to dedicated repos (Pattern B)

cards.description:
## Background

KAN-538's `chocolateyinstall.ps1` relies on `Install-ChocolateyZipPackage` to unzip the Windows release archive into `$toolsDir`. Chocolatey then auto-shims every `.exe` it finds there. The behavior on a partial archive (e.g. archive layout drift where `kanban-mcp.exe` is missing) is silent: `choco install kanban` succeeds, `kanban --version` works, `kanban-mcp` resolves to "command not found" with no error linking it to the package.

The user-visible failure mode is "I installed kanban via choco and it half-works." Cause is far from effect; debugging requires the user to know that this package was supposed to ship two binaries.

This card adds a defensive presence check so the install fails loudly if either binary is missing.

## Two options for where the check lives

### Option A — In `chocolateyinstall.ps1` itself (runtime check on every install)

After the `Install-ChocolateyZipPackage` call:

```powershell
foreach ($exe in 'kanban.exe', 'kanban-mcp.exe') {
  $path = Join-Path $toolsDir $exe
  if (-not (Test-Path $path)) {
    $found = (Get-ChildItem $toolsDir -Filter *.exe | Select-Object -ExpandProperty Name) -join ', '
    throw "kanban Chocolatey package is missing $exe. Found in `$toolsDir: [$found]. Report at https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/issues."
  }
}
```

**Pros**: catches drift on every install (including end-user installs).
**Cons**: lives in `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1`; every choco user runs this code. Tiny perf cost (negligible).

### Option B — In KAN-541's smoke install step (CI-only)

In the `Smoke install from local nupkg` step in `.github/workflows/release.yml`, after the install + `--version` calls:

```powershell
foreach ($exe in 'kanban.exe', 'kanban-mcp.exe') {
  $found = Get-Command $exe -ErrorAction SilentlyContinue
  if (-not $found) {
    throw "$exe shim not found on PATH after choco install"
  }
}
```

**Pros**: catches drift before the bad package reaches users; doesn't bloat the install script every consumer runs.
**Cons**: only catches issues we cause in CI; a hypothetical "Chocolatey strips a file during moderation" wouldn't be caught.

### Recommendation

**Both**, but if you only have time for one, pick Option A. The CI smoke install already calls `kanban-mcp --version`, which implicitly checks the shim exists — Option B is largely redundant. Option A catches the failure case where the install script itself is wrong, on any consumer's machine, with a clear message pointing at the project.

## Scope

- Edit `packaging/chocolatey/tools/chocolateyinstall.ps1` to add the Option A check.
- Optional: also tighten KAN-541's smoke step with Option B (small belt-and-suspenders).
- Test locally if you have a Windows environment (or accept that the next real release will validate it).

## Acceptance

- [ ] `chocolateyinstall.ps1` contains a presence assertion for both binaries.
- [ ] The error message points at the project's GitHub issues URL.
- [ ] On the next real release, the smoke install passes (proof the check isn't over-eager).
- [ ] Manual test (optional): pack a deliberately broken package locally and confirm the install fails loud with the expected message.

## Out of scope

- Adding more substantive integration tests for the install behavior (run `kanban` against a test board, etc.) — out of scope; the Chocolatey package's job is to deliver the binaries, not test them.
- Validating archive layout in `build-windows` (KAN-539) — already partially covered by the `--version` smoke calls there.

## Related

- **KAN-537** — parent (Chocolatey umbrella).
- **KAN-538** — sibling, source of the install script being patched.
- **KAN-541** — sibling, where Option B would live.
cards.title: choco: defensive binary-presence check after Install-ChocolateyZipPackage

cards.description:
## Background

KAN-539's `build-windows` job (the first CI job that ships a *binary* — as opposed to source — to end users) uses `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable`, which floats to whatever Rust stable is on the day the workflow runs. Two consecutive releases of `kanban` could be compiled with different compiler versions; behavior could subtly differ between them.

For source-build registries (AUR `makepkg`, Homebrew `brew install`), this floats anyway — each user's machine has its own Rust version, and that's the established convention for those registries. Pinning there would force non-system toolchains, which violates packager expectations.

For binary release, pinning matters more. The binary in `releases/download/v$VERSION/kanban-v$VERSION-x86_64-pc-windows-msvc.zip` is *the* binary every Chocolatey user (and future winget user) installs. Compiler determinism is worth a small amount of toolchain-maintenance effort.

This card is scoped to **binary-release CI jobs only**. The PKGBUILD, brew formula, and nix flake stay as-is.

## Scope

### 1. Pin the toolchain in `build-windows`

In `.github/workflows/release.yml`:

```yaml
- uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@stable
  with:
    targets: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc
```

becomes:

```yaml
- uses: dtolnay/rust-toolchain@1.93.1     # current stable as of 2026-05-25
  with:
    targets: x86_64-pc-windows-msvc
```

(Read the actual current stable version from `rustc --version` in the dev shell before committing — the example here may have drifted by the time this card is picked up.)

### 2. Document the bump process

Add a short note to `CONTRIBUTING.md` (or wherever the packaging maintainer docs live):

```
### Bumping Rust toolchain for release builds

The `build-windows` job in release.yml pins to a specific Rust version
for binary-release reproducibility. AUR, Homebrew, and Nix builds use
distro-managed Rust and are unaffected.

To bump:
1. Update `dtolnay/rust-toolchain@X.Y.Z` in release.yml.
2. Run CI on a release candidate branch to confirm the workspace
   still compiles under the new version.
3. Mention the bump in the release notes if downstream Chocolatey
   users could be affected (rarely).

Cadence: bump every ~6 months (matches Rust's release schedule). Don't
chase every stable point release — only bump when there's a feature
or fix you need.
```

### 3. Apply the same pattern to any future binary-release jobs

Any future job that produces a binary attached to a GitHub release (e.g. macOS/Linux artifact uploads, future winget archive builds) should follow the same pin. Add a comment near the pin pointing at this card so the convention is discoverable.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `dtolnay/rust-toolchain` action in `build-windows` is pinned to a specific version.
- [ ] `CONTRIBUTING.md` (or equivalent) documents the bump process.
- [ ] Next release's binary is bit-similar to a re-run of the same source against the same pin (validation: re-trigger workflow on the same commit, compare uploaded artifact SHAs — note `Compress-Archive` is non-deterministic so the ZIP SHA may differ; check the inner `kanban.exe` SHA instead).

## Out of scope

- Pinning toolchain in `aur/PKGBUILD` — Arch users compile with system Rust by convention; forcing a specific version goes against Arch packaging norms.
- Pinning toolchain in `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap`'s formula — same convention argument for brew users.
- Adding `rust-toolchain.toml` at workspace root — would break AUR (`makepkg` doesn't use rustup) and brew (brew Rust formula doesn't manage other toolchains). Per-CI-job pinning is the right scope.
- SHA-pinning third-party Actions (separate card).

## Trade-offs (decided)

**Pro pinning:** binary-release reproducibility. Catches "compiler regression in stable" before users do.
**Con pinning:** manual bump cadence. If you skip bumps, you accumulate technical debt against newer stable.

Cadence ~6 months matches Rust's release schedule and avoids being a chore.

## Related

- **KAN-539** — sibling, source of the `build-windows` job being pinned.
- **KAN-537** — grandparent (Chocolatey umbrella).
- **KAN-543** — Pattern B migration; future per-registry repos that build binaries should inherit this same pinning convention.
cards.title: CI: pin Rust toolchain in build-windows and future binary-release jobs

cards.description:
## Background

The project landing page currently lives at `https://kanban.yoon.se`, deployed under the `yoon.se` umbrella domain. A dedicated TLD `kanban.rs` has been acquired (2026-05-25). Time to migrate.

This card covers:

1. The in-repo URL changes (small, mechanical).
2. The package-metadata `homepage` field updates (decision: point at kanban.rs vs the GitHub repo).
3. The operational migration (DNS, deployment, redirect).

## In-repo changes

### Must change

- **`web/default.nix:34`** — `homepage = "https://kanban.yoon.se";` → `homepage = "https://kanban.rs";`. This is the Nix metadata of the `kanban-web` derivation; canonical reference.

### Should change (canonical homepage decision)

The project currently has two competing "homepage" identities:

| File | Field | Current value |
|---|---|---|
| `Cargo.toml:22` | workspace `homepage` | `https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban` |
| `web/default.nix:34` | nix meta `homepage` | `https://kanban.yoon.se` |
| `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` | `<projectUrl>` | `https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban` |
| `aur/PKGBUILD` | `url` | `https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban` |
| `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap/Formula/kanban.rb` | `homepage` | `https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban` |
| `fulsomenko/nixpkgs/pkgs/by-name/ka/kanban/package.nix:32` | nix meta `homepage` | `https://kanban.yoon.se` (literal yoon.se reference) |

Now that `kanban.rs` exists as a stable landing page, it's the right canonical answer. Crates.io, chocolatey.org, AUR, and brew all surface the `homepage` / `projectUrl` field prominently — pointing at a hand-crafted landing page beats pointing at a code repo. The GitHub URL still belongs in fields like `repository`, `bugTrackerUrl`, `packageSourceUrl`.

Recommendation: update every `homepage`/`projectUrl` field to `https://kanban.rs`. Leave `repository`/`bugTracker`/etc. pointing at GitHub.

### Should also touch

- **`web/styles.css:3`** — comment currently reads `"Terminal-inspired dark theme, matching yoon.se design system"`. The design HERITAGE is yoon.se, but the page no longer brands itself as yoon.se. Update to something like `"Terminal-inspired dark theme for kanban.rs"`.
- **`README.md`** — add a link to `https://kanban.rs` somewhere prominent (currently has none; if a landing page exists, README should point at it).

### Leave alone (historical)

- **`kanban.json`** — contains a historical card description from the original landing-page build referencing `kanban.yoon.se`. That's history; don't rewrite.

## Cross-repo updates

The Homebrew tap formula lives in `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap` (separate repo). Its `homepage` field needs the same update — propagate as part of the next tap formula bump, or do it in a one-off commit on the tap repo.

## Operational migration (out-of-repo, but in-scope for this card)

1. **DNS**: configure `kanban.rs` to resolve to the same web host that currently serves `kanban.yoon.se` (or wherever the new deployment lands).
2. **TLS**: get a Let's Encrypt cert for `kanban.rs`.
3. **Deployment**: `nix build .#kanban-web && cp -r result/* /var/www/kanban.rs/` (or wherever the new vhost root is).
4. **Redirect**: configure `kanban.yoon.se` → `kanban.rs` (HTTP 301) until external backlinks die out. Keep the redirect for at least 6 months; ideally longer.
5. **Index update**: submit the new domain to Google Search Console / Bing Webmaster; submit a change-of-address from the old domain.

## Order of operations (recommended)

1. Land in-repo changes first (PR). Keep `web/default.nix` substitute pointing at `kanban.rs`.
2. Deploy the new build to `kanban.rs` host. Test.
3. Set up redirect from `kanban.yoon.se`. Verify.
4. Update Cargo.toml + AUR + brew + nuspec `homepage` fields in a follow-up PR (or same PR; small enough). These take effect on next release.
5. Decommission yoon.se vhost only after a few release cycles confirm nothing depends on it.

## Acceptance

- [ ] `kanban.rs` resolves with a valid TLS cert and serves the landing page.
- [ ] `kanban.yoon.se` 301-redirects to `kanban.rs`.
- [ ] `web/default.nix` `homepage` field points at `kanban.rs`.
- [ ] `web/styles.css` comment updated.
- [ ] `Cargo.toml` workspace `homepage` updated.
- [ ] `aur/PKGBUILD` and `aur/.SRCINFO` `url` (and `pkgdesc` if it mentions yoon.se) updated. AUR push happens on next release via existing CI.
- [ ] `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` `<projectUrl>` updated. Chocolatey publish picks up on next release.
- [ ] `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap/Formula/kanban.rb` `homepage` updated in the tap repo.
- [ ] `fulsomenko/nixpkgs/pkgs/by-name/ka/kanban/package.nix` `homepage` updated to `https://kanban.rs` in the user's nixpkgs fork, and submitted upstream as a PR to `NixOS/nixpkgs` along with the next version bump (combining the homepage update with the version bump avoids a noisy single-line PR).
- [ ] `README.md` has at least one prominent link to `kanban.rs`.
- [ ] On the next release: crates.io, chocolatey.org, AUR, and brew tap all show `https://kanban.rs` in their respective package pages.

## Out of scope

- **Migrating the design system itself away from yoon.se influence** — the current dark/terminal aesthetic is fine; this card only relocates and renames, not redesigns.
- **Building any new pages** (blog, docs, etc.) — the existing landing page moves wholesale.
- **Buying additional TLDs** — done.
- **Pattern B / per-registry repo extraction** (KAN-543) — orthogonal; if Pattern B lands first, the `homepage` updates land in the respective registry repos instead of here.

## Related

- **KAN-547** — `/master/` URL audit; this card is the same "audit and update embedded URLs" pattern but for a different target.
- **KAN-543** — Pattern B repo extraction; affects WHERE the `homepage` fields live but not the value.
- **KAN-465** — Homebrew tap; the formula in that repo needs its `homepage` updated as part of this card.
- **KAN-537** — Chocolatey package; nuspec `<projectUrl>` needs updating.
cards.title: Migrate landing page domain from kanban.yoon.se to kanban.rs

cards.description: The save error banner (KAN-174) clips long paths at the terminal edge. Truncate the message before formatting so the actionable error type is always visible.
cards.title: fix(tui): truncate long save error messages in banner to fit terminal width

cards.description:
## Background

Several packaging files hardcode `/master/` in URLs that point back at this repo. Examples surfaced during KAN-538 review:

- `packaging/chocolatey/kanban.nuspec` — `<licenseUrl>https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/blob/master/LICENSE.md</licenseUrl>` and `<packageSourceUrl>...master/packaging/chocolatey</packageSourceUrl>`.
- Other packaging files likely have similar references (PKGBUILD, future winget manifests, README install instructions, CI workflows that compute SHA against tarball URLs, etc.).

These URLs work today because the repo's default branch is `master`. If the default branch is ever renamed (e.g. to `main`, which is the GitHub default for new repos and increasingly expected), every hardcoded `/master/` URL silently:

- Returns 404 (Chocolatey moderation fetches `<licenseUrl>` automatically — could cause re-moderation rejection on next version bump).
- Or — worse — quietly serves stale content if a branch named `master` still exists but is no longer being updated.

No urgency, but worth doing once and being done. Also lowers cognitive overhead for "is this URL safe to change?"

## Scope

### 1. Audit

Grep the repo for hardcoded `/master/` and `/blob/master/` and `/tree/master/` references:

```sh
git grep -nE '/(master|main)/' \
  packaging/ aur/ .github/workflows/ \
  README.md CONTRIBUTING.md
```

Categorize each hit by what it's referencing and what should replace it:

| Hit type | Replace with |
|---|---|
| Released-asset URLs (`/releases/download/v$VERSION/...`) | OK as-is — tag-pinned, not branch-pinned. |
| Source URLs that should always point at HEAD of default branch | `HEAD` — GitHub resolves this to the repo's default branch dynamically. Survives rename. |
| Source URLs that should point at a specific tag (e.g. AUR PKGBUILD's tarball URL) | `v${pkgver}` — tag-pinned, fully reproducible. |
| Documentation links to "current source" (READMEs, CONTRIBUTING) | `HEAD` — same reason as above. |

### 2. Fix

Apply the replacements. Most hits become either tag-pinned (better — reproducible) or `HEAD`-resolved (also better — rename-safe).

### 3. Verify

- For nuspec / brew / AUR / winget URLs: confirm the substituted URL resolves with `curl -sI <url>` (or `gh api repos/fulsomenko/kanban/contents/<path>?ref=HEAD`).
- For documentation: visit the rendered README and click through.

### 4. Optional follow-up: enforce via CI

Add a `master-branch-audit` lint step that fails CI if `/master/` or `/main/` appears outside an allow-list:

```sh
git grep -nE '/(master|main)/' \
  packaging/ aur/ .github/workflows/ \
  | grep -vE 'master-branch-audit-allowlist:'
```

Out-of-scope for this card unless trivial; recommend filing as a separate small card if you want it.

## Acceptance

- [ ] Audit completed and results documented in this card's comments (or in a temp file commited as a one-off "audit-2026.md" if useful).
- [ ] Every actionable `/master/` hit is replaced with either a tag-pinned reference or `HEAD`.
- [ ] `xmllint` clean on any modified XML packaging files (e.g. `kanban.nuspec`).
- [ ] On `gh repo edit --default-branch <newname>` (hypothetical), all packaging-related URLs still resolve. Easiest validation: temporarily rename a fork's default branch and re-run a release; revert.

## Out of scope

- Actually renaming the default branch — that's a separate decision (and a meaningful one — affects PRs, CI, deploy targets, etc.).
- Enforcing the audit via CI (file separately if wanted).
- URLs that are intentionally branch-pinned for a specific reason (e.g. develop-branch documentation links from a workflow). Document those as exceptions.

## Why this matters now

Two registries (homebrew tap, AUR) already fetch `<licenseUrl>` or similar on automated moderation. A silent 404 there could cause re-moderation rejection on a future version bump. The cost of fixing all hardcodes proactively is low; the cost of debugging "why did this release fail?" weeks later is higher.

## Related

- **KAN-537** — siblings (KAN-538 nuspec is the most visible example).
- **KAN-452** — grandparent.
- **KAN-465** — Homebrew formula at `fulsomenko/homebrew-tap/Formula/kanban.rb` also uses `/blob/master/` URLs and would need the same treatment in that repo.
cards.title: Audit hardcoded /master/ branch URLs across packaging files

cards.description:
## Where

The kanban TUI uses an async save architecture: every mutation (create, edit, delete, move, assign) is applied to the in-memory model immediately, then a flush signal is queued for the background save worker to persist asynchronously. Since KAN-174, flush failures surface as a red banner — but the in-memory state is never rolled back. If the worker cannot write to disk (permission denied, disk full, locked filesystem, etc.), the user continues working against a model that has diverged from the file. On next startup the unwritten changes are silently gone.

## Why this matters

The on-disk file is the sole source of truth. In-memory state is a transient cache of it. Any durable divergence between the two is data loss. The current design guarantees that divergence whenever a save fails: the user sees changes that will not survive a restart.

## How the data flows (and where it breaks)

### Mutation path
1. User action (keypress) -> handler calls `App::execute_command` / `execute_commands_batch`
   - `app/mod.rs:1584` `execute_command`
   - `app/mod.rs:1612` `execute_commands_batch`
2. Delegates to `TuiContext::execute_commands_batch` (`tui_context.rs:43`)
3. Which calls `KanbanContext::execute` (`context.rs:191`) — applies command to in-memory backend, sets `dirty = true`
4. `SaveCoordinator::queue_flush` (`state/mod.rs:96`) sends a `()` signal on the bounded flush channel and increments `pending_saves`

### Save worker path
5. Worker wakes on signal (`app/mod.rs:394` `while rx.recv().await.is_some()`)
6. Calls `backend.flush()` (`json_backend.rs:324`)
   - Swaps `dirty` to false BEFORE the write
   - Calls `do_flush()`: serialises the in-memory snapshot, runs `AtomicWriter::write_atomic` (temp file + rename in same directory)
   - On I/O error: restores `dirty = true` and returns the error
7. Save worker receives `Err(e)`:
   - `app/mod.rs:437`: logs the error and sends it to `save_error_tx` (KAN-174)
   - Does NOT signal save completion — `pending_saves` stays > 0
   - **Does NOT reload or rollback** — in-memory model is ahead of disk from this point on

### What is missing
The error reaches the banner channel (`persistence.save_error_rx`) and `App::set_save_error` is called, but the code stops there. There is no call to `auto_reload_from_external_change` or any equivalent rollback.

The reload path already exists and is used on external file-change events:
- `app/mod.rs:2318` `auto_reload_from_external_change` — calls `ctx.reload()`, clears dirty, calls `prepare_frame`
- `context.rs:314` `KanbanContext::reload` — nulls the in-memory backend cache, clears undo stack, marks clean
- `json_backend.rs:335` `reload` — sets the inner `Option<JsonFileStore>` to `None` so next read triggers a fresh load

It just is not wired to the save-error path.

## Offenders — every mutation entry point

All 29 call sites in 6 handler files follow the same pattern: call `execute_command` or `execute_commands_batch`, then proceed. None of them guard against or recover from a pending save error. Representative sites:

- `card_handlers.rs:151` — toggle card completion
- `card_handlers.rs:380` — create card
- `board_handlers.rs:95` — create board
- `column_handlers.rs:109,165,238,291,387,519` — create / rename / delete / reorder columns
- `sprint_handlers.rs:62,113,210` — create / activate / complete sprint
- `popup_handlers.rs:76,132,212,274,294,373,398` — all popup-confirm mutations
- `dialog_handlers.rs:156,200,228,256,292,320,350` — all dialog-confirm mutations
- `detail_view_handlers.rs:255,409` — inline edit saves

## Proposed solution

On `save_error_rx` message receipt in the event loop (`app/mod.rs` tokio::select branch added by KAN-174), instead of only calling `set_save_error`, also call `auto_reload_from_external_change` (or an equivalent synchronous variant). This discards the in-memory delta and reloads from the last successfully persisted snapshot. The user sees their change disappear immediately rather than discovering data loss on next startup.

The banner (KAN-174) should remain after reload to explain why the change was lost. It should only clear when a subsequent save succeeds.

No changes are needed at the 29 call sites — the fix is entirely in the save-error branch of the event loop.
cards.title: fix(tui): reload from disk when save fails to keep in-memory state consistent with persistence

cards.description:
## What

Replace the `&'static str` `entity` parameter on the `KanbanError` resolver-error constructors with a typed `Entity` enum. Affects four constructors and the underlying `DomainError` variant fields:

- `KanbanError::not_found(entity: &str, id: Uuid)`
- `KanbanError::not_found_by_name(entity: &str, name, available)`
- `KanbanError::ambiguous(entity: &str, name, matches)`
- `KanbanError::batch_resolution_failed(entity: &str, failures)`

Internal field changes: `DomainError::NotFound.entity`, `NotFoundByName.entity`, `Ambiguous.entity`, `BatchResolutionFailed.entity`.

## Why

The entity name is currently a stringly-typed parameter at ~70 call sites. The compiler cannot help with:

- **Typos** — `KanbanError::not_found("borad", id)` compiles.
- **Inconsistent spelling** — `"card"` vs `"archived card"` vs `"archived_card"` are all valid strings; only one is the intended display form.
- **Discoverability** — nothing tells a contributor what the valid set is.

The drift is already real in the codebase. Live inconsistency between `not_found` (lowercase) and `not_found_by_name` / `batch_resolution_failed` (capitalised):

- `crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs:628` — `KanbanError::not_found("card", id)`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/operations.rs:233` — `KanbanError::not_found_by_name("Sprint", raw, available)`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/operations.rs:365` — `KanbanError::batch_resolution_failed("Card", failures)`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/error.rs:494` — `KanbanError::not_found_by_name("Card", "KAN-999", Vec::new())`
