| TUI external editor (`e` on Metadata) | `crates/kanban-domain/src/editable.rs:98` | **Strict RFC3339 — the bug** |

The first three are already correct. The fix in `editable.rs` brings
the fourth into line.

## Current buggy behaviour

**Serialization** (`editable.rs:67-74`):

```rust
fn from_entity(card: &Card) -> Self {
    Self {
        priority: format!(\"{:?}\", card.priority),
        status:   format!(\"{:?}\", card.status),
        points:   card.points,
        due_date: card.due_date.map(|dt| dt.to_rfc3339()),
    }
}
```

User opens the editor and sees:

```json
{
  \"priority\": \"High\",
  \"status\":   \"Todo\",
  \"points\":   null,
  \"due_date\": \"2024-01-15T14:30:00+00:00\"
}
```

If unset: `\"due_date\": null` with zero format hint.

**Deserialization** (`editable.rs:97-101`):

```rust
if let Some(due_date_str) = self.due_date {
    if let Ok(dt) = chrono::DateTime::parse_from_rfc3339(&due_date_str) {
        card.due_date = Some(dt.with_timezone(&chrono::Utc));
    }
}
```

`parse_from_rfc3339` rejects `\"2024-01-15\"` because the time and
timezone are missing. The inner `if let Ok` is skipped and the field
is left untouched. `Editable::apply_to(self, &mut T)` returns `()` so
the failure cannot propagate to the command result at
`commands/card_commands.rs:622`. The card is upserted with the prior
due_date and the user sees no banner, no log line.

## Proposed approach

### Part 1 — extract a shared lenient parser

Add `parse_date_input` to `kanban-domain` (or `kanban-core` if a
chrono dependency there is undesirable). The CLI and MCP call sites
all implement the same body:

```rust
chrono::DateTime::parse_from_rfc3339(s)
    .map(|dt| dt.with_timezone(&Utc))
    .or_else(|_| {
        chrono::NaiveDate::parse_from_str(s, \"%Y-%m-%d\")
            .map(|d| Utc.from_utc_datetime(&d.and_hms_opt(0, 0, 0).unwrap()))
            // …error mapping…
    })
```

Lift it to a helper:

```rust
pub fn parse_date_input(input: &str) -> Result<DateTime<Utc>, String> {
    let s = input.trim();
    if let Ok(dt) = DateTime::parse_from_rfc3339(s) {
        return Ok(dt.with_timezone(&Utc));
    }
    if let Ok(d) = NaiveDate::parse_from_str(s, \"%Y-%m-%d\") {
        return Ok(Utc.from_utc_datetime(&d.and_hms_opt(0, 0, 0).unwrap()));
    }
    Err(format!(
        \"'{input}' is not a recognised date (expected YYYY-MM-DD or RFC3339)\"
    ))
}
```

Replace all four call sites:
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/card.rs:411-417` — call helper.
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/sprint.rs:7-13` — call helper.
- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:169-174` — call helper.
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/editable.rs:97-101` — call helper (THIS
  is what fixes the user-reported bug).

### Part 2 — stop swallowing parse errors in the editor flow

The `Editable` trait signature is `fn apply_to(self, entity: &mut T)`
— no Result. Two options:

**Option A (minimal):** Add a `tracing::warn!(\"due_date parse failed: \
{err}\")` at the editor parse site, so failures at minimum appear in
the log. Doesn't surface to TUI users but stops the silent drop in
the literal sense.

**Option B (better UX):** Have `CardMetadataDto::apply_to` collect
parse errors into a Vec on the DTO (or change return type with a
new sibling method `apply_to_result(self, &mut Card) -> Vec<String>`).
The TUI handler at
`crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs:235-280` then
checks the returned errors and pushes an entry into the existing
error-banner queue (see `crates/kanban-tui/src/state/`).

**Recommend Option B.** Option A is acceptable as a follow-up if
Option B blows the scope.

### Part 3 (optional polish) — round-trip-friendly default

When `from_entity` serializes a `due_date` whose time component is
exactly midnight UTC, write it as `\"2024-01-15\"` instead of
`\"2024-01-15T00:00:00+00:00\"`. Pros: the round-trip is lossless,
the editor shows the format the user is likely to type, and the
schema becomes self-documenting. Cons: a subsequent edit that wants
a specific time forces the user to write the longer form.

Track separately if Part 3 is descoped.

### Part 4 (optional polish) — format hint in the temp file

Either prepend a JSONC comment (`// due_date: YYYY-MM-DD or RFC3339`)
or write a top-level `\"_format\": \"...\"` key. The JSONC route is
more visible but requires serde_json to allow comments on read.
Defer to a follow-up — the user-reported bug is the silent drop, not
the missing hint.

## Affected files

Mandatory:
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/editable.rs` — replace 97-101 with helper
  call; collect error per Part 2.
- New helper in `crates/kanban-domain/src/lib.rs` (or a new
  `date_input.rs` module).
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/card.rs:408-417` — call helper.
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/sprint.rs:5-13` — call helper.
- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:166-175` — call helper.

If Part 2 (Option B):
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/detail_view_handlers.rs:235-280` —
  inspect DTO errors, push banner.
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/state/` — banner queue is already there.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] Editing a card via `e` on the Metadata section and saving
      `\"due_date\": \"2024-01-15\"` updates due_date to
      `2024-01-15T00:00:00Z`.
- [ ] Editing and saving `\"due_date\": \"2024-01-15T14:30:00+00:00\"`
      continues to work unchanged (RFC3339 round-trip preserved).
- [ ] Editing and saving a malformed `due_date` (e.g.
      `\"due_date\": \"yesterday\"`) does NOT silently discard the
      edit; the user sees either an error banner (Option B) or, at
      minimum, a `tracing::warn!` line in the log (Option A).
- [ ] CLI `kanban card create --due-date 2024-01-15` continues to work
      (regression test for the helper extraction).
- [ ] CLI `kanban card create --due-date 2024-01-15T14:30:00Z`
      continues to work.
- [ ] MCP `create_card` and `update_card` accept both formats
      (regression test).
- [ ] Inline unit tests on the new helper cover:
  - YYYY-MM-DD → midnight UTC
  - Full RFC3339 → preserved DateTime<Utc>
  - Whitespace tolerance (`\" 2024-01-15 \"`)
  - Garbage input → Err with a useful message
- [ ] `cargo test --workspace`, `cargo clippy --all-targets --all-features
      -- -D warnings`, `cargo fmt --all --check` are clean.

## Out of scope

- Rewriting the `Editable` trait to return `KanbanResult<()>` across
  all three implementations. Track as a follow-up if Option B lands
  but reveals further DTO-error needs.
- Accepting other natural date forms (US `MM/DD/YYYY`, UK
  `DD/MM/YYYY`, relative offsets like `+3d`). Bring up only if users
  ask.
- BoardSettingsDto / AppConfigDto changes — issue is specific to
  card due_date.
- JSONC comment hints in the temp file (Part 4).

## Sources

- GitHub issue #312:
  https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/issues/312
- `chrono::NaiveDate::parse_from_str` docs:
  https://docs.rs/chrono/latest/chrono/naive/struct.NaiveDate.html#method.parse_from_str
- Existing CLI lenient parser:
  `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/card.rs:411-417`
- Existing MCP lenient parser + schema:
  `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:169-174` (parser),
  `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:514` (schema description already
  promises both formats).
cards.title: fix(domain): accept YYYY-MM-DD in external-editor due_date field

cards.description:
### Status

**Shipped** on branch `KAN-644/add-due-date-sort-field-for-cards` (PR #315).

The PR went further than this card originally scoped. The due-date variant landed as planned, but the Phase 2 "push sorting into the service" goal also picked up filter resolution — `CardListFilter` now carries the TUI's sprint multi-select, hide-assigned, and full-text search filters, and a pure `kanban_domain::filter_and_sort_cards` is the single source of truth for "given a `CardListFilter`, which cards belong in the result, in what order". `KanbanContext::list_cards`, `KanbanContext::list_cards_full` (new), `CardQueryBuilder::execute`, and the TUI `App::get_sorted_board_cards` all delegate to it. The duplicated 5-arm resolution match between `KanbanContext::apply_sort` and `KanbanOperations::list_archived_cards_sorted` was collapsed into a shared `resolve_sort` helper.

The card's prediction "drop the TUI's client-side sort" is now actually true: the TUI no longer owns filter or sort logic; the layout strategies still use `CardQueryBuilder` for the typed builder API but its body is one line.

---

### Overview

**Where**: Card sorting lives in `kanban-domain::sort` and is driven by `Board.task_sort_field: SortField` in `kanban-domain::board`. Today the TUI sorts client-side via `get_sorter_for_field`; `KanbanContext::list_cards` returns storage order, so CLI and MCP cannot sort at all. The CLI's `kanban relation parents/children --sort` is the only existing sort surface outside the TUI.
**Why**: Users can sort by every time-ish field on `Card` (`CreatedAt`, `UpdatedAt`) but not by `due_date`, the field they actually plan around. There is no way to answer "what is due next" in any frontend.
**How**: Add `DueDate` to `SortField` and `SortBy` with `Option<DateTime<Utc>>` ordering (undated cards last, matching `Points`). **Then push sorting into `KanbanContext::list_cards`** so all three frontends inherit it: TUI drops its client-side sort, CLI and MCP just plumb optional overrides through. Close two existing gaps (CLI `card list` doesn't sort, MCP exposes no sort surface at all) by adding flags and tool params that map to a new `SortOverride`.

---

### Engineering detail — domain core

~~~
crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs:12       — enum SortField: +DueDate
crates/kanban-domain/src/sort/mod.rs:14    — enum SortBy: +DueDate
crates/kanban-domain/src/sort/mod.rs:25    — SortBy::compare: +Self::DueDate arm (None-last, mirrors Points)
crates/kanban-domain/src/sort/mod.rs:82    — get_sorter_for_field: +SortField::DueDate => SortBy::DueDate
crates/kanban-domain/src/sort/mod.rs:280   — exhaustive tiebreaker test: include SortBy::DueDate
~~~

`Card.due_date: Option<DateTime<Utc>>` already at `crates/kanban-domain/src/card.rs:67`. Suggested compare arm:

~~~rust
Self::DueDate => match (a.due_date, b.due_date) {
    (Some(ad), Some(bd)) => ad.cmp(&bd),
    (Some(_), None)      => Ordering::Less,
    (None, Some(_))      => Ordering::Greater,
    (None, None)         => Ordering::Equal,
},
~~~

---

### Engineering detail — service (the architectural shift)

**Push sorting into the service layer** so all frontends consume sorted output rather than each re-implementing it.

~~~
crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs:840   — list_cards: accept Option<SortOverride>, apply sorter
crates/kanban-service/src/context.rs:??    — list_archived_cards: same
crates/kanban-service/src/sort.rs (new)    — resolve_and_sort(cards, override, board) helper
~~~

`SortOverride { field: SortField, order: SortOrder }`. Resolution: `override.unwrap_or((board.task_sort_field, board.task_sort_order))` → `get_sorter_for_field` → `OrderedSorter::sort_by`.

**SOLID rationale (Dependency Inversion + Single Responsibility)**: today the TUI knows how to translate `SortField → SortBy → OrderedSorter`; the CLI and MCP would otherwise grow the same knowledge. Moving it behind a service helper means frontends depend on an abstraction (`list_cards(... , Option<SortOverride>)`) and sorting has one owner.

**Caller audit required** before changing the contract — any caller relying on storage order must be flagged. Quick grep over `KanbanContext::list_cards` and `list_archived_cards` consumers.

---

### Engineering detail — TUI

**Plumbing for the popup** (six index sites — append `DueDate` at the end of the popup array to minimise churn):

~~~
crates/kanban-tui/src/components/selection_dialog.rs:122  — options_count: 7 → 8
crates/kanban-tui/src/components/selection_dialog.rs:130  — sort_fields array: append SortField::DueDate
crates/kanban-tui/src/components/selection_dialog.rs:149  — field_name match: +SortField::DueDate => "Due Date"
crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/popup_handlers.rs:159      — next(7) → next(8)
crates/kanban-tui/src/handlers/popup_handlers.rs:168      — index → SortField match: +new arm
crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:2441                     — get_current_sort_field_selection_index: +SortField::DueDate arm
~~~

**Drop client-side sorting** once the service does it:

~~~
crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:1439                — remove client-side get_sorter_for_field call
crates/kanban-domain/src/query/mod.rs:112            — review: still needed? if only TUI calls it, remove
~~~

Render code just uses the order `list_cards` returns. No keybinding/help-text changes (descriptions are generic). Snapshot/state at `crates/kanban-tui/src/state/snapshot.rs:33` reads `board.task_sort_field` generically — unchanged.

A SSoT refactor — replace the index↔variant fan-out with a single `const SORT_FIELD_POPUP_ORDER: &[(SortField, &str)]` — is worthwhile as a follow-up commit (Open/Closed: future variants only edit the slice).

---

### Engineering detail — CLI

Three surfaces, all small now that the service sorts:

**(a) `kanban relation parents/children --sort`** — existing:

~~~
crates/kanban-cli/src/cli.rs:268  — enum SortKey: +DueDate
crates/kanban-cli/src/cli.rs:285  — SortKey::to_sort_by: +arm
~~~

**(b) Gap — `kanban card list` does not surface sort.** With service-layer sorting in place, the handler already gets sorted output. Add `--sort <SortKey>` and `--order <SortDir>` flags that build an `Option<SortOverride>` and pass through — no `OrderedSorter` wiring in the CLI itself. Apply the same flags to any sibling listing command (`flatten` via clap `SortArgs` struct if more than one — Interface Segregation: each subcommand opts in when it lists cards).

**(c) Gap — no `board update --sort-field` command.** Add `kanban board update --sort-field <SortKey> --sort-order <SortDir>` mapping to `BoardUpdate.task_sort_field` / `task_sort_order` (domain support already at `crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs:411`).

---

### Engineering detail — MCP

Same shape as the CLI thanks to service-layer sorting — handlers just plumb optional overrides.

~~~
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:136-166  — +parse_sort_field helper alongside parse_priority/parse_status
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:355      — UpdateBoardRequest: +task_sort_field, task_sort_order
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:803      — tool_update_board description: extend property list
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:810      — tool_update_board: map request fields onto BoardUpdate
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:455      — ListCardsRequest: +optional sort, order
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:475      — ListArchivedCardsRequest: +optional sort, order
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:976      — tool_list_cards: build SortOverride, pass to list_cards
crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:1151     — tool_list_archived_cards: same
~~~

No `OrderedSorter` calls in MCP — the service does the work.

---

### Engineering detail — persistence (no work needed)

`SortField` derives `Serialize`/`Deserialize`; everything round-trips generically:
- **JSON** (`crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/json_file_store.rs:218`) — derived serde, no migration touches `task_sort_field`.
- **SQLite** (`crates/kanban-persistence-sqlite/src/sqlite_store.rs:64-80`) — generic `p_enum`/`ser_enum`; `schema.sql:22` is `TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'Default'` with no `CHECK`.
- **Export/import** (`crates/kanban-domain/src/export/importer.rs:175`) — derived serde.
- **Board commands** (`crates/kanban-domain/src/commands/board_commands.rs:190,252`) — generic `Option<SortField>` field.

Hardcoded fixtures (`crates/kanban-service/tests/migrate.rs:105,152`) use `"Default"` and don't enumerate variants — no update needed.

---

### Implementation phases (TDD per step, commits split test from feat)

**Phase 1 — Domain.** Add variant, ordering, mapping, exhaustive-test entry. Self-contained; shippable alone.

**Phase 2 — Service.** Audit `list_cards` callers; extend `list_cards` and `list_archived_cards` to accept `Option<SortOverride>` and default to `board.task_sort_field`; extract `service::sort::resolve_and_sort` helper; **drop the TUI's client-side sort** (single source of truth).

**Phase 3 — TUI popup.** Six index sites + optional SSoT refactor so users can pick "Due Date" in the popup.

**Phase 4 — CLI.** `SortKey::DueDate`, `card list --sort/--order` flags, `board update --sort-field/--sort-order` command.

**Phase 5 — MCP.** `parse_sort_field`, expose sort fields in `UpdateBoardRequest`, accept `sort`/`order` on `ListCardsRequest`/`ListArchivedCardsRequest`.

Dependency: Phase 1 blocks all; Phase 2 blocks Phases 3/4/5; Phases 3–5 are independent of each other.

---

### Considerations

- **Service contract change**: `list_cards` returning storage order today is the most load-bearing risk. Caller audit is the gate for Phase 2.
- **None-handling**: undated cards last in ascending (mirrors `Points`). Worth a one-line note in the help/MCP description.
- **Index-mapping fragility**: append `DueDate` at the end of the popup array (even if enum-declaration order differs) to minimise churn. Long-term SSoT fix turns six match arms into a slice.
- **Forward compatibility**: serde JSON enum representation means old binaries reading a board with `DueDate` fail to deserialize. Acceptable (single user, single binary).
- **Tiebreaker**: `OrderedSorter` already breaks ties on `card_number` — no extra work for equal due dates.
- **Tests**: add `test_due_date_sorter_none_handling` mirroring `test_points_sorter_none_handling`, plus the exhaustive-tiebreaker entry, plus service-layer integration tests for board-default vs override.

**Pros**: Single source of truth for sorting. CLI and MCP get sort "for free" once Phase 2 lands. Closes long-standing gaps without per-frontend duplication. Reuses every existing primitive (`OrderedSorter`, `card_number` tiebreaker, derived serde, `BoardUpdate`).
**Cons**: `list_cards` contract change requires a caller audit and a coordinated TUI cleanup. Six TUI index sites still fan out until the SSoT refactor lands.

---

### Alternative solutions

**1. Keep sorting client-side, duplicate across CLI/MCP.** Smaller blast radius, no service contract change, but every new sort field then needs three implementations and the CLI/MCP gaps stay open longer. Rejected: the duplication cost compounds with each future sort feature.

**2. Secondary sort layered on top of existing sort.** Treat due date as a tiebreaker before `card_number`. Rejected: users asking for due-date sort want it primary, and it would break the existing deterministic-tiebreaker contract.

**3. Refactor `SortField`/`SortBy` into a trait-object registry first.** Eliminates the fan-out entirely but is a much bigger refactor than this feature warrants. Track separately if the variant list keeps growing.
cards.title: add due-date sort field for cards

cards.description:
### Overview

**Where**: `crates/kanban-domain/src/sort/mod.rs` owns the entire sort mechanism (`SortBy`, `OrderedSorter`, the tiebreaker, `SortOrder` indirectly via `board.rs`). Domain crates and frontends consume it only for `Card`; every other listable entity (`Board`, `Column`, `Sprint`, `ArchivedCard`, dependency-graph parents/children, edges) ships hand-coded `Vec::sort_by` / `sort_by_key` calls scattered across the codebase.
**Why**: Sorting is duplicated and entity-specific today. A new sort surface (sprint sort, edge sort, cross-entity composition like "spawned-children-by-due-date") forces a new ad-hoc match block at every site. The mechanism itself (ascending/descending wrapping + deterministic tiebreaker + None-last handling) has nothing card-specific in it and belongs in `kanban-core`.
**How**: Lift the generic mechanism (a `Sorter<T>` trait, an `OrderedSorter<S, T>` wrapper that composes a `Sorter` with a `SortOrder` and a tiebreaker `Fn(&T, &T) -> Ordering`, plus the `SortOrder` enum itself) into `kanban-core::sort`. Domain crates then provide entity-specific `Sorter<T>` impls (`CardSortBy: Sorter<Card>`, `SprintSortBy: Sorter<Sprint>`, etc.) plus the `SortField` enums that pick which `Sorter` to dispatch to.

---

### Engineering detail

**Datapath today** (Card-only):
1. Caller picks a `SortField` (domain enum at `crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs:12`).
2. `get_sorter_for_field(field)` at `sort/mod.rs:88` maps `SortField → SortBy` (an enum of card-specific variants).
3. `OrderedSorter::new(SortBy, SortOrder)` wraps it.
4. `OrderedSorter::sort_by::<T: Borrow<Card>>(&mut [T])` at `sort/mod.rs:76` does the actual sort with a hardcoded `card_number` tiebreaker.

The mechanism is fused to `Card`. Every step assumes `Card` in its type — `SortBy::compare(&Card, &Card)`, `Borrow<Card>` on the input slice, `a.card_number.cmp(&b.card_number)` for the tiebreaker.

**Where ad-hoc per-entity sorts live** (each is a place that would benefit from a unified mechanism):

```
crates/kanban-domain/src/in_memory_store.rs:124,155,162,194,209,221,366,373,422,425,428,431,434
  — boards.sort_by_key(|b| b.position)
  — columns.sort_by_key(|c| c.position)
  — cards.sort_by_key(|c| c.position)
  — archived_cards.sort_by(|a, b| a.archived_at.cmp(&b.archived_at))
  — sprints.sort_by_key(|s| s.sprint_number)
crates/kanban-domain/src/sprint.rs:179,180
  — sprints.sort_by(|a, b| b.sprint_number.cmp(&a.sprint_number))   // descending
crates/kanban-domain/src/card_lifecycle.rs:76,274
  — cols.sort_by_key(|c| c.position)
  — indices.sort_by_key(|&i| cards[i].position)
crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/relation.rs:162,170
  — sort_and_summarize(cards, sort, order)   // parents/children of a card; only sortable today because the
                                              // intermediate type is Card, not the edge itself
```

None of these go through `OrderedSorter`; each re-implements the comparison inline. None of them have a deterministic tiebreaker, so they inherit whatever order the storage backend hands out for equal keys (same drift problem the existing `OrderedSorter` was built to fix for `Card`).

**`SortOrder` is in the wrong crate.** `crates/kanban-domain/src/board.rs:22-26`:

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq, Serialize, Deserialize)]
pub enum SortOrder {
    Ascending,
    Descending,
}
```

This has no domain content. It's literally `enum { Asc, Desc }`. Lives in domain only because that's where the rest of the sort code lives.

**Suggested solution** (proposed shape, not final):

`kanban-core::sort`:

```rust
pub enum SortOrder { Ascending, Descending }

/// Pure mechanism: compare two values of T. No knowledge of what T is.
pub trait Sorter<T> {
    fn compare(&self, a: &T, b: &T) -> Ordering;
}

/// Wraps a Sorter with direction + a deterministic tiebreaker.
/// The tiebreaker stays ascending under descending primary so direction
/// toggles do not reshuffle tied items.
pub struct OrderedSorter<S, T, B>
where
    S: Sorter<T>,
    B: Fn(&T, &T) -> Ordering,
{
    sorter: S,
    order: SortOrder,
    tiebreaker: B,
}

impl<S, T, B> OrderedSorter<S, T, B>
where
    S: Sorter<T>,
    B: Fn(&T, &T) -> Ordering,
{
    pub fn new(sorter: S, order: SortOrder, tiebreaker: B) -> Self { ... }

    pub fn sort_by<U: Borrow<T>>(&self, items: &mut [U]) { ... }
}

/// Convenience: tiebreaker that compares ids for entities with stable identity.
pub fn id_tiebreaker<T, F: Fn(&T) -> Uuid>(get_id: F) -> impl Fn(&T, &T) -> Ordering { ... }
```

`kanban-domain::sort` shrinks to entity-specific dispatch:

```rust
pub enum CardSortField { Points, Priority, CreatedAt, UpdatedAt, DueDate, Status, Position, Default }
pub enum CardSortBy   { Points, Priority, CreatedAt, UpdatedAt, DueDate, Status, CardNumber, Position }

impl Sorter<Card> for CardSortBy { fn compare(...) -> Ordering { ... } }

pub fn card_sorter_for_field(f: CardSortField) -> CardSortBy { ... }

// New, parallel, opt-in:
pub enum SprintSortField { SprintNumber, StartDate, EndDate, Status, ... }
impl Sorter<Sprint> for SprintSortBy { ... }
```

Edges compose naturally: an edge sorter can wrap a `Sorter<Card>` plus a `Fn(&Edge) -> &Card` projection, so "sort spawns-edges by the child card's due date" is a one-liner without any new `EdgeSortField` enum.

---

### Considerations

- **Migration scope is wide but mechanical.** The 14+ ad-hoc `sort_by_key` sites in `in_memory_store.rs`, `sprint.rs`, `card_lifecycle.rs` don't *have* to switch — they're internal storage-order conveniences, not user-facing sort surfaces. The refactor should focus on the user-facing sort surfaces (CardListFilter, ArchivedCardListFilter, CLI `relation parents/children`, the future sprint/edge listings) and leave the internal storage sorts alone unless they happen to overlap.
- **`OrderedSorter::sort_by` today returns nothing; new design must preserve in-place semantics** so callers don't allocate a new Vec.
- **Tiebreaker per-entity, not per-call.** The existing `card_number` tiebreaker assumes single-board slices. Generalising forces every entity to declare its own stable-identity comparison. `id_tiebreaker(|c| c.id)` is the lazy-but-correct default; entities with a richer natural key (`(board_id, card_number)`) can supply that instead.
- **`SortField` proliferation risk.** A naive design has one `SortField` enum per entity, which mirrors the runtime types but creates parallel sets. Acceptable if the domain stays small; if it grows, a `SortKey` trait associated with each entity (e.g. `trait Sortable { type Field: Copy + Eq; fn sorter_for(f: Self::Field) -> impl Sorter<Self>; }`) consolidates the dispatch.
- **Serde compatibility.** `SortOrder` is in `Board.task_sort_order` and round-trips as `"Ascending"`/`"Descending"`. Moving it from domain to core must keep the same `Serialize` representation or migrations break. Re-export from domain for the persistence boundary.
- **No new user-visible behaviour.** This is a refactor — pre/post behaviour must be identical for the existing Card sort. The win is unlocking future work (sprint sort, edge sort, cross-entity composition) without paying the duplication tax each time.

**Pros**: Pure mechanism in core, domain-aware adapters in domain — clean Dependency Inversion + Interface Segregation. Future sort surfaces (sprint sort UI, edge listing, cross-board card view) become a `Sorter<T>` impl rather than a whole new mechanism. Removes ~80 lines of duplicated sort plumbing once all the entity-specific surfaces migrate.
**Cons**: Generic bounds in `OrderedSorter<S, T, B>` add type-parameter noise to call sites. A naive impl has three type parameters that propagate to fields like `Filter.sort: Box<dyn Sorter<Card>>` or similar; the closure-based tiebreaker is awkward to store in structs unless boxed. Risk of over-generalising before the second user (sprint/edge sort) actually shows up.

---

### Alternative solutions

**1. Keep the mechanism in domain, add an entity-parameterised trait there.** Move `SortField` and `OrderedSorter` into a generic shape but stay inside `kanban-domain`. Smaller blast radius — no core change, no `SortOrder` migration — but leaves the layering wart that the generic machinery is co-located with `Card`. Worth doing as a stepping stone if the full core move feels premature.

**2. Skip generalisation, add per-entity sort modules ad-hoc.** When sprint sort or edge sort is eventually needed, copy-paste the Card pattern into `sprint/sort.rs`, `edge/sort.rs`, etc. Lowest immediate cost, highest long-term cost — every new variant means re-implementing ordering wrap + tiebreaker + None-last. Rejected by the user's premise: they want "everything in a list view to be sortable" with one mechanism.

**3. Adopt an existing crate (`sort-by`, `itertools::sorted_by`).** External crates handle the comparator combinators but none ship a deterministic-tiebreaker + serializable-direction-enum + persisted-default-resolution package. Would still need a thin wrapper for the kanban semantics; not a meaningful saving.

---

### Suggested phasing (TDD, SOLID-per-step commits)

1. **`feat(core): add SortOrder, Sorter, OrderedSorter` (with tests in core).** No dependent moves yet — core's new module exists in isolation, every existing site keeps working.
2. **`refactor(domain): re-export SortOrder from core, swap internal uses to the alias.** Persistence-visible serialisation unchanged.
3. **`refactor(domain): rewrite CardSortBy to impl core::Sorter<Card>, port existing OrderedSorter call sites.** Domain-internal change; the public `SortField`/`SortBy` API stays the same (or grows a `Card` prefix).
4. **`feat(domain): add SprintSortBy (Sorter<Sprint>) + sprint_sorter_for_field.** First non-Card consumer — proves the abstraction.
5. **`feat(service): extend ArchivedCardListFilter / CardListFilter to use the generic helper.** Service-layer `apply_sort` becomes type-parameterised.
6. **`feat(cli/mcp): expose sprint sort via the same flag/param plumbing as card sort.** Demonstrates the cross-frontend reach.
7. **Optional follow-up: edge sort.** Sorts `Vec<EdgeRef>` via a projection (`|e| &cards[e.child_id]`) and a `Sorter<Card>`.
cards.title: refactor sort into a generic core trait consumed by every listable entity

cards.description:
### Overview

**Where**: `crates/kanban-domain/src/operations.rs` is 590 lines after KAN-644 — well over the CONTRIBUTING.md ceiling of 300 per file. It bundles three unrelated concerns:

1. Filter shapes (`CardListFilter`, `ArchivedCardListFilter`).
2. Filter+sort engine (`filter_and_sort_cards`, `count_filtered_cards`, `passes_filter`, `build_searcher`, `allowed_column_ids`).
3. The `KanbanOperations` trait (top-level service contract every backend implements).

**Why**: SRP at the module level. Today a contributor opening `operations.rs` cannot tell whether they're in trait-contract territory, filter-engine internals, or filter type definitions. The trait surface keeps changing (every new domain feature adds a method) — keeping the engine code in the same file makes both harder to navigate and produces noisy diffs in PRs that should touch only one of the two.

**How**: Extract the filter+sort engine into a new module. The trait stays where it is.

---

### Target shape

```
crates/kanban-domain/src/
├── operations.rs       # CardListFilter, ArchivedCardListFilter, KanbanOperations trait
└── filter_sort.rs      # filter_and_sort_cards, count_filtered_cards, passes_filter,
                        # build_searcher, allowed_column_ids
```

Estimated line counts after the split:

- `operations.rs`: ~380 lines (filter structs + trait + default impl). Still above 300 — see "follow-up" below.
- `filter_sort.rs`: ~210 lines.

The default impl of `KanbanOperations::list_archived_cards_sorted` calls `filter_and_sort_cards`, so `operations.rs` keeps a `use crate::filter_sort::filter_and_sort_cards;` and the dependency arrow stays one-way (`operations` -> `filter_sort`).

---

### Engineering detail

~~~
crates/kanban-domain/src/operations.rs      -> keep: CardListFilter, ArchivedCardListFilter, KanbanOperations
crates/kanban-domain/src/filter_sort.rs     -> new: filter_and_sort_cards, count_filtered_cards,
                                                    passes_filter (private), build_searcher (private),
                                                    allowed_column_ids (private)
crates/kanban-domain/src/lib.rs             -> add `pub mod filter_sort;` and re-export the two
                                                public helpers alongside the existing
                                                `operations::*` re-exports
~~~

`passes_filter`, `build_searcher`, `allowed_column_ids` stay `pub(crate)` or private to `filter_sort.rs` — they were already file-private in `operations.rs`. The public surface of the new module is exactly the two functions consumers care about.

Re-exports preserve `kanban_domain::filter_and_sort_cards` and `kanban_domain::count_filtered_cards` so no downstream code (kanban-service, kanban-tui, kanban-domain::query) has to change. This is a zero-API-impact refactor.

---

### Tests

Unit tests for `filter_and_sort_cards` and `count_filtered_cards` move with their code into `filter_sort.rs`'s `#[cfg(test)]` module. Tests on `CardListFilter::Default` and the trait default impl stay in `operations.rs`. `cargo test -p kanban-domain` must remain green; `cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings` must remain clean.

---

### Considerations

- **`operations.rs` still over 300 after the split**: the `KanbanOperations` trait has ~30 methods and that's where the bulk lives. A second pass could split it by sub-domain (board ops, column ops, card ops, sprint ops, archive ops, batch ops), but that's a bigger architectural decision than this card.
- **No behavior change**: this is module reorganisation only. The PR diff should be a near-perfect "move + re-export" with maybe a `use` line update inside `query/mod.rs`.
- **Why not split immediately during KAN-644**: KAN-644 was already a wide PR (20 files). Splitting `operations.rs` would have buried the SSoT refactor under noise. Doing it as its own card keeps the diff reviewable.

---

### Pros / cons

**Pros**:
- Brings `operations.rs` and `filter_sort.rs` each closer to the 300-line ceiling.
- Cleaner SRP at module level: trait contract vs filter engine.
- Future PRs touching one don't show diff noise in the other.
- Zero downstream impact (re-exports preserve the public API).

**Cons**:
- `operations.rs` still over 300 lines after this card; full compliance requires a follow-up to also split the trait.

---

### Alternative solutions

**1. Split the `KanbanOperations` trait by sub-domain first.** Bigger architectural change; touches every implementor (`KanbanContext`, `CliContext`, `McpContext`, `TuiContext`). Rejected as the first move — extracting the engine is the cheaper, lower-risk win.

**2. Leave `operations.rs` at 590 lines.** The 300-line guideline is widely violated in the existing codebase. Rejected because the file genuinely conflates three responsibilities, and we just had a PR review flag it.
cards.title: split kanban-domain/src/operations.rs into trait + filter_sort engine
Tighten KAN-250 string validation (whitespace + sprint fields)
Preserve count of unacknowledged save errors in banner
Harmonize standalone (V1,V2)/(V1,V3)/(V2,V3) migration arms with the V→V7 outer backup wrap

cards.description:
## Stakeholders

- **All users opening a kanban file** with a binary that supports a newer format version (V6 → V7 today; V7 → V8 next time the format moves). Today they get silent file mutation; the request is explicit consent + clear feedback.
- **Users sharing a file across mixed-version environments** (one machine still on 0.6.x, another on 0.7.x). Silent auto-migration on the newer machine renders the file unreadable on the older one with no warning beforehand.
- **LLM clients via MCP** — currently see no signal at all when the server auto-migrates a file underneath them.
- **TUI users on long-lived sessions** — currently see no notice that a startup migration happened, even though `.v{N}.backup` files appear on disk.

## Where

### Persistence / migrator (the engine all three apps depend on)

- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/json_file_store.rs:395-406` — `load_sync` auto-triggers `migrate_to_v7_sync` when `current_version < V7`. No consent check; no user-facing message.
- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/json_file_store.rs:90-130` — `migrate_to_v7_sync` (sync orchestrator post-KAN-650).
- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs:49-122` — `Migrator::migrate` (async orchestrator).
- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/migrator.rs:14-46` — `Migrator::detect_version` / `detect_version_from_value` (the version probe).
- Existing tracing calls in `migrator.rs` and the sync helpers at INFO level (`"Created pre-V7 backup at ..."`, `"Migration to V7 verified, backup removed"`, per-step `"Migrated {path} from VN to VN+1"`). No timing, no progress indicator, no step-of-N counter.
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/error.rs:199-203` — `KanbanError::UnsupportedFutureVersion` exists for newer-than-binary files (KAN-522). No symmetric variant for newer-than-file binary cases.

### CLI

- `crates/kanban-cli/src/cli.rs:40,563-580` — `kanban migrate <source> <backend> [--output] [--source-backend]` **exists, but it's for cross-backend conversion** (JSON ↔ SQLite via `StoreManager::migrate_store`), not for in-version migration. Naming collision to resolve.
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/handlers/migrate.rs:20-35` — current handler prints two lines: `"Migrating {source} to {output}"` and `"Migration completed successfully"`. No format-version awareness.
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/cli/context.rs:17-31` — `CliContext::load()` is what triggers auto-migration on every command invocation.
- `crates/kanban-cli/src/app.rs:43` — default tracing level is `warn`. CLI users see **nothing** about migration unless they set `RUST_LOG=info`.
- No `--dry-run`, no `--no-migrate`, no flag of any kind to control migration behavior.

### MCP

- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/lib.rs:810-821` — `KanbanMcpServer::new` triggers the same silent auto-migration via `McpContext::new` → `KanbanContext::open` → first `DataStore` call.
- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/error.rs:32-52` — `UnsupportedFutureVersion` already maps to `INVALID_PARAMS`. No symmetric case for migration-required.
- `crates/kanban-mcp/src/server.rs:130` — default tracing level is `info` so operators see migration logs on stderr, but the LLM client sees nothing.

### TUI

- `crates/kanban-tui/src/app/mod.rs:229-293` — `App::new_with_store` awaits `KanbanContext::open` during construction. Migration happens before the first frame renders; the user sees nothing.
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/error_log.rs:132-248` — F12 diagnostics popup shows File / Format / Writer / Binary / Saved at. KAN-522 added the **forward-direction** "newer than this binary" yellow warning (`error_log.rs` writer-row logic). **No backward warning** for the binary-is-newer-than-file case.
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/ui/error_log.rs:46` — Format row has `warn: false` unconditionally.
- `crates/kanban-tui/src/components/footer.rs:88` — Footer hint "F12: diagnostics" only shows when the error log has unread entries; a routine migration log entry would trigger it, but the user wouldn't know that's what F12 is for.
- No load-time banner exists. KAN-174's banner (`render_save_error_bar` in `ui/mod.rs`) is save-only.

## Why

Three concrete failure modes today, all of them silent:

1. **Mixed-version data corruption.** User runs 0.7.0 against a file shared with a colleague's 0.6.x install. First open silently rewrites the file to V7. Colleague's next open errors with "unknown format version" with no diagnostic context. Today the user has no way to know this is about to happen.
2. **MCP agent mutation surprise.** LLM-driven workflow opens a kanban file through MCP. The MCP server silently migrates V6 → V7. Agent now writes through MCP. The file is no longer readable by the user's 0.6.x CLI if they have one. Agent has no signal of the format flip.
3. **No timing/progress on long migrations.** A board with thousands of cards through a multi-step chain (V3 → V4 → V5 → V6 → V7) could take seconds to migrate. TUI startup blocks during this with no indication that anything is happening; CLI returns control with no info about what changed.

Trade-off: silent migration is friendlier for the single-user single-machine common case. Explicit migration is friendlier for everyone else and is consistent with KAN-522's existing principle that the binary should refuse to silently mutate data when the operation is non-trivial.

## How

The work decomposes naturally into four layers. Each can land independently; later layers depend on earlier ones.

### Layer 1 — Persistence: new error variant + explicit-consent gate

- Add `DomainError::MigrationRequired { file_version: u32, binary_max: u32 }` (mirror of `UnsupportedFutureVersion`). Predicate `is_migration_required()` on `KanbanError`.
- Add a per-store config flag `auto_migrate: bool` (default `true` for 0.7.x backward-compat, flip default to `false` in a later major release). When `false`, both `load` and `load_sync` return `MigrationRequired` instead of triggering the migration chain.
- The `auto_migrate` flag flows from `AppConfig` through `StoreManager`. Each application can default-override (CLI/MCP eventually opt-out by default; TUI may stay opt-in).

### Layer 2 — Migrator: structured, timed, step-by-step logging + dry-run

- Introduce a `MigrationReport` struct: `{ from_version, to_version, steps: Vec<MigrationStep>, total_duration: Duration, backup_path: Option<PathBuf>, dry_run: bool }` where each `MigrationStep` is `{ from, to, duration, status: Ok | Failed(String) }`.
- `Migrator::migrate` and `migrate_to_v7_sync` return this report (today they return `()` / `Vec<u8>`). Each per-step function returns the duration it took.
- New `Migrator::migrate_dry_run` and `migrate_to_v7_sync_dry_run` siblings produce the same report shape without mutating the user's file. Implementation: copy the source file to a `tempfile::NamedTempFile` in the same parent directory, run the full migration chain against the copy (real transforms, real disk I/O, real failure detection — same fidelity as a live run), then delete the temp on return regardless of outcome. This catches the same class of mid-chain failures (e.g. V6 both-keys ambiguity) a real run would, without touching the user's file. Backup-path field of the dry-run report still says where the backup *would* be written, but no `.v{N}.backup` is created on disk.
- Replace ad-hoc `tracing::info!` calls with a single rendered `MigrationReport::to_user_string()` call at the end. Logs still fire per-step at DEBUG; the user-visible output is one cohesive multi-line message:
  ```
  Migrating kanban.json: V3 → V7
    V3 → V4: 12ms
    V4 → V5: 8ms
    V5 → V6 (split graph): 42ms
    V6 → V7 (rename spawns bucket): 6ms
  Backup written to kanban.v3.backup (will be removed on success)
  Migration complete in 68ms. Backup removed.
  ```
  For dry-run the leading verb changes and a footer is added:
  ```
  Dry-run: would migrate kanban.json: V3 → V7
    V3 → V4: 12ms
    ...
  Would write backup to kanban.v3.backup.
  No changes made to kanban.json.
  ```

### Layer 3 — CLI: explicit migration command + opt-out flag

Two ways to resolve the `kanban migrate` naming collision:

- **(A)** Keep `kanban migrate <source> <backend>` for cross-backend. Add `kanban upgrade [<path>]` for in-version migration. Two verbs, clear intent.
- **(B)** Promote `migrate` to a subcommand-of-subcommand: `kanban migrate backend <source> <backend>` and `kanban migrate version <path>`. One verb, hierarchical disambiguation. Breaking change for any existing `kanban migrate` user — but it's a new command from KAN-451 (PR #280) era, low usage.

Recommendation: **(A)** for less churn. Document in the changeset that `migrate` and `upgrade` are distinct.

`kanban upgrade` behavior:
- `kanban upgrade` (no args) — operates on the default file. Prints the `MigrationReport`. Idempotent: V7 → V7 reports "Already at V7, nothing to do" and exits 0.
- `kanban upgrade --dry-run` — prints what would happen without writing.
- `kanban upgrade --yes` — skip the confirmation prompt (default is to prompt for non-dry-run).
- All other commands gain `--allow-migrate` / `-y` (or read it from config) for one-shot consent without invoking `upgrade`. With Layer 1's `auto_migrate=false` default, every other command on a non-current file errors with a message pointing at `kanban upgrade`.

### Layer 4 — MCP: structured error + tool for migration (with dry-run)

- When opening a file that needs migration with `auto_migrate=false`, the MCP server returns an `INVALID_PARAMS` error whose message clearly states: `"file format v{file_version} is older than this binary's max v{binary_max}; the file requires a migration step. Run 'kanban upgrade {path}' from a shell, call the migrate_file tool with dry_run=true to preview, or call migrate_file with dry_run=false to apply, then retry."`.
- New MCP tool `migrate_file(path: String, dry_run: bool = false)` that triggers the migration on demand. Returns the structured `MigrationReport` as the tool result so the LLM can summarize for the user. Idempotent. With `dry_run=true` the tool returns the same report shape but no file changes occur — agents should default to running dry-run first and showing the report to the human before invoking with `dry_run=false`. The tool description explicitly directs LLM clients to use this two-step pattern.

### Layer 5 — TUI: dual-direction diagnostics warning + migration popup

- Extend `error_log.rs` row logic so the Format row colour-warns when `binary_max > file_version` (the migration-candidate case), with a `(file older than this binary; run 'kanban upgrade' to update)` suffix. Symmetric with the existing `(newer than this binary)` forward warning from KAN-522.
- Add a blocking startup popup when `auto_migrate=false` and the file needs migration: `"This file is at format V{N}. Press [M] to migrate now, [P] to preview (dry-run), [Q] to quit, [F12] for diagnostics."` Pressing `P` runs `migrate_to_v7_sync_dry_run` against the file and replaces the popup body with the rendered `MigrationReport` plus the new keys `[M]igrate now [B]ack [Q]uit`. Pressing `M` (either from the initial popup or from the dry-run preview) runs the real migration and replaces the popup with the post-migration `MigrationReport` and a single `[Esc] continue` action.
- Add a non-blocking notification banner (same surface family as KAN-174's save-error banner) for successful migrations: `"File migrated from V{from} to V{to} in {duration}. Backup at {path}."` Dismissible with Esc; auto-clears after 10s.

## Offender examples

```
# CLI today — silent
$ kanban card list
KAN-1  TODO  My task
# (file was just rewritten from V6 to V7 with no message)

$ ls
kanban.json  kanban.v6.backup
# (where did .v6.backup come from? what just happened?)
```

```
# MCP today — silent
# Agent calls list_boards tool
# Server silently migrates the file before responding
# Agent receives boards JSON; no signal that the file format changed
```

```
# TUI today — silent
$ cargo run
# (TUI splash → board view, no indication that V6 → V7 just happened)
# F12 → Format row reads "v7" with no contextual info that it WAS v6 thirty seconds ago
```

## Solution examples

```
# CLI explicit
$ kanban card list
Error: kanban.json is at format V6; this binary requires V7.
Run 'kanban upgrade kanban.json' to migrate, or pass --allow-migrate to upgrade in-place.

$ kanban upgrade kanban.json
Migrating kanban.json: V6 → V7
  V6 → V7 (rename spawns bucket): 8ms
Backup written to kanban.v6.backup (will be removed on success)
Migration complete in 8ms. Backup removed.

$ kanban upgrade kanban.json   # second run, idempotent
kanban.json is already at V7. Nothing to do.

$ kanban upgrade --dry-run kanban.json
Would migrate kanban.json from V6 to V7 (1 step). No file changes.
```

```
# MCP explicit (recommended two-step pattern: dry-run, then apply)
# Agent calls list_boards on a V6 file
# Server returns INVALID_PARAMS pointing at migrate_file.

# Step 1: agent dry-runs first to show the human what's about to happen.
# Agent calls migrate_file({"path": "kanban.json", "dry_run": true})
{
  "report": {
    "from_version": 6,
    "to_version": 7,
    "steps": [{"from": 6, "to": 7, "duration_ms": 8, "status": "ok"}],
    "total_duration_ms": 8,
    "backup_path": "kanban.v6.backup",
    "dry_run": true
  }
}
# Agent summarizes for the human, gets confirmation.

# Step 2: agent applies the migration.
# Agent calls migrate_file({"path": "kanban.json", "dry_run": false})
{
  "report": {
    "from_version": 6,
    "to_version": 7,
    "steps": [{"from": 6, "to": 7, "duration_ms": 8, "status": "ok"}],
    "total_duration_ms": 8,
    "backup_path": null,
    "dry_run": false
  }
}
# Agent retries list_boards, succeeds.
```

```
# TUI explicit (blocking popup on startup)
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  File requires migration                                 │
│                                                          │
│  kanban.json is at format V6.                           │
│  This binary (kanban 0.7.1) writes V7.                  │
│                                                          │
│  Migrating will rewrite the file in place. A backup     │
│  will be written to kanban.v6.backup.                   │
│                                                          │
│  [M]igrate now    [P]review (dry-run)                   │
│  [Q]uit           [F12] diagnostics                     │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# After [P]:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  Preview: would migrate kanban.json V6 → V7              │
│                                                          │
│    V6 → V7 (rename spawns bucket): 6ms                  │
│                                                          │
│  Would write backup to kanban.v6.backup.                │
│  No changes made.                                        │
│                                                          │
│  [M]igrate now    [B]ack    [Q]uit                      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# After [M]:
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│  ✓ Migrated kanban.json: V6 → V7 in 8ms                 │
│  Backup at kanban.v6.backup                              │
│  Press [Esc] to continue                                 │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

## Acceptance criteria

- `DomainError::MigrationRequired { file_version, binary_max }` variant added with `is_migration_required()` predicate.
- `MigrationReport` struct returned by `Migrator::migrate` and `migrate_to_v7_sync`; both report per-step timing.
- `kanban upgrade [<path>]` command exists, idempotent, supports `--dry-run` and `--yes`.
- CLI's other commands surface a clear `MigrationRequired` error message that names the required action when `auto_migrate=false`.
- MCP returns `INVALID_PARAMS` for migration-required cases with an actionable message; new `migrate_file(path, dry_run)` MCP tool exists with `dry_run` defaulting to `false`; tool description explicitly tells agents to run dry-run first and confirm with the human.
- Dry-run for all three surfaces uses the same in-temp-file mechanism in `Migrator`, so dry-run reports have the same fidelity as real runs (catches both-keys ambiguity and other mid-chain failure modes).
- TUI F12 diagnostics colour-warns the Format row when the binary is newer than the file (symmetric with KAN-522's forward warning).
- TUI shows a blocking startup popup for migration-required when `auto_migrate=false`, with M/P/Q/F12 actions. The dry-run preview shows the same `MigrationReport` content the CLI and MCP surfaces show.
- TUI shows a non-blocking success banner after a migration completes.
- `auto_migrate` defaults to `true` in this release for backward compatibility; document the planned flip in a future major bump.
- All three apps share the SAME error message template for migration-required, with the only variation being the application-appropriate "how to fix" suffix (CLI: `run 'kanban upgrade'`, MCP: `call migrate_file`, TUI: `press M in the popup`).
- Integration tests pin each of: idempotent `kanban upgrade` on V7 file, refusal under `auto_migrate=false`, structured `MigrationReport` shape, MCP `migrate_file` tool.

## Sizing and scope

**This card is ~13 points as a single deliverable.** Layers are independently shippable; a clean split would be:

- **Layer 1 + 2 (3pt med):** New error variant + `MigrationReport` plumbing. Foundation; nothing else builds without it.
- **Layer 3 (3pt med):** CLI `upgrade` command + opt-out flag wiring.
- **Layer 4 (3pt med):** MCP error mapping + `migrate_file` tool.
- **Layer 5 (5pt high):** TUI diagnostics symmetry + startup popup + success banner. Largest because it touches UI rendering and key handlers.

If the sprint capacity is tight, ship Layers 1+2 + a dim-the-future-direction `tracing::warn!` in 0.7.x and defer Layers 3-5 to 0.7.x+1. The user-visible improvement lands incrementally with no behavior breakage.

## Risks

- Flipping `auto_migrate=false` is a usability regression for the single-user single-machine case (the most common case). Keep it `true` by default for the foreseeable future; the value of this card is the **opt-in machinery**, not the default flip.
- `kanban upgrade` adds a verb collision with `kanban migrate` (cross-backend). Resolved via the recommendation in Layer 3 (A): two distinct verbs.
- TUI blocking startup popup means users can't read their board until they decide to migrate. Acceptable because the alternative (read but don't write) is a more confusing data-loss vector.
- The MCP `migrate_file` tool needs to be SAFE: should not be invoked accidentally by an LLM in a tight loop. Mark it with a strong "this rewrites the file in place" description; the LLM should ask the human first.

## Out of scope

- Re-architecting the entire persistence layer for version-tolerant reads (would be a separate, much larger effort).
- Cross-format migration (JSON ↔ SQLite). The existing `kanban migrate` handles that; this card is purely in-version.
- The pre-existing V2 → V7 backup gap (covered by KAN-660).
- Atomic-writer fsync (covered by KAN-658).
- Migrating boards or cards (different domain entirely; this card is about format versioning of the persistence file).
cards.title: Migration UX: explicit-consent migrate command, cross-app mismatch surfacing, step-by-step progress logs
Fault-injection mechanism for migration failure-preservation tests across V1/V2 sources

cards.description:
## Stakeholders

- **External library consumers** of `kanban-persistence-json::V1ToV2Migration` and the `kanban-persistence::MigrationStrategy` trait — if any exist, removing these is a breaking change for them.
- **Future maintainers** of the persistence module — one fewer abstraction to understand, one fewer public-API surface to keep stable.

## Where

- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/v1_to_v2.rs:1-23` — defines `pub struct V1ToV2Migration` and its `MigrationStrategy` impl. The impl body is a thin wrapper over `Migrator::migrate(from, to, path)`.
- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/migration/mod.rs:11` — `pub use v1_to_v2::V1ToV2Migration;` re-exports it.
- `crates/kanban-persistence/src/traits.rs:210` — defines `pub trait MigrationStrategy`. Its only impl in this workspace is `V1ToV2Migration`.

## Why

`V1ToV2Migration` exists for the historical "migration strategy" abstraction in `kanban-persistence::MigrationStrategy`. The intent was presumably to let multiple strategies coexist (e.g. per-version-pair or per-backend) and be swappable at runtime. In practice:

1. **No production callers in this workspace** use `V1ToV2Migration`. `grep -rn "V1ToV2Migration" crates/` finds only the definition, the re-export, and its own tests.
2. **No other `MigrationStrategy` impls exist.** The trait has exactly one implementor (the one we'd be auditing for removal). A trait with one impl is just a worse version of a free function.
3. **The wrapper adds zero value.** Both methods delegate straight to `Migrator::migrate*` with no transformation, validation, or composition.
4. **It's actively misleading post-KAN-660.** As documented in the KAN-660 changeset, calling `V1ToV2Migration::migrate(V1, V2, ...)` standalone no longer writes a `.v1.backup`. A library consumer reading "this is a migration strategy" probably expects backup semantics that the wrapper doesn't actually provide.

External uptake is unknown but plausibly zero — `kanban-persistence-json` is a niche internal-ish crate of a kanban tool. Worth checking before removal, but the expected answer is "delete both."

## How

1. **Audit step**: search crates.io and GitHub for any public uptake of `V1ToV2Migration` or external `MigrationStrategy` impls. Examples to grep:
   - `cargo download kanban-persistence-json --version '*'` reverse-deps lookup
   - GitHub code search `"V1ToV2Migration" language:rust`
   - GitHub code search `"impl MigrationStrategy" language:rust`
2. **If zero external uptake found**: file follow-up PR to delete `v1_to_v2.rs`, drop the re-export from `migration/mod.rs`, and delete the `MigrationStrategy` trait from `kanban-persistence`. Update `Migrator::migrate` doc to point external callers at `Migrator::migrate` directly.
3. **If non-trivial external uptake found**: file follow-up PR to mark both `#[deprecated(since = "0.X.0", note = "use Migrator::migrate directly")]` and plan removal for a later major release. Add a no-op cycle so external users see compile-time warnings.

Either way: the changeset for the eventual removal must be `bump: major` (or aligned with the next major bump) because deleting public items is breaking by Rust semver convention.

## Offender example

```rust
// Current public API surface — confusing because:
// (a) it has the same signature as Migrator::migrate with nothing added,
// (b) post-KAN-660, calling .migrate(V1, V2, path) no longer writes the
//     backup a "migration strategy" name implies.
pub struct V1ToV2Migration;

#[async_trait::async_trait]
impl MigrationStrategy for V1ToV2Migration {
    async fn detect_version(&self, path: &Path) -> PersistenceResult<FormatVersion> {
        Migrator::detect_version(path).await  // pure delegation
    }
    async fn migrate(&self, from: FormatVersion, to: FormatVersion, path: &Path)
        -> PersistenceResult<PathBuf>
    {
        Migrator::migrate(from, to, path).await?;  // pure delegation
        Ok(path.to_path_buf())
    }
}
```

## Solution example

Post-deletion: external consumers (if any) call `Migrator::migrate` and `Migrator::detect_version` directly. The `MigrationStrategy` trait is gone; `V1ToV2Migration` is gone; the persistence API surface is one trait and one struct smaller.

```rust
// External consumer migration path:
// Before:
let strategy = V1ToV2Migration;
strategy.migrate(V1, V7, &path).await?;

// After:
Migrator::migrate(V1, V7, &path).await?;
```

## Acceptance criteria

- Audit completed; external uptake quantified (zero, or specific consumers identified).
- Deletion PR opened (or deprecation PR if uptake is found).
- `MigrationStrategy` trait removed from `kanban-persistence` if removal-path is chosen.
- `kanban-persistence-json::migration::v1_to_v2` module deleted.
- All existing tests continue to pass (the wrapper's own tests get deleted along with the module).
- Changeset: `bump: minor` for deprecation, `bump: major` for removal.

## Out of scope

- Replacing the trait with a different abstraction (no use case for one yet; if a real second strategy emerges, design at that point).
- Renaming or restructuring `Migrator` itself (covered by KAN-661 if the user-experience refactor reshapes the migration API).

## Why low priority + post-major-bump

- Zero in-repo callers; no behavioural impact on the kanban binary or any of the workspace crates.
- Removing public API is breaking by Rust semver — needs deliberate major-bump cycle.
- Cleanup, not gap-closure. No defect resolved.

Defer until the next major release window, possibly alongside KAN-661's broader migration-UX overhaul (which would naturally retire the strategy abstraction if it persists in a redesigned API).
cards.title: Audit V1ToV2Migration strategy + MigrationStrategy trait for retirement (post-major)

cards.description:
## Stakeholders

- **Release manager (Max)** — needs a sane recovery path after a post-`Push to master` failure in the release workflow.
- **Future maintainers** — should not need to memorize the manual fallback commands from `docs/release-recovery.md` for every release surface.

## Where

- `.github/workflows/release.yml` — top-level `on:` block, `release` job condition (line 20), per-step `if:` conditions throughout.
- `docs/release-recovery.md` — every "If you must finish by hand" section becomes "trigger workflow_dispatch with input: …" after this card lands.

## Why

Today, re-running the release workflow after the `Push to master` step has succeeded is a **silent no-op**. The `Check for changesets` step at the top of the `release` job exits cleanly when `.changeset/` is empty, and downstream steps are gated on `steps.check.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'`. Once the release commit (which deletes the changesets via `aggregate-changelog.sh`) is on master, a fresh checkout sees zero changesets and skips everything.

This means the entire failure surface from `Publish to crates.io` (line 105) through `Sync develop with master` (line 219) is **not recoverable via GitHub Actions today**. The runbook at `docs/release-recovery.md` documents manual `git tag`, `gh release create`, `cargo publish`, `git push` to AUR / Homebrew, and `choco push` flows — each requires the maintainer to have local credentials that normally only live in GHA secrets, which is a security regression.

A `workflow_dispatch` trigger with a `tag` input would let the release manager:
1. Click "Run workflow" in the GH Actions UI.
2. Enter `v0.7.0` (the tag that was already created in the failed run, or that they want to publish).
3. The workflow re-executes the publish surfaces (crates.io, GitHub Release, AUR, Homebrew, Windows build, Chocolatey) against the tagged commit, using their existing per-step idempotency guards to skip whatever already succeeded.

No secrets leave the GHA secret store. Recovery becomes a one-click action instead of a multi-step credentials shuffle.

## How

### Design

Two complementary changes:

**1. Add `workflow_dispatch` trigger with a `tag` input.**

```yaml
on:
  pull_request:
    branches:
      - master
    types:
      - closed
  workflow_dispatch:
    inputs:
      tag:
        description: "Existing tag to re-publish (e.g. v0.7.0). Skips bump/commit/push and runs the publish surfaces."
        required: true
        type: string
```

**2. Gate the bump/commit/push steps to the merge-trigger path; gate the publish steps to both paths.**

Per-step `if:` conditions need to widen. Pattern:

```yaml
# Bump + commit + push: merge-trigger only
- name: Bump version
  if: github.event_name == 'pull_request' && steps.check.outputs.has_changesets == 'true'
  ...

# Publish surfaces: both paths
- name: Publish to crates.io
  if: |
    (github.event_name == 'pull_request' && steps.check.outputs.has_changesets == 'true')
    || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
  ...
```

The `release` job's top-level condition also widens:

```yaml
if: |
  (github.event_name == 'pull_request' && github.event.pull_request.merged == true)
  || github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
```

**3. For the dispatch path, populate `steps.release.outputs.new` from the input.**

The `Commit release` step (line 83-91) currently sets `steps.release.outputs.new` from the bumped Cargo.toml. On the dispatch path, that step is skipped, so we need a parallel step that extracts the version from the input tag:

```yaml
- name: Extract version from dispatch input
  if: github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch'
  id: release_from_input
  run: echo "new=${INPUT_TAG#v}" >> "$GITHUB_OUTPUT"
  env:
    INPUT_TAG: ${{ inputs.tag }}
```

Then the job-level `outputs.new` becomes a coalesce:

```yaml
outputs:
  new: ${{ steps.release.outputs.new || steps.release_from_input.outputs.new }}
```

**4. Checkout the tag on the dispatch path, master on the merge path.**

```yaml
- uses: actions/checkout@v4
  with:
    fetch-depth: 0
    ref: ${{ github.event_name == 'workflow_dispatch' && inputs.tag || 'master' }}
```

### Per-step idempotency relies on what KAN-649 already established

The dispatch-triggered re-execution depends on the per-step guards already in place:
- crates.io: per-crate "already exists" skip in `scripts/publish-crates.sh`
- Git tag: exists-check guard (KAN-649)
- GitHub Release: `softprops/action-gh-release@v2` upserts
- AUR: empty-commit skip + `allow_empty_commits: false`
- Homebrew: empty-commit skip
- Chocolatey: OData pre-check (KAN-657)

So re-running the publish chain via dispatch is safe — anything already done is skipped.

## Risks

- **Wrong tag input.** A maintainer typo could re-trigger publish against an unintended tag. Mitigation: the `actions/checkout` step will fail fast if the tag does not exist. Worst case is a green workflow that did nothing (because every guard sees the version is already published).
- **Concurrent dispatch + merge.** Already mitigated by the existing `concurrency: { group: release, cancel-in-progress: false }` block.
- **Dispatch by any maintainer with write access.** Currently every collaborator with `write` can trigger workflow_dispatch. For a single-maintainer project this is fine; document the constraint in the runbook.
- **`build-windows` and `publish-chocolatey` jobs use `needs.release.outputs.new`.** After the coalesce-output change, both should keep working. Verify with a dry-run dispatch on a test tag before relying on it.
- **Sync develop with master step (line 219).** This step runs at the very end of the release job. On a dispatch re-run it would attempt to sync again; the rebase/merge logic must be re-entrant. Audit before adding.

## Offender example

Today, if `Publish to crates.io` fails after `Push to master` succeeded:

```bash
# Maintainer's local machine, with credentials normally locked in GHA:
export CARGO_REGISTRY_TOKEN=$(pass show kanban/cargo-token)
git checkout master && git pull
cargo publish -p kanban-core --allow-dirty
cargo publish -p kanban-domain --allow-dirty
# ... 7 more crates, with 10s sleeps, hoping nothing else fails
git tag v0.7.0
git push origin v0.7.0
gh release create v0.7.0 --generate-notes
# Then AUR, Homebrew, Windows build, Chocolatey... all by hand
```

## Solution example

After this card:

```
GH Actions UI → Workflows → Release → Run workflow → tag: v0.7.0 → Run
```

The workflow re-executes the publish surfaces using the same code path as the merge-trigger. Per-step guards skip whatever is done. Maintainer watches the run.

## Acceptance criteria

- `workflow_dispatch` trigger added with a required `tag` input.
- `release` job's top-level `if:` widened to accept both triggers.
- Bump/commit/push steps gated to `pull_request` event only.
- Publish steps gated to accept both events.
- `steps.release.outputs.new` is correctly populated on both paths (extract-from-input step on dispatch).
- `actions/checkout` uses the input tag on dispatch, `master` on merge.
- `build-windows` and `publish-chocolatey` jobs work unchanged (consume `needs.release.outputs.new`).
- `docs/release-recovery.md` updated: every "manual fallback" section gains a "dispatch fallback" alternative.
- End-to-end smoke test: dispatch against an already-published tag (e.g. v0.7.0 after release) returns green with every step skipped via its guard.

## Out of scope

- A "Sync develop with master" idempotency audit — file separately if the audit surfaces issues.
- Reusable workflow extraction (splitting `release` job into a `bump-commit-push.yml` + reusable `publish.yml`). Cleaner long-term but a bigger lift; deferred unless complexity demands it.
- Notification/alerting on a half-successful dispatch run — covered by the sibling chocolatey-notification card.
- Auth scoping (restricting dispatch to specific GitHub teams) — fine for single-maintainer now.

## Sizing

~3pt. The YAML changes are mechanical but touch many `if:` conditions. The output-coalesce and dispatch-input-extract steps are small additions. The riskiest piece is the smoke test — needs an end-to-end dispatch against a real tag, which can only be done once 0.7.0 has shipped.

## Why post-0.7.0

- The card surfaces a real gap (re-run after Push to master is a no-op for publish steps), but the manual recovery path in `docs/release-recovery.md` is sufficient as a stopgap.
- Touching the release workflow during a release cycle is high-risk; better to land this between releases and prove it on the next dispatch.

## References

- KAN-649 ([PR #324](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/pull/324)) — the parent card that surfaced this gap during review.
- KAN-657 — per-step Chocolatey pre-check that this design depends on for idempotency.
- KAN-656 — per-step asset-poll-via-gh-api that this design depends on for idempotency.
cards.title: Add workflow_dispatch recovery trigger to release.yml

cards.description:
## Stakeholders

- **Release manager (Max)** — needs to know when a Chocolatey publish failed silently without manually inspecting every workflow run.
- **Downstream Chocolatey users** — would otherwise be stuck on a stale version with no signal that the publish broke.

## Where

- `.github/workflows/release.yml` — `publish-chocolatey` job (lines 309-429). Specifically, the existing pre-check + try/catch around `choco push` (lines 378-416, from KAN-657) and the new `continue-on-error: true` at job level (line 314, from KAN-649).
- `docs/release-recovery.md` — `## Job: publish-chocolatey` section already documents the silent-failure caveat and points at this card.

## Why

KAN-649 added `continue-on-error: true` to the `publish-chocolatey` job so that a stuck moderation queue or transient API failure does not turn the entire release workflow red after crates.io, GitHub Release, AUR, and Homebrew have already succeeded. The trade-off is that GitHub Actions only sends email notifications on workflow-level failures, not on `continue-on-error` job failures — so a failed Chocolatey publish becomes a silent miss.

Failure modes for the silent miss:
- Six months later: "why is Chocolatey at 0.5.0 when crates.io shipped 0.7.0 ages ago?"
- New maintainer onboarding: assumes Chocolatey is up to date because the release workflow is green.
- Compounding silent misses: every patch release that fails Chocolatey adds another version behind, and the gap is never noticed until a user complains.

The fix is a visible-failure surface inside the chocolatey job that triggers on any non-success path (pre-check skip is not a failure — only `choco push` exit-non-zero or unhandled error).

## How

Three layers, increasing in infrastructure cost. Pick a subset for v1.

### Layer 1 (zero infra, recommended for v1)

Add an `if: failure()` step at the end of `publish-chocolatey` that writes a GitHub `::warning::` annotation and a `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` markdown block:

```yaml
- name: Surface chocolatey failure
  if: failure()
  shell: bash
  run: |
    echo "::warning title=Chocolatey publish failed::v$VERSION did not publish to Chocolatey. See packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md for recovery steps."
    {
      echo "## ⚠️ Chocolatey publish failed for v$VERSION"
      echo ""
      echo "The Chocolatey push step exited non-zero. The rest of the release"
      echo "(crates.io, GitHub Release, AUR, Homebrew) shipped successfully."
      echo ""
      echo "**Next step:** follow [packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md](../packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md)."
    } >> "$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY"
  env:
    VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
```

**What this gets you:**
- The warning annotation appears at the top of the PR's checks panel and at the top of the workflow run summary in the GH UI.
- The step summary is rendered as markdown on the run summary page.
- Zero new permissions, zero new secrets, zero external infrastructure.

**What it does NOT get you:**
- Push notification (email, Slack, etc.). The maintainer still has to look at the run.

### Layer 2 (adds `issues: write` permission, persistent record)

Add a step that opens (or comments on) a GitHub issue when the push fails:

```yaml
- name: File issue for chocolatey failure
  if: failure()
  shell: bash
  env:
    GH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
    VERSION: ${{ needs.release.outputs.new }}
  run: |
    gh issue create \
      --title "Chocolatey publish failed for v$VERSION" \
      --label "release-failure,chocolatey" \
      --body "Workflow run: $GITHUB_SERVER_URL/$GITHUB_REPOSITORY/actions/runs/$GITHUB_RUN_ID\n\nFollow packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md."
```

Requires `permissions: { contents: read, issues: write }` on the chocolatey job. The `release-failure` label needs to exist (one-time setup or auto-create).

**De-duplication risk:** if Chocolatey is broken for weeks, every release creates a fresh issue. Mitigation options:
- Use `gh issue list --label release-failure --state open` first and comment on the existing issue rather than open a new one.
- Accept the duplication — multiple issues is a feature, not a bug, because each represents a release that needs recovery.

### Layer 3 (adds external webhook, push notification)

A Slack/Discord webhook step that POSTs on failure. Requires adding a `RELEASE_FAILURE_WEBHOOK_URL` secret. **Skip this for v1** — the project has no existing webhook infrastructure and Max is the sole maintainer; layers 1+2 are sufficient.

## Risks

- **Annotation noise.** A single warning per failed release is fine. If we ever add `continue-on-error` to other jobs (build-windows asset upload, AUR push), the pattern needs to scale.
- **Issue-creation permission scope.** Granting `issues: write` to a job that already has `contents: read` is a small privilege escalation; acceptable for a maintainer-controlled workflow.
- **De-duplication footgun.** As noted, weeks of broken Chocolatey = wall of issues. Solvable by the comment-on-existing-issue pattern but adds complexity.
- **`if: failure()` semantics with `continue-on-error: true`.** Per GH Actions docs, step-level `if: failure()` *inside* a `continue-on-error: true` job evaluates against the job's "actual" status, not the workflow-propagated success. So the surface step does fire when an earlier step failed. Verify with a deliberate-failure test before relying on it.

## Offender example

Today (after KAN-649):

```
Workflow run: ✅ green (no email sent)
└─ release           ✅ ok
└─ build-windows     ✅ ok
└─ publish-chocolatey ❌ red (silent — continue-on-error swallows it)
```

The maintainer goes about their day. Six weeks later a Chocolatey user opens an issue: "kanban on choco is still 0.6.0."

## Solution example

After this card (Layer 1):

```
Workflow run: ✅ green with a yellow ⚠️ banner: "Chocolatey publish failed for v0.7.0"
PR checks panel: warning annotation visible without clicking through.
Run summary page: markdown block with the recovery runbook link.
```

After Layer 2 (optional):

```
GH issue auto-filed: #NNN "Chocolatey publish failed for v0.7.0" (label: release-failure)
Maintainer's GH inbox: notification on issue assignment.
```

## Acceptance criteria

- Layer 1: an `if: failure()` step in `publish-chocolatey` writes a `::warning::` annotation AND a `$GITHUB_STEP_SUMMARY` markdown block linking to `packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md`.
- Verified via deliberate-failure test: a synthetic failure in the `choco push` step produces the annotation and summary without failing the workflow.
- Layer 2 (optional, decide at implementation time): `if: failure()` step opens a GitHub issue with label `release-failure`, including a link to the workflow run.
- `docs/release-recovery.md` updated: the "silent failure" caveat in the `publish-chocolatey` section is replaced with "you will see a warning annotation; recovery is in packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md".
- The new step is itself `continue-on-error: true` (or wrapped in try/catch) so a failure to write the annotation does not turn the workflow red, defeating the purpose of KAN-649.

## Out of scope

- Slack/Discord webhook integration (Layer 3).
- Notification surface for `build-windows` or any other future `continue-on-error: true` job — file separately when that comes up.
- Auto-recovery (e.g. workflow retries `choco push` on transient failure with backoff) — Chocolatey's moderation queue makes auto-retry harmful, not helpful.
- Audit of every `continue-on-error: true` use across all workflows — there is only one today.

## Sizing

~1pt. Single YAML step, zero external dependencies for Layer 1. Layer 2 is another ~1pt if pursued.

## Why post-0.7.0

- KAN-649 already lands the `continue-on-error: true` change with a runbook section that documents the silent-failure trade-off. The trade-off is acceptable as a stopgap.
- Adding notification is a UX layer on top of correct behaviour; not a regression to fix urgently.
- Better landed between releases when it can be exercised by a real failure (or a deliberate-failure test).

## References

- KAN-649 ([PR #324](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/pull/324)) — the parent card that added `continue-on-error: true`.
- KAN-657 — the per-step pre-check + try/catch + RECOVERY.md that this card surfaces.
- `packaging/chocolatey/RECOVERY.md` — the runbook this card links from the failure annotation.
cards.title: Add visible-failure notification for publish-chocolatey

cards.description:
## Stakeholders

- **Future maintainers** adding new entity types to the domain (e.g. tags, attachments, comments) — should not have to read the variant doc-comment to learn the casing convention; the type system should make it impossible to get wrong.
- **Library consumers** pattern-matching on `entity` fields — would gain compile-time exhaustiveness checking instead of relying on stringly-typed string comparisons.
- **Reviewers** of future PRs that touch `KanbanError::not_found(...)` / `not_found_by_name(...)` / `ambiguous(...)` / `batch_resolution_failed(...)` — would not need to verify the entity tag's casing by inspection.

## Where

- `crates/kanban-domain/src/error.rs:84-125` — `DomainError` enum. Four variants take an `entity: &'static str` field:
  - `NotFound { entity, id }`
  - `NotFoundByName { entity, name, available }`
  - `Ambiguous { entity, name, matches }`
  - `BatchResolutionFailed { entity, failures }`
- `crates/kanban-domain/src/error.rs:234-271` — `KanbanError::not_found` / `not_found_by_name` / `ambiguous` / `batch_resolution_failed` constructors. All take `entity: &'static str`.
- ~70 call sites across the workspace using lowercase-then-capitalized convention (post-KAN-659 all capitalized: `"Board"` / `"Card"` / `"Column"` / `"Sprint"`).
- `crates/kanban-service/tests/create_card_with_sprint.rs:117` — one of the few pattern-match consumers that compares `entity` against a string literal.

## Why

KAN-659 normalized entity-tag casing across `NotFound` and `NotFoundByName` to capitalized. The convention is now documented in the variant doc-comments:

> "`entity` is rendered as a sentence-leading noun (e.g. `"Card"`, `"Sprint"`). All call sites of `KanbanError::not_found(entity, id)` must pass a capitalized noun..."

A doc-comment is the weakest form of enforcement:
- A future contributor who misses the doc-comment can add `KanbanError::not_found("widget", id)` for a new entity and reintroduce the asymmetry. The existing tests only catch this for the four current entity types (`Card` / `Column` / `Sprint` / `Board`); a new fifth entity slips through.
- A typo (e.g. `"Crad"` instead of `"Card"`) compiles fine, ships to production, and the user sees `"Crad <uuid> not found"`. No tests reference `"Crad"` so no failure.
- Pattern-match consumers (`match entity { "Card" => ..., "Sprint" => ... }`) silently mis-handle typos as the catch-all arm.

The structural fix is to replace `entity: &'static str` with a typed enum:

```rust
pub enum Entity { Board, Card, Column, Sprint }

impl fmt::Display for Entity {
    fn fmt(&self, f: &mut fmt::Formatter<'_>) -> fmt::Result {
        match self {
            Entity::Board => f.write_str("Board"),
            Entity::Card => f.write_str("Card"),
            Entity::Column => f.write_str("Column"),
            Entity::Sprint => f.write_str("Sprint"),
        }
```
