```rust
    }
}
```

Then:
- Typos at construction become compile errors.
- Casing convention is structurally enforced, not commented.
- Adding a new entity is one enum variant + one Display arm; the compiler walks contributors through every call site that needs an update via exhaustiveness checks.

## How

Five mechanical steps. Each lands as one commit on a single branch.

### Step 1: introduce `Entity` enum

Add `pub enum Entity { Board, Card, Column, Sprint }` in `crates/kanban-domain/src/entity.rs` (new file). `Display` impl renders the canonical capitalized form. `Debug` derive for tests. `PartialEq`, `Eq`, `Hash`, `Copy`, `Clone` for ergonomic use.

Optional convenience: `impl From<Entity> for &'static str` so existing string-using code paths can be migrated incrementally if needed. (Probably not needed; the four `DomainError` fields are the only consumers.)

### Step 2: switch `DomainError` field types

`entity: &'static str` → `entity: Entity` on all four variants (`NotFound`, `NotFoundByName`, `Ambiguous`, `BatchResolutionFailed`). Update the `#[error("...")]` attributes and the `fmt_not_found_by_name` / `fmt_ambiguous` / `fmt_batch_resolution_failed` formatters to take `entity: Entity` and use the Display impl.

### Step 3: switch `KanbanError` constructor signatures

`KanbanError::not_found(entity: &'static str, id: Uuid)` → `KanbanError::not_found(entity: Entity, id: Uuid)`. Same for the three siblings.

### Step 4: update all call sites

`grep -rn "KanbanError::not_found\b" crates/ --include="*.rs"` and similar for the three siblings. ~70 sites. Mechanical:
- `KanbanError::not_found("Board", id)` → `KanbanError::not_found(Entity::Board, id)`
- `KanbanError::not_found_by_name("Sprint", name, available)` → `KanbanError::not_found_by_name(Entity::Sprint, name, available)`

The compiler walks contributors through every site after the signature change.

### Step 5: update pattern-match consumers

One site at `crates/kanban-service/tests/create_card_with_sprint.rs:117` compares `entity` against `"Sprint"`. After this card, becomes `Entity::Sprint`. Plus any test that does string comparison on the variant field — grep `entity\s*==` in the test directories to find them.

Doc-comments on the variants can be simplified — the "must be capitalized" requirement disappears because it's structurally enforced.

### Test plan

- Existing tests all keep passing after mechanical conversion of their string assertions.
- Add a smoke test: `assert_eq!(Entity::Sprint.to_string(), "Sprint")` for each variant. Pins the user-facing rendering.
- No new behavioural tests needed — this is a refactor, not a feature.

## Offender example

Today:

```rust
// Constructs a bogus entity tag; compiles fine; runtime renders "widget X not found"
return Err(KanbanError::not_found("widget", id));

// Typo; compiles fine; ships
return Err(KanbanError::not_found("Crad", id));
```

```rust
// Pattern-match consumer; the "Crad" arm above falls through to the catch-all
match entity {
    "Card" => /* handle card-specific case */,
    "Sprint" => /* handle sprint-specific case */,
    _ => /* generic */,  // <-- "Crad" lands here silently
}
```

## Solution example

After this card:

```rust
// Compile error: no Entity::Widget variant
return Err(KanbanError::not_found(Entity::Widget, id));
//                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ help: variants in `Entity`: `Board`, `Card`, `Column`, `Sprint`

// Compile error: no Entity::Crad variant
return Err(KanbanError::not_found(Entity::Crad, id));
//                                ^^^^^^^^^^^^ help: similar variant exists: `Card`
```

```rust
// Exhaustiveness check catches new entity additions
match entity {
    Entity::Card => /* ... */,
    Entity::Sprint => /* ... */,
    // Compile error: non-exhaustive patterns: `Board`, `Column` not covered
}
```

## Acceptance criteria

- New `pub enum Entity { Board, Card, Column, Sprint }` in `kanban-domain` with `Display` rendering the canonical capitalized form.
- All four `DomainError` variants use `entity: Entity` instead of `entity: &'static str`.
- All four `KanbanError::*` constructors take `entity: Entity`.
- All ~70 call sites migrated to `Entity::Foo` variants.
- The `_ "must pass a capitalized noun" _` doc-comment guidance on the variants is removed (the type system now enforces it).
- All workspace tests pass after mechanical string-to-enum conversion.
- One smoke test per variant pins the `Display` output.
- Changeset notes the public API change for library consumers exhaustively matching on these fields.

## Out of scope

- Adding new entities (e.g. `Tag`, `Attachment`). The enum is intentionally minimal to match the current call sites; new entities get added with their first user.
- Replacing the `entity: &'static str` in `BatchResolutionFailure` (a non-error type that may have different conventions; audit separately).
- Migrating the `MigrationStrategy` trait's stringly-typed surfaces (covered by KAN-664 if filed).

## Sizing

~3-5pt. Each step is mechanical, but step 4 touches many files. Worth a single PR rather than split, because the migration is type-safe and the compiler walks reviewers through every site.

## Why post-0.7.0

- This is a refactor, not a fix or feature.
- It is a public API breaking change for library consumers (the `entity` field type changes). Should land in a deliberate minor or major bump, not in the patch-fix cadence.
- KAN-659 already provides the user-visible benefit (consistent capitalization). This card hardens against regressions; it's defensive depth, not gap-closure for any reachable defect today.

## References

- KAN-659 ([PR #323](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/pull/323)) — the convention-via-doc-comment fix this card supersedes.
- KAN-655 ([PR #320](https://github.com/fulsomenko/kanban/pull/320)) — where the asymmetry was first surfaced.
cards.title: Replace &'static str entity tags with typed Entity enum on DomainError variants

cards.description:
## Stakeholders

- **All users of the JSON backend** — power loss or kernel panic during save can lose committed work.
- **Users on FS configurations with weak ordering guarantees** (network mounts, some FUSE setups, ZFS without sync writes).

## Where

- `crates/kanban-persistence-json/src/atomic_writer.rs:13-43` — `AtomicWriter::write` (temp file + rename).

## Why

`AtomicWriter` correctly uses the temp-file + rename pattern, which protects against partial-write corruption (the rename is atomic from the user's perspective). But neither the temp file nor the parent directory is fsynced before the rename. The full guarantee chain to survive a power loss is:

1. Write all bytes to the temp file.
2. **fsync the temp file** — forces data blocks to durable storage.
3. Rename temp → target.
4. **fsync the parent directory** — forces the directory entry change to durable storage.

Skip (2): the rename can land in the metadata journal while the data blocks are still in cache, and a crash leaves the target file pointing at undefined or zero blocks. Skip (4): the rename itself can be lost across a crash, leaving the user with the old file (or no file, if there was no prior version).

`tempfile::NamedTempFile::persist()` (which the code uses) does not fsync automatically — it just does the rename.

This is not a release blocker because the failure mode requires power loss or kernel panic during the millisecond window between write and rename. But for a persistence-focused tool with multi-board user data, defense in depth is warranted.

## How

```rust
use std::fs::File;
use std::os::unix::io::AsRawFd;

pub fn write(path: &Path, bytes: &[u8]) -> Result<(), AtomicWriterError> {
    let parent = path.parent().ok_or(AtomicWriterError::NoParent)?;
    let mut tmp = tempfile::NamedTempFile::new_in(parent)?;
    tmp.write_all(bytes)?;
    tmp.as_file().sync_all()?;             // (2) data + metadata to disk
    let _persisted = tmp.persist(path)?;   // (3) atomic rename
    
    // (4) fsync the parent directory so the directory entry change is durable
    let dir = File::open(parent)?;
    dir.sync_all()?;
    Ok(())
}
```

Note: on Windows, `File::sync_all` on a directory is a no-op; NTFS journals the rename so we get the equivalent guarantee for free. Add a cfg-gate or simply let the no-op slide.

Note: `sync_all` is heavyweight; benchmark on a representative board to confirm the latency is acceptable (typically < 5ms on SSD, possibly tens of ms on HDD).

## Offender example

```
T+0ms:  write bytes to /tmp/kanban-XXXX.tmp
T+1ms:  rename /tmp/kanban-XXXX.tmp → ~/notes/memes/kanban.json
T+2ms:  *** POWER LOSS ***

# Post-reboot:
$ cat ~/notes/memes/kanban.json
<empty or zero-filled file>
# Filesystem journal recorded the rename but the data blocks
# never made it out of the page cache.
```

## Solution example

```rust
#[test]
fn test_atomic_write_fsyncs_data_and_parent() {
    // Hard to test fsync directly; use strace/ktrace in a manual verification.
    // Instead, assert that on a corruption-injection FS (loopback FUSE),
    // a sequence of write+kill leaves the file readable as either old or new,
    // never garbage.
}
```

Manual verification:

```
$ strace -e fsync,fdatasync,rename cargo run -- card create ...
fsync(3) = 0          # temp file fsync (NEW)
rename("/tmp/...", "/home/.../kanban.json") = 0
fsync(4) = 0          # parent dir fsync (NEW)
```

## Acceptance criteria

- `AtomicWriter::write` calls `sync_all` on the temp file before persist.
- `AtomicWriter::write` calls `sync_all` on the parent directory after persist.
- An `strace` of a save confirms both fsyncs are present.
- A microbenchmark documents the latency impact (expected: single-digit ms on SSD).
- This is a 0.7.1+ ticket, not a 0.7.0 blocker.
cards.title: fsync atomic JSON writer and parent dir (post-0.7.0)
